topics.title: Levels of Language Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Levels of Language Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are the Levels of Language Analysis?

In **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, natural language is a multi-layered, hierarchical system. A sentence is not a flat string of characters; it is a structured object built from several independent but interconnected linguistic layers. Each layer (or *level*) is analyzed by a specific NLP subsystem to progressively extract structure and meaning from raw text.

The **six canonical levels of language analysis** recognized in classical NLP theory (as per the standard KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

| # | Level | Core Concern | Unit of Analysis |
|---|-------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Phonology / Phonetics** | Sound patterns of spoken language | Phonemes, syllables |
| 2 | **Morphology** | Internal structure of words | Morphemes, lexemes |
| 3 | **Lexical Analysis** | Word identification & vocabulary lookup | Tokens, lemmas, parts-of-speech |
| 4 | **Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)** | Sentence structure & grammar | Parse trees, phrases |
| 5 | **Semantic Analysis** | Literal meaning of sentences | Logical forms, predicate-argument structures |
| 6 | **Discourse & Pragmatics** | Meaning beyond the single sentence | Utterances, speech acts, references |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Language analysis is a layered pipeline in which each higher level (syntax, semantics, pragmatics) operates on the structured output of the level immediately below it (phonology → morphology → lexical → syntax → semantics → pragmatics). Failure at any level cascades upwards, making the pipeline *fragile but interpretable*."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building a House" Intuition

Imagine a team of civil engineers constructing a 10-storey building. They cannot just jump to painting the 10th floor — the layers *must* be built from the ground up:

- **Foundation** → **Phonology** (raw acoustic signal / raw text characters)
- **Bricks & Tiles** → **Morphology** (the smallest meaningful building blocks — *unhappiness* = *un* + *happy* + *ness*)
- **Doors & Windows** → **Lexical Analysis** (identifying *what* each piece is — a "door", a "window")
- **Floor Plan & Walls** → **Syntax** (arranging the pieces according to architectural rules — subject-verb-object)
- **Functional Rooms** → **Semantics** (the *purpose* — this room is a kitchen, that is a bedroom)
- **Family Living in the House** → **Pragmatics** (real-world *use* — *"Can you pass the salt?"* is a *request*, not a *question*)

Just as you cannot furnish a floor that has not been poured, an NLP system cannot extract meaning from a sentence whose syntactic structure is unknown. This is the essence of **hierarchical language analysis**.

### 1.3 The Two Operational Modes: Speech vs. Text

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:**
> - **Phonology/Phonetics** is mandatory for **Spoken Language Understanding (SLU)** systems such as voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant, ASR systems).
> - For **written text input**, the pipeline typically starts at the **Morphological** level, since the acoustic signal is absent.

The standard KTU pipeline for **text-based NLP** is therefore:

$$\text{Characters} \rightarrow \text{Morphology} \rightarrow \text{Lexical} \rightarrow \text{Syntactic} \rightarrow \text{Semantic} \rightarrow \text{Discourse/Pragmatic}$$

> [!TIP]
> Modern transformer-based LLMs (BERT, GPT) handle *all* of these levels in a single end-to-end model, but conceptually they still perform these levels implicitly through different attention heads and layers. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the **classical pipeline view** for examination purposes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Levels — Operative Breakdown

### Level 1 — Phonology / Phonetics
- **Phonemes** are the smallest *contrastive* sound units in a language. English has approximately **44 phonemes**.
- Phonological rules govern how phonemes combine (e.g., English syllable structure: `(C)V(C)C`).
- **Phonetics** is the physical study of sound waves; **Phonology** is the abstract cognitive study of sound systems.
- **Engineering Utility:** Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Text-to-Speech (TTS), speaker identification, emotion detection.

### Level 2 — Morphology
- A **morpheme** is the smallest *meaning-bearing* unit. *"unhappiness"* contains three morphemes: `un-` (negation), `happy` (root), `-ness` (state).
- Two sub-types:
  - **Inflectional morphology** — grammatical variations (*walk*, *walked*, *walking*).
  - **Derivational morphology** — new word formation (*happy* → *happiness*, *happy* → *unhappy*).
- **Engineering Utility:** Stemming, lemmatization, morphological analyzers (e.g., `nltk.WordNetLemmatizer`).

### Level 3 — Lexical Analysis
- The **lexicon** of a language is its vocabulary. Lexical analysis performs:
  1. **Tokenization** — splitting text into tokens.
  2. **Stop-word removal** — filtering common words (*the*, *is*, *a*).
  3. **Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging** — assigning grammatical category (noun, verb, adjective...).
  4. **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — selecting the correct meaning from a polysemous word.
- **Engineering Utility:** Search engines, keyword extractors, vocabulary normalization, TF-IDF computation.

### Level 4 — Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)
- Establishes the **grammatical structure** of a sentence using formal grammar (CFG, PCFG, Dependency Grammar).
- Produces either:
  - **Constituency (Phrase) parse trees** — group words into nested noun-phrases, verb-phrases.
  - **Dependency parse trees** — direct *head–dependent* relations (*subject*, *object*, *modifier*).
- **Engineering Utility:** Grammar checkers, machine translation preprocessing, information extraction, question-answering pipelines.

### Level 5 — Semantic Analysis
- Extracts the **literal, context-free meaning** — who did what to whom, when, where, how.
- Uses:
  - **Predicate Logic / First-Order Logic (FOL) representations.**
  - **Lambda calculus** for compositional semantics.
  - **Frame semantics** (e.g., FrameNet) and **selectional restrictions**.
- **Engineering Utility:** Knowledge graph construction, sentiment analysis, semantic search, RAG pipelines.

### Level 6 — Discourse & Pragmatics
- **Discourse Analysis** — coherence and coreference across multiple sentences (anaphora resolution: *"John said **he** was tired"* — *he* = *John*).
- **Pragmatics** — meaning *in context*, including:
  - **Speech acts** (Austin & Searle): *assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declarations.*
  - **Implicature** (Grice): what is *meant* vs what is *said*.
  - **Presuppositions**, **deixis** (*here, there, tomorrow*).
- **Engineering Utility:** Conversational AI, chatbots, dialogue state tracking, intent classification.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **standardized mathematical models** that students must memorize for KTU 2024 Part B questions on language levels.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Model** | **Key Parameter** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phoneme Probability (HMM-ASR)** | $P(W \mid O) = \dfrac{P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(O)}$ | Bayes' Rule; $W$=word, $O$=acoustic observation | Speech Recognition |
| **N-gram Language Model** | $P(w_i \mid w_{1}^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$ | Order $n$ (unigram, bigram, trigram) | Autocomplete, ASR |
| **Morphological Segmentation** | $w = m_1 \oplus m_2 \oplus \dots \oplus m_k$ | $m_j$ = morpheme, $\oplus$ = concatenation | Stemming, Lemmatization |
| **TF-IDF Weight (Lexical)** | $\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | $N$=corpus size, $\text{df}$=document frequency | Information Retrieval |
| **CKY Parsing (Syntax)** | $T_{i,j} = \big\{A \,\vert\, \exists\, A \rightarrow B\,C \in P, \, B \in T_{i,k}, \, C \in T_{k,j}\big\}$ | Chomsky Normal Form, $i,j,k$=tree spans | Syntactic Parsing |
| **Predicate Logic (Semantic)** | $\forall x (\text{Man}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$ | FOL quantifiers $\forall, \exists$ | Knowledge Representation |
| **Cosine Similarity (Semantic)** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A}\Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B}\Vert}$ | Vector representations $A,B$ | Document Similarity |
| **Pointwise Mutual Information** | $\text{PMI}(x,y) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}\right)$ | Co-occurrence statistics | Collocation Extraction |
| **Shannon Entropy (Language)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits per symbol | Language Complexity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note for Students:**
> The above formulas are universal NLP fundamentals and may appear in *any* KTU 2024 exam. Memorize the **forms** (numerator/denominator structure), not just the names. KTU examiners test the *variables* and *boundary conditions*.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Rationale (Very High KTU Yield):**
> Each level exists because the unit of analysis at that level is **irreducible** to lower-level units. For example:
> - You *cannot* extract meaning from a single phoneme.
> - You *cannot* determine grammatical role without knowing word class (lexical).
> - You *cannot* resolve *he* without discourse-level coreference tracking.

This is why the pipeline is **strictly bottom-up** in classical symbolic NLP. Modern neural systems relax this rigidity but still implicitly encode the hierarchy through learned representations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Tracing a Sentence Through All Six Levels

To consolidate all six levels, we will trace the sentence:

> **"The cat chased the mouse."**

### STEP 1 — Phonetic Transcription (Phonology)
We first convert the spoken form to IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet).

The acoustic waveform is mapped to discrete phonemes:

$$\text{IPA: } / \text{ðə kæt tʃeɪst ðə maʊs} /$$

Each phoneme is then vectorized using acoustic features (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients — **MFCCs**). The Hidden Markov Model computes:

$$P(W \mid O) = \frac{P(O \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(O)}$$

**Explanation of variables:**
- $W$ = the candidate word sequence (`cat`, `chased`, ...).
- $O$ = the observed acoustic feature vector.
- $P(O \mid W)$ = acoustic model score (probability of sound given word).
- $P(W)$ = language model probability (next sub-section).
- $P(O)$ = normalization constant (ignored in decoding).

---

### STEP 2 — Morphological Decomposition
Decompose each inflected word into its morphemes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{The} &\rightarrow \text{det (function word, no internal morpheme structure)} \\
\text{cat} &\rightarrow \text{cat} + \emptyset \\
\text{chased} &\rightarrow \text{chase} + \text{ed} \quad (\text{root} + \text{past-tense suffix}) \\
\text{the} &\rightarrow \text{det (function word)} \\
\text{mouse} &\rightarrow \text{mous} + \emptyset \quad (\text{already a noun, no inflection here}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering note:** The morphological parser uses a **lexicon** of stems and a set of **morphotactic rules** to rebuild surface forms.

---

### STEP 3 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenization + POS Tagging)
Each token is mapped to a lexical entry:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Token Sequence} &= \big[ \text{``The''}, \text{``cat''}, \text{``chased''}, \text{``the''}, \text{``mouse''}, \text{``.``} \big] \\
\text{POS Tags} &= \big[ \text{DT}, \text{NN}, \text{VBD}, \text{DT}, \text{NN}, \text{.} \big] \\
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- **DT** = Determiner
- **NN** = Noun, singular
- **VBD** = Verb, past tense

A lexicon look-up assigns each token a **lemma** and a list of possible **senses** from a database like WordNet. For example, `mouse` has senses: (a) the rodent, (b) the computer device. The next levels will disambiguate.

---

### STEP 4 — Syntactic Parsing
Using a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** of the form $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where $V$=non-terminals, $\Sigma$=terminals, $R$=rules, $S$=start symbol, we construct a constituency parse tree.

For the given sentence, the parser produces:

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{NP VP}.$$

$$\text{NP} \rightarrow \text{DT NN} \quad (\text{twice})$$

$$\text{VP} \rightarrow \text{VBD NP}$$

**Constituency tree (textual form):**

```
            [S]
           /   \
        [NP]   [VP]
        / \    /  \
     [DT][NN][VBD][NP]
     |    |   |   / \
    The  cat chased [DT][NN]
                     |    |
                    the  mouse
```

Alternatively, the **dependency parse** yields head–dependent relations:

| **Head** | **Dependent** | **Relation** |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| chased | cat | nsubj (nominal subject) |
| chased | mouse | dobj (direct object) |
| cat | The | det |
| mouse | the | det |

---

### STEP 5 — Semantic Analysis (FOL Representation)
The parse tree is converted to a **First-Order Logic (FOL) predicate calculus** form using lambda-calculus-style composition:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Predicate:} & \quad \text{chase}(e) \text{ where } e = \text{the chasing event} \\
\text{Agent of } e: & \quad \text{cat}(x_1) \\
\text{Theme of } e: & \quad \text{mouse}(x_2) \\
\text{Definite Reference:} & \quad \iota x.\ \text{cat}(x) \quad \text{(the unique cat)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Final semantic form (Lambda-NDRT-style):**

$$\text{chase}(\iota x.\ \text{cat}(x),\ \iota y.\ \text{mouse}(y))$$

**English gloss:** *"The unique cat chased the unique mouse."*

This logical form is the canonical *meaning representation* and is the input to downstream tasks like question-answering.

---

### STEP 6 — Discourse & Pragmatic Analysis
We extend the analysis to multi-sentence context. Consider the dialogue:

> **A:** "The cat chased the mouse."
> **B:** "**It** was very fast."

**Discourse task:** Anaphora resolution of *It*.

Using **Hobbs' algorithm** or a modern neural coreference resolver, *It* is linked to the most recent salient entity satisfying selectional constraints. Pragmatically, "It" most likely refers to *the cat* (subject position is typically preferred for pronouns → English *subject preference* rule).

**Speech act analysis** (Searle's classification):

| Utterance | Speech Act Type |
|-----------|-----------------|
| "The cat chased the mouse." | **Assertive** (describes a state of affairs) |
| "Pass the salt." | **Directive** (request) |
| "I apologize." | **Expressive** (psychological state) |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Each Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Question Type:** *"What happens to higher levels when a lower level fails?"*

| **Level of Failure** | **Example Input** | **Cascading Effect on Higher Levels** |
|---|---|---|
| **Phonological** | Misrecognized speech: "Cat" → "Cap" | Wrong lexical item, wrong parse, wrong semantics |
| **Morphological** | "unreliability" split incorrectly | Poor POS tagging, broken parse, semantics degraded |
| **Lexical** | "Bank" (river vs. financial) | Wrong WSD, wrong semantic predicate, wrong frame |
| **Syntactic** | "I saw the man with the telescope" (ambiguous) | Two valid parses → two semantic interpretations |
| **Semantic** | "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously" (Chomsky) | Grammatical but semantically anomalous — NLU may fail |
| **Pragmatic** | "Can you pass the salt?" | Literally a yes/no question; intent = *request* |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The famous Chomsky sentence *"Colorless green ideas sleep furiously"* is **syntactically valid** but **semantically absurd** — proving that syntax and semantics are *independent* levels. This is a frequent KTU Part-A question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Classical NLP Language Analysis Pipeline (Master Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Text or Speech] --> B[Level 1 Phonology]
    B --> C[Level 2 Morphology]
    C --> D[Level 3 Lexical Analysis]
    D --> E[Level 4 Syntactic Analysis]
    E --> F[Level 5 Semantic Analysis]
    F --> G[Level 6 Discourse and Pragmatics]
    G --> H[Final Meaning Representation]
    H --> I[Downstream Task QAsumTransChat]

    B -.Error Cascade.-> E
    D -.Error Cascade.-> F
    E -.Error Cascade.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The downward arrows are the **forward processing flow** (bottom-up construction of meaning). The dashed arrows show **error propagation** — a single mistake at the lexical level can corrupt the entire semantic interpretation. This is the central engineering challenge in classical NLP.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Constituency Parse Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[S Sentence] --> R1[NP Noun Phrase]
    R --> R2[VP Verb Phrase]
    R2 --> R3[VBD chased]
    R2 --> R4[NP Noun Phrase]
    R1 --> R5[DT The]
    R1 --> R6[NN cat]
    R4 --> R7[DT the]
    R4 --> R8[NN mouse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the constituency parse tree for the sentence *"The cat chased the mouse."* Each node is a non-terminal of the CFG; leaves are terminal tokens with POS tags.

---

## 4.3 Dependency Parse Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[chased HEAD] --> B[cat nsubj]
    A --> C[mouse dobj]
    B --> D[The det]
    C --> E[the det]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **dependency grammar**, every word is connected to exactly one *head* (its governor). `chased` is the root. `cat` is the nominal subject, `mouse` is the direct object. Determiners depend on their nouns. This representation is computationally more efficient for many NLP tasks (e.g., information extraction).

---

## 4.4 Level-to-Application Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhonLevel [Level 1 Phonology]
        P1[ASR Engine]
        P2[TTS Synthesizer]
    end
    subgraph MorphLevel [Level 2 Morphology]
        M1[Stemmer]
        M2[Lemmatizer]
    end
    subgraph LexLevel [Level 3 Lexical]
        L1[Tokenizer]
        L2[POS Tagger]
        L3[WSD Module]
    end
    subgraph SyntLevel [Level 4 Syntax]
        S1[CFG Parser]
        S2[Dependency Parser]
    end
    subgraph SemLevel [Level 5 Semantics]
        SE1[FOL Converter]
        SE2[FrameNet Mapper]
    end
    subgraph PragLevel [Level 6 Pragmatics]
        PR1[Coreference Resolver]
        PR2[Speech Act Classifier]
    end
    PhonLevel --> MorphLevel --> LexLevel --> SyntLevel --> SemLevel --> PragLevel
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture shows the **modules** that implement each level. Each box is a *function* or *subsystem* in a classical NLP software stack (e.g., spaCy, NLTK, Stanford CoreNLP). Understanding the modular decomposition is essential for KTU Part-B questions on NLP system design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]
**List and briefly define the six levels of language analysis used in NLP.**

**Model Answer:**

The six levels of language analysis in NLP, processed in hierarchical order, are:

1. **Phonology/Phonetics** — Analysis of speech sounds; the smallest unit is the *phoneme*. Used in ASR and TTS.
2. **Morphology** — Analysis of the internal structure of words; the smallest unit is the *morpheme*. Used in stemming and lemmatization.
3. **Lexical Analysis** — Identification and classification of individual words; the unit is the *token*; performs POS tagging, WSD.
4. **Syntactic Analysis** — Determines the grammatical structure of sentences; produces *parse trees* using CFG rules.
5. **Semantic Analysis** — Extracts the literal meaning of sentences; uses *predicate logic, frames, selectional restrictions*.
6. **Discourse & Pragmatics** — Analyzes meaning across sentences and in context; includes *anaphora resolution, speech acts, implicature*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Insight (3 marks):** Examiners award **0.5 mark** per correctly named + briefly defined level. State *all six* — students commonly miss Discourse or Pragmatics.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Why is "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously" considered a valid test sentence in NLP? Which two levels does it test?**

**Model Answer:**

This sentence, coined by **Noam Chomsky (1957)**, is a classic test case in NLP because it demonstrates the **independence of syntax and semantics**.

- **Syntactically**, the sentence is **grammatically valid** — it obeys all rules of English grammar (Subject–Verb–Object structure, correct word classes, correct plural agreement).
- **Semantically**, the sentence is **anomalous** — *colorless green things* and *furiously sleeping ideas* violate our real-world knowledge of colors, sleep, and ideas.

Thus, the sentence proves that a valid syntactic parse does **not guarantee** a valid semantic interpretation. This is a foundational observation that motivates **separate syntactic and semantic processing stages** in classical NLP pipelines.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has an **internal choice** (either–or). We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternative options, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Full Module Coverage]**

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **morphological and lexical levels** of language analysis. Discuss the differences between **inflectional and derivational morphology** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the process of **syntactic analysis** using a Context-Free Grammar. Illustrate with a constituency parse tree for the sentence *"The intelligent student solved the difficult problem."* *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(a)

**Morphology** is the level of language analysis concerned with the **internal structure of words**. The fundamental unit is the **morpheme** — the smallest indivisible unit that carries meaning.

**Two Sub-Types:**

| Property | Inflectional Morphology | Derivational Morphology |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Express grammatical categories (tense, number, case) | Create new words |
| **Positional** | Always at the word's periphery (suffixes) | Prefix or suffix |
| **Word Class** | Does NOT change word class | Often changes word class |
| **Productivity** | Highly regular | Less regular |
| **Examples** | walk → walked, walk → walking, cat → cats | happy → happiness (adj → noun), teach → teacher (v → n) |

**Lexical Analysis** is the next level, where text is split into *tokens* and each token is looked up in a **lexicon** (vocabulary database). Key sub-tasks include:

1. **Tokenization** — *"The cat sat."* → `[The, cat, sat, .]`
2. **Stop-word removal** — filter common words.
3. **Part-of-Speech tagging** — assign grammatical category (`The/DT`, `cat/NN`, `sat/VBD`).
4. **Lemmatization** — reduce inflected forms to dictionary form (`sat` → `sit`).
5. **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — select correct meaning from polysemous words.

**Engineering relevance:** Morphological analyzers like **Porter Stemmer**, **Snowball Stemmer**, and **WordNet Lemmatizer** are essential preprocessing steps for information retrieval and machine translation.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(b)

**Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)** establishes the **grammatical structure** of a sentence according to a formal grammar. The most common formalism is the **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**, defined as:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

Where:
- $V$ = set of non-terminal symbols (e.g., S, NP, VP).
- $\Sigma$ = set of terminal symbols (words).
- $R$ = set of production rules ($A \rightarrow \beta$).
- $S$ = start symbol.

**Example CFG rules for English:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \text{NP VP} \\
\text{NP} &\rightarrow \text{DT AdjN N} \mid \text{DT N} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{V NP} \\
\text{DT} &\rightarrow \text{The} \mid \text{the} \\
\text{AdjN} &\rightarrow \text{intelligent} \mid \text{difficult} \\
\text{N} &\rightarrow \text{student} \mid \text{problem} \\
\text{V} &\rightarrow \text{solved} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Constituency parse tree for "The intelligent student solved the difficult problem":**

```
                [S]
               /   \
            [NP]   [VP]
           / | \   /  \
         [DT][AdjN][N] [V]  [NP]
          |    |    |   |   / | \
        The intelligent student solved  [DT][AdjN][N]
                                          |    |    |
                                        the difficult problem
```

The tree shows that:
- `S` decomposes into `NP` (subject) and `VP` (predicate).
- The first `NP` is `DT + AdjN + N` → *"The intelligent student"*.
- The `VP` is `V + NP` → *"solved [the difficult problem]"*.
- The second `NP` is again `DT + AdjN + N` → *"the difficult problem"*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU valuation, awarding marks for parse trees is **binary** — the structure *must* be top-down hierarchical with all sub-constituents. Flattened or improperly nested trees receive **0 marks** for that part.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the **semantic level** of language analysis. How is **First-Order Logic (FOL)** used to represent the meaning of the sentence *"All students read a book"*? Show the conversion step by step. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **discourse and pragmatic levels** of analysis. Differentiate between **speech acts, implicature, and anaphora** with examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(a)

**Semantic Analysis** is the level of language analysis concerned with extracting the **literal, context-independent meaning** of sentences. It operates on the output of the syntactic parser and produces a *meaning representation* — typically in the form of **First-Order Logic (FOL)**, **lambda calculus expressions**, or **semantic frames**.

**Step-by-step FOL conversion of "All students read a book":**

**Step 1: Identify the quantifier.**
The word *"All"* introduces a **universal quantifier** ($\forall$).

**Step 2: Identify the predicate.**
The verb *"read"* is the main predicate, taking two arguments: the *reader* and the *thing read*.

**Step 3: Identify the noun phrases (logical subjects).**
- *"students"* — universal quantifier scope.
- *"a book"* — existential quantifier scope.

**Step 4: Compose the FOL formula:**

$$\forall x \Big( \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \big( \text{Book}(y) \land \text{Read}(x, y) \big) \Big)$$

**English gloss:** *"For every entity x, if x is a student, then there exists some entity y such that y is a book and x reads y."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambiguity to note:** The English sentence *"All students read a book"* is **scope-ambiguous** in natural language. It can mean either (a) every student read the *same* book, or (b) every student read *some* book (possibly different). The FOL representation above encodes interpretation (b) using the **existential inside the universal** — which is the most natural reading.

Other semantic tools include:
- **Selectional restrictions** — the verb *eat* requires its object to be a *food-type* entity.
- **Frame semantics** (FrameNet) — frames are schematic representations of events (e.g., the *Commerce_buy* frame has slots for *Buyer*, *Seller*, *Goods*).
- **Lexical semantics** — WordNet-based hypernym/hyponym relations.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(b)

**Discourse Analysis** examines how sentences connect to form a coherent text. **Pragmatics** studies how context affects meaning.

| Concept | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Speech Act** (Searle) | The *intent* of an utterance | "Can you pass the salt?" — *Directive* (request), not a literal question about ability |
| **Implicature** (Grice) | Meaning implied but not literally said | "John has three cars." — *Implicature*: John has *exactly* three, not more |
| **Anaphora** | A pronoun refers back to a prior noun | "Maria came in. **She** sat down." — *She* = *Maria* |
| **Presupposition** | Background assumption for an utterance to make sense | "John stopped smoking." — *Presupposes*: John used to smoke |
| **Deixis** | Context-dependent reference (here, there, tomorrow) | "I'll see you **tomorrow**." |

**Discourse coherence relations** (Hobbs, Mann & Thompson):

- *Temporal*: She left **then** arrived.
- *Causal*: It rained **so** we stayed inside.
- *Contrast*: He is rich **but** unhappy.
- *Elaboration*: We went to Paris. **In particular, we visited the Louvre.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pragmatics with semantics.** Many students answer *"implicature"* questions by giving a *semantic* (literal) meaning. Always emphasize the *inferred, contextual* aspect.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the type of speech act.** A full 2-mark answer on Searle's theory must **name the type** (Directive, Assertive, Commissive, Expressive, Declaration).
> 3. **Not distinguishing anaphora from cataphora.** *Anaphora* refers *backward*; *cataphora* refers *forward* ("*When he arrived, John was tired."*).
> 4. **Skipping the FOL conversion steps in Part (a).** The 7-mark question expects **step-by-step logical derivation**, not just a final formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision List — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] The **six levels** in order: **Phonology → Morphology → Lexical → Syntactic → Semantic → Discourse/Pragmatics**.
- [ ] The unit of analysis at each level: **Phoneme, Morpheme, Token/Lemma, Parse Tree, Logical Form, Speech Act**.
- [ ] **Morphology** has two branches: **Inflectional** (grammar) vs. **Derivational** (new words).
- [ ] **Lexical Analysis** includes **tokenization, stop-word removal, POS tagging, lemmatization, WSD**.
- [ ] **Syntactic Analysis** uses **CFG**; produces **constituency trees** or **dependency trees**.
- [ ] **Semantic Analysis** converts parse trees to **First-Order Logic (FOL)** or **lambda expressions**.
- [ ] **Discourse Analysis** resolves **anaphora, coherence, presupposition**.
- [ ] **Pragmatics** handles **speech acts, implicature, deixis**.
- [ ] **Chomsky's "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously"** — syntactic-valid, semantically-absurd.
- [ ] **Bayes' Rule** $P(W|O) = P(O|W)\cdot P(W)/P(O)$ is the foundation of ASR.
- [ ] **TF-IDF**, **N-gram LM**, **PMI**, **Cosine Similarity** are key lexical/semantic formulas.
- [ ] The pipeline is **strictly bottom-up** in classical NLP; **error cascades upward**.
- [ ] Modern neural NLP (Transformers) handles all levels *end-to-end* but implicitly preserves the hierarchy.
- [ ] Always provide **step-by-step derivations** in FOL conversion questions (not just the final formula).
- [ ] Always **draw the parse tree top-down** with proper indentation in syntactic analysis answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy Note:**
> For Part-A (3-mark) questions, **memorize all six levels with examples**. For Part-B (14-mark) questions, **pick the option that lets you draw diagrams or write FOL formulas** — they are the easiest to score full marks on if your tree structure and logical steps are clean.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Syntax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Syntax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Syntax in NLP

In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Syntax** refers to the formal study of the *structural arrangement of words and phrases in a sentence*, governed by a set of grammatical rules. It is the surface-level architecture of language that dictates how legitimate sentences are constructed from constituent elements (words/morphemes), independent of meaning (semantics) or context (pragmatics).

Mathematically, syntactic structure is typically modeled using a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**, defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where:
- $V$ = finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (grammatical categories like Noun, Verb, Determiner)
- $\Sigma$ = finite set of **terminal symbols** (the actual vocabulary/words)
- $R$ = finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$
- $S \in V$ = designated **start symbol** (typically `S` for Sentence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Syntax is the *second* level of the NLP pipeline (after Morphology). It bridges the gap between word-level tokens and sentence-level meaning. Without syntax, a sentence like "The dog the cat chased ran" is uninterpretable to a machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **railway interlocking system** at a major junction. The trains (words) cannot simply be allowed to move in any order — there are switches, signals, and route regulations (grammar rules). A *dispatcher* (the parser) checks whether the proposed sequence of trains forms a valid route from the origin to the destination, otherwise, the train is halted. 

Similarly, a **syntactic parser** verifies whether a sequence of tokens forms a structurally valid sentence according to a grammar. The **dispatcher's route map** is exactly what we call a **parse tree**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Syntax is NOT about meaning. The sentence "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously" (Chomsky, 1957) is syntactically valid but semantically anomalous. NLP engines must pass the syntax stage *before* attempting semantics.

## 1.3 Why Syntax Matters in Modern NLP

Even with the rise of **Transformer-based deep learning** (BERT, GPT), syntax remains foundational because:

- It provides **structural inductive bias** for low-resource languages.
- It enables **explainable AI (XAI)** — dependency arcs can be visualized and audited.
- It supports **rule-based systems** in legal, clinical, and low-data domains.
- Modern parsers like **Stanza**, **spaCy**, and **Stanford CoreNLP** still rely on transition-based or graph-based syntactic models.

---

**Standard Syntactic Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation:**

- **Parse Accuracy (LABELLED F1):** $\mathbf{> 90\%}$ for state-of-the-art English parsers.
- **Coverage:** Proportion of input sentences that receive a parse. **Goal:** **100%**.
- **Cross-Bracketing Rate (CBR):** Number of crossing dependency arcs — lower is better.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chomsky Hierarchy (Foundation for All Formal Grammars)

Noam Chomsky's hierarchy classifies grammars by their generative power. This is **high-yield** for KTU theory questions.

| Grammar Type | Languages Generated | Recognizer (Automaton) | Production Rule Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type-0** (Unrestricted) | Recursively Enumerable | Turing Machine | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ (no constraints) |
| **Type-1** (Context-Sensitive) | Context-Sensitive | Linear-Bounded Automaton | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ |
| **Type-2** (Context-Free) | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ |
| **Type-3** (Regular) | Regular | Finite State Automaton | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focal Point:** Almost all natural-language syntax work uses **Type-2 (Context-Free Grammars)** as the baseline because regular grammars are *too weak* to capture nested structures (e.g., center-embedding), while context-sensitive grammars are *computationally intractable*.

## 2.2 Constituency vs. Dependency Structure

There are two dominant formalisms for representing syntax:

### A. Constituency Structure (Phrase Structure)
- Breaks the sentence into nested **constituents** (Noun Phrase, Verb Phrase, etc.).
- Represented as a **parse tree** or **phrase-structure tree**.
- Directly corresponds to CFG derivations.

### B. Dependency Structure
- Represents syntax as a set of **directed binary relations** (head-dependent).
- Represented as a **directed graph** (typically a tree).
- Each word (except the root) has exactly **one head**.

### Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | Constituency Grammar | Dependency Grammar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basic Unit** | Phrase / Constituent | Word-pair (head-modifier) |
| **Output** | Parse Tree | Dependency Tree (graph) |
| **Captures** | Phrase boundaries | Functional roles |
| **Free Word Order** | Difficult | Excellent |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher ($O(n^3)$ worst case) | Lower ($O(n)$ transition-based) |
| **KTU Example** | Penn Treebank | Universal Dependencies (UD) |

## 2.3 Parsing Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

**Parsing** is the algorithmic process of mapping a sentence $w_{1:n}$ to a syntactic structure.

### Top-Down Parsing
- Starts from the **start symbol $S$** and expands rules until terminals are matched.
- Risk: **Left-recursion** causes infinite loops.
- Algorithms: **Recursive Descent Parser (RDP)**, **Earley Parser**, **Predictive Parser (LL(k))**.

### Bottom-Up Parsing
- Starts from the **input tokens** and builds up to $S$ by applying rules in reverse.
- Risk: **Useless work** — may build structures that lead to dead-ends.
- Algorithms: **Shift-Reduce Parser**, **CYK Algorithm**, **LR Parsers**.

### Chart Parsing (Dynamic Programming)
- Stores partial results in a **chart** to avoid redundant computation.
- Guarantees **$O(n^3)$** complexity for CFGs (polynomial).
- Examples: **CYK (Cocke–Kasami–Younger)**, **Earley**.

## 2.4 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)

Since CFGs can produce multiple parse trees for one sentence (ambiguity), we attach probabilities.

**Production Rule:** $A \rightarrow \beta$ with probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$.

**Constraint (Probability Axiom):** For every non-terminal $A$, the sum of probabilities of all its productions must equal 1:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1, \quad \forall A \in V$$

**Probability of a Parse Tree $T$:**

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$$

where $(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$ is the $i$-th rule used in deriving tree $T$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | CFG Definition | $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ | Formal grammar specification |
| 2 | Derivation | $S \Rightarrow^* w$ | Generates string $w$ from start symbol |
| 3 | PCFG Probability | $P(T) = \prod_{i} P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$ | Score a parse tree |
| 4 | CYK Complexity | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | Worst-case parsing time |
| 5 | Inside Probability | $\alpha_{i,j}(A)$ | Total prob. of subtree rooted at $A$ over $w_{i:j}$ |
| 6 | Outside Probability | $\beta_{i,j}(A)$ | Prob. of generating $w_{1:i-1} A w_{j+1:n}$ |
| 7 | Head-Percolation | $\text{head}(A \rightarrow \beta) = \arg\max \text{score}$ | Determines head word in constituency |
| 8 | Dependency Arc Score | $s(h, m, \ell)$ | Head-modifier-label triple score |
| 9 | MST (Max Spanning Tree) | $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \sum_{(h,m) \in T} s(h,m)$ | Best dependency tree |
| 10 | Chomsky Type | $\text{Type-2} \Rightarrow \text{PDA}$ | Recognize CFG |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications of Syntactic Parsing

- **Machine Translation (MT):** Syntax-alignment models (e.g., synchronous CFGs).
- **Information Extraction:** Event-argument roles depend on syntactic position.
- **Question Answering:** Syntactic distance proxies semantic relatedness.
- **Code Generation / Auto-Completion:** AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) is the direct analogue.
- **Biomedical NLP:** Extracting protein–protein interactions from PubMed.
- **Grammar Checking (Grammarly, Ginger):** Detects agreement errors via dependency mismatch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Constituency Parse Tree Derivation

**Sentence:** "The cat sleeps on the mat."

### Step 1 — Define a Toy CFG

The grammar $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ is defined as follows:

$$S \rightarrow NP \ VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow Det \ N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \ PP$$
$$PP \rightarrow P \ NP$$
$$Det \rightarrow \text{the} \mid \text{a}$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{cat} \mid \text{mat}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{sleeps}$$
$$P \rightarrow \text{on}$$

### Step 2 — Leftmost Derivation

A derivation is the sequence of rule applications replacing the leftmost non-terminal at each step.

$$S$$
$$\Rightarrow NP \ VP$$
$$\Rightarrow Det \ N \ VP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ N \ VP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ VP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ V \ PP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ PP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ P \ NP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ \text{on} \ NP$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ \text{on} \ Det \ N$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ \text{on} \ \text{the} \ N$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{the} \ \text{cat} \ \text{sleeps} \ \text{on} \ \text{the} \ \text{mat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The string "The cat sleeps on the mat" is **derivable** from $S$ in 12 derivation steps. We denote this as $S \Rightarrow^* w$, proving $w \in L(G)$.

### Step 3 — Probability Calculation (PCFG Extension)

Suppose we assign the following rule probabilities:

$$P(S \rightarrow NP \ VP) = 1.0$$
$$P(NP \rightarrow Det \ N) = 0.8, \quad P(NP \rightarrow NP \ PP) = 0.2$$
$$P(VP \rightarrow V \ PP) = 0.7, \quad P(VP \rightarrow V) = 0.3$$
$$P(PP \rightarrow P \ NP) = 1.0$$
$$P(Det \rightarrow \text{the}) = 0.6, \quad P(Det \rightarrow \text{a}) = 0.4$$
$$P(N \rightarrow \text{cat}) = 0.5, \quad P(N \rightarrow \text{mat}) = 0.5$$
$$P(V \rightarrow \text{sleeps}) = 1.0$$
$$P(P \rightarrow \text{on}) = 1.0$$

The probability of the resulting parse tree $T$ is the product of all rule probabilities used:

$$P(T) = (1.0) \cdot (0.8) \cdot (0.6) \cdot (0.5) \cdot (0.7) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (0.6) \cdot (0.5)$$

$$P(T) = 1.0 \times 0.8 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 0.5$$

We evaluate step by step:

$$P(T) = 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48$$
$$P(T) = 0.48 \times 0.5 = 0.24$$
$$P(T) = 0.24 \times 0.7 = 0.168$$
$$P(T) = 0.168 \times 1.0 = 0.168$$
$$P(T) = 0.168 \times 1.0 = 0.168$$
$$P(T) = 0.168 \times 0.6 = 0.1008$$
$$P(T) = 0.1008 \times 0.5 = 0.0504$$

$$\boxed{P(T) = 0.0504}$$

### Step 4 — Sanity Check (Probability Axiom)

For $NP$, the probabilities should sum to 1:

$$P(NP \rightarrow Det \ N) + P(NP \rightarrow NP \ PP) = 0.8 + 0.2 = 1.0 \ \checkmark$$

For $VP$:

$$P(VP \rightarrow V \ PP) + P(VP \rightarrow V) = 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0 \ \checkmark$$

For $Det$:

$$P(Det \rightarrow \text{the}) + P(Det \rightarrow \text{a}) = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 \ \checkmark$$

For $N$:

$$P(N \rightarrow \text{cat}) + P(N \rightarrow \text{mat}) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \ \checkmark$$

> All axioms are satisfied, confirming a **valid PCFG**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the CKY (Cocke–Kasami–Younger) Algorithm Logic

CYK is the canonical bottom-up chart parser taught at KTU. It requires the grammar to be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**.

**CNF Restriction:** Every production must be of one of two forms:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (binary branching)
- $A \rightarrow a$ (terminal emission)

No $\epsilon$-productions or unit productions are allowed.

### CYK Recurrence Relation

For input string $w_{1:n}$ and span $[i, j]$ where $1 \leq i \leq j \leq n$:

$$cell[i][j] = \left\{ A \in V \ \vert \ \exists \, k \text{ with } i \leq k < j, \, B \in cell[i][k], \, C \in cell[k+1][j], \text{ and } A \rightarrow BC \in R \right\}$$

**Base Case (length-1 spans):**

$$cell[i][i] = \left\{ A \in V \ \vert \ A \rightarrow w_i \in R \right\}$$

**Acceptance Criterion:** The string is in $L(G)$ iff $S \in cell[1][n]$.

### Worked Mini-Example

**Grammar in CNF:**
$$S \rightarrow NP \ VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow Det \ N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \ NP$$
$$Det \rightarrow \text{the}$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{cat} \mid \text{mat}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{sleeps}$$

**Input:** "the cat" (length $n=2$).

**Initialize $cell[i][i]$:**
- $cell[1][1] = \{ Det \}$ (because $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$)
- $cell[2][2] = \{ N \}$ (because $N \rightarrow \text{cat}$)

**Compute $cell[1][2]$ (span of length 2):**

We check whether there exists a rule $A \rightarrow BC$ with $B \in cell[1][1]$, $C \in cell[2][2]$. The only split is $k=1$:

- $B = Det$, $C = N$
- Is there a rule $A \rightarrow Det \ N$? **Yes**: $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
- So $NP \in cell[1][2]$.

$$\boxed{cell[1][2] = \{ NP \}}$$

Since $S \notin cell[1][2]$, the string "the cat" is **NOT in $L(G)$** as a complete sentence (it is an $NP$ fragment, not a full $S$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parsing Algorithm Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Direction | Grammar Form | Complexity | Handles Ambiguity? | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Recursive Descent | Top-Down | Any (no left-recursion) | $O(2^n)$ worst case | No | High |
| Shift-Reduce | Bottom-Up | Any | $O(n^3)$ worst, $O(n)$ practical | Partial (greedy) | Very High |
| CYK | Bottom-Up | CNF only | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | Yes (via Viterbi) | Very High |
| Earley | Top-Down | Any | $O(n^3)$ worst, $O(n^2)$ typical | Yes | Medium |
| Chart Parser (General) | Both | Any | $O(n^3)$ | Yes | High |
| Arc-Eager (Transition) | Bottom-Up (dep.) | Projective only | $O(n)$ | No | Medium |
| Arc-Standard (Transition) | Bottom-Up (dep.) | Projective only | $O(n)$ | No | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Syntactic Parsing Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **functional architecture flow** of a typical NLP syntactic analysis pipeline, from raw text to a parsed dependency/constituency structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[rawTextInput] --> B[tokenizerModule]
    B --> C[posTagger]
    C --> D[lemmatizerUnit]
    D --> E[shallowParserChunker]
    E --> F{grammarChoice}
    F -- CFG --> G[cfgConstituencyParser]
    F -- Dependency --> H[transitionDependencyParser]
    F -- DeepLearning --> I[neuralParserModel]
    G --> J[parseTreeOutput]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[semanticRoleLabeler]
    K --> L[downstreamNLPTask]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Pipeline Stage Explanation:**

1. **rawTextInput:** Unprocessed natural language string.
2. **tokenizerModule:** Splits text into individual word tokens.
3. **posTagger:** Assigns Part-of-Speech tags (NN, VB, DT, etc.).
4. **lemmatizerUnit:** Reduces words to base forms (running → run).
5. **shallowParserChunker:** Identifies base noun/verb phrases.
6. **grammarChoice:** Routing decision based on chosen formalism.
7. **cfgConstituencyParser / transitionDependencyParser / neuralParserModel:** Three competing parser implementations.
8. **parseTreeOutput:** Final syntactic structure (tree or graph).
9. **semanticRoleLabeler:** Next stage — assigns predicate-argument structure.
10. **downstreamNLPTask:** Final consumer (MT, QA, Summarization).

---

## 4.2 Constituency Parse Tree (Mermaid Visualization)

The following diagram visualizes the constituency parse tree for the sentence **"The cat sleeps on the mat"** based on the CFG defined in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[S]
    nodeNP1[NP]
    nodeVP[VP]
    nodeDet1[Det]
    nodeN1[N]
    nodeV[V]
    nodePP[PP]
    nodeP[P]
    nodeNP2[NP]
    nodeDet2[Det]
    nodeN2[N]
    nodeThe1[the]
    nodeCat[cat]
    nodeSleeps[sleeps]
    nodeOn[on]
    nodeThe2[the]
    nodeMat[mat]

    nodeS --- nodeNP1
    nodeS --- nodeVP
    nodeNP1 --- nodeDet1
    nodeNP1 --- nodeN1
    nodeDet1 --- nodeThe1
    nodeN1 --- nodeCat
    nodeVP --- nodeV
    nodeVP --- nodePP
    nodePP --- nodeP
    nodePP --- nodeNP2
    nodeP --- nodeOn
    nodeNP2 --- nodeDet2
    nodeNP2 --- nodeN2
    nodeDet2 --- nodeThe2
    nodeN2 --- nodeMat
    nodeV --- nodeSleeps

    style nodeS fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeNP1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeNP2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeVP fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodePP fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeDet1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeDet2 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeN1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeN2 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeV fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeP fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeThe1 fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeCat fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeSleeps fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeOn fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeThe2 fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeMat fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Dependency Graph (Mermaid Visualization)

The same sentence represented as a **dependency graph** where each arc connects a *head* to a *dependent*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    headCat[cat]
    headSleeps[sleeps]
    headOn[on]
    headMat[mat]
    depThe1[the]
    depThe2[the]

    headSleeps -- nsubj --> headCat
    headCat -- det --> depThe1
    headSleeps -- case --> headOn
    headSleeps -- obl --> headOn
    headMat -- det --> depThe2
    headOn -- case_marker --> headOn
    headSleeps -- root --> headSleeps

    style headSleeps fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style headCat fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style headOn fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style headMat fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style depThe1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style depThe2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the graph:** `sleeps` is the **root** (ROOT) of the sentence. `cat` is the nominal subject (nsubj) of `sleeps`. `the` is the determiner (det) of `cat`. `on` is a case marker (case), and the prepositional phrase is an oblique (obl) modifier.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Define Context-Free Grammar (CFG). List its components with an example suitable for NLP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical model used to specify the syntax of a language. It is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

**Components:**
- **$V$** — Finite set of *non-terminal* symbols (variables representing syntactic categories, e.g., $V = \{S, NP, VP, PP, Det, N, V, P\}$).
- **$\Sigma$** — Finite set of *terminal* symbols (the vocabulary words, e.g., $\Sigma = \{$the, a, cat, mat, sleeps, on$\}$).
- **$R$** — Finite set of *production rules* of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in V$ and $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$.
- **$S$** — A designated *start symbol* (typically $S$ for Sentence), with $S \in V$.

**Example Rule:** $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$

*(Full mark allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for components, 0.5 mark for example rule.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Differentiate between Constituency Grammar and Dependency Grammar.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Constituency Grammar | Dependency Grammar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Structure | Nested phrases (NP, VP, PP) | Head–dependent word pairs |
| Output | Parse tree (phrase-structure) | Dependency graph/tree |
| Emphasis | Phrase boundaries | Functional relationships |
| Suitable For | English, fixed word-order languages | Free word-order languages (Hindi, Tamil) |

*(Full mark allocation: 1 mark for each distinction × 3 distinctions.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) formally and explain each of its four components with a suitable linguistic example. Generate the parse tree for the sentence **"Students read books"** using a CFG of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Chomsky Hierarchy of formal grammars. Why are Context-Free Grammars preferred for modeling natural language syntax? Discuss with at least two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

A **CFG** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$.

**Components Explained:**
1. **$V$ (Non-Terminals):** Symbols that represent abstract grammatical categories (do not appear in the final sentence). Example: $V = \{S, NP, VP, N, V, Det\}$.
2. **$\Sigma$ (Terminals):** The actual lexicon of the language. Example: $\Sigma = \{$students, read, books$\}$.
3. **$R$ (Rules):** Productions of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$. Example: $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$.
4. **$S$ (Start Symbol):** The designated entry point for derivations, typically $S$.

**Grammar for "Students read books":**

$$S \rightarrow NP \ VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \ NP$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{students} \mid \text{books}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{read}$$

**Parse Tree (Textual Representation):**

```
                S
              /   \\
            NP      VP
             |      /\\
             N     V  NP
             |     |   |
        students  read  N
                       |
                     books
```

**Derivation:**

$$S \Rightarrow NP \ VP \Rightarrow N \ VP \Rightarrow \text{students} \ VP \Rightarrow \text{students} \ V \ NP \Rightarrow \text{students} \ \text{read} \ NP \Rightarrow \text{students} \ \text{read} \ N \Rightarrow \text{students} \ \text{read} \ \text{books}$$

*(Mark Split: 2 marks definition + 1 mark components + 2 marks grammar design + 2 marks parse tree + 1 mark derivation = 8 → rescale to 7.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Chomsky Hierarchy (in decreasing order of expressive power):**

1. **Type-0 (Unrestricted Grammars):** Production rules of form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with no restrictions. Recognize recursively enumerable languages. Recognizer: Turing Machine.

2. **Type-1 (Context-Sensitive Grammars):** Productions of form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ where $\gamma \neq \epsilon$. Recognizer: Linear-Bounded Automaton.

3. **Type-2 (Context-Free Grammars):** Productions of form $A \rightarrow \gamma$ where $A$ is a single non-terminal. Recognizer: Pushdown Automaton.

4. **Type-3 (Regular Grammars):** Productions restricted to $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$. Recognizer: Finite State Automaton.

**Why CFGs are preferred for NLP Syntax:**

- **Sufficient Expressiveness:** Natural languages exhibit nested structures (e.g., center-embedding: "the rat [that the cat [that the dog chased] caught] ate"). Regular grammars (Type-3) **cannot** capture this nesting — they only handle flat patterns.
- **Efficient Polynomial Parsing:** CYK, Earley, and chart parsers parse CFGs in **$O(n^3)$** time, which is tractable for typical sentence lengths.
- **Linguistic Alignment:** CFG non-terminals (NP, VP, PP) map directly to phrase categories identified by linguists (Chomsky's X-bar theory).

**Limitations of CFGs:**

1. **Long-Distance Dependencies:** CFGs cannot easily model dependencies across arbitrary gaps (e.g., "The book [that you said [that he recommended [that I read]]] was good"). This requires augmentation with mechanisms like **unification grammars** or **TAG (Tree-Adjoining Grammars)**.

2. **Free Word-Order Languages:** Languages like Hindi, Tamil, and Malayalam exhibit substantial word-order flexibility. CFG parse trees become deeply ambiguous, and the rigid hierarchy fails to capture the underlying predicate-argument relations. **Dependency grammars** or **Statistical/Lexicalized PCFGs** handle these better.

3. **Lack of Semantic Integration:** CFGs are purely structural and ignore lexical selectional preferences. This leads to over-generation — accepting strings like "The rock slept furiously" as syntactically valid.

*(Mark Split: 2 marks hierarchy list + 2 marks CFG preference justification + 3 marks for two clear limitations.)*

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern] (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is a **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)**? Explain with a numerical example how the probability of a parse tree is computed. Discuss how PCFGs help resolve **structural ambiguity**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CYK (Cocke–Kasami–Younger) algorithm** for parsing. What is **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**? Apply CYK to the sentence **"She eats cake"** given a CNF grammar. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

A **PCFG** is a Context-Free Grammar augmented with a probability distribution over each non-terminal's production rules:

$$P(A \rightarrow \beta) \quad \text{for every rule } A \rightarrow \beta \in R$$

subject to the constraint:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1 \quad \forall A \in V$$

**Numerical Example:**

Consider the ambiguous sentence "I saw the man with the telescope" — it has two parses:
- **Parse 1:** PP-attachment to VP (saw using the telescope)
- **Parse 2:** PP-attachment to NP (the man who has the telescope)

Suppose:

$$P(VP \rightarrow VP \ PP) = 0.7, \quad P(VP \rightarrow V \ NP) = 0.9$$
$$P(PP \rightarrow P \ NP) = 1.0, \quad P(NP \rightarrow Det \ N \ PP) = 0.4$$
$$P(NP \rightarrow Det \ N) = 0.6$$

**Parse 1 probability:**
$$P(T_1) = 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.42$$

**Parse 2 probability:**
$$P(T_2) = 0.9 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.36$$

Since $P(T_1) > P(T_2)$, the parser selects **Parse 1** (telescope modifies the verb "saw").

**Resolving Structural Ambiguity:**
- Given a sentence with $k$ possible parse trees $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k\}$, the parser returns the **Viterbi (most probable) tree:**

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(w)} P(T)$$

- The **CYK algorithm** with PCFG extends naturally to find $\hat{T}$ by tracking back-pointers along the maximum-probability path.

*(Mark Split: 2 marks PCFG definition + 2 marks probability calculation + 3 marks ambiguity resolution explanation.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**CYK Algorithm Overview:**

CYK is a **bottom-up dynamic programming** parser for CFGs in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. It runs in $O(n^3 \cdot \vert R \vert)$ time, where $n$ is the sentence length.

**Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):**

A CFG is in CNF if every production has one of two forms:
1. $A \rightarrow BC$ (two non-terminals on the right)
2. $A \rightarrow a$ (single terminal)

No $\epsilon$-productions or unit-productions ($A \rightarrow B$) are allowed. Any CFG can be mechanically converted to CNF.

**CYK Working Principle:**

We fill a triangular chart $cell[i][j]$ representing the set of non-terminals that can derive the substring $w_{i:j}$ (1-indexed, inclusive).

**Base Case (length-1 spans):**
$$cell[i][i] = \{ A \in V \mid A \rightarrow w_i \in R \}$$

**Recursive Case (longer spans):**
$$cell[i][j] = \bigcup_{k=i}^{j-1} \{ A \in V \mid \exists B \in cell[i][k], C \in cell[k+1][j], A \rightarrow BC \in R \}$$

**Acceptance:** $w \in L(G)$ iff $S \in cell[1][n]$.

**Application to "She eats cake":**

**CNF Grammar:**
$$S \rightarrow NP \ VP$$
$$NP \rightarrow N$$
$$VP \rightarrow V \ NP$$
$$N \rightarrow \text{she} \mid \text{cake}$$
$$V \rightarrow \text{eats}$$

**Input:** $w = [w_1, w_2, w_3] = [\text{she}, \text{eats}, \text{cake}]$, $n=3$.

**Step 1 — Length-1 spans:**
- $cell[1][1] = \{ N \}$ (because $N \rightarrow \text{she}$)
- $cell[2][2] = \{ V \}$ (because $V \rightarrow \text{eats}$)
- $cell[3][3] = \{ N \}$ (because $N \rightarrow \text{cake}$)

**Step 2 — Length-2 spans:**

Compute $cell[1][2]$:
- Check $k=1$: $B \in cell[1][1] = \{N\}$, $C \in cell[2][2] = \{V\}$
- Is there a rule $A \rightarrow NV$? Looking at $R$: **No**.
- Thus $cell[1][2] = \emptyset$.

Compute $cell[2][3]$:
- Check $k=2$: $B \in cell[2][2] = \{V\}$, $C \in cell[3][3] = \{N\}$
- Is there a rule $A \rightarrow VN$? Looking at $R$: **No**.
- Thus $cell[2][3] = \emptyset$.

**Step 3 — Length-3 span $cell[1][3]$:**

Check splits:
- $k=1$: $B \in cell[1][1] = \{N\}$, $C \in cell[2][3] = \emptyset$. No rules.
- $k=2$: $B \in cell[1][2] = \emptyset$, $C \in cell[3][3] = \{N\}$. No rules.

So $cell[1][3] = \emptyset$.

**Wait — this suggests "She eats cake" is REJECTED.**

**Correction:** The grammar is *too restrictive* for CYK because rules like $NP \rightarrow N$ are in CNF, but $NP \rightarrow N$ applied to span $[1,1]$ only gives us $N$, NOT $NP$. We need to add a unit-production shortcut. Let us revise by extending the grammar to allow:

$$NP \rightarrow \text{she}, \quad NP \rightarrow \text{cake}$$
$$VP \rightarrow \text{eats}$$

**Revised CNF-compatible rules** (terminal rules become their own non-terminal pre-expansions for CYK):
$$N \rightarrow \text{she}, \quad N \rightarrow \text{cake}, \quad V \rightarrow \text{eats}$$

After propagating unit productions $NP \rightarrow N$ to $NP \rightarrow \text{she}$, $NP \rightarrow \text{cake}$ and $VP \rightarrow V$ to $VP \rightarrow \text{eats}$, we get:

**Step 1 — Length-1 spans:**
- $cell[1][1] = \{ N, NP \}$ (because $N \rightarrow \text{she}$ and $NP \rightarrow \text{she}$)
- $cell[2][2] = \{ V, VP \}$ (because $V \rightarrow \text{eats}$ and $VP \rightarrow \text{eats}$)
- $cell[3][3] = \{ N, NP \}$ (because $N \rightarrow \text{cake}$ and $NP \rightarrow \text{cake}$)

**Step 2 — Length-2 spans:**

$cell[1][2]$:
- $k=1$: $B \in \{N, NP\}$, $C \in \{V, VP\}$
  - Is there $A \rightarrow N \, V$? No.
  - Is there $A \rightarrow N \, VP$? No.
  - Is there $A \rightarrow NP \, V$? No.
  - Is there $A \rightarrow NP \, VP$? **Yes!** $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$.
- So $cell[1][2] = \{ S \}$.

$cell[2][3]$:
- $k=2$: $B \in \{V, VP\}$, $C \in \{N, NP\}$
  - $A \rightarrow V \, N$? No.
  - $A \rightarrow V \, NP$? No.
  - $A \rightarrow VP \, N$? No.
  - $A \rightarrow VP \, NP$? **Yes!** $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ matches $A = S, B = NP, C = VP$ only if we swap. We need $A \rightarrow B C$ — looking at rule $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$, so we need $B=NP, C=VP$.
- But $B \in \{V, VP\}$ and $C \in \{N, NP\}$, so $B=VP$ and $C=NP$ doesn't match $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ order.
- So $cell[2][3] = \emptyset$.

**Step 3 — Length-3 span $cell[1][3]$:**

- $k=1$: $B \in cell[1][1] = \{N, NP\}$, $C \in cell[2][3] = \emptyset$. No rules.
- $k=2$: $B \in cell[1][2] = \{S\}$, $C \in cell[3][3] = \{N, NP\}$.
  - Is there $A \rightarrow S \, N$? No.
  - Is there $A \rightarrow S \, NP$? No.

So $cell[1][3] = \emptyset$.

**Final outcome:** $S \notin cell[1][3]$, so "She eats cake" is **REJECTED by this particular grammar**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **propagate unit productions** before applying CYK. A rule like $NP \rightarrow N$ in the original grammar does NOT directly appear in CNF form. You MUST first eliminate it (or extend the chart) before running CYK. Failure to do this is the #1 reason students lose **3–4 marks** in CYK-based questions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Students forget that the parser must check **all split points** $k$ in the recurrence. Omitting even one split can cause a parse to fail incorrectly. Always state the recurrence range as $\forall k \text{ with } i \leq k < j$.

*(Mark Split: 2 marks CNF definition + 2 marks CYK algorithm description + 1 mark complexity + 2 marks worked application.)*

---

## 🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls for the "Syntax" Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CFG Components:** Writing $\Sigma$ when meaning $V$ costs 1 mark. Always label each component explicitly.
> 2. **Skipping Derivation Steps:** In derivation questions, write the FULL chain $S \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow w$. Truncating to a few steps costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Probability Axiom:** In PCFG sums, students often forget to verify $\sum P = 1$. KTU evaluators specifically test this.
> 4. **Mixing Constituency and Dependency:** Do not draw a dependency arc INSIDE a constituency tree or vice versa. They are distinct formalisms.
> 5. **CYK Without CNF:** Running CYK on a non-CNF grammar is invalid. Always show the CNF conversion step explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Syntax**
>
> - **Definition:** Syntax = structural arrangement of words per grammatical rules.
> - **Formal Model:** CFG = $(V, \Sigma, R, S)$ — memorize the 4 components.
> - **Chomsky Hierarchy:** Type-0 > Type-1 > Type-2 > Type-3. CFG = Type-2.
> - **Recognizer for CFG:** **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)**.
> - **Two Formalisms:** Constituency (phrase trees) vs. Dependency (head-modifier arcs).
> - **PCFG:** Probability of a parse tree = product of rule probabilities. Sum rule = 1.
> - **CYK Algorithm:** Bottom-up, $O(n^3 \cdot \vert R \vert)$, **requires CNF**.
> - **CNF Form:** Only $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. No $\epsilon$, no units.
> - **CYK Acceptance:** $S \in cell[1][n]$.
> - **Parsing Directions:** Top-down (root → leaves) vs. Bottom-up (leaves → root).
> - **Ambiguity Resolution:** Viterbi / maximum-probability parse via PCFG.
> - **Real-World Use:** MT, QA, Grammar-checking, Information Extraction, Code ASTs.
> - **KTU Buzzwords:** Left-recursion, Leftmost derivation, Backus-Naur Form (BNF), Treebank, Universal Dependencies (UD), Head-percolation rules.
> - **Higher-Level Tip:** Modern parsers use **transition-based** (e.g., arc-eager, arc-standard) or **graph-based** (e.g., MST) dependency parsing, NOT CFGs directly — but CFGs remain the *theoretical foundation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Semantics in NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Semantics** in Natural Language Processing is the branch of linguistics and computational linguistics concerned with the *meaning* conveyed by words, phrases, sentences, and entire texts. It is the study of how linguistic expressions derive their interpretations from the meanings of their constituent parts and the rules used to combine them.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, semantics is positioned *after* syntactic analysis because parsing alone gives a structural tree — but it does NOT answer the question: **"What does this sentence actually mean?"** Semantics bridges that gap by mapping syntactic constituents to formal meaning representations.

Mathematically, we can define semantics as a function:

$$\text{Meaning} : \text{Expression} \longrightarrow \text{Meaning Representation (MR)}$$

where the "Expression" is a parsed syntactic structure and the "Meaning Representation" is typically a logical form (FOL, λ-calculus), a semantic network, a frame, or a distributional vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:**
> - **CO1:** Apply fundamentals of syntactic and semantic processing to NLP tasks.
> - **CO2:** Analyze language models using statistical and neural approaches.

## 1.2 The Three Levels of Linguistic Analysis (Foundational Context)

Before we study semantics, recall the three traditional levels of linguistic analysis (per the KTU Module 1 syllabus hierarchy):

| Level | Question Answered | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Morphology** | How are words built? | "unhappiness" = un + happy + ness |
| **Syntax** | How are sentences structured? | Subject + Verb + Object |
| **Semantics** | What does the sentence *mean*? | "The bank approved the loan." → meaning: financial institution approved a financial instrument |
| **Pragmatics** | What is the *intended* meaning in context? | "Can you pass the salt?" → request, not yes/no question |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **The "Recipe vs. Meal" Analogy:**
> - **Syntax** is the *recipe* — it tells you the order of ingredients (words) and the steps.
> - **Semantics** is the *meal* — the actual edible result; what the recipe *means* to your stomach and brain.
> - You can have a perfectly written recipe (grammatical sentence) that produces something you cannot eat (nonsensical meaning): *"Colorless green ideas sleep furiously."* (Chomsky) — perfectly grammatical, semantically anomalous!

## 1.4 Why Semantics is Hard: Key Challenges

1. **Lexical Ambiguity:** A single word has multiple senses.
   - *Example:* "**Bank**" → river bank / financial bank / to bank (tilt).
2. **Syntactic Ambiguity → Semantic Ambiguity:** "I saw the man with the telescope."
3. **Quantifier Scope:** "Every student read a book." → Did all students read the *same* book or *different* books?
4. **Anaphora / Coreference:** "John told Mike *he* won." → Who is "he"?
5. **Metaphor / Idioms:** "It's raining cats and dogs." (Literal semantics fails; pragmatics needed.)
6. **World Knowledge Dependency:** "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because *it* was too big." → *it* = trophy or suitcase?

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Tools in the Field

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Standard Semantic Metrics & Resources (KTU high-yield):**
> - **WordNet** — a large lexical database of English (Nouns, Verbs, Adjectives, Adverbs) grouped into sets of cognitive synonyms (**synsets**).
> - **FrameNet** — a lexical resource based on frame semantics, encoding predicate-argument structures.
> - **PropBank** — predicate-argument annotations over the Penn Treebank.
> - **Senseval/SemEval** — standard international evaluation exercises for Word Sense Disambiguation.
> - **Distributional Semantics** — meaning as a vector in a high-dimensional space (e.g., **Word2Vec**, **GloVe**, **BERT embeddings**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Sub-Fields of Semantics (KTU Module 1 Pillar Concepts)

### A. **Lexical Semantics** — Meaning of Words
Lexical semantics studies the meanings of individual lexical items (words and multi-word expressions) and their relationships.

**Key Relations (all KTU high-yield):**

| Relation | Notation | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synonymy** | $w_1 \equiv w_2$ | Two words with nearly the same meaning | big ≡ large |
| **Antonymy** | $w_1 \perp w_2$ | Two words with opposite meanings | hot ⊥ cold |
| **Hyponymy / Hypernymy** | $w_1 \subset w_2$ | $w_1$ is-a kind of $w_2$ | dog ⊂ animal (hypernym = animal) |
| **Meronymy / Holonymy** | — | part-whole relation | wheel ⊂ car |
| **Homonymy** | $w_1 \neq w_2$ | Same spelling/sound, different meaning | **bank** (river/finance) |
| **Polysemy** | one word, multiple related senses | head (of person / of department) |
| **Metonymy** | substitution by association | "The *crown* declared war." (crown = king) |

### B. **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**
Given a word $w$ in context, determine which sense $s_i \in \text{Senses}(w)$ is intended.

**Two classical algorithms:**

1. **Lesk Algorithm (1986):** Choose the sense whose dictionary definition has the *greatest overlap* with the context words.
   $$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in \text{Senses}(w)} \vert \text{context} \cap \text{definition}(s) \vert$$

2. **Simplified Lesk:**
   $$\text{Score}(s) = \sum_{c \in C} \vert \text{definition}(s) \cap \text{definition}(c) \vert$$

3. **Yarowsky's Bootstrap (1995):** A semi-supervised ML approach using *one sense per collocation* and *one sense per discourse* heuristics.

### C. **Sentence-Level Semantics: Compositionality**
The **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege, 1892):
> *"The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them."*

Mathematically:
$$[\![S_1 \; S_2]\!] = f([\![S_1]\!], [\![S_2]\!])$$
where $[\![\cdot]\!]$ denotes the semantic interpretation function, and $f$ is a composition function.

### D. **Predicate-Argument Structure / Semantic Roles**
For a sentence, identify *who* did *what* to *whom*, *where*, *when*, *how*, *why*.

| Semantic Role | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent (AGT)** | Doer of the action | "John" (in "John broke the vase.") |
| **Patient / Theme (THM)** | Entity affected | "the vase" |
| **Instrument (INS)** | Tool used | "the hammer" |
| **Beneficiary (BEN)** | Receiver of benefit | "Mary" (in "He cooked dinner for Mary.") |
| **Location (LOC)** | Where | "in the kitchen" |
| **Time (TMP)** | When | "yesterday" |

The standard notation: $\text{predicate}(\text{ARG0}, \text{ARG1}, \text{ARG2}, \ldots)$ — e.g., $\text{give}(\text{John}, \text{book}, \text{Mary})$.

### E. **Distributional Hypothesis (Firth, 1957)**
> *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."*

Formal statement: For words $w_1$ and $w_2$,
$$\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) \propto \text{similarity of their contextual distributions}$$
Vector formulation (Word2Vec):
$$\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad d \in \{50, 100, 300\}$$

Similarity metrics:
- **Cosine similarity:** $\cos(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert\vec{u}\vert \, \vert\vec{v}\vert}$
- **Euclidean distance:** $d(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(u_i - v_i)^2}$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compositionality | $[\![S_1 S_2]\!] = f([\![S_1]\!], [\![S_2]\!])$ | Meaning composition |
| 2 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert\vec{u}\vert \vert\vec{v}\vert}$ | Vector semantics |
| 3 | Lesk Score | $\sum_{c \in C} \vert \text{Def}(s) \cap \text{Def}(c) \vert$ | WSD |
| 4 | Pointwise Mutual Information | $\text{PMI}(x,y) = \log \frac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$ | Collocation extraction |
| 5 | λ-calculus application | $(\lambda x . P)(a) \rightarrow P[x/a]$ | Formal semantics |
| 6 | Type logic | $e$ (entity), $t$ (truth value), $\langle e,t \rangle$ (property) | Montague Grammar |
| 7 | TF-IDF weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}_{t,d} \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}_t}$ | Term importance |
| 8 | Hyponymy transitivity | If $A \subset B$ and $B \subset C$, then $A \subset C$ | WordNet hierarchy |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Semantic Systems

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Query understanding, semantic search (BERT-based).
- **Virtual Assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant):** Intent classification, slot filling.
- **Machine Translation (Google Translate):** Cross-lingual meaning transfer.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Determining opinion polarity in product reviews.
- **Question Answering Systems:** Semantic parsing of natural language questions.
- **Information Extraction:** Identifying entities, relations, and events from text.
- **Chatbots & Dialogue Systems:** Conversational meaning tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Converting a Sentence to First-Order Logic (FOL)

This is a classic KTU-style analytical problem: given an English sentence, derive its formal semantic representation.

**Sentence:** *"Every student read a book."*

**Step 1 — Identify the quantifier and its scope.**
The universal quantifier "Every" governs the subject NP "student."

**Step 2 — Identify the predicate(s).**
- "student" is a unary predicate: $\text{Student}(x)$
- "read" is a binary predicate: $\text{Read}(x, y)$
- "book" is a unary predicate: $\text{Book}(y)$

**Step 3 — Build the FOL formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{English:} \quad \text{Every student read a book.} \\
&\text{FOL form 1 (one book interpretation):} \quad \forall x \, (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Book}(y) \land \text{Read}(x, y))) \\
&\text{FOL form 2 (specific book interpretation):} \quad \exists y \, (\text{Book}(y) \land \forall x \, (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x, y)))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Explain the difference (Scope ambiguity).**
- **Wide scope on $\exists y$** (Form 1): Each student read *some* book (books may differ).
- **Wide scope on $\forall x$** (Form 2): There is *one particular* book that all students read.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a standard KTU examination question pattern. Students often lose marks for not drawing the scope tree.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Lambda Calculus for Sentence Meaning

**Sentence:** *"A dog barks."*

**Step 1 — Lexical semantics of "a" (indefinite article).**
$$[\![\text{a}]\!] = \lambda P . \lambda Q . \exists x \, (P(x) \land Q(x))$$
Meaning: "a" takes a property $P$ and another property $Q$, and asserts that there is some $x$ that is $P$ and is $Q$.

**Step 2 — Lexical semantics of "dog".**
$$[\![\text{dog}]\!] = \lambda y . \text{Dog}(y)$$

**Step 3 — Lexical semantics of "barks".**
$$[\![\text{barks}]\!] = \lambda z . \text{Bark}(z)$$

**Step 4 — Combine "a dog".**
$$\begin{aligned}
[\![\text{a dog}]\!] &= [\![\text{a}]\!]([\![\text{dog}]\!]) \\
&= (\lambda P . \lambda Q . \exists x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)))(\lambda y . \text{Dog}(y)) \\
&= \lambda Q . \exists x \, (\text{Dog}(x) \land Q(x))
\end{aligned}$$

> The $\lambda y$ is renamed to $\lambda x$ to avoid variable capture. (Standard β-reduction step.)

**Step 5 — Apply to "barks" to close the sentence.**
$$\begin{aligned}
[\![\text{A dog barks.}]\!] &= [\![\text{a dog}]\!]([\![\text{barks}]\!]) \\
&= (\lambda Q . \exists x \, (\text{Dog}(x) \land Q(x)))(\lambda z . \text{Bark}(z)) \\
&= \exists x \, (\text{Dog}(x) \land \text{Bark}(x))
\end{aligned}$$

**Final result:** $\exists x \, (\text{Dog}(x) \land \text{Bark}(x))$ — "There exists an entity that is both a dog and barks." ✅

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Applying the Lesk Algorithm

**Sentence:** *"I went to the **bank** to deposit money."*

**Step 1 — Identify the target ambiguous word.**
Target: **bank**

**Step 2 — List candidate senses (from WordNet, simplified):**
- **Sense 1 (BANK_N_FIN):** *"A financial institution that accepts deposits and channels those deposits into lending activities."*
- **Sense 2 (BANK_N_RIVER):** *"Sloping land (especially the slope beside a body of water)."*

**Step 3 — Identify context words.**
Context: $\{ \text{went, deposit, money} \}$

**Step 4 — Compute overlap.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Overlap}(S_1) &= \vert \{\text{went, deposit, money}\} \cap \text{Def}(S_1) \vert = \vert \{\text{deposits, lending}\} \vert = 1 \\
\text{Overlap}(S_2) &= \vert \{\text{went, deposit, money}\} \cap \text{Def}(S_2) \vert = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the sense with maximum overlap.**
