Only trees where every equation holds are valid parses. ### Step 4: Rejection of Ill-Formed Strings A string for which *no* feature-consistent parse exists is declared **ungrammatical** by the augmented grammar — even if a pure CFG would happily accept it. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Rule | Mathematical Form | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | AG Formal Definition | $AG = (V_N, V_T, \Sigma, S, P, F, A)$ | Complete formal tuple | | 2 | Number Constraint | $\text{NP.NUM} = \text{VP.NUM}$ | Subject–verb number agreement | | 3 | Person Constraint | $\text{NP.PERS} = \text{VP.PERS}$ | Subject–verb person agreement | | 4 | Gender Constraint | $\text{DET.GEN} = \text{N.GEN}$ | Determiner–noun gender agreement | | 5 | Case Constraint | $\text{PRO.CASE} = \text{position}$ | Pronoun in nominative must be subject | | 6 | Lexical Feature Bundle | $w : C[f_1=v_1, f_2=v_2, \dots]$ | Word's feature fingerprint | | 7 | Agreement Equation | $X.f = Y.f$ | Identity of features X and Y | | 8 | Unification (⊕) | $FS_1 \oplus FS_2$ | Merging compatible feature structures | | 9 | Number Domain | $\text{NUM} \in \{sg, pl\}$ | Cardinality | | 10 | Person Domain | $\text{PERS} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | Grammatical person | | 11 | Gender Domain | $\text{GEN} \in \{masc, fem, neut\}$ | (English: 3-valued) | | 12 | Case Domain | $\text{CASE} \in \{nom, acc, dat, gen\}$ | Syntactic role marking | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Isolation Note**: In KTU answer sheets, always wrap your equations in a single aligned block with `$$ ... $$` on its own line — never embed them in paragraph text. Examiners deduct marks for improper math formatting. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Augmented Grammars underpin every **production-grade NLP system** that needs to *parse, not just pattern-match*. Concrete engineering applications: 1. **Machine Translation (Google Translate, DeepL)** — ensures translated subject and verb forms agree. 2. **Grammar Correction Tools (Grammarly, MS Editor)** — flags *"He don't know"* as erroneous by detecting number mismatch. 3. **Question-Answering Systems** — pronoun resolution requires antecedent agreement detection. 4. **Speech Recognition Post-Processing** — converts *"they was"* → *"they were"* using AG-based rules. 5. **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** — picks correct verb conjugation for synthesized sentences. 6. **Information Extraction** — agreement helps identify entity boundaries. ## 2.5 Decomposition of Agreement Phenomena in CFG A pure CFG describes *which strings are syntactically well-formed*. An AG refines this by adding three layers: **Layer 1 — Structural Validity** (provided by CFG): *"Is the bracket sequence of constituents correct?"* **Layer 2 — Lexical-Class Validity** (provided by subcategorization): *"Are the parts-of-speech appropriate (Det before N, V after NP)?"* **Layer 3 — Feature Compatibility** (provided by AG): *"Do the lexical and phrasal features unify successfully?"* Only when **all three layers pass** is a sentence accepted as grammatical. ## 2.6 Worked Formal Example — Annotated Production Rule Consider the production: $$S \rightarrow NP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; VP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p]$$ The variables `?n` and `?p` are **unification variables**. The rule asserts: *whatever value `n` and `p` take on the subject NP, the verb phrase's number and person must take the identical values*. This is a **declarative feature equation** rather than a procedural check. Substituting: - `NP = "the dogs"` with `[NUM=pl, PERS=3rd]` → `?n=pl, ?p=3rd` - VP must accept `[NUM=pl, PERS=3rd]` → selects `VP → "bark"` - The string *"the dogs bark"* parses successfully. Conversely, *"the dog bark"* fails because `dog` gives `[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd]` but `bark` requires `[NUM=pl]`. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Building an AG for English Subject–Verb Agreement ### Step 1 — Start with the underlying CFG $$S \rightarrow NP \; VP$$ $$NP \rightarrow Det \; N$$ $$NP \rightarrow Pronoun$$ $$VP \rightarrow V \; NP \; \mid \; V$$ $$Det \rightarrow \text{the} \mid \text{a} \mid \text{this} \mid \text{these}$$ $$N \rightarrow \text{dog} \mid \text{cats} \mid \text{book} \mid \text{books}$$ $$V \rightarrow \text{run} \mid \text{runs} \mid \text{bark} \mid \text{barks}$$ $$Pronoun \rightarrow \text{I} \mid \text{you} \mid \text{he} \mid \text{she} \mid \text{it} \mid \text{we} \mid \text{they}$$ ### Step 2 — Augment the Lexicon with Features Each terminal is given an explicit feature bundle: $$\text{the} : Det[NUM=sg \mid pl, GEN=masc \mid fem \mid neut]$$ $$\text{this} : Det[NUM=sg]$$ $$\text{these} : Det[NUM=pl]$$ $$\text{dog} : N[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{dogs} : N[NUM=pl, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{cats} : N[NUM=pl, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{book} : N[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{run} : V[NUM=pl, TENSE=pres]$$ $$\text{runs} : V[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, TENSE=pres]$$ $$\text{bark} : V[NUM=pl, TENSE=pres]$$ $$\text{barks} : V[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, TENSE=pres]$$ ### Step 3 — Augment the Phrasal Rules with Equations The phrasal rules now carry **feature constraints**: $$S \rightarrow NP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; VP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p]$$ $$NP \rightarrow Det[NUM=?n, GEN=?g] \; N[NUM=?n, GEN=?g]$$ $$VP \rightarrow V[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; NP \quad \text{(object NP unconstrained w.r.t. V)}$$ $$VP \rightarrow V[NUM=?n, PERS=?p]$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate a Sample Sentence Sentence: **"The dog runs."** **Lexical lookup:** - `The` → `Det[NUM=sg, GEN=neut]` (compatible with all 3 genders) - `dog` → `N[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut]` - `runs` → `V[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, TENSE=pres]` **Building the NP:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NP} \Rightarrow \; & Det[NUM=?n, GEN=?g] \; N[NUM=?n, GEN=?g] \\ & \text{with } Det = \text{"the"}, N = \text{"dog"} \\ & \Rightarrow Det[NUM=sg, GEN=neut] + N[NUM=sg, GEN=neut] \\ & \text{Unification: } ?n = sg, ?g = neut \; \text{(consistent)} \\ & \Rightarrow NP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd, GEN=neut] \end{aligned}$$ **Building the VP:** $$\begin{aligned} VP \Rightarrow \; & V[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \\ & \text{with } V = \text{"runs"} \\ & \Rightarrow VP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd] \end{aligned}$$ **Combining S:** $$\begin{aligned} S \Rightarrow \; & NP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; VP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \\ & \text{with } NP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd], \; VP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd] \\ & \text{Unification: } ?n = sg, ?p = 3rd \; \text{(consistent)} \\ & \Rightarrow S \; \text{— parsed successfully!} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate an Ill-Formed Counter-Example Sentence: **"The dogs run."** (Note: *should be "The dogs run." is grammatical but let's try a violation*) Counter-example: **"The dog run."** **Lexical lookup:** - `The` → `Det[NUM=sg]` - `dog` → `N[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd]` - `run` → `V[NUM=pl, TENSE=pres]` **Building the NP:** $$NP \Rightarrow Det[NUM=sg] + N[NUM=sg] \Rightarrow NP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd]$$ **Building the VP:** $$VP \Rightarrow V[NUM=pl] \Rightarrow VP[NUM=pl]$$ **Combining S:** $$\begin{aligned} S \Rightarrow \; & NP[NUM=sg, PERS=3rd] \; VP[NUM=pl, PERS=3rd] \\ & \text{Equation: } NP.NUM = VP.NUM \\ & \text{Required: } sg = pl \; \text{— FAIL!} \end{aligned}$$ The augmented grammar correctly **rejects** *"The dog run."* with a feature unification failure. ## 3.2 The Unification Algorithm — Detailed Trace Given two feature structures $FS_1$ and $FS_2$, the unification operation $\oplus$ is defined recursively: $$\begin{aligned} \text{UNIFY}(\emptyset, FS_2) &= FS_2 \\ \text{UNIFY}(FS_1, \emptyset) &= FS_1 \\ \text{UNIFY}([f=v] \cup FS_1, \; [f=v'] \cup FS_2) &= \begin{cases} [v/v'] \cup \text{UNIFY}(FS_1, FS_2) & \text{if } v = v' \\ \text{FAIL} & \text{if } v \neq v' \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ Where $[v/v']$ denotes a substitution binding variable $v$ to value $v'$. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Common KTU Scenarios | Sentence | NP Features | V Features | Constraint Check | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | *He runs* | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | ✓ matches | **Grammatical** | | *They run* | `[NUM=pl, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=pl]` | ✓ matches | **Grammatical** | | *He run* | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=pl]` | ✗ NUM mismatch | **Rejected** | | *They runs* | `[NUM=pl, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | ✗ NUM mismatch | **Rejected** | | *She walk* | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=pl]` | ✗ NUM mismatch | **Rejected** | | *I goes* | `[NUM=sg, PERS=1]` | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | ✗ PERS mismatch | **Rejected** | | *It barks* | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` | ✓ matches | **Grammatical** | ## 3.4 Multi-Constraint Derivation: Determiner–Noun + Subject–Verb Simultaneously Consider: **"These dogs bark."** **Constraints to satisfy simultaneously:** 1. `Det.NUM = N.NUM` → `these` (NUM=pl) = `dogs` (NUM=pl) ✓ 2. `NP.NUM = VP.NUM` → `dogs` (NUM=pl) = `bark` (NUM=pl) ✓ 3. `NP.PERS = VP.PERS` → both `3rd` ✓ All three constraints unify successfully — sentence is **accepted**. **Counter-example: "These dog barks."** 1. `Det.NUM = N.NUM` → `these` (pl) ≠ `dog` (sg) ✗ **FAIL at Det–N constraint** The parser halts immediately without ever reaching subject–verb agreement. ## 3.5 Complexity Analysis of AG Parsing Let $n$ be the sentence length and $k$ be the number of features per non-terminal. The augmented parser's complexity relative to the underlying CFG is: $$\text{Time}_{\text{AG-Parser}} = \text{Time}_{\text{CFG-Parser}} \times O(k \cdot c)$$ where $c$ is the average cost of a unification operation (typically $O(k)$ for attribute-value structures). For a CKY-based AG parser: $$T(n) = O(n^3 \cdot |P| \cdot k^2)$$ This polynomial blow-up is the chief practical limitation of purely feature-based augmented grammars and motivates probabilistic alternatives (PCFGs, HPSG, Lexical-Functional Grammar) discussed in advanced KTU electives. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Augmented Grammar Parser The following diagram depicts the end-to-end data flow inside an AG-based parsing pipeline: ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 classDef proc fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C classDef store fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 classDef reject fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C IN[Input Sentence Tokens]:::input LEX[Augmented Lexicon Lookup Module]:::proc LFB[Lexical Feature Bundles]:::store PARSER[Bottom-Up Chart Parser with AG Constraints]:::proc RULES[Augmented Grammar Rule Base]:::store UNIF[Feature Unification Engine]:::proc AGCHK[Agreement Constraint Checker]:::proc DEC{All Feature Constraints Satisfied?}:::proc OUTP[Output Parse Tree with Features]:::out REJ[Reject Ill-Formed Sentence]:::reject ERR[Diagnostic Error Report]:::reject IN --> LEX LEX -->|queries| RULES LEX --> LFB LFB --> PARSER PARSER --> UNIF RULES --> PARSER PARSER --> AGCHK UNIF --> AGCHK AGCHK --> DEC DEC -->|Yes| OUTP DEC -->|No| REJ REJ --> ERR ``` ## 4.2 Feature Propagation Flow Through a Parse Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef npNode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 classDef vpNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C classDef vNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 classDef detNode fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,color:#880E4F classDef nNode fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,color:#311B92 classDef sNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#BF360C SNode[S
NUM=sg, PERS=3]:::sNode NPNP[NP subject
NUM=sg, PERS=3]:::npNode VPNode[VP
NUM=sg, PERS=3]:::vpNode DetNode[Det the
NUM=sg]:::detNode NNode[N dog
NUM=sg, PERS=3]:::nNode VNode[V runs
NUM=sg, PERS=3]:::vNode NPobj[NP object
NUM=sg]:::npNode SNode --> NPNP SNode --> VPNode NPNP --> DetNode NPNP --> NNode VPNode --> VNode VPNode --> NPobj ``` Each downward arrow represents a **feature inheritance** pass; each upward arrow represents a **feature unification** check. ## 4.3 Agreement Constraint Propagation Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant LEX as Augmented Lexicon participant NP as NP Builder participant VP as VP Builder participant S as S Builder participant UN as Unification Engine LEX->>NP: Det "the" [NUM=sg, GEN=any] LEX->>NP: N "dog" [NUM=sg, GEN=neut] NP->>UN: Check Det.NUM = N.NUM UN-->>NP: ✓ Pass (sg=sg) NP->>S: NP [NUM=sg, PERS=3] LEX->>VP: V "runs" [NUM=sg, PERS=3] VP->>UN: Verify V features internally UN-->>VP: ✓ Pass VP->>S: VP [NUM=sg, PERS=3] S->>UN: Check NP.NUM = VP.NUM UN->>UN: sg = sg ✓ S->>UN: Check NP.PERS = VP.PERS UN->>UN: 3 = 3 ✓ UN-->>S: All constraints satisfied — parse accepted ``` ## 4.4 Failure Path — Ill-Formed Sentence Detection ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef ok fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 classDef bad fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C classDef mid fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C A[Sentence: The dog run]:::bad B{NP Stage
Det.NUM = N.NUM?}:::mid C[NP Pass:
the+dog NUM=sg]:::ok D{VP Stage
V has compatible NUM?}:::mid E[V run NUM=pl]:::ok F{S Stage
NP.NUM = VP.NUM?}:::mid G[FAIL:
sg ≠ pl]:::bad H[Reject Sentence
Diagnostic: Number Mismatch
Suggest runs]:::bad A --> B B -->|Yes| C C --> D D -->|V accepted| E E --> F F -->|Mismatch| G G --> H ``` ## 4.5 Type Hierarchy of Agreement Features ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef root fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C classDef lvl2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C classDef leaf fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 ROOT[Grammatical Agreement Features]:::root N[NUM Number]:::lvl2 P[PERS Person]:::lvl2 G[GEN Gender]:::lvl2 C[CASE Case]:::lvl2 NSG[sg]:::leaf NPL[pl]:::leaf P1[1st: I, we]:::leaf P2[2nd: you]:::leaf P3[3rd: he, she, it, they]:::leaf GMASC[masc: he, boy]:::leaf GFEM[fem: she, girl]:::leaf GNEUT[neut: it, thing]:::leaf CNOM[nom: subject]:::leaf CACC[acc: object]:::leaf ROOT --> N ROOT --> P ROOT --> G ROOT --> C N --> NSG N --> NPL P --> P1 P --> P2 P --> P3 G --> GMASC G --> GFEM G --> GNEUT C --> CNOM C --> CACC ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Define Augmented Grammar. Why is it needed when we already have a Context-Free Grammar for English syntax?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Augmented Grammar (AG)** is a Context-Free Grammar extended with a set of **linguistic features** (such as number, person, gender, case) and a set of **agreement constraints** that enforce feature compatibility between syntactically related constituents. Formally: $$AG = (V_N, V_T, \Sigma, S, P, F, A)$$ It is needed because a **pure CFG** cannot enforce linguistic agreement — it only checks hierarchical structure. For instance, CFGs cannot distinguish *"The dog runs"* from *"The dog run"*, since both have identical tree structure. The AG attaches **feature bundles** like `[NUM=sg]` to terminals and adds **feature equations** to phrasal rules (e.g., `NP.NUM = VP.NUM`), so ungrammatical feature combinations are rejected. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **List any four types of agreement phenomena in English with one example sentence for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Agreement Type | Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Subject–Verb** (number) | She *writes* code. (NOT: She *write* code.) | | 2 | **Determiner–Noun** | *This* book is new. (NOT: *These* book is new.) | | 3 | **Pronoun–Antecedent** | The students took *their* bags. (NOT: The students took *its* bags.) | | 4 | **Person Agreement** | *I* am tired. / *We* are tired. | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]` **a)** *Explain the formal structure of an Augmented Grammar. Define its components clearly. How does it differ from a Context-Free Grammar?* **(7 marks)** **b)** *Consider the sentence "These cats run." Build an Augmented Grammar that accepts this sentence but rejects "This cat run" and "These cats runs." Show the parse tree with feature unification.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for 5.2(a) **Formal Structure of AG (7 marks):** An Augmented Grammar is a 7-tuple: $$AG = (V_N, V_T, \Sigma, S, P, F, A)$$ **Each component:** 1. **$V_N$ (Non-terminals):** Set of syntactic categories, e.g., `{S, NP, VP, Det, N, V, Pronoun}`. 2. **$V_T$ (Terminals):** Set of lexical items, e.g., `{the, a, dog, dogs, run, runs, I, he, …}`. 3. **$\Sigma$ (Root Alphabet):** The set of all symbols (typically $V_N \cup V_T$). 4. **$S$ (Start symbol):** The distinguished non-terminal from which all parses begin. 5. **$P$ (Productions):** Set of CFG rules augmented with **feature equations**, e.g., $S \rightarrow NP \; VP \; \{\text{NP.NUM} = \text{VP.NUM}\}$. 6. **$F$ (Features):** A finite set of attribute names with their domains, e.g., `{NUM ∈ {sg, pl}, PERS ∈ {1, 2, 3}, GEN ∈ {masc, fem, neut}, CASE ∈ {nom, acc}}`. 7. **$A$ (Agreement Rules):** Equations / constraints that must hold across constituents. These may be **lexical equations** (built into terminal entries) or **structural equations** (attached to phrasal rules). **Difference from CFG:** | Aspect | CFG | AG | |---|---|---| | Feature handling | None | Explicit feature bundles | | Acceptance criterion | Structural validity only | Structural + feature validity | | Rejection power | Cannot reject feature mismatches | Rejects feature mismatches | | Annotation | Bare symbols | Symbols with attribute-value matrices | | Generative capacity | Context-free languages | Strict subset (filtered by agreement) | #### Model Solution for 5.2(b) **Step 1: Define the Augmented Lexicon (3 marks)** $$\text{this} : Det[NUM=sg]$$ $$\text{these} : Det[NUM=pl]$$ $$\text{cat} : N[NUM=sg, PERS=3, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{cats} : N[NUM=pl, PERS=3, GEN=neut]$$ $$\text{run} : V[NUM=pl, PERS=any, TENSE=pres]$$ $$\text{runs} : V[NUM=sg, PERS=3, TENSE=pres]$$ **Step 2: Define the Augmented Rules (2 marks)** $$S \rightarrow NP[NUM=?n] \; VP[NUM=?n]$$ $$NP \rightarrow Det[NUM=?n] \; N[NUM=?n]$$ $$VP \rightarrow V[NUM=?n]$$ **Step 3: Verify the Three Test Sentences (2 marks)** | Sentence | Det–N Check | S Check (NP.NUM=VP.NUM) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | *"These cats run"* | `these`(pl) = `cats`(pl) ✓ | NP(pl) = `run`(pl) ✓ | **Accepted** | | *"This cat run"* | `this`(sg) = `cat`(sg) ✓ | NP(sg) ≠ `run`(pl) ✗ | **Rejected** | | *"These cats runs"* | `these`(pl) = `cats`(pl) ✓ | NP(pl) ≠ `runs`(sg) ✗ | **Rejected** | The parse tree for the accepted sentence shows `?n=pl` unifying consistently throughout. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]` **a)** *Explain Subject–Verb agreement and Determiner–Noun agreement with formal feature constraints. Why is feature unification essential in an Augmented Grammar?* **(7 marks)** **b)** *Given the lexicon and rules below, verify whether each of the following is grammatical or not using the Augmented Grammar: (i) "She walks", (ii) "They walks", (iii) "We runs". Show the unification trace for each.* **(7 marks)** > Lexicon: `she: Pron[NUM=sg, PERS=3]`, `they: Pron[NUM=pl, PERS=3]`, `we: Pron[NUM=pl, PERS=1]`, `walks: V[NUM=sg, PERS=3]`, `runs: V[NUM=pl, PERS=any]` > Rules: $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$; $NP \rightarrow Pron$; $VP \rightarrow V$; Constraint: $NP.NUM = VP.NUM, \; NP.PERS = VP.PERS$ #### Model Solution for 5.2 Alternative (a) **Subject–Verb Agreement (3 marks):** The verb must carry the **same number and person features** as its subject NP. Formal constraint: $$S \rightarrow NP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; VP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p]$$ Example: *"He runs"* → both `he` and `runs` have `[NUM=sg, PERS=3]` — unifies ✓. **Determiner–Noun Agreement (2 marks):** The determiner must agree with the noun in **number** (and in some languages, gender). Formal constraint: $$NP \rightarrow Det[NUM=?n, GEN=?g] \; N[NUM=?n, GEN=?g]$$ Example: *"this book"* (sg–sg) ✓ but *"these book"* (pl–sg) ✗. **Why Feature Unification is Essential (2 marks):** Feature unification is the *unification-based constraint-solving mechanism* that merges two feature structures if they are compatible and rejects them if they conflict. It is essential because: 1. It enforces **declarative, monotonic** feature checking — no procedural ordering bugs. 2. It supports **multi-feature joint constraints** (e.g., number AND person simultaneously). 3. It is the formal basis for advanced formalisms (LFG, HPSG, TAG) in modern NLP. #### Model Solution for 5.2 Alternative (b) **(i) "She walks" (2 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} & Pron: [NUM=sg, PERS=3] \\ & V: [NUM=sg, PERS=3] \\ & \text{Check } NP.NUM = VP.NUM \Rightarrow sg = sg \; ✓ \\ & \text{Check } NP.PERS = VP.PERS \Rightarrow 3 = 3 \; ✓ \\ & \text{Verdict: GRAMMATICAL} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) "They walks" (2 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} & Pron: [NUM=pl, PERS=3] \\ & V: [NUM=sg, PERS=3] \\ & \text{Check } NP.NUM = VP.NUM \Rightarrow pl = sg \; ✗ \\ & \text{Check } NP.PERS = VP.PERS \Rightarrow 3 = 3 \; ✓ \\ & \text{Verdict: UNGRAMMATICAL (Number Mismatch)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) "We runs" (3 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} & Pron: [NUM=pl, PERS=1] \\ & V: [NUM=pl, PERS=any] \\ & \text{Check } NP.NUM = VP.NUM \Rightarrow pl = pl \; ✓ \\ & \text{Check } NP.PERS = VP.PERS \Rightarrow 1 = any \\ & \quad \text{(Unification Rule: variable bound to its value)} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{Unifies as } any \to 1 \\ & \text{Verdict: GRAMMATICAL} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: > 1. **The biggest mark-loser** is failing to write **both** number AND person checks when the question says "subject–verb agreement." Always show two equations. > 2. Students often **omit the Lexicon annotation** and start directly with the rules — the Lexicon is where feature information originates, and examiners allocate at least 2–3 marks for it. > 3. Do **not** confuse `[PERS=any]` with "wildcard that matches everything regardless of context." It is a variable that gets **bound** by unification, not a universal quantifier. > 4. Forgetting to write the `?variable` unification notation explicitly loses marks on derivations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Augmented Grammar (AG)** = CFG + Features + Agreement Constraints. Formal tuple: $AG = (V_N, V_T, \Sigma, S, P, F, A)$. - **Agreement** is a co-variation phenomenon where one word's form mirrors another's grammatical features. - The **four primary agreement types** for English NLP: Subject–Verb, Determiner–Noun, Pronoun–Antecedent, Tense Agreement. - The **four standard features** in English AG: `NUM ∈ {sg, pl}`, `PERS ∈ {1, 2, 3}`, `GEN ∈ {masc, fem, neut}`, `CASE ∈ {nom, acc}`. - **Feature unification** is the declarative mechanism that merges two feature structures if compatible and rejects conflicting ones. - **Subject–verb rule template:** $S \rightarrow NP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p] \; VP[NUM=?n, PERS=?p]$ — variables must unify across constituents. - **Determiner–noun rule template:** $NP \rightarrow Det[NUM=?n, GEN=?g] \; N[NUM=?n, GEN=?g]$. - **Pure CFG cannot reject** feature-mismatch sentences — that is the **fundamental motivation** for AG. - **Augmented parsing complexity** for CKY is $O(n^3 \cdot |P| \cdot k^2)$ where $n$ = sentence length, $k$ = features per node. - **Lexicon augmentation** is mandatory — terminals must carry feature bundles before any phrasal rule can reference them. - **Variable binding via `?`** is the KTU-board-accepted notation for feature unification across rule constituents. - AGs are foundational stepping stones to **Lexical-Functional Grammar (LFG)** and **Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG)**, which are extensions covered in advanced NLP electives. - In answer sheets: **always draw a feature table** for the test sentences before writing the parse; examiners reward systematic tabular verification. - **Mnemonic**: "**A**ugmented = **A**greement + **A**ttributes" — three A's to recall what AG adds over CFG.

topics.title: Grammars for Natural Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Grammars for Natural Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Grammars for Natural Language

## 1.1 Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **grammar** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a formal mathematical device used to describe the syntactic structure of natural language sentences. In the KTU 2024 scheme, a **context-free grammar (CFG)** is defined as the quadruple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where:
- $N$ = a finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (syntactic categories like Noun, Verb, NP, VP)
- $\Sigma$ = a finite set of **terminal symbols** (the actual words)
- $R$ = a finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$
- $S \in N$ = a distinguished **start symbol** (typically $S$)

A grammar **generates** a string by recursively replacing non-terminals with right-hand-side expansions until only terminals remain. A string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ is in the language $L(G)$ if and only if $S \stackrel{*}{\Rightarrow} w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 covers *Context-Free Grammars (CFG)*, *Grammar Equivalence and Normal Forms*, *Treebanks*, *Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar*, *Categorial Grammar*, *Dependency Grammar*, and *Augmented Grammar Rules (AGR)*. Mastery of CFG formalism is the foundation of constituency parsing in KTU examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sentence Recipe Book"

Think of a grammar as a **recipe book for sentences**. The recipe book has two parts:
1. **The Vocabulary Page (Lexicon)** — lists every ingredient (word) and what *kind* of ingredient it is (Noun, Verb, Determiner, etc.).
2. **The Recipe Rules (Productions)** — tell you how ingredients can be combined. For example:
   - *"A Sentence is made of a Noun Phrase followed by a Verb Phrase."*
   - *"A Noun Phrase is made of a Determiner followed by a Noun."*

Just as a chef follows recipes to build a cake layer-by-layer, a parser uses grammar rules to build a **parse tree** of a sentence layer-by-layer. The topmost layer is $S$ (Sentence), the bottommost layer contains the actual words, and every intermediate node is a structural category.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *grammar* is a *declarative* specification — it tells us what strings are valid and how they are structured. A *parser* is an *algorithmic* procedure that takes a grammar and a string, and decides whether/how the string can be derived.

---

## 1.2 The Lexicon and Grammar Rules — Working Together

### Example Lexicon (Toy Fragment)

| Word | Part of Speech |
| :--- | :--- |
| the | Det |
| a | Det |
| dog | N |
| cat | N |
| sees | V |
| runs | V |
| John | N (Proper) |
| Mary | N (Proper) |

### Example Production Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\ VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det\ N \\
NP &\rightarrow Det\ N\ PP \\
VP &\rightarrow V\ NP \\
PP &\rightarrow P\ NP
\end{aligned}
$$

Together, the lexicon and rules generate tree structures. The rule $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ declares that **every declarative English sentence consists of a Noun Phrase followed by a Verb Phrase** — a structural fact known as the *binary branching hypothesis* of English syntax (Chomsky, 1957).

> [!TIP]
> **Why CFG Matters in KTU:** CFG is the *lingua franca* of syntactic formalisms in NLP. Even modern neural parsers implicitly learn CFG-like structures from treebank data. KTU questions frequently test your ability to write rules, build trees, and convert grammars to **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**.

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituency, Heads, and Phrase Structure

A **constituent** is a group of words that behaves as a single unit within a sentence. In CFG, every phrase subtree (NP, VP, PP) is a constituent. Constituents are evidenced by four classic linguistic tests:

1. **Substitution Test** — can the group be replaced by a pronoun?
2. **Movement Test** — can the group be moved as a unit?
3. **Coordination Test** — can the group be conjoined with another of the same type?
4. **Question Test** — can the group be the answer to a wh-question?

### The Head of a Phrase

Every phrase has a **head** — the central word that determines the phrase's grammatical properties.

| Phrase Type | Head Word Category | Example Head |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NP | Noun (N) | *dog* in "the big brown dog" |
| VP | Verb (V) | *chased* in "chased the cat" |
| PP | Preposition (P) | *with* in "with a spoon" |
| AP | Adjective (A) | *happy* in "very happy" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Point:** Phrases project from their heads. This is the cornerstone of *Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG)* and *Dependency Grammar*, both required KTU topics.

## 2.2 Treebanks — Empirical Grammar Learning

A **treebank** is a corpus in which every sentence has been manually annotated with a syntactic parse tree. The most famous is the **Penn Treebank** (Marcus et al., 1993), containing ~1 million words of Wall Street Journal text.

### Why Treebanks Matter

Treebanks solve the **rule coverage problem** of hand-crafted grammars. Rather than writing thousands of rules by intuition, linguists annotate real text, then use machine learning to **extract a probabilistic grammar** directly from the annotated data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Treebanks form the empirical backbone of statistical and neural parsing. Be prepared for questions linking CFG to Treebank-based grammar induction.

## 2.3 Grammar Equivalence and Normal Forms

Two grammars $G_1$ and $G_2$ are **weakly equivalent** if $L(G_1) = L(G_2)$ (they generate the same set of strings). They are **strongly equivalent** if they assign the same parse tree structure to every string.

### Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form** if every rule is of one of two forms:
- $A \rightarrow B\ C$ where $A, B, C \in N$
- $A \rightarrow a$ where $A \in N$ and $a \in \Sigma$

CNF is essential because it enables the **CKY (Cocke-Kasami-Younger) algorithm** for parsing in $O(n^3)$ time, where $n$ is the sentence length.

## 2.4 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)

A PCFG augments CFG with rule probabilities. Each rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ has probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$.

### Core Probability Constraints

For every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

The probability of a parse tree $T$ with nodes $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ is the product of the rule probabilities used:

$$P(T, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(rule_i)$$

The probability of a sentence $s$ is the sum over all possible parse trees:

$$P(s) = \sum_{T:\ yield(T) = s} P(T, s)$$

## 2.5 Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG)

HPSG is a **lexicalist**, **constraint-based**, **unification-based** grammar formalism. Instead of rules rewriting strings, HPSG uses:
- **Typed feature structures (TFS)** — complex attribute-value matrices
- **Lexical heads** — phrases inherit properties from lexical heads
- **Principles** — universal constraints (Head Feature Principle, Valence Principle, etc.)

In HPSG, every word is a complex object with attributes like `HEAD`, `VALENCE`, `SUBJ`, `COMPS`. Grammar rules are replaced by **immediate dominance (ID) schemas** that combine headed phrases via **unification**.

## 2.6 Categorial Grammar

In **categorial grammar** (Ajdukiewicz, Bar-Hillel), categories are built from a small set of basic categories using two type-forming operators:

- $X / Y$ — "an $X$ that needs a $Y$ on its right"
- $X \backslash Y$ — "an $X$ that needs a $Y$ on its left"

The two inference rules are:

| Rule | Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forward Application** | $X / Y, Y \Rightarrow X$ | Combine $X/Y$ with $Y$ on its right |
| **Backward Application** | $Y, X \backslash Y \Rightarrow X$ | Combine $X\backslash Y$ with $Y$ on its left |

Example: assign to "John" the category $NP$, to "sleeps" the category $S \backslash NP$ (a sentence missing a left NP). Then:

$$\frac{NP \quad S \backslash NP}{S}$$

yields $S$ (the sentence "John sleeps").

### Combinatory Categorial Grammar (CCG)

CCG extends categorial grammar with **combinators** like forward ($B$) and backward ($B'$) composition, capturing long-distance dependencies elegantly.

## 2.7 Dependency Grammar

**Dependency grammar** models syntax as a set of **binary asymmetric relations** (dependencies) between words. Each word (except the root) has exactly one **head** it depends on.

Key properties:
- **Head-dependent relations** are labeled (e.g., `nsubj`, `dobj`, `amod`)
- Dependencies form a **tree** (or graph with projectivity constraints)
- Word order is derived from linearization of the dependency tree

Dependency grammar is simpler than CFG, more robust to free-word-order languages, and underlies many modern parsers (e.g., Stanford Dependencies, Universal Dependencies).

## 2.8 Augmented Grammar Rules (AGR) — Handling Agreement and Subcategorization

Pure CFGs cannot easily capture **subject-verb agreement** (*he runs* vs *they run*) or **verb subcategorization** (verbs selecting specific complements). **Augmented Grammar Rules** attach features to non-terminals to solve this.

### Subject-Verb Number Agreement

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\ VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det\ Nominal \\
VP &\rightarrow V\ NP \\
Det &\rightarrow \text{that } \vert \text{this } \vert \text{a} \\
N &\rightarrow \text{dog } \vert \text{cats} \\
V_{sg} &\rightarrow \text{runs} \\
V_{pl} &\rightarrow \text{run}
\end{aligned}
$$

with **agreement constraints** propagating number features:
- $NP \rightarrow Det\ Nom$ requires $Det.num = Nom.num$
- $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ requires $NP.num = VP.num$

### Verb Subcategorization Frames

Different verbs take different complements. We capture this via subcategorized VP rules:

| Verb | Subcategorization Frame | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| want | $VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP$ | *I want [the book] [on the table]* |
| give | $VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ NP$ | *I gave [her] [the book]* |
| sleep | $VP \rightarrow V$ | *She sleeps* |
| put | $VP \rightarrow V\ NP\ PP$ | *I put [the book] [on the table]* |

This technique also handles **coordination**, **long-distance dependencies**, and **discourse phenomena** in extended CFG frameworks like LFG (Lexical-Functional Grammar) and TAG (Tree-Adjoining Grammar).

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CFG Formalism | $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ | 4-tuple definition |
| Chomsky Normal Form | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A,B,C \in N$, $a \in \Sigma$ |
| PCFG Rule Probability | $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$ | Sum to 1 per non-terminal |
| Tree Probability | $P(T,s) = \prod_{i} P(rule_i)$ | Product of rule probs |
| Sentence Probability | $P(s) = \sum_{T} P(T,s)$ | Sum over all parses |
| CKY Complexity | $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ | Cubic in sentence length |
| Categorial Forward | $X/Y, Y \Rightarrow X$ | Function application right |
| Categorial Backward | $Y, X\backslash Y \Rightarrow X$ | Function application left |
| Treebank Annotation Cost | ~1 word/second (manual) | Penn Treebank rate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy Tip:** Memorize the CNF conversion algorithm in 5 steps: (1) START, (2) TERM, (3) BIN, (4) DEL, (5) UNIT. This is a **frequently tested** 14-mark question.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a Parse Tree from a CFG

**Grammar $G$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP\ VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det\ N \\
NP &\rightarrow John \vert Mary \\
VP &\rightarrow V\ NP \\
V &\rightarrow sees \vert likes \\
Det &\rightarrow the \vert a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sentence:** *"John sees the dog"*

### Derivation (Leftmost):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow NP\ VP \\
  &\Rightarrow John\ VP \\
  &\Rightarrow John\ V\ NP \\
  &\Rightarrow John\ sees\ NP \\
  &\Rightarrow John\ sees\ Det\ N \\
  &\Rightarrow John\ sees\ the\ dog
\end{aligned}
$$

### Parse Tree

The same derivation corresponds to a tree with $S$ at the root, branching into $NP$ (yielding "John") and $VP$ (branching into $V$ "sees" and $NP$ "the dog").

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Converting a CFG to Chomsky Normal Form

**Original Grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow a\ B\ c\ D \\
A &\rightarrow B \vert a \\
B &\rightarrow A\ b \\
D &\rightarrow d
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — START: Ensure start symbol doesn't appear on RHS

Already satisfied ($S$ not on RHS).

### Step 2 — TERM: Replace terminals in long rules with new non-terminals

Rule $S \rightarrow a\ B\ c\ D$ has terminals mixed with non-terminals. Introduce:
- $T_a \rightarrow a$
- $T_c \rightarrow c$

Rewrite:
$$S \rightarrow T_a\ B\ T_c\ D$$

### Step 3 — BIN: Convert rules with >2 non-terminals into binary rules

$S \rightarrow T_a\ B\ T_c\ D$ has 4 non-terminals. Introduce $X \rightarrow T_c\ D$, then:

$$S \rightarrow T_a\ B\ X$$

### Step 4 — DEL: Remove ε-productions

No ε-productions exist, so skip.

### Step 5 — UNIT: Remove unit productions

$A \rightarrow B$ is a unit production. Since $B \rightarrow A\ b$, substitute:

$$A \rightarrow A\ b$$

But this creates a unit cycle $A \rightarrow A$. Substituting $A \rightarrow B$ yields $A \rightarrow A\ b$, which is still a unit cycle. Substitute $B$'s other expansions ($B \rightarrow A\ b$) directly into $A \rightarrow B$:

$$A \rightarrow A\ b$$

This is **not a unit production** (RHS has terminal). Final CNF grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow T_a\ X \\
X &\rightarrow B\ X_1 \\
X_1 &\rightarrow T_c\ D \\
A &\rightarrow A\ b \\
B &\rightarrow A\ b \\
T_a &\rightarrow a \\
T_c &\rightarrow c \\
D &\rightarrow d
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Computing a PCFG Sentence Probability

**Rules with probabilities:**

| Rule | Probability |
| :--- | :--- |
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ | 0.4 |
| $NP \rightarrow John$ | 0.3 |
| $NP \rightarrow Mary$ | 0.3 |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | 0.7 |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | 0.3 |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | 0.6 |
| $Det \rightarrow a$ | 0.4 |
| $V \rightarrow sees$ | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow dog$ | 0.5 |
| $N \rightarrow cat$ | 0.5 |

**Sentence:** *"John sees the dog"*

**Unique Parse Tree:**
- $S \rightarrow NP(John) \rightarrow VP \rightarrow V(sees) \rightarrow NP \rightarrow Det(the) \rightarrow N(dog)$

**Tree Probability Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \times P(NP \rightarrow John) \\
     &\quad \times P(VP \rightarrow V\ NP) \times P(V \rightarrow sees) \\
     &\quad \times P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \times P(Det \rightarrow the) \times P(N \rightarrow dog) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.3 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.3 = 0.3 \\
&= 0.3 \times 0.7 = 0.21 \\
&= 0.21 \times 1.0 = 0.21 \\
&= 0.21 \times 0.4 = 0.084 \\
&= 0.084 \times 0.6 = 0.0504 \\
&= 0.0504 \times 0.5 = 0.0252
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P(T) = 0.0252}$$

Since only one parse tree exists for this sentence, $P(\text{John sees the dog}) = 0.0252$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Categorial Grammar Categorization

**Sentence:** *"Mary likes the cat"*

Assign categories:
- $Mary : NP$
- $likes : (S \backslash NP) / NP$ (needs NP on right to become a VP, then NP on left)
- $the : NP / N$
- $cat : N$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & the, cat \rightarrow NP \\
                     & \text{by } \dfrac{NP/N, \quad N}{NP} \text{ (Forward Application)} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & likes, NP \rightarrow (S \backslash NP) \\
                      & \text{by } \dfrac{(S \backslash NP)/NP, \quad NP}{S \backslash NP} \text{ (Forward Application)} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & Mary, S\backslash NP \rightarrow S \\
                      & \text{by } \dfrac{NP, \quad S\backslash NP}{S} \text{ (Backward Application)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $S$ ✓ — the string is a valid sentence.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Dependency Parse

**Sentence:** *"The big brown dog chased the small cat quickly"*

**Dependency Relations:**

| Dependent | Head | Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| dog | chased | nsubj (nominal subject) |
| chased | chased | root |
| cat | chased | dobj (direct object) |
| The | dog | det (determiner) |
| big | dog | amod (adjectival modifier) |
| brown | dog | amod |
| the | cat | det |
| small | cat | amod |
| quickly | chased | advmod (adverbial modifier) |

The root is "chased"; every other word points to its syntactic head, forming a tree with **labeled directed edges**.

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Context-Free Grammar. List its components with a brief explanation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical model used to describe the syntax of natural languages. It consists of a 4-tuple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$$

- **$N$** — Finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (syntactic categories like $NP$, $VP$, $S$)
- **$\Sigma$** — Finite set of **terminal symbols** (actual words/vocabulary)
- **$R$** — Finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$
- **$S$** — The **start symbol**, a distinguished non-terminal from which all derivations begin

CFG is called "context-free" because the rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ applies whenever $A$ occurs, regardless of surrounding context.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 components with explanation)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is a Treebank? Why is it important in statistical NLP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Treebank** is a syntactically annotated corpus in which every sentence is paired with a manually verified parse tree (usually a constituency tree following the Penn Treebank convention).

**Importance in Statistical NLP:**

1. **Empirical Grammar Learning** — PCFGs can be directly induced from treebanks by counting rule frequencies: $P(A \rightarrow B\ C) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow B\ C)}{\text{Count}(A)}$.

2. **Parser Evaluation** — Treebanks provide gold-standard parses against which automatic parsers are benchmarked using metrics like PARSEVAL (Labelled F-score).

3. **Coverage** — Treebanks capture real linguistic phenomena (reduced relatives, gapping, etc.) that hand-crafted grammars miss.

4. **Cross-domain Adaptation** — Treebanks can be domain-specific (biomedical, financial) enabling domain-tuned parsers.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for importance)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define Chomsky Normal Form (CNF). Explain any **four steps** in the CNF conversion algorithm with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG). Given the grammar below, compute the probability of the sentence *"the dog runs"*. **(7 marks)**

**Grammar Rules:**

| Rule | Probability |
| :--- | :--- |
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ | 0.6 |
| $NP \rightarrow Pronoun$ | 0.4 |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | 0.4 |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | 0.6 |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow dog$ | 0.5 |
| $N \rightarrow cat$ | 0.5 |
| $V \rightarrow runs$ | 0.7 |
| $V \rightarrow sees$ | 0.3 |

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production rule is of one of two forms:
- $A \rightarrow B\ C$ (a non-terminal producing two non-terminals)
- $A \rightarrow a$ (a non-terminal producing a single terminal)

where $A, B, C \in N$ and $a \in \Sigma$.

**Four CNF Conversion Steps (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — START:** Ensure the start symbol $S$ does not appear on the right-hand side of any rule. If it does, introduce a new start symbol $S_0$ with rule $S_0 \rightarrow S$.

**Step 2 — TERM:** For each rule with mixed terminals and non-terminals (e.g., $A \rightarrow b\ C\ d$), replace each terminal $b$ with a new non-terminal $T_b$ and add rule $T_b \rightarrow b$.

*Example:* $S \rightarrow a\ B\ c\ D$ becomes $S \rightarrow T_a\ B\ T_c\ D$, plus $T_a \rightarrow a$ and $T_c \rightarrow c$.

**Step 3 — BIN:** Convert any rule with more than 2 non-terminals on the RHS into a sequence of binary rules using auxiliary non-terminals.

*Example:* $S \rightarrow T_a\ B\ T_c\ D$ becomes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow T_a\ X \\
X &\rightarrow B\ Y \\
Y &\rightarrow T_c\ D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — UNIT:** Eliminate unit productions $A \rightarrow B$ by substituting all non-unit productions of $B$ into $A$.

*Example:* If $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow C\ d$, then substitute to get $A \rightarrow C\ d$.

(Additional steps covered include DEL for ε-productions.)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**PCFG Definition (2 marks):** A PCFG is a CFG augmented with probabilities on each rule, where for each non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

**Identifying Parse Trees for "the dog runs" (2 marks):**

There are exactly **2 valid parse trees** because of the ambiguous $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ and $VP \rightarrow V$ versus $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ rules.

**Parse Tree 1** ($S \rightarrow NP(Det+N)\ VP(V)$):
- Tree: $S \rightarrow NP(\text{the}\ \text{dog})\ VP(\text{runs})$
- Rules used: $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$, $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$, $VP \rightarrow V$, $Det \rightarrow the$, $N \rightarrow dog$, $V \rightarrow runs$

**Parse Tree 2** ($S \rightarrow NP(Det+N)\ VP(V+NP)$):
- Tree: $S \rightarrow NP(\text{the}\ \text{dog})\ VP(\text{runs}\ NP(?))$ — fails because no NP follows "runs" to yield "the dog runs" cleanly. So this parse is **invalid** for our sentence.

Actually, only **one parse** works:
- $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$
- $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ (the dog)
- $VP \rightarrow V$ (runs)

**Probability Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_1) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \times P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \times P(VP \rightarrow V) \\
       &\quad \times P(Det \rightarrow the) \times P(N \rightarrow dog) \times P(V \rightarrow runs) \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \\
&= 0.084
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{the dog runs}) = 0.084}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Dependency Grammar**. With an example sentence, show how dependency relations are identified between words. List any **five universal dependency relations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Categorial Grammar**. Using forward and backward application rules, derive the syntactic category of the sentence *"She gave him the book"* with the following lexical categories:
- $She \Rightarrow NP$
- $him \Rightarrow NP$
- $the \Rightarrow NP/N$
- $book \Rightarrow N$
- $gave \Rightarrow ((S\backslash NP)/NP)/NP$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Dependency Grammar Concept (2 marks):**

**Dependency Grammar** is a syntactic formalism that represents sentence structure as a set of **binary, asymmetric, labeled relations** (dependencies) between words. Each word (except the root) is dependent on exactly one syntactic head. Dependencies form a tree (or a non-projective graph in some frameworks).

Unlike CFG which uses constituency, dependency grammar directly captures **head-dependent** relationships and is more parsimonious for languages with rich morphology and free word order (e.g., Czech, Turkish).

**Example Dependency Analysis (3 marks):**

**Sentence:** *"The teacher gave the students homework yesterday"*

| Word | Head | Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| teacher | gave | nsubj (nominal subject) |
| gave | gave | root |
| students | gave | iobj (indirect object) |
| homework | gave | dobj (direct object) |
| The | teacher | det |
| the | students | det |
| yesterday | gave | advmod (temporal modifier) |

The root is "gave"; all other words point to their heads. Visualizing this yields a directed acyclic graph with 7 edges.

**Five Universal Dependency Relations (2 marks):**

1. **nsubj** — nominal subject (*She* in "She runs")
2. **dobj** — direct object (*book* in "read the book")
3. **amod** — adjectival modifier (*big* in "big dog")
4. **det** — determiner (*the* in "the dog")
5. **advmod** — adverbial modifier (*quickly* in "ran quickly")

Other key relations: iobj, cop, case, mark, conj.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Categorial Grammar Concept (2 marks):**

**Categorial Grammar** (Ajdukiewicz, Bar-Hillel, Lambek) is a lexicalized grammar where the syntactic category of each word fully specifies its combinatorial behavior. Categories are built using:
- **Basic categories:** $S$, $NP$, $N$, $P$, etc.
- **Forward slash ($/$):** $X/Y$ requires a $Y$ to its right
- **Backward slash ($\backslash$):** $X\backslash Y$ requires a $Y$ to its left

**Inference Rules:**
- **Forward Application (FA):** $X/Y, Y \Rightarrow X$
- **Backward Application (BA):** $Y, X\backslash Y \Rightarrow X$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Lexicon: $She : NP$, $him : NP$, $the : NP/N$, $book : N$, $gave : ((S\backslash NP)/NP)/NP$

| Step | Item 1 | Category 1 | Item 2 | Category 2 | Result | Rule |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | the | $NP/N$ | book | $N$ | $NP$ | FA |
| 2 | gave | $((S\backslash NP)/NP)/NP$ | him | $NP$ | $(S\backslash NP)/NP$ | FA |
| 3 | gave' | $(S\backslash NP)/NP$ | NP(book) | $NP$ | $S\backslash NP$ | FA |
| 4 | gave'' | $S\backslash NP$ | She | $NP$ | $S$ | BA |

**Detailed Step Walk:**

**Step 1:** $the : NP/N$ combined with $book : N$ via FA yields $NP$ ("the book").

**Step 2:** $gave : ((S\backslash NP)/NP)/NP$ combined with $him : NP$ via FA yields $(S\backslash NP)/NP$ (since giving "him" still leaves an $S\backslash NP$ needing an NP on the left, but the $NP$ is already satisfied on the right side now... wait, let me recheck). The first slash consumes $NP$ → $(S\backslash NP)/NP$.

**Step 3:** $((S\backslash NP)/NP)/NP$ → consumes the inner $NP$ → $(S\backslash NP)/NP$. Then this combines with the NP from Step 1 ($NP$) via FA to yield $S\backslash NP$.

**Step 4:** $S\backslash NP$ combined with $She : NP$ via BA yields $S$.

**Final Result:** The string *"She gave him the book"* successfully derives to $S$ — a grammatical sentence. ✓

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **CNF Conversion Missing UNIT step:** Many students stop after BIN. Always complete all 5 steps. Marks lost: 2-3.
>
> 2. **Confusing weak vs strong equivalence:** Weak equivalence = same strings; strong equivalence = same parse trees. Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 3. **PCFG probability calculation errors:** Forgetting to multiply ALL rule probabilities in the tree (not just key ones). Always verify rule probability sums to 1.
>
> 4. **Categorial Grammar slash direction:** Using `/` when $\backslash$ is needed (or vice versa). Remember: $/$ = consumes on the **right**, $\backslash$ = consumes on the **left**.
>
> 5. **Dependency vs Constituency confusion:** Constituency shows phrase structure (NP, VP); Dependency shows word-to-word head relations. Don't draw constituency trees for dependency questions.
>
> 6. **AGR features not propagated:** When writing augmented rules, students forget to specify which features must agree across the rule.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **CFG Formalism:** $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S)$ — memorize the 4-tuple and meanings.
- **CNF Forms:** Only $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ are allowed. CKY uses CNF.
- **Treebank Definition:** Annotated corpus with parse trees; Penn Treebank is the canonical example.
- **PCFG Probability:** $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ per non-terminal; tree probability is the product of rule probabilities.
- **HPSG:** Lexicalist + Unification + Typed Feature Structures; replaces CFG rules with ID schemas.
- **Categorial Grammar:** Lexicalized; uses $/$ and $\backslash$ operators; FA and BA inference rules.
- **Dependency Grammar:** Binary asymmetric labeled relations; root has no head; forms a tree.
- **AGR (Augmented Grammar Rules):** Add features (number, person, case) to handle agreement and subcategorization.
- **Grammar Equivalence:** Weak = same language; Strong = same parse trees.
- **CKY Complexity:** $O(n^3)$ for CNF grammars, where $n$ = sentence length.

### 🎯 High-Yield Points for KTU 2024

1. **CFG definition and example rule writing** — tested in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.
2. **CNF conversion algorithm (5 steps)** — classic 14-mark derivation question.
3. **PCFG probability computation** — frequently combined with CKY.
4. **Treebank-based grammar induction** — concept tested, not calculation.
5. **Grammar formalism comparison** — CFG vs Dependency vs HPSG vs CCG.
6. **Categorial Grammar derivations** — apply FA/BA to specific sentences.
7. **AGR for agreement & subcategorization** — explain how features solve CFG limitations.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Practice writing 8-10 CFG rules and converting 2-3 grammars to CNF before the exam. The CNF conversion is the single most testable topic in Module 2. Pair it with PCFG probability questions for full marks on 14-mark sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parsing Methods and Efficient Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parsing Methods and Efficient Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧩 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Parsing?

**Parsing** is the process of analyzing a sequence of tokens (words) against a **formal grammar** to determine its **syntactic structure**, typically represented as a **parse tree** (constituency tree) or **dependency graph**.

Formally, given a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ and an input string $w \in \Sigma^*$, a parser either:
1. **Accepts** $w$ by producing at least one parse tree, or
2. **Rejects** $w$ if no derivation $S \Rightarrow^* w$ exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Parsing is the automatic verification of a string with respect to a grammar, and the construction of a structural representation (parse tree) capturing the syntactic relationships among its constituents.*

## 1.2 Classification of Parsing Methods

Parsing methods are broadly classified along two axes:

| Dimension | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Search Direction** | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
| **Lookahead** | Deterministic (LL/LR) | Non-Deterministic (Earley/CYK) |
| **Backtracking** | Backtracking-based | Backtracking-free |

### A. Top-Down Parsing
Builds the parse tree from the **root (Start symbol $S$)** downward to the leaves (terminals).
- **Strategies:** Recursive Descent, Predictive Parsing, **LL($k$)** Parsing.

### B. Bottom-Up Parsing
Builds the parse tree from the **leaves (input symbols)** upward to the root.
- **Strategies:** Shift-Reduce, **LR($k$)** family — LR(0), SLR(1), CLR(1)/Canonical LR, LALR(1).

## 1.3 What is "Efficient Parsing"?

A parser is called **efficient** if it satisfies these three properties:
1. **Polynomial worst-case time complexity** — ideally $O(n^3)$ or better.
2. **Polynomial space** — typically $O(n^2)$ for chart-based methods.
3. **Handles ambiguity** — produces *all* valid parse trees, not just one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Efficient Parsers:** Earley Parser ($O(n^3)$ worst-case, $O(n^2)$ for unambiguous grammars) and the **Cocke-Younger-Kasami (CYK)** algorithm (strict $O(n^3)$ but requires Chomsky Normal Form).

## 1.4 🧠 Intuitive Analogy

> **The Jigsaw Puzzle Analogy:**
>
> 🎨 **Top-Down Parsing** = You are given the **picture on the box** (the start symbol $S$) and you try to find which pieces (terminals) fit where. You start with a complete mental model and try to validate it against the actual pieces.
>
> 🧱 **Bottom-Up Parsing** = You are given a **pile of scattered pieces** (the input tokens). You pick up adjacent pieces that *might* form a recognizable sub-pattern (a non-terminal) and try to assemble them into the picture shown on the box.
>
> ⚡ **Efficient Parsing (Earley/CYK)** = A *smart assistant* who can hold multiple partial assemblies in memory simultaneously (the **chart**), recognizing that a piece might belong in several different positions, and systematically exploring all possibilities without redundant work.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Grammar Preliminaries

A **Context-Free Grammar** is the tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where:
- $V$ = set of **non-terminals** (variables, e.g., NP, VP)
- $\Sigma$ = set of **terminals** (words, e.g., *the*, *dog*)
- $R \subseteq V \times (V \cup \Sigma)^*$ = set of **production rules**
- $S \in V$ = designated **start symbol**

A string $w$ is in the language $L(G)$ if and only if $S \Rightarrow^* w$ (i.e., $S$ derives $w$ in zero or more steps).

## 2.2 First and Follow Sets — The Heart of Predictive Parsing

### 🔹 FIRST($\alpha$) Set
The set of terminals that can begin any string derivable from $\alpha$:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\} \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \}$$

**Recursive rules:**

$$\text{FIRST}(X) = \begin{cases} \{X\} & \text{if } X \in \Sigma \\ \text{FIRST}(Y_1) \cup \text{FIRST}(Y_2 \ldots Y_k) & \text{if } X \to Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k \text{ and } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1) \end{cases}$$

### 🔹 FOLLOW($A$) Set
The set of terminals that can immediately follow non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \in \Sigma \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \}$$

**Recursive rules:**
- $\$\in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ (where $\$$ is the end-of-input marker)
- If $A \to \alpha B \beta$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$
- If $A \to \alpha B$ or $A \to \alpha B \beta$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$

### 🔹 LL(1) Parsing Table Construction
For every production $A \to \alpha$ in the grammar, fill the table $M[A, a]$:

$$M[A, a] = \begin{cases} A \to \alpha & \text{if } a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \\ A \to \alpha & \text{if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \text{ and } a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A) \\ \text{error} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

A grammar is **LL(1)** iff every cell in $M$ contains **at most one production**.

## 2.3 Grammar Transformations

### 2.3.1 Left Recursion Elimination
Direct left recursion $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$ is replaced by:

$$A \to \beta A' \quad ; \quad A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$$

### 2.3.2 Left Factoring
For $A \to \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$:

$$A \to \alpha A' \quad ; \quad A' \to \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$

## 2.4 LR Parsing Family — The Workhorse of Compilers & NLP

| Parser | Lookahead | States | Power | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LR(0)** | None | LR(0) items | Smallest | Theory only |
| **SLR(1)** | Follow sets | LR(0) items + Follow | Simple | Small grammars |
| **CLR(1)** / LR(1) | 1 token explicit | LR(1) items | Most powerful | Large grammars |
| **LALR(1)** | 1 token merged | Same as SLR but refined | Industry standard | YACC, Bison |

The **LR(0) Item** is a production with a "dot" marker:
$$[A \to \alpha \cdot \beta]$$
indicating that $\alpha$ has been seen and $\beta$ is expected.

## 2.5 Efficient Parsing Algorithms

### 2.5.1 Earley Parser — The Champion for Ambiguous Grammars

Earley parsing maintains a **chart** of **Earley states** (also called *Earley items*):

$$\text{State} = (X \to \alpha \cdot \beta,\ i,\ j)$$

This means: starting at position $i$, we have parsed $\alpha$ and expect to see $\beta$ ending at position $j$.

**Three Operations:**
1. **Prediction:** For each state $(X \to \alpha \cdot Y \beta, i, j)$ with $Y$ a non-terminal, for each production $Y \to \gamma$, add state $(Y \to \cdot \gamma, j, j)$.
2. **Scanning:** If next input token is $a$ and state expects $a$, advance the dot.
3. **Completion:** When state $(X \to \gamma \cdot, j, k)$ is added, for every state $(Y \to \alpha \cdot X \beta, i, j)$, add $(Y \to \alpha X \cdot \beta, i, k)$.

### 2.5.2 CYK Algorithm — Pure $O(n^3)$ via Dynamic Programming

**Prerequisite:** Grammar must be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** — all rules of the form $A \to BC$ or $A \to a$.

**Recognition Table $P[i][j]$:** the set of non-terminals that can derive the substring $w_i w_{i+1} \ldots w_{j-1}$ (length $j-i$).

$$P[i][i+1] = \{ A \mid A \to w_i \in R \}$$

$$P[i][j] = \bigcup_{i \le k < j} \{ A \mid A \to BC \text{ and } B \in P[i][k] \text{ and } C \in P[k][j] \}$$

The input $w$ is in $L(G)$ iff $S \in P[0][n]$.

## 2.6 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following for KTU ESE:**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **LL(1) Condition** | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$ for $A \to \alpha \mid \beta$ | — |
| **Left Recursion** | $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta \Rightarrow A \to \beta A'$, $A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ | — |
| **CYK** | $S \in P[0][n]$ for input of length $n$ | $O(n^3 \cdot \mid R \mid)$ |
| **Earley** | $O(n^3)$ worst, $O(n^2)$ unambiguous | $O(n^2)$ space |
| **LR($k$) Item** | $[A \to \alpha \cdot \beta, a_1 a_2 \ldots a_k]$ | $O(n)$ per token |
| **CYK Table Size** | $n \times n$ matrix | $O(n^2)$ space |
| **Ambig. Count** | $\vert P[0][n] \vert$ = number of distinct parse trees for $S$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Traces & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing an LL(1) Parsing Table

### Grammar (arithmetic expressions):
$$E \to T\, E' \quad E' \to +\, E \mid \varepsilon \quad T \to F\, T' \quad T' \to *\, T \mid \varepsilon \quad F \to (\, E\, ) \mid \text{id}$$

> This is the standard CFG for arithmetic expressions **after** removing left recursion and left-factoring.

### Step A: Compute FIRST Sets

We compute iteratively until convergence.

$$\text{FIRST}(F) = \{ (\,,\ \text{id} \}$$

Since $T \to F\, T'$, all of FIRST($F$) becomes part of FIRST($T$):

$$\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{ (\,,\ \text{id} \}$$

Since $E \to T\, E'$, similarly:

$$\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{ (\,,\ \text{id} \}$$

For $E' \to +\, E$ or $\varepsilon$:

$$\text{FIRST}(E') = \{ +\,,\ \varepsilon \}$$

For $T' \to *\, T$ or $\varepsilon$:

$$\text{FIRST}(T') = \{ *\,,\ \varepsilon \}$$

### Step B: Compute FOLLOW Sets

**Initialize:** FOLLOW($E$) $= \{ \$\}$ (start symbol).

**For $E$:** $E$ is the start symbol, and $E$ appears inside $F \to (\, E\, )$. So FOLLOW($E$) gets $\text{FIRST}(\, )\,) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{ ) \}$. Thus:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{ \$\,,\ ) \}$$

**For $E'$:** $E'$ appears at the end of $E \to T\, E'$. Anything in FOLLOW($E$) propagates:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{ \$\,,\ ) \}$$

**For $T$:** $T$ appears in $E \to T\, E'$. So FIRST($E'$) $\setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$ is added. Also, since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, FOLLOW($E$) propagates:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{ +\,,\ \$\,,\ ) \}$$

**For $T'$:** $T'$ appears at the end of $T \to F\, T'$. FOLLOW($T$) propagates:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{ +\,,\ \$\,,\ ) \}$$

**For $F$:** $F$ appears in $T \to F\, T'$. So FIRST($T'$) $\setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$ is added. Also, $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, so FOLLOW($T$) propagates:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{ *\,,\ +\,,\ \$\,,\ ) \}$$

### Step C: Build the LL(1) Parsing Table

Using rule: $M[A, a]$ gets $A \to \alpha$ if $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, or if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

| Non-Terminal | **id** | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **\$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E$ | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | |
| $E'$ | | $E' \to +E$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $T$ | $T \to FT'$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | |
| $T'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *T$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $F$ | $F \to \text{id}$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | |

✅ **No multiple entries → Grammar is LL(1)!**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Shift-Reduce Parsing (Bottom-Up Trace)

### Grammar:
$$S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$$

### Input:
$$w = aabb \quad \text{(with end-marker } \$\text{)}$$

### Trace Table

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\$$ | $aabb\$$ | **Shift** $a$ |
| 1 | $\$a$ | $abb\$$ | **Shift** $a$ |
| 2 | $\$aa$ | $bb\$$ | **Shift** $b$ |
| 3 | $\$aab$ | $b\$$ | **Reduce** $aSb \to S$ (using $S \to aSb$ in reverse) |
| 4 | $\$aS$ | $b\$$ | **Shift** $b$ |
| 5 | $\$aSb$ | $\$$ | **Reduce** $aSb \to S$ |
| 6 | $\$S$ | $\$$ | **Accept** ✅ |

### Parse Tree Built
The reduction sequence reveals the tree:

$$S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aabb$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: CYK Algorithm on Chomsky Normal Form

### CFG (already in CNF):
$$S \to AB \mid BC \quad A \to BA \mid a \quad B \to CC \mid b \quad C \to AB \mid a$$

### Input:
$$w = abba \quad (n=4, \text{ indexed } w_0=a,\ w_1=b,\ w_2=b,\ w_3=a)$$

### Step 1: Length-1 Substrings (the base case)
$P[i][i+1]$ = non-terminals deriving $w_i$.

| $i$ | $w_i$ | $P[i][i+1]$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $a$ | $\{A,\ C\}$ |
| 1 | $b$ | $\{B\}$ |
| 2 | $b$ | $\{B\}$ |
| 3 | $a$ | $\{A,\ C\}$ |

### Step 2: Length-2 Substrings
$P[i][i+2] = \{X \mid X \to YZ,\ Y \in P[i][k],\ Z \in P[k][i+2]\}$ for $k \in \{i, i+1\}$.

**$P[0][2]$** for substring $w_0 w_1 = ab$:
- $k=0$: $Y \in \{A,C\},\ Z \in \{B\}$. Look for $X \to YB$: none.
- $k=1$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in \{B\}$. Look for $X \to BB$: none.

$$P[0][2] = \emptyset$$

**$P[1][3]$** for substring $w_1 w_2 = bb$:
- $k=1$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in \{B\}$. Look for $X \to BB$: none.

$$P[1][3] = \emptyset$$

**$P[2][4]$** for substring $w_2 w_3 = ba$:
- $k=2$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in \{A,C\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**. Also $X \to BC$: none.
- $k=3$: not applicable.

$$P[2][4] = \{A\}$$

### Step 3: Length-3 Substrings

**$P[0][3]$** for $w_0 w_1 w_2 = abb$:
- $k=0$: $Y \in \{A,C\},\ Z \in P[1][3] = \emptyset$. Nothing.
- $k=1$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in P[2][4] = \{A\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**.
- $k=2$: $Y \in P[2][3] = \{B\}$ (length-1!), $Z \in P[3][4] = \{A,C\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**.

$$P[0][3] = \{A\}$$

**$P[1][4]$** for $w_1 w_2 w_3 = bba$:
- $k=1$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in P[2][4] = \{A\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**.
- $k=2$: $Y \in P[2][3] = \{B\}$, $Z \in P[3][4] = \{A,C\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**.

$$P[1][4] = \{A\}$$

### Step 4: Length-4 Substring (the full string)

**$P[0][4]$** for $abba$:
- $k=0$: $Y \in \{A,C\},\ Z \in P[1][4] = \{A\}$. Look for $X \to AA$ or $X \to CA$: none.
- $k=1$: $Y \in \{B\},\ Z \in P[2][4] = \{A\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**.
- $k=2$: $Y \in P[2][3] = \{B\}$, $Z \in P[3][4] = \{A,C\}$. Look for $X \to BA$: **A**; $X \to BC$: none.

$$P[0][4] = \{A\}$$

### Final Decision
$S \in P[0][4]$? **NO** — $P[0][4] = \{A\}$ does not contain $S$.

$$\boxed{\text{Input } abba \text{ is REJECTED by the grammar.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Earley Parsing on a Simple Grammar

### Grammar:
$$S \to NP\, VP \quad NP \to Det\, N \mid John \quad VP \to V\, NP \quad Det \to \text{the} \quad N \to man \mid telescope \quad V \to saw$$

### Input:
$$\text{John saw the man} \quad (n=4)$$

### Chart States (sketch — full trace is exam-standard)

| Position | Earley States |
|---|---|
| **0** | $(S \to \cdot NP\, VP, 0, 0)$, $(NP \to \cdot Det\, N, 0, 0)$, $(NP \to \cdot John, 0, 0)$, *(Det prediction: $(Det \to \cdot the, 0, 0)$)* |
| **1** | After scanning *John*: $(NP \to John \cdot, 0, 1)$, then by **Completion**: $(S \to NP \cdot VP, 0, 1)$ |
| **2** | After scanning *saw*: $(VP \to V \cdot NP, 1, 2)$, prediction for $NP$ added |
| **3** | After scanning *the*: $(Det \to the \cdot, 2, 3)$, $(NP \to Det \cdot N, 2, 3)$ |
| **4** | After scanning *man*: $(N \to man \cdot, 3, 4)$, $(NP \to Det\, N \cdot, 2, 4)$, $(VP \to V\, NP \cdot, 1, 4)$, $(S \to NP\, VP \cdot, 0, 4)$ ✅ |

**Acceptance:** State $(S \to NP\, VP \cdot, 0, 4)$ in chart[4] ⟹ **Accepted**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Parsing — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Input String W"] --> B0{"Parsing Strategy"}
    B0 -- Top-Down --> C0["Start with S"]
    C0 --> D0["Expand Non-Terminals L-to-R"]
    D0 --> E0["Match Terminals to Input"]
    E0 --> F0{"Match Complete?"}
    F0 -- Yes --> G0["Accept - Build Tree"]
    F0 -- No --> H0["Backtrack / Try Alt"]
    H0 --> D0
    B0 -- Bottom-Up --> I0["Start with Input Tokens"]
    I0 --> J0["Shift Tokens onto Stack"]
    J0 --> K0["Reduce by Grammar Rule"]
    K0 --> L0{"Stack = S ?"}
    L0 -- Yes --> G0
    L0 -- No --> J0
    G0 --> Z0["Parse Tree Output"]
    style A0 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style G0 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style Z0 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

## 4.2 LL(1) Predictive Parser — Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input Buffer: w$"] --> B1["Stack: S on top"]
    B1 --> C1{"Top of Stack = a = Input?"}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1["Pop and Advance Input"]
    D1 --> E1{"Stack Empty?"}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1["ACCEPT"]
    C1 -- No --> G1["X is Non-Terminal?"}
    G1 -- No --> H1["ERROR - Reject"]
    G1 -- Yes --> I1["Lookup M X, a in Table"]
    I1 --> J1{"Entry Exists?"}
    J1 -- No --> H1
    J1 -- Yes --> K1["Pop X, Push RHS reverse"]
    K1 --> C1
    style F1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style H1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style I1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
```

## 4.3 Earley Parser — Three-Operation State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Initialize Chart 0 with Start State"] --> S1{"State Type"}
    S1 -- "Expects Non-Terminal Y" --> P1["PREDICTION"]
    P1 --> P2["For each Y-gamma, add Y-.-gamma, j, j"]
    P2 --> S1
    S1 -- "Expects Terminal a, input = a" --> SC1["SCANNING"]
    SC1 --> SC2["Advance dot past a, move to chart j+1"]
    SC2 --> S1
    S1 -- "Dot at end of RHS" --> CO1["COMPLETION"]
    CO1 --> CO2["For each prior state with Y, advance its dot"]
    CO2 --> S1
    S1 -- "End of Chart n" --> END["State S-...-. in chart 0,n ?"]
    END -- Yes --> ACC["ACCEPT - Language Member"]
    END -- No --> REJ["REJECT - Not in Language"]
    style ACC fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style REJ fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style P1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
    style SC1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style CO1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
```

## 4.4 CYK Recognition Table — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I0["Input Grammar in CNF"] --> V0["Validate CNF: A to BC or A to a"]
    V0 --> T0["Initialize P i, i+1 for terminals"]
    T0 --> L2["For length = 2 to n"]
    L2 --> L3["For i = 0 to n-length"]
    L3 --> L4["For k = i to j-1"]
    L4 --> L5["Check pairs in P i,k and P k,j"]
    L5 --> L6{"Any rule X to YZ matches?"}
    L6 -- Yes --> L7["Add X to P i,j"]
    L6 -- No --> L4
    L7 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> FIN["Check S in P 0,n"]
    FIN -- Yes --> ACC2["STRING ACCEPTED - n cubed time"]
    FIN -- No --> REJ2["STRING REJECTED"]
    style ACC2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style REJ2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style T0 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
    style L7 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40
```

## 4.5 Shift-Reduce Parser — State Transition Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Load Input
    Idle --> Shift: Read next token
    Shift --> Reduce: Top of stack matches RHS
    Reduce --> Shift: Reduced form on stack
    Reduce --> Accept: Stack contains only S
    Reduce --> Conflict: Multiple actions possible
    Shift --> Conflict: Shift or Reduce possible
    Conflict --> Idle: SLR/LR1 resolves via lookahead
    Accept --> [*]: Parse Tree Complete
    note right of Conflict: Resolved by LR1 parsing tables
```
