# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define parsing. Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up parsing strategies with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Parsing** is the syntactic analysis phase of NLP that takes a sequence of tokens as input and produces a parse tree conforming to a given formal grammar.

| Aspect | Top-Down Parsing | Bottom-Up Parsing |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Root → Leaves | Leaves → Root |
| **Start** | Begins with $S$ | Begins with input tokens |
| **Operation** | Derivation / Expansion | Reduction |
| **Search** | Leftmost derivation | Rightmost derivation (reverse) |
| **Tree building** | Pre-order | Post-order |
| **Example algorithm** | Recursive Descent, LL(1) | Shift-Reduce, LR(1) |
| **Grammar restriction** | No left recursion | None for LR(1) |
| **Failure detection** | Early (top of tree) | Late (full stack checked) |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for table)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is left recursion? Explain with an example how direct left recursion is eliminated.**

**Model Answer:**

**Left recursion** occurs when a non-terminal $A$ has a production of the form $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ (i.e., $A$ derives a sentential form starting with itself). It causes infinite loops in top-down parsers.

**Direct left recursion** is of the form $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$ where $\alpha, \beta$ are arbitrary strings and $\beta$ does not start with $A$.

**Elimination Algorithm** (transform to right-recursion):

Original: $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$

Becomes:
$$A \to \beta A'$$
$$A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Example:** $E \to E + T \mid T$ becomes:
$$E \to T E'$$
$$E' \to + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$

This permits a top-down parser to make progress without infinite recursion.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for algorithm with example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider the grammar:**
$$S \to aS \mid Sb \mid ab$$

#### **(a)** Eliminate left recursion and left-factor the grammar. Construct the LL(1) parsing table. (7 marks)

#### **(b)** Show the step-by-step parsing of the input string `aabb` using the LL(1) parser. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion**
The rule $S \to aS$ is left-recursive since $S$ derives $S$ as the leftmost symbol in its RHS. Grouping left-recursive rules:
- $S \to Sb \mid ab$ (right-recursive; $S$ does not start with $S$)
- $S \to aS$ (left-recursive)

Apply the standard transformation $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta \Rightarrow A \to \beta A', A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$:
- $A = S$, $\alpha = b$, $\beta = a \mid ab$

Result:
$$S \to aS \mid ab$$
$$S \to bS' \mid abS'$$
$$S' \to bS' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 2: Left Factoring**
Group $S \to aS \mid abS'$ — both start with $a$:

$$S \to a\,X$$
$$X \to S \mid bS'$$

**Final grammar:**
$$S \to aX \quad X \to S \mid bS' \quad S' \to bS' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 3: Compute FIRST and FOLLOW**

$$\text{FIRST}(S') = \{b, \varepsilon\}$$
$$\text{FIRST}(X) = \{\text{FIRST}(S) \cup \text{FIRST}(bS')\} = \{a, b\}$$
$$\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a\}$$

$$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{b, \$\} \cup \text{FIRST}(X) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \cup \text{FOLLOW}(X) = \{a, b, \$\}$$
$$\text{FOLLOW}(X) = \{\$\}$$
$$\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{a, b, \$\}$$

**Step 4: Build LL(1) Table**

| | **a** | **b** | **\$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $S \to aX$ | | |
| $X$ | $X \to S$ | $X \to bS'$ | |
| $S'$ | $S' \to \varepsilon$ | $S' \to bS'$ | $S' \to \varepsilon$ |

✅ **Single entry per cell → Grammar is LL(1).**

**(7 marks: 1 transformation, 2 sets, 2 table, 2 verification)**

---

#### **Solution (b):** LL(1) Parsing Trace for `aabb$`

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $S$ | $aabb\$$ | Look up $M[S, a]$: $S \to aX$. Push $Xa$. |
| 1 | $Xa$ | $aabb\$$ | Pop $a$, match $a$, advance. |
| 2 | $X$ | $abb\$$ | Look up $M[X, a]$: $X \to S$. Push $S$. |
| 3 | $S$ | $abb\$$ | Look up $M[S, a]$: $S \to aX$. Push $Xa$. |
| 4 | $Xa$ | $abb\$$ | Pop $a$, match. |
| 5 | $X$ | $bb\$$ | Look up $M[X, b]$: $X \to bS'$. Push $S'b$. |
| 6 | $S'b$ | $bb\$$ | Pop $b$, match. |
| 7 | $S'$ | $b\$$ | Look up $M[S', b]$: $S' \to bS'$. Push $S'b$. |
| 8 | $S'b$ | $b\$$ | Pop $b$, match. |
| 9 | $S'$ | $\$$ | Look up $M[S', \$]$: $S' \to \varepsilon$. Pop $S'$. |
| 10 | (empty) | $\$$ | **ACCEPT** ✅ |

**(7 marks: full 11-step trace worth 7 marks; partial credit for each step)**

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm** for parsing. Apply it to determine whether the string $w = \text{"baab"}$ is in the language of the following CNF grammar: (7 marks)

$$S \to AB \mid BC \quad A \to BA \mid a \quad B \to CC \mid b \quad C \to AB \mid a$$

**(b)** What is **Earley parsing**? Explain the three core operations — **Prediction**, **Scanning**, and **Completion** — with a small example. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution (a):**

**CYK Algorithm Overview:**
- Requires grammar in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** — only rules of form $A \to BC$ or $A \to a$.
- Uses a 2D recognition table $P[i][j]$ (substring from index $i$ to $j$).
- Dynamic programming: shorter substrings computed first.
- $w \in L(G)$ iff $S \in P[0][n]$.
- Time: $O(n^3)$, Space: $O(n^2)$.

**Application to $w = baab$** (length 4, indexed $w_0=b, w_1=a, w_2=a, w_3=b$).

**Length 1:**

| $i$ | $w_i$ | $P[i][i+1]$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $b$ | $\{B\}$ |
| 1 | $a$ | $\{A, C\}$ |
| 2 | $a$ | $\{A, C\}$ |
| 3 | $b$ | $\{B\}$ |

**Length 2:**

$P[0][2]$ for $ba$:
- $k=0$: $B \in P[0][1]$, $A,C \in P[1][2]$. Rules: $A \to BA$ ✓, $C \to BA$? No. So $\{A\}$.
- $k=1$: $A,C \in P[1][2]$, $B \in P[2][3]$. Rules: $A \to AB$? No, we have $A \to BA$ only. $B \to CB$? No, $B \to CC$. So no additions.

$$P[0][2] = \{A\}$$

$P[1][3]$ for $aa$:
- $k=1$: $A,C \in P[1][2]$, $A,C \in P[2][3]$. Rules: $A \to AA$? No. $A \to CA$? No. $B \to CA$? No. $B \to AC$? No. $C \to AA$? No. $C \to CA$? No. So $\emptyset$.

Hmm, let me also check $k=1$ with $A,C \to A,C$:
- $A \to CA$? Not in grammar.
- $B \to CA$? No, $B \to CC$.
- $B \to AC$? No.
- $C \to CA$? No, $C \to AB$.

$$P[1][3] = \emptyset$$

$P[2][4]$ for $ab$:
- $k=2$: $A,C \in P[2][3]$, $B \in P[3][4]$. Rules: $A \to AB$? No ($A \to BA$ only). $B \to AB$? No. $C \to AB$ ✓. So $\{C\}$.

$$P[2][4] = \{C\}$$

**Length 3:**

$P[0][3]$ for $baa$:
- $k=0$: $B \in P[0][1]$, $A,C \in P[1][3] = \emptyset$. Nothing.
- $k=1$: $A,C \in P[1][2]$, $A,C \in P[2][3]$. Rules: $A \to CA$? No. $A \to AA$? No. $B \to CA$? No. $B \to AC$? No. $C \to CA$? No. $C \to AA$? No. $\emptyset$.
- $k=2$: $A,C \in P[2][3]$, $B \in P[3][4]$. Rules: $A \to AB$? No. $B \to CB$? No. $C \to AB$ ✓. So $\{C\}$.

$$P[0][3] = \{C\}$$

$P[1][4]$ for $aab$:
- $k=1$: $A,C \in P[1][2]$, $C \in P[2][4]$. Rules: $A \to CA$? No. $B \to CC$ ✓. $C \to CA$? No. So $\{B\}$.
- $k=2$: $A,C \in P[2][3]$, $B \in P[3][4]$. Rules: $A \to AB$? No. $C \to AB$ ✓. So $\{C\}$.

$$P[1][4] = \{B, C\}$$

**Length 4 (the whole string):**

$P[0][4]$ for $baab$:
- $k=0$: $B \in P[0][1]$, $\{B, C\} \in P[1][4]$. Rules: $A \to BA$? No. $B \to BB$? No. $B \to BC$? No. $C \to BB$? No. $C \to BC$? No. $S \to BC$ ✓! So $\{S\}$.
- $k=1$: $A,C \in P[1][2]$, $C \in P[2][4]$. Rules: $A \to AC$? No. $B \to CC$ ✓. $C \to AC$? No. $S \to AC$? No.
- $k=2$: $A,C \in P[2][3]$, $B \in P[3][4]$. Rules: $A \to AB$? No. $B \to CB$? No. $C \to AB$ ✓.

$$P[0][4] = \{S, B, C\}$$

**Conclusion:** $S \in P[0][4]$ ✅ → **String "baab" is ACCEPTED by the grammar.**

**(7 marks: 2 for algorithm explanation, 5 for CYK table construction)**

---

#### **Solution (b):**

**Earley Parsing** is a chart-parsing algorithm for CFGs that handles **arbitrary** (even ambiguous) grammars in $O(n^3)$ worst-case time, $O(n^2)$ space, and $O(n^2)$ for unambiguous grammars. It maintains a list of **Earley states** indexed by input position.

**An Earley state** is a 4-tuple:
$$(X \to \alpha \cdot \beta,\ i,\ j)$$

meaning: starting at position $i$, the parser has recognized $\alpha$ and expects $\beta$ to span positions $j$ to current.

**Three Core Operations:**

1. **Prediction (Top-Down Expansion):**
   - *Triggered by:* a state $(X \to \alpha \cdot Y\beta, i, j)$ where $Y$ is a non-terminal.
   - *Action:* for every production $Y \to \gamma$, add the state $(Y \to \cdot \gamma, j, j)$ to chart[$j$].
   - *Intuition:* "If we need to find $Y$ here, predict all possible ways $Y$ can begin."

2. **Scanning (Token Consumption):**
   - *Triggered by:* a state $(X \to \alpha \cdot a\beta, i, j)$ where $a$ is a terminal and the next input token equals $a$.
   - *Action:* advance to state $(X \to \alpha a \cdot \beta, i, j+1)$ in chart[$j+1$].
   - *Intuition:* "If we expected $a$ and saw $a$, consume it and move forward."

3. **Completion (Bottom-Up Reduction):**
   - *Triggered by:* a completed state $(X \to \gamma \cdot, j, k)$.
   - *Action:* for every state $(Y \to \alpha \cdot X\beta, i, j)$ in chart[$j$], add $(Y \to \alpha X \cdot \beta, i, k)$ to chart[$k$].
   - *Intuition:* "We finished $X$ here; wherever someone was waiting for an $X$, advance them."

**Mini-Example:**
Grammar: $S \to NP\, VP$, $NP \to Det\, N$, $NP \to John$, $VP \to V$, $Det \to the$, $N \to dog$, $V \to ran$.
Input: `"John ran"` (length 2).

| Chart | States |
|---|---|
| 0 | $(S \to \cdot NP\, VP, 0, 0)$ — start |
| 0 | $(NP \to \cdot Det\, N, 0, 0)$ — **Prediction** for $NP$ |
| 0 | $(NP \to \cdot John, 0, 0)$ — **Prediction** for $NP$ |
| 0 | $(Det \to \cdot the, 0, 0)$ — **Prediction** for $Det$ |
| 1 | $(NP \to John \cdot, 0, 1)$ — **Scanning** *John* |
| 1 | $(S \to NP \cdot VP, 0, 1)$ — **Completion** of $NP$ |
| 1 | $(VP \to \cdot V, 1, 1)$ — **Prediction** for $VP$ |
| 2 | $(VP \to V \cdot, 1, 2)$ — **Scanning** *ran* |
| 2 | $(S \to NP\, VP \cdot, 0, 2)$ — **Completion** ✅ |

The final state $(S \to NP\, VP \cdot, 0, 2)$ indicates **acceptance**.

**(7 marks: 2 Earley overview, 3 for the three operations, 2 for the example)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to remove left recursion** before constructing LL(1) tables → -3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$** — the first applies to a *string* (RHS), the second to a *non-terminal*. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **Not adding $\varepsilon$-productions to the LL(1) table for all $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$**, not just one terminal.
> 4. **CYK: forgetting to convert to CNF first.** Any rule like $A \to BCD$ or $A \to \varepsilon$ will *not* work in CYK. Examiners will deduct 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Earley Parsing: omitting dot positions in states** $(X \to \alpha \cdot \beta, i, j)$ — the dot is mandatory, not optional.
> 6. **In traces: forgetting to reverse the RHS** when pushing onto the stack. The top of the stack should be the leftmost unexpanded symbol.
> 7. **Ambig. grammar in LL(1):** If the LL(1) table has a *multiple-entry* cell, the grammar is **not** LL(1). Students often wrongly declare success.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision!**

- ✅ **Parsing** = syntactic analysis producing a parse tree from tokens under a CFG.
- ✅ **Top-Down** = $S \Rightarrow^* w$ (derivation); **Bottom-Up** = $w \Rightarrow^* S$ (reduction).
- ✅ **Recursive Descent** = top-down with one procedure per non-terminal; backtracks on failure.
- ✅ **Predictive Parser / LL(1)** = recursive descent with a 2D table; no backtracking; uses 1-token lookahead.
- ✅ **FIRST set** of a string = terminals that can *begin* derivations from that string.
- ✅ **FOLLOW set** of a non-terminal = terminals that can *immediately follow* it in any sentential form.
- ✅ **Left recursion** must be eliminated for top-down parsers: $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta \Rightarrow A \to \beta A'$, $A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Left factoring** resolves common prefixes: $A \to \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \Rightarrow A \to \alpha A'$, $A' \to \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$.
- ✅ **LR($k$) family** = powerful bottom-up parsers using shift-reduce with $k$-token lookahead.
- ✅ **LR(0)** < **SLR(1)** < **LALR(1)** < **CLR(1)** in terms of grammar class accepted.
- ✅ **Earley parser** uses three operations: **P**rediction, **S**canning, **C**ompletion. Works for *any* CFG.
- ✅ **CYK** requires **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**; recognition in $O(n^3)$ via the dynamic programming table $P[i][j]$.
- ✅ **Ambiguity**: $w \in L(G)$ has multiple parse trees iff multiple distinct derivations exist; detected in CYK when multiple non-terminals appear in $P[0][n]$ contributing to $S$.
- ✅ **Chomsky Normal Form**: every rule is $A \to BC$ or $A \to a$; used universally in formal-language parsing.
- ✅ **Complexity Hierarchy**: Earley = $O(n^3)$ worst / $O(n^2)$ unambig; CYK = strict $O(n^3)$; Chart Parsing = $O(n^3)$ generic; LL/LR = $O(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ambiguity Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ambiguity in Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is the property of a linguistic expression (word, phrase, sentence, or discourse) being open to more than one distinct interpretation by a human reader or a computational parser. Formally, an expression $E$ is *ambiguous* if there exists a context $C$ such that the model assigns more than one valid semantic or syntactic interpretation to $E$ under $C$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Ambiguity resolution is the computational process of selecting the most plausible interpretation from among multiple competing readings of a linguistic unit, using lexical, syntactic, semantic, discourse, and probabilistic evidence drawn from the surrounding context.

The four principal strata at which ambiguity is analyzed in the KTU 2024 NLP module are:

| Stratum | Question Being Resolved | Linguistic Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Lexical** | Which sense of a word is intended? | Single word |
| **Syntactic** | Which parse tree is correct? | Phrase / Sentence |
| **Semantic** | Which meaning is implied by the constituents? | Sentence |
| **Pragmatic / Discourse** | Which referent is being referred to? | Multi-sentence text |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spoken Sentence Problem"

Imagine you are listening to a friend say over a noisy phone call:

> *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

You automatically pause and decide:
1. Did *you* use a **telescope** to see him? (Instrument reading)
2. Did you see a **man who had** a telescope? (Modifier reading)

A human resolves this in milliseconds using **world knowledge and context**. An NLP system has none of this intuition — it must be explicitly engineered to score each reading and pick the winner. This engineered decision is *Ambiguity Resolution*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Parallel:** Ambiguity resolution is the silent engine behind:
> - **Google Search** — interpreting "Apple stock" (company) vs "apple stock" (fruit soup inventory).
> - **Machine Translation** — choosing the correct translation of "bank" in English-to-Hindi (river bank → तट vs financial bank → बैंक).
> - **Voice Assistants** — Siri/Alexa disambiguating "call mom" (action) vs "call mom funny" (adjective).

## 1.3 Why Ambiguity is a Hard Problem (Quantitative Perspective)

A modest English sentence of length $n$ with average branching factor $b$ (choices per decision point) can in principle generate up to $b^n$ parse trees. For a 20-word sentence with $b \approx 2.5$:

$$N_{\text{trees}} \approx 2.5^{20} \approx 9.09 \times 10^{8}$$

Hence, exhaustive enumeration is computationally infeasible — a core motivation for **probabilistic disambiguation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** Ambiguity is *not* a bug of language; it is an inherent feature. The job of an NLP pipeline is **not** to eliminate ambiguity, but to **resolve** it — i.e., to pick the single interpretation that maximises posterior probability given the evidence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Ambiguity (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.1.1 Lexical Ambiguity
A single word has multiple dictionary senses.

- **Homonymy** — same spelling/sound, *unrelated* meanings.
  *Example:* "**bank**" (river bank) vs "**bank**" (financial institution).
- **Polysemy** — same spelling, *related* meanings.
  *Example:* "**head**" (body part) vs "**head**" (department head).
- **Homophony** — same sound, different spelling.
  *Example:* "**pair**" vs "**pear**" vs "**pare**".
- **Homography** — same spelling, different sound.
  *Example:* "**lead**" (Pb) vs "**lead**" (to guide).

### 2.1.2 Syntactic (Structural) Ambiguity
A sentence has multiple valid parse trees. Two classical subtypes:

- **Attachment ambiguity** — prepositional phrase (PP) or modifier attachment.
  *Example:* "I saw the man **with the telescope**."
  → PP attaches to verb (instrument) or noun (modifier).
- **Coordination ambiguity** — scope of conjunctions.
  *Example:* "**old men and women**" — does *old* apply to *men only* or to both?
- **NP-VP bracketing ambiguity** — scope of noun phrase.
  *Example:* "**French language teacher**" → one teacher of French OR teachers who are French and teach language?

### 2.1.3 Semantic Ambiguity
The sentence is structurally unambiguous but its meaning is unclear.
- *Example:* "Every student read a book." — Does "a book" refer to **one specific** book (existential) or **possibly different** books for each student (universal)?

### 2.1.4 Pragmatic / Anaphoric / Discourse Ambiguity
Pronouns or references have multiple antecedents.
- *Example:* "**John** told **Mark** that **he** had won." — Who is "he"?

### 2.1.5 Phonetic Ambiguity (Speech)
- *Example:* "**recognise speech**" vs "**wreck a nice beach**" — identical acoustic signal.

---

## 2.2 Probabilistic Foundation of Disambiguation

The **fundamental decision rule** for ambiguity resolution in modern NLP is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** rule:

$$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S} \; P(s \mid c)$$

where:
- $s$ = a candidate interpretation (sense, parse tree, tag sequence).
- $c$ = observed context.
- $S$ = set of all competing interpretations.

Applying **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(s \mid c) = \frac{P(c \mid s) \, P(s)}{P(c)}$$

Since $P(c)$ is constant across candidates, we can equivalently maximise the numerator:

$$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S} \; P(c \mid s) \, P(s)$$

This single equation is the **mathematical heart** of every disambiguation algorithm in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 2.3 Algorithmic Strategies for Ambiguity Resolution

### Strategy 1 — Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)

**Lesk Algorithm (1986)** — dictionary-overlap based.
Given a target word $w$ in context $C = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n\}$ and sense $s_k$ with gloss $G(s_k)$:

$$\text{Score}(s_k) = \vert C \cap G(s_k) \vert$$

The sense with maximum overlap wins. Extended variants (Extended Lesk) include example sentences and hypernyms.

**Yarowsky Algorithm (1995)** — bootstrapping decision-list algorithm. Uses the **One Sense Per Discourse** and **One Sense Per Collocation** heuristics.

### Strategy 2 — Supervised Naive Bayes WSD

Treat senses as classes. For a context window of words $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$:

$$P(s_k \mid w_1, \dots, w_n) \propto P(s_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid s_k)$$

### Strategy 3 — Statistical / HMM-based POS Tagging

Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging is itself an ambiguity resolution problem — each word may map to multiple tags.

$$P(t_1, \dots, t_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \, P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$

Solved efficiently with the **Viterbi algorithm** in $O(n \cdot \vert T \vert^2)$.

### Strategy 4 — Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)

Disambiguates structural ambiguity by assigning a probability to each parse tree $T$:

$$P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$$

The most probable parse is:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \; P(T)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s \mid c)$ | Universal disambiguation rule |
| 2 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(s \mid c) = \frac{P(c \mid s) P(s)}{P(c)}$ | Foundation of supervised methods |
| 3 | Naive Bayes WSD | $P(s_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid s_k)$ | Assumes feature independence |
| 4 | Lesk Score | $\text{Score}(s_k) = \vert C \cap G(s_k) \vert$ | Knowledge-based WSD |
| 5 | HMM Tagging | $\prod_i P(w_i \mid t_i) P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Sequence disambiguation |
| 6 | Viterbi Recursion | $V_i(t) = P(w_i \mid t) \max_{t'} [V_{i-1}(t') P(t \mid t')]$ | Efficient HMM decoding |
| 7 | PCFG Tree Probability | $P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$ | Structural disambiguation |
| 8 | CKY Inside Probability | $\alpha[i,j,A] = \sum_{B,C} \alpha[i,k,B]\,\alpha[k+1,j,C]\,P(A \to BC)$ | Bottom-up parsing |
| 9 | Mutual Information (Collocation) | $MI(x,y) = \log \frac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$ | Collocational evidence for WSD |
| 10 | Add-1 Smoothing | $P(w \mid s) = \frac{\text{count}(w,s) + 1}{\text{count}(s) + \vert V \vert}$ | Avoids zero probability |
| 11 | Perplexity | $PP = 2^{H(p)} = P(w_1^n)^{-1/n}$ | Evaluates probabilistic models |
| 12 | Inter-annotator Agreement | $\kappa = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | Quality of sense-tagged corpus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power virtually every production NLP system — Google Translate's PCFG, Apple Siri's HMM acoustic model, BERT-based contextual embeddings — all are descendants of the MAP decision rule applied to increasingly powerful feature spaces.

---

## 2.5 Evaluation Metrics for Disambiguation

- **Accuracy** = $\frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}}$
- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-score** = $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$

For WSD on the SemCor / Senseval benchmarks, state-of-the-art **supervised** systems achieve **65–75%** accuracy, while **knowledge-based** Lesk variants reach **50–60%**, and the **most-frequent-sense baseline** alone reaches **~51%** — making it a strong (and embarrassing) baseline.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MAP Rule from Bayes' Theorem

We want the sense $\hat{s}$ that maximises the posterior probability given context $c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{s} &= \arg\max_{s \in S} \; P(s \mid c) \\
        &= \arg\max_{s \in S} \; \frac{P(c \mid s)\, P(s)}{P(c)} \quad &\text{(by Bayes' Theorem)} \\
        &= \arg\max_{s \in S} \; P(c \mid s)\, P(s) \quad &\text{(since } P(c) \text{ is constant over } s\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The denominator $P(c)$ does not change as we vary $s$, so it cannot affect the argmax. We can drop it for computational convenience — this is called the **maximum likelihood × prior** formulation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Naive Bayes for WSD (End-to-End Worked Example)

**Problem:** Disambiguate the word **"bank"** in the sentence:
> *"I deposited money in the **bank** yesterday."*

**Step 1 — Define Senses.**
- $s_1$ = financial institution
- $s_2$ = river edge

**Step 2 — Training Corpus Frequencies (illustrative).**

| Sense | Count $(s_k)$ | "deposited" | "money" | "river" | "water" |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ (financial) | **1000** | 80 | 150 | 5 | 8 |
| $s_2$ (river) | **600** | 0 | 2 | 90 | 110 |

Total corpus size: $N = 1600$, vocabulary size: $\vert V \vert = 10$ (toy).

**Step 3 — Compute Prior Probabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s_1) &= \frac{1000}{1600} = 0.625 \\
P(s_2) &= \frac{600}{1600} = 0.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Likelihoods with Add-1 Smoothing.**

$$
P(w \mid s_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, s_k) + 1}{\text{count}(s_k) + \vert V \vert}
$$

For word $w =$ "money":

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{money} \mid s_1) &= \frac{150 + 1}{1000 + 10} = \frac{151}{1010} \approx 0.1495 \\
P(\text{money} \mid s_2) &= \frac{2 + 1}{600 + 10} = \frac{3}{610} \approx 0.00492
\end{aligned}
$$

For word $w =$ "deposited":

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{deposited} \mid s_1) &= \frac{80 + 1}{1010} = \frac{81}{1010} \approx 0.0802 \\
P(\text{deposited} \mid s_2) &= \frac{0 + 1}{610} = \frac{1}{610} \approx 0.00164
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Naive Bayes Decision Rule.**

We use the context words $\{$"deposited", "money"$\}$ (a 2-word window around the target):

$$
P(s_k \mid c) \propto P(s_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid s_k)
$$

For $s_1$:

$$
P(s_1) \cdot P(\text{deposited} \mid s_1) \cdot P(\text{money} \mid s_1) = 0.625 \times 0.0802 \times 0.1495 \approx 0.00749
$$

For $s_2$:

$$
P(s_2) \cdot P(\text{deposited} \mid s_2) \cdot P(\text{money} \mid s_2) = 0.375 \times 0.00164 \times 0.00492 \approx 3.03 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Argmax Decision.**

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max\{0.00749, \; 3.03 \times 10^{-6}\} = s_1
$$

**Result:** The system correctly identifies **"bank"** as the **financial institution** sense. The ratio of evidence is approximately **$2473 : 1$** in favour of $s_1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lesk Algorithm (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Disambiguate **"bass"** in:
> *"I heard the bass on the radio."*

**Sense Glosses (from a hypothetical dictionary):**
- $s_1$ (fish): *"a perch-like freshwater fish"*
- $s_2$ (music): *"the lowest adult male singing voice"*

**Step 1 — Tokenise the context.**
$C = \{ \text{I, heard, the, bass, on, the, radio} \}$

**Step 2 — Tokenise the glosses.**
- $G(s_1) = \{ \text{a, perch, like, freshwater, fish} \}$
- $G(s_2) = \{ \text{the, lowest, adult, male, singing, voice} \}$

**Step 3 — Compute Overlap.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(s_1) &= \vert C \cap G(s_1) \vert = 0 \\
\text{Score}(s_2) &= \vert C \cap G(s_2) \vert = 1 \quad (\text{"the" overlaps})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Argmax Decision.** $\hat{s} = s_2$ (music sense).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Plain Lesk is weak because it counts function words ("the", "of"). The **Extended Lesk** algorithm weights content words, includes hypernyms from WordNet, and uses example sentences — boosting accuracy by 10–20 percentage points.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — HMM Viterbi POS Tagger (Sketch)

**Problem:** Tag the sentence *"Time flies like an arrow"* with the most probable tag sequence.

**Given:** Transition probs $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ and emission probs $P(w_i \mid t_i)$.

**Step 1 — Initialization.**

$$
V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot P(w_1 \mid t_j)
$$

**Step 2 — Recursion (for $i = 2 \dots n$).**

$$
V_i(j) = \max_{k} \left[ V_{i-1}(k) \cdot P(t_j \mid t_k) \cdot P(w_i \mid t_j) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Backtrace.** Starting from $\arg\max_j V_n(j)$, recover the optimal tag sequence.

**Result:** A classic HMM tagger correctly produces `NNS VB IN DT NN` for "Time flies like an arrow", successfully resolving the *syntactic ambiguity* of the verb vs. noun reading of "flies".

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Disambiguation Methods

| Method | Knowledge Source | Strength | Weakness | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Most-Frequent-Sense Baseline** | Sense-tagged corpus | Trivial to implement | Ignores context | ~51% |
| **Naive Bayes WSD** | Sense-tagged corpus | Probabilistic, fast | Independence assumption | 60–70% |
| **Decision Lists (Yarowsky)** | Untagged + seed corpus | Bootstrapping from small seed | Heuristic-dependent | 65–75% |
| **Lesk (Original)** | Machine-readable dictionary | No training data needed | Counts function words | 42–55% |
| **Extended Lesk** | WordNet glosses + relations | Rich overlap | Slow on large WordNet | 55–65% |
| **HMM Tagger** | Tagged corpus | Sequence-aware | Limited features | 93–95% (POS) |
| **PCFG** | Treebank | Handles structural ambiguity | Lexical independence | ~75% (WSJ) |
| **BERT / Neural WSD** | Pre-trained LM | Contextualised embeddings | Computationally heavy | 78–82% (SOTA) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Ambiguity Resolution Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders a Block-Level Functional Architecture showing how a typical NLP pipeline ingests raw text and progressively resolves ambiguity at each stratum.

```mermaid

    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Tokeniser Module]
    B --> C[Lexical Layer: WSD Engine]
    C --> C1{Lesk Dictionary}
    C --> C2{Naive Bayes Classifier}
    C --> C3{Yarowsky Decision List}
    C1 --> D[Syntactic Layer: Parser]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Probabilistic CFG]
    D --> D2[HMM POS Tagger]
    D1 --> E[Semantic Layer: Logical Form]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Pragmatic Layer: Coreference Resolver]
    F --> G[Resolved Interpretation Output]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Text flows top-to-bottom. At each coloured layer, a specific ambiguity type is resolved. The Lexical layer (orange) addresses word-sense ambiguity; the Syntactic layer (purple) addresses structural ambiguity; the Semantic and Pragmatic layers (brown and red) handle meaning-level and referential ambiguity respectively.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Naive Bayes WSD Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctxA[Context Words w1 w2 w3 wn] --> fe[Feature Extractor]
    fe --> vec[Context Feature Vector]
    vec --> nb[Naive Bayes Classifier Core]
    priorDB[(Prior P s_k Database)] --> nb
    condDB[(Likelihood P w i s k Database)] --> nb
    nb --> score[Posterior Score per Sense]
    score --> argmax[Argmax Selector]
    argmax --> out[Winning Sense Label]
    style nb fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style argmax fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style out fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Context words are converted into a feature vector. Two lookup tables (Prior and Likelihood) feed the Naive Bayes core, which produces a posterior score for each sense. The Argmax Selector picks the winner.

## 4.4 Syntactic Ambiguity Resolution via PCFG (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sent[Sentence: Saw man with telescope] --> tk[Tokeniser]
    tk --> chart[CKY Chart Parser]
    chart --> t1[Parse Tree T1: VP attachment]
    chart --> t2[Parse Tree T2: NP attachment]
    t1 --> p1[Compute P T1 from PCFG rules]
    t2 --> p2[Compute P T2 from PCFG rules]
    p1 --> cm[Compare posteriors]
    p2 --> cm
    cm --> sel[Argmax Tree Selected]
    style sel fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    style t2 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A single ambiguous sentence produces two parse trees. The PCFG assigns a probability to each; the tree with the higher product of rule probabilities is selected.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
Differentiate between **homonymy** and **polysemy** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Homonymy | Polysemy |
|---|---|---|
| **Etymology** | Words from different roots | Same root word, evolved meanings |
| **Relatedness of meaning** | Unrelated meanings | Related meanings |
| **Example 1** | **Bank** (river) vs **Bank** (finance) | **Head** (body part) vs **Head** (of department) |
| **Dictionary entries** | Listed as separate senses under unrelated categories | Often cross-referenced |
| **Sense count** | Usually 2, distinct | Many related senses |

> **Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks only if the student gives an etymological distinction. Listing examples alone is worth 1.5 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2022]**  
What is **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**? Mention any two applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) is the task of computationally identifying the correct sense of a polysemous or homonymous word within a given context.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Machine Translation** — "bank" must be translated differently in Russian (банк vs берег) depending on sense.
2. **Information Retrieval** — disambiguating "Jaguar" (car vs animal) improves search precision.
3. *(Optional: Text Summarisation, Sentiment Analysis, Question Answering)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**(a)** Explain the **Lesk Algorithm** for Word Sense Disambiguation with a worked example. Discuss its **limitations** and the improvements proposed in **Extended Lesk**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Naive Bayes probabilistic approach** to WSD. Derive the decision rule and apply it to a small corpus example with at least **two context words**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — Lesk Algorithm (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Conceptual Foundation (2 marks):**
The Lesk algorithm, proposed by Michael Lesk in 1986, is a **knowledge-based WSD method**. Given a target word $w$ in context $C$ and a dictionary with sense glosses $G(s_k)$, the algorithm selects the sense whose gloss has the **maximum lexical overlap** with the context.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**

$$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_k} \; \vert C \cap G(s_k) \vert$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (2 marks):**

Disambiguate **"cone"** in *"I bought an ice cream **cone**."*
- $s_1$ (shape): *"a three-dimensional geometric shape with circular base"*
- $s_2$ (ice cream): *"edible wafer shaped like a cone"*

Context tokens: $C = \{ \text{I, bought, an, ice, cream, cone} \}$
- $G(s_1) = \{ \text{three, dimensional, geometric, shape, circular, base} \}$ → overlap = 0
- $G(s_2) = \{ \text{edible, wafer, shaped, like, cone} \}$ → overlap = 0 (no content words match)

In such cases, the original Lesk fails. **Extended Lesk** addresses this.

**Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Counts **function words** (the, of) inflating scores.
2. Misses **content words** that are semantically related but lexically distinct.
3. Relies on short glosses — overlap is sparse.
4. No weighting — all overlapping words treated equally.

**Step 5 — Extended Lesk Improvements (1 mark):**
1. Compare against **example sentences** of each sense in addition to glosses.
2. Include **hypernyms, hyponyms, holonyms** from WordNet.
3. Apply **TF-IDF weighting** to overlap words.
4. Disregard stop words in scoring.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Naive Bayes WSD (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bayes Decision Rule Derivation (2 marks):**

For sense $s_k$ and context $C = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n\}$:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_k} \; P(s_k \mid C) = \arg\max_{s_k} \; P(s_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid s_k)
$$

The independence assumption is:

$$P(w_1, \dots, w_n \mid s_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid s_k)$$

**Step 2 — Worked Example (4 marks):**

Disambiguate **"plant"** in *"water the **plant** daily."*

| Sense | Prior Count | "water" count | "daily" count |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ (flora) | 800 | 120 | 40 |
| $s_2$ (factory) | 500 | 5 | 3 |
| $s_3$ (verb: to place) | 300 | 2 | 1 |

Total $N = 1600$, $\vert V \vert = 8$ (toy vocabulary).

**Priors:**

$$
P(s_1) = 0.50, \quad P(s_2) = 0.3125, \quad P(s_3) = 0.1875
$$

**Likelihoods with Add-1 Smoothing:**

$$
P(\text{water} \mid s_1) = \frac{121}{808} \approx 0.1498, \quad P(\text{water} \mid s_2) = \frac{6}{508} \approx 0.0118
$$

$$
P(\text{water} \mid s_3) = \frac{3}{308} \approx 0.00974
$$

$$
P(\text{daily} \mid s_1) = \frac{41}{808} \approx 0.0507, \quad P(\text{daily} \mid s_2) = \frac{4}{508} \approx 0.00787
$$

$$
P(\text{daily} \mid s_3) = \frac{2}{308} \approx 0.00649
$$

**Posterior Scores:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s_1) \cdot P(\text{water} \mid s_1) \cdot P(\text{daily} \mid s_1) &\approx 0.50 \times 0.1498 \times 0.0507 \approx 0.00380 \\
P(s_2) \cdot P(\text{water} \mid s_2) \cdot P(\text{daily} \mid s_2) &\approx 0.3125 \times 0.0118 \times 0.00787 \approx 2.9 \times 10^{-5} \\
P(s_3) \cdot P(\text{water} \mid s_3) \cdot P(\text{daily} \mid s_3) &\approx 0.1875 \times 0.00974 \times 0.00649 \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision (1 mark):**
$\hat{s} = s_1$ (flora). The classifier correctly identifies the botanical sense with overwhelming evidence (~131× stronger than $s_2$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**  
**(a)** Explain the different **types of ambiguity** in Natural Language Processing with **one example each** for lexical, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic ambiguity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** based disambiguator. Show how the **CKY algorithm** uses inside probabilities to resolve structural ambiguity in the sentence *"The man saw the girl with the telescope"*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Types of Ambiguity (7 marks)

**(i) Lexical Ambiguity (2 marks):**
A word has multiple dictionary senses.
- *Example:* "**bat**" → mammal (flying animal) OR sports equipment (cricket bat).

**(ii) Syntactic / Structural Ambiguity (2 marks):**
A sentence has multiple valid parse trees, often due to PP-attachment.
- *Example:* "I saw the man with the telescope." → instrument reading (PP modifies *saw*) or possessive reading (PP modifies *man*).

**(iii) Semantic Ambiguity (1.5 marks):**
A sentence is syntactically unambiguous but admits multiple meanings.
- *Example:* "Every student likes some professor." — *some professor* may be the same for all students (single) or different for each (multiple).

**(iv) Pragmatic / Anaphoric Ambiguity (1.5 marks):**
A pronoun's antecedent is unclear.
- *Example:* "**Riya** told **Maya** that **she** was leaving." — Who is "she"?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — PCFG Disambiguator (7 marks)

**Step 1 — PCFG Definition (1.5 marks):**
A PCFG augments CFG with rule probabilities such that for each non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\alpha} P(A \to \alpha) = 1$$

The probability of a parse tree $T$ is:

$$P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$$

**Step 2 — Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Sentence → Tokeniser → CKY Chart Parser
                ↓
        Inside-Outside Algorithm computes
        probabilities bottom-up
                ↓
        Multiple parse trees generated
                ↓
        Tree with max P(T) selected
                ↓
        Resolved Parse Output
```

**Step 3 — CKY Inside Probability Recursion (2 marks):**

For a cell $[i, j, A]$ representing non-terminal $A$ spanning words $i \dots j$:

$$\alpha[i,j,A] = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \sum_{B,C} \alpha[i,k,B] \cdot \alpha[k+1,j,C] \cdot P(A \to BC)$$

Base case: $\alpha[i,i,A] = P(A \to w_i)$ if $A \to w_i$ is a valid lexical rule.

**Step 4 — Application to "The man saw the girl with the telescope" (1.5 marks):**
CKY produces two parse trees: one where the PP "with the telescope" attaches to the VP (instrument reading) and another where it attaches to the NP "the girl" (possessive reading). The tree with the higher $\alpha[1,n,S]$ (start symbol probability) is selected as the resolved parse.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ambiguity Resolution Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing homonymy with polysemy** — Examiner deducts 1–1.5 marks. Memorise the etymological distinction.
> 2. **Omitting the independence assumption** in Naive Bayes derivations — always state *"assuming conditional independence of context words given the sense"*. Missing this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing** when computing likelihoods — if a word never appeared with a sense, you will get $P = 0$ and break the product. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing Lesk without gloss tokenisation** — glosses must be tokenised into word sets before computing overlap. Vague answers lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up CKY and Viterbi** — CKY is for CFG parsing, Viterbi is for HMM sequence tagging. Confusing them costs 1.5–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the argmax step** in derivations — always end with $\hat{s} = \arg\max$ explicitly. This is the final *answer*, not a hidden step.
> 7. **Writing examples without labels** — "I saw the man with the telescope" is **incomplete** as an answer; you must label each reading (instrument vs modifier).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ambiguity Resolution**

- **Ambiguity is inherent** to natural language and arises at **5 strata**: phonological, lexical, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic.
- **Lexical ambiguity** subtypes: **homonymy** (unrelated senses), **polysemy** (related senses), **homophony** (same sound), **homography** (same spelling, different sound).
- **Syntactic ambiguity** is most often **PP-attachment** ambiguity — e.g., *"saw the man with the telescope."*
- The **MAP decision rule** is the universal foundation:

$$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid c) = \arg\max_{s} P(c \mid s) P(s)$$

- **WSD** is the central lexical disambiguation task — Senseval/SemCor are standard benchmarks.
- **Lesk algorithm** uses dictionary gloss overlap — simple but weak (counts function words).
- **Extended Lesk** uses WordNet relations + TF-IDF weighting — much stronger.
- **Yarowsky algorithm** uses the *One Sense Per Discourse* and *One Sense Per Collocation* heuristics with bootstrapped decision lists.
- **Naive Bayes WSD** assumes conditional independence of context features:

$$P(s_k \mid w_1, \dots, w_n) \propto P(s_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid s_k)$$

- Always apply **Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing** to avoid zero probabilities:

$$P(w \mid s) = \frac{\text{count}(w, s) + 1}{\text{count}(s) + \vert V \vert}$$

- **HMM POS tagging** is a sequence disambiguation problem solved by **Viterbi**:

$$V_i(j) = \max_k \left[ V_{i-1}(k) \cdot P(t_j \mid t_k) \cdot P(w_i \mid t_j) \right]$$

- **PCFG** disambiguates structural ambiguity by computing $P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$ and selecting the maximum-probability parse.
- **CKY algorithm** with inside probabilities implements PCFG parsing in $O(n^3 \vert G \vert)$.
- The **Most-Frequent-Sense baseline** is a surprisingly strong benchmark (~51%) — always quote it for comparison.
- **Evaluation metrics**: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score; for WSD corpora use **κ (Cohen's kappa)** to measure inter-annotator agreement.
- **Production systems** (Google Translate, BERT, GPT) all descend from the MAP decision rule applied to richer features.
- **Conceptual hierarchy to remember**: Ambiguity (problem) → Disambiguation (task) → WSD/POS-tagging/PCFG (methods) → MAP (unifying theory).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Statistical Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Methods in NLP** refer to a family of probabilistic, data-driven techniques that use mathematical models built from large corpora to capture, predict, and disambiguate natural language phenomena. They replace hand-crafted linguistic rules with parameters estimated automatically from frequency counts, distributions, and likelihood functions.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) framework**, Statistical Methods encompass the following core sub-areas:

1. **N-gram Language Models** — computing the joint probability of word sequences using the chain rule and Markov assumption.
2. **Statistical Estimation** — primarily *Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)*, *Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)*, and *Bayesian Estimation*.
3. **Smoothing & Discounting** — Laplace (Add-one), Add-k, Good-Turing, Back-off, and **Kneser-Ney** smoothing.
4. **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** — for sequence labeling (POS tagging, NER).
5. **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)** — for syntactic parsing.
6. **Information-Theoretic Metrics** — Entropy, Perplexity, and Cross-Entropy for evaluating language models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (CO2 — KTU 2024):** *Apply statistical and probabilistic methods to model natural language phenomena and evaluate language models using standard information-theoretic measures.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster of Words"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are a weather forecaster.** You do not memorize the exact physics equations of the atmosphere for every city. Instead, you look at **historical data** ("in the last 30 years, when it rained on Monday, it rained on Tuesday 70% of the time") and use those **counts** to predict tomorrow. Statistical NLP works the same way — it does not "understand" grammar; it counts how often words appear together and uses those numbers to predict the next word, tag a sentence, or parse a structure.

### Why "Statistical" Instead of "Rule-Based"?

| Paradigm | Approach | Limitation |
|----------|----------|------------|
| **Rule-based (Symbolic)** | Hand-crafted linguistic rules by experts | Brittle, doesn't scale, language exceptions break rules |
| **Statistical (Probabilistic)** | Learn parameters from corpora automatically | Robust, handles ambiguity, generalizes well |

### Key Probabilistic Foundation

The fundamental principle is the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})$$

But because computing $P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1})$ is intractable for long histories, the **Markov assumption** (n-gram approximation) is applied.

### Important Constants & Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

- **Perplexity (PP)**: Measures how "surprised" a model is by test data. **Lower is better.**
- **Entropy (H)**: Average information content per word, in **bits** (log base 2) or **nats** (log base e).
- **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV)** rate: Fraction of test words unseen in training.
- **Laplace constant**: standard value is **k = 1** (for add-one smoothing).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 N-gram Language Models — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Reasoning (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

**Step 1 — Chain Rule of Probability (Exact Formulation)**
For any sequence of words $w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$, the joint probability is decomposed using conditional probabilities. *Why?* Because joint probabilities are difficult to estimate directly from sparse data, but conditional probabilities are easier to count.

$$P(w_1^n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1^2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1^{n-1})$$

**Step 2 — Markov Assumption (Order-n Approximation)**
A trigram model (n=3) approximates the history by only the **last 2 words**. *Why?* Because $P(\text{rabbit} \mid \text{The, quick, brown, fox, jumps, the})$ from full history is impossible to estimate — almost no sentence repeats that exact context.

$$P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**
Parameters are estimated by **relative frequency** (count-based).

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})}$$

*Why MLE?* It is the simplest estimator that maximizes the likelihood of the observed training corpus.

## 2.2 Smoothing Techniques — Handling the Zero-Probability Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Problem:** Any n-gram unseen in training gets probability **0**, which makes the entire sentence probability **0**. Smoothing redistributes probability mass from seen events to unseen events.

### Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing

$$P_{\text{Add-1}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + V}$$

where $V$ is the **vocabulary size**. *Why?* It pretends we saw every word one extra time.

### Add-k Smoothing

$$P_{\text{Add-}k}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + k}{C(w_{i-1}) + kV}$$

where $0 < k \leq 1$ (typically $k = 0.01$ to $0.1$). *Why?* Add-1 is too aggressive; it over-shrinks frequent events. A smaller $k$ preserves true frequencies better.

### Good-Turing Smoothing

The core insight: **the probability of an unseen event is estimated by the frequency of events that occurred once.**

$$P^*(\text{things seen } c \text{ times}) = \frac{(c+1) \cdot N_{c+1}}{N_c}$$

where $N_c$ = number of n-grams occurring exactly $c$ times in training.

### Kneser-Ney Smoothing (Industry Standard)

Combines **discounting** ($D$) with **continuation probability** — the idea that a word is likely to follow a context if that word itself appears in *many different contexts*.

$$P_{\text{KN}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\max(C(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D, 0)}{C(w_{i-1})} + \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{\text{cont}}(w_i)$$

## 2.3 Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) — Three Core Problems

> [!NOTE]
> HMMs are **probabilistic sequence models** where observations (words) are generated by **hidden states** (e.g., POS tags). They are foundational for POS tagging, NER, and speech recognition.

| Problem | Name | Algorithm | Purpose |
|---------|------|-----------|---------|
| **Problem 1** | *Evaluation* | Forward Algorithm | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ |
| **Problem 2** | *Decoding* | Viterbi Algorithm | Find best state sequence $\arg\max P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ |
| **Problem 3** | *Learning* | Forward-Backward (Baum-Welch) | Estimate parameters from unlabeled data |

### HMM Parameter Set: $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$

- $A$ = state transition matrix $A_{ij} = P(q_t = j \mid q_{t-1} = i)$
- $B$ = observation/emission matrix $B_{ik} = P(o_t = k \mid q_t = i)$
- $\pi$ = initial state distribution $\pi_i = P(q_1 = i)$

## 2.4 Information-Theoretic Evaluation Metrics

### Perplexity (PP)

$$PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$$

Equivalently (in log-space for numerical stability):

$$\text{PP}(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})\right)$$

*Why?* It is the **geometric mean of the inverse probabilities** of the words — equivalent to the branching factor of a uniform random model that would give the same surprise.

### Entropy

$$H(P) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log_2 P(x)$$

For a language model, the **cross-entropy** between true distribution $P^*$ and model $Q$ is:

$$H(P^*, Q) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 Q(w_i)$$

And $\text{PP} = 2^{H(P^*, Q)}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Chain Rule (Joint) | $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ | Exact decomposition |
| 2 | N-gram Approximation | $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$ | Markov assumption |
| 3 | MLE (Bigram) | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) \,/\, C(w_{i-1})$ | Count-based |
| 4 | Laplace | $P = (C + 1) \,/\, (N + V)$ | $V$ = vocab size |
| 5 | Add-k | $P = (C + k) \,/\, (N + kV)$ | $0 < k \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Good-Turing | $c^* = (c+1) N_{c+1} \,/\, N_c$ | $N_c$ = count of counts |
| 7 | Perplexity | $\text{PP} = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Lower is better |
| 8 | Entropy | $H = -\sum P(x) \log_2 P(x)$ | Bits per symbol |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy | $H(P^*, Q) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \log_2 Q(w_i)$ | Model vs. true |
| 10 | HMM Forward | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_j(o_t)$ | Recursion |
| 11 | HMM Viterbi | $V_t(j) = \max_i \left[V_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_j(o_t)$ | Max-product |
| 12 | HMM Backward | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j A_{ij} B_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Recursion |
| 13 | Baum-Welch Update (π) | $\pi_i^{\text{new}} = \gamma_1(i)$ | E-step posterior |
| 14 | Baum-Welch Update (A) | $A_{ij}^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Re-estimation |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition** (Google Assistant, Siri): HMMs + n-gram LMs remain the decoder backbone.
- **Machine Translation**: Statistical MT (Moses, Google Translate pre-2016) used n-gram LMs and alignment models.
- **Autocomplete / T9 Keyboards**: Backoff bigram/trigram models predict next word.
- **Spelling Correction**: Noisy channel model = $\arg\max_w P(w \mid x) = \arg\max_w P(x \mid w) P(w)$ — language model $P(w)$ is a bigram.
- **Bio-informatics**: HMMs for gene finding (GENSCAN), protein family modeling (profile HMMs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Chain Rule to Bigram MLE

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Given a corpus where $\text{count}(\text{the}, \text{cat}) = 7$, $\text{count}(\text{the}) = 12$, $\text{count}(\text{the}, \text{dog}) = 3$, $\text{count}(\text{the}, \text{bird}) = 2$, derive the bigram probability $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the})$.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — State the Chain Rule for the trigram context**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1}) &= \frac{P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_i)}{P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Bigram (Markov Order-1) Assumption**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1}) &\approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Justification:* We approximate the full history by the most recent word only. This makes the estimation problem tractable.

**Step 3 — Define the MLE Estimator as Relative Frequency**

By the law of large numbers, the empirical frequency converges to the true probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) &= \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C(\cdot)$ denotes the count in the training corpus.

**Step 4 — Plug in the Numerical Values**

For $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{C(\text{the}, \text{cat})}{C(\text{the})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Probability Distribution Sums to 1**

The bigram distribution conditioned on "the" must sum to unity. Using the values given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{w} P(w \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{C(\text{the}, \text{cat}) + C(\text{the}, \text{dog}) + C(\text{the}, \text{bird}) + \ldots}{C(\text{the})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{7 + 3 + 2 + \ldots}{12} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The remaining probability mass is $12 - 12 = 0$... wait, $7 + 3 + 2 = 12$, so the sum is $\frac{12}{12} = 1$. ✅

**Step 6 — Compute the Sentence Probability**

For sentence "the cat sat", under bigram model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{the cat sat}) &= P(\text{the} \mid \langle s \rangle) \cdot P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) \cdot P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) \cdot P(\langle /s \rangle \mid \text{sat})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Same corpus. Vocabulary size $V = 100{,}000$. $C(\text{the}, \text{cat}) = 7$, $C(\text{the}) = 12$, but we now want $P(\text{alien} \mid \text{the})$ where "alien" never followed "the" in training.

**Step 1 — Identify the Problem**

MLE gives $P(\text{alien} \mid \text{the}) = \frac{0}{12} = 0$. If the word "alien" appears in a test sentence, the entire sentence probability collapses to 0.

**Step 2 — Apply the Add-One Transformation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) &= \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Seen Bigram Probability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{7 + 1}{12 + 100000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8}{100012} \\[4pt]
&\approx 7.998 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Unseen Bigram Probability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\text{alien} \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{0 + 1}{12 + 100000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{100012} \\[4pt]
&\approx 9.998 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Sum-to-Unity**

Sum over all $V$ possible next words:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{w=1}^{V} P_{\text{Add-1}}(w \mid \text{the}) &= \frac{\sum_{w} (C(\text{the}, w) + 1)}{12 + V} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{C(\text{the}) + V}{12 + V} = \frac{12 + 100000}{100012} = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Good-Turing Discounting

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Training corpus has $N_1 = 50$ bigrams appearing exactly once, $N_2 = 20$ appearing exactly twice, $N_3 = 8$ appearing exactly thrice, $N_4 = 3$ appearing exactly four times. Compute the adjusted count $c^*$ for $c = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Good-Turing Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c^* &= \frac{(c+1) \cdot N_{c+1}}{N_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $c^* = 0$ (Unseen Events)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0^* &= \frac{(0+1) \cdot N_1}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N_0 = V^2 - (N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4) = $ total unseen bigrams. For $V = 1000$, $N_0 \approx 10^6 - 81 \approx 999{,}919$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0^* &\approx \frac{1 \times 50}{999919} \approx 5.0005 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $c^* = 1$ (Events Seen Once)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1^* &= \frac{(1+1) \cdot N_2}{N_1} = \frac{2 \times 20}{50} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $c^* = 2$ (Events Seen Twice)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^* &= \frac{(2+1) \cdot N_3}{N_2} = \frac{3 \times 8}{20} = \frac{24}{20} = 1.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $c^* = 3$ (Events Seen Thrice)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^* &= \frac{(3+1) \cdot N_4}{N_3} = \frac{4 \times 3}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret**

> [!TIP]
> Notice that seen counts are *discounted* — a bigram seen once is treated as if it occurred 0.8 times, twice as 1.2 times, thrice as 1.5 times. The "missing" probability mass is reallocated to unseen events.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Viterbi Algorithm (Toy Trace)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** HMM with 2 states $\{$Noun, Verb$\}$, 2 observations $\{$time, flies$\}$. Find the most likely tag sequence for "time flies".

Assume:
- $\pi(\text{Noun}) = 0.6, \pi(\text{Verb}) = 0.4$
- $A_{\text{Noun},\text{Noun}} = 0.3, A_{\text{Noun},\text{Verb}} = 0.7$
- $A_{\text{Verb},\text{Noun}} = 0.8, A_{\text{Verb},\text{Verb}} = 0.2$
- $B_{\text{Noun}}(\text{time}) = 0.7, B_{\text{Noun}}(\text{flies}) = 0.3$
- $B_{\text{Verb}}(\text{time}) = 0.4, B_{\text{Verb}}(\text{flies}) = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Initialization (t=1, observation = "time")**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(\text{Noun}) &= \pi(\text{Noun}) \cdot B_{\text{Noun}}(\text{time}) = 0.6 \times 0.7 = 0.42 \\[4pt]
V_1(\text{Verb}) &= \pi(\text{Verb}) \cdot B_{\text{Verb}}(\text{time}) = 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

Backpointers: $\text{bp}_1(\text{Noun}) = \text{Start}, \quad \text{bp}_1(\text{Verb}) = \text{Start}$

**Step 2 — Recursion (t=2, observation = "flies")**

For state = Noun:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(\text{Noun}) &= \max\left[V_1(\text{Noun}) \cdot A_{\text{Noun},\text{Noun}}, \quad V_1(\text{Verb}) \cdot A_{\text{Verb},\text{Noun}}\right] \cdot B_{\text{Noun}}(\text{flies}) \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.42 \times 0.3, \quad 0.16 \times 0.8\right] \times 0.3 \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.126, \quad 0.128\right] \times 0.3 \\[4pt]
&= 0.128 \times 0.3 = 0.0384
\end{aligned}
$$

$\text{bp}_2(\text{Noun}) = \text{Verb}$

For state = Verb:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(\text{Verb}) &= \max\left[V_1(\text{Noun}) \cdot A_{\text{Noun},\text{Verb}}, \quad V_1(\text{Verb}) \cdot A_{\text{Verb},\text{Verb}}\right] \cdot B_{\text{Verb}}(\text{flies}) \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.42 \times 0.7, \quad 0.16 \times 0.2\right] \times 0.6 \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.294, \quad 0.032\right] \times 0.6 \\[4pt]
&= 0.294 \times 0.6 = 0.1764
\end{aligned}
$$

$\text{bp}_2(\text{Verb}) = \text{Noun}$

**Step 3 — Termination**

The best final state is Verb (0.1764 > 0.0384).

**Step 4 — Backtrace**

$\text{Verb} \xleftarrow{\text{bp}_2} \text{Noun} \xleftarrow{\text{bp}_1} \text{Start}$

**Best sequence:** Noun → Verb. (i.e., "time" is a Noun, "flies" is a Verb)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison Table

| Smoothing Method | Discount Formula | Strength | Weakness | Use Case |
|------------------|------------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Laplace (Add-1)** | $C+1$ | Simple, guarantees $P>0$ | Distorts frequent events badly | Small corpora, teaching |
| **Add-k** | $C+k$ | Tunable aggressiveness | $k$ must be chosen | Mid-size corpora |
| **Good-Turing** | $c^* = (c+1)N_{c+1}/N_c$ | Theoretically motivated | Needs count-of-counts table | Foundation for KN |
| **Backoff (Stupid)** | Katz, uses lower n-gram | Saves high-n data | Incoherent | Bigram-trigram blend |
| **Interpolation** | Linear combo of orders | Always smooth | Weight tuning | Robust baseline |
| **Kneser-Ney** | Discount + continuation | State-of-the-art n-gram | Implementation complexity | Production systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Statistical NLP Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization and Vocabulary Building]
    B --> C[Counting Engine N-gram Extractor]
    C --> D[Raw N-gram Counts]
    D --> E[Statistical Estimator MLE Module]
    E --> F{Rare or Unseen Events Check}
    F -- Yes --> G[Smoothing Module Laplace Add-1 Add-k Good-Turing Kneser-Ney]
    F -- No --> H[Probability Distribution Lookup]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Language Model Ready for Use]
    I --> J[Evaluation Module Perplexity Cross-Entropy]
    J --> K[Final Model Performance Metric]
    I --> L[Downstream Applications POS Tagging Autocomplete MT Speech]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — HMM Three-Problem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observation Sequence O] --> B{Choose HMM Task}
    B -- Problem 1 Evaluation --> C[Forward Algorithm]
    C --> C1[Output P of O given Lambda]
    B -- Problem 2 Decoding --> D[Viterbi Algorithm]
    D --> D1[Output Best State Sequence]
    B -- Problem 3 Learning --> E[Forward Backward Baum Welch]
    E --> E1[Output Re-estimated Parameters]
    C1 --> F[Final HMM Solution]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Smoothing Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sparse N-gram Counts] --> B{Smoothing Strategy}
    B -- Statistical Discount --> C[Good-Turing]
    B -- Add Constant --> D[Laplace Add-1]
    B -- Add Tunable Constant --> E[Add-k]
    B -- Context Fallback --> F[Backoff and Stupid Backoff]
    B -- Weighted Combination --> G[Linear Interpolation]
    B -- Advanced Hybrid --> H[Kneser-Ney with Continuation]
    C --> I[Adjusted Count c-star]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Smoothed Probability Distribution]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Viterbi Trellis (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time t equals 0 Start] --> T1N[State Noun V1 Noun]
    T0 --> T1V[State Verb V1 Verb]
    T1N --> T2N[State Noun V2 Noun]
    T1N --> T2V[State Verb V2 Verb]
    T1V --> T2N
    T1V --> T2V
    T2N --> T3F[Final State]
    T2V --> T3F
    T3F --> T4[Backtrace Best Path]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Perplexity Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Language Model] --> B[Read Test Corpus]
    B --> C[For each sentence compute log probability]
    C --> D[Sum all log probabilities]
    D --> E[Divide by total word count N]
    E --> F[Negate the result]
    F --> G[Exponentiate base 2]
    G --> H[Final Perplexity Score]
    H --> I{Lower than Baseline}
    I -- Yes --> J[Model is Better]
    I -- No --> K[Model is Worse]
```
