# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define an n-gram language model. What is the Markov assumption that underlies it?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **n-gram language model** is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability to a sequence of $n$ words, computed from the relative frequencies of word tuples in a training corpus. Formally:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \ldots, w_{i-1})
$$

The **Markov assumption** states that the probability of a word depends only on a fixed window of $n-1$ preceding words, not on the entire history. This makes probability estimation tractable from finite corpora by reducing the context to a manageable size.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is perplexity? Why is it preferred over raw probability for evaluating language models?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Perplexity (PP)** is the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities of a sequence of $N$ words, given a language model. It is computed as:

$$
\text{PP}(W) = \left[ P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N) \right]^{-1/N}
$$

**Why preferred over raw probability:**
1. **Length independence:** Raw probability decreases exponentially with sentence length; perplexity normalizes by word count.
2. **Interpretability:** It represents the effective branching factor — equivalent to the average number of choices a model is confused among.
3. **Monotonicity:** Lower PP always indicates a better model, regardless of corpus size.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to add the sentence boundary markers $\langle s \rangle$ and $\langle /s \rangle$ when computing n-gram probabilities, leading to incorrect chain-rule expansions and losing **2–3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Laplace smoothing, students use $V$ as the total corpus size instead of the **vocabulary size** — this is the most frequent error. Always re-check.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Viterbi backpointers are often skipped. You must include both the **forward pass (V values)** and the **backward pass (backpointers)** to receive full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For Kneser-Ney, students only present the formula but not the **continuation probability** component. Always explain both parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**A. (a)** Given the following training corpus:

```
<s> I am Sam </s>
<s> Sam I am </s>
<s> I am Sam </s>
<s> I do not like green eggs and ham </s>
```

Compute the **bigram probabilities** $P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am})$, $P(\text{I} \mid \text{Sam})$, and $P(\text{am} \mid \text{I})$ using Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Also compute the probability of the sentence "I am Sam" under the bigram model. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** Explain **Laplace (Add-1) smoothing** with a suitable formula. If the vocabulary size $V = 20$ and the same bigram counts as above are used, recompute the probability of "I am Sam" using Add-1 smoothing. State one limitation of Laplace smoothing. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for A(a) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — Count unigrams and bigrams from the corpus**

Unigram counts:
$C(\text{I}) = 4$, $C(\text{am}) = 4$, $C(\text{Sam}) = 4$, $C(\text{do}) = 1$, $C(\text{not}) = 1$, $C(\text{like}) = 1$, $C(\text{green}) = 1$, $C(\text{eggs}) = 1$, $C(\text{and}) = 1$, $C(\text{ham}) = 1$, $C(\langle s \rangle) = 4$, $C(\langle /s \rangle) = 4$.

Bigram counts:
$C(\text{am}, \text{Sam}) = 2$, $C(\text{Sam}, \langle /s \rangle) = 2$, $C(\langle s \rangle, \text{I}) = 3$, $C(\text{I}, \text{am}) = 3$, $C(\text{am}, \langle /s \rangle) = 1$, $C(\langle s \rangle, \text{Sam}) = 1$, $C(\text{Sam}, \text{I}) = 1$, $C(\langle s \rangle, \text{I}, \text{am})$... (we just need bigrams).

Key bigrams needed:
$C(\text{am}, \text{Sam}) = 2$, $C(\text{am}) = 4$, $C(\text{Sam}, \text{I}) = 1$, $C(\text{Sam}) = 4$, $C(\text{I}, \text{am}) = 3$, $C(\text{I}) = 4$.

**Step 2 — Apply MLE Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) &= \frac{C(\text{am}, \text{Sam})}{C(\text{am})} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \\[6pt]
P(\text{I} \mid \text{Sam}) &= \frac{C(\text{Sam}, \text{I})}{C(\text{Sam})} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \\[6pt]
P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) &= \frac{C(\text{I}, \text{am})}{C(\text{I})} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sentence Probability**

For the sentence "I am Sam" with start and end tokens, the bigram chain-rule expansion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\langle s \rangle \, \text{I am Sam} \, \langle /s \rangle) &= P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) \cdot P(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) \cdot P(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) \cdot P(\langle /s \rangle \mid \text{Sam})
\end{aligned}
$$

From the corpus:
$P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) = 3/4 = 0.75$, $P(\langle /s \rangle \mid \text{Sam}) = 2/4 = 0.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.75 \times 0.75 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \\[4pt]
&= 0.75 \times 0.75 \times 0.25 \\[4pt]
&= 0.140625
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Model Solution for A(b) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — Define Laplace Smoothing**

Laplace (Add-1) smoothing adds 1 to every bigram count to ensure no zero probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + V}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V$ is the **vocabulary size** (unique word types). The "+V" in the denominator preserves the probability distribution's sum-to-unity property.

**Step 2 — Recompute Individual Probabilities (V = 20)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) &= \frac{3 + 1}{4 + 20} = \frac{4}{24} = 0.1667 \\[6pt]
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) &= \frac{3 + 1}{4 + 20} = \frac{4}{24} = 0.1667 \\[6pt]
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\text{Sam} \mid \text{am}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 20} = \frac{3}{24} = 0.125 \\[6pt]
P_{\text{Add-1}}(\langle /s \rangle \mid \text{Sam}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 20} = \frac{3}{24} = 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to Get Sentence Probability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Add-1}} &= 0.1667 \times 0.1667 \times 0.125 \times 0.125 \\[4pt]
&= 0.02778 \times 0.015625 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.34 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Limitation of Laplace Smoothing**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Laplace smoothing assigns too much probability mass to unseen events. It uniformly subtracts probability from *all* seen events, distorting frequent bigrams disproportionately. In large-vocabulary tasks (e.g., $V > 10^5$), the +V in the denominator can shrink true probabilities by orders of magnitude. This is why more refined methods like **Kneser-Ney** are preferred in practice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**B. (a)** Describe the architecture of a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)**. Define the parameters $A$, $B$, $\pi$. State the **three fundamental problems of HMMs** and name the algorithm that solves each. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** Consider the HMM with 2 states (S1, S2) and observations (A, B). Given:
- $\pi = (0.6, 0.4)$
- $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$
- $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}$

Apply the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the most likely state sequence for the observation sequence "A B". Show all steps including backpointers. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B(a) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — HMM Architecture**

An HMM is a doubly stochastic process where:
- The **hidden layer** is a Markov chain over states $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T$.
- At each time step, the current hidden state emits an **observable symbol** $o_t$.
- The observation depends *only* on the current state (the **emission independence assumption**).

**Step 2 — Parameters**

- **Initial state distribution:** $\pi_i = P(q_1 = i)$
- **Transition matrix:** $A_{ij} = P(q_t = j \mid q_{t-1} = i)$ — probability of moving from state $i$ to state $j$.
- **Emission matrix:** $B_{ik} = P(o_t = k \mid q_t = i)$ — probability of observing symbol $k$ in state $i$.

The complete model is $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.

**Step 3 — Three Problems and Algorithms**

| # | Problem | Algorithm | Output |
|---|---------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | **Evaluation:** Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Forward | Likelihood of observation sequence |
| 2 | **Decoding:** Find $\arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ | Viterbi | Best state sequence |
| 3 | **Learning:** Estimate $\lambda$ from observations | Forward-Backward (Baum-Welch, EM) | Updated parameters |

#### **Model Solution for B(b) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — Initialization (t=1, observation = A)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(S_1) &= \pi(S_1) \cdot B_{S_1}(A) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54 \\[6pt]
V_1(S_2) &= \pi(S_2) \cdot B_{S_2}(A) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

$\text{bp}_1(S_1) = \text{Start}, \quad \text{bp}_1(S_2) = \text{Start}$

**Step 2 — Recursion (t=2, observation = B)**

For state $S_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(S_1) &= \max\left[V_1(S_1) \cdot A_{11}, \quad V_1(S_2) \cdot A_{21}\right] \cdot B_{S_1}(B) \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.54 \times 0.7, \quad 0.12 \times 0.4\right] \times 0.1 \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.378, \quad 0.048\right] \times 0.1 \\[4pt]
&= 0.378 \times 0.1 = 0.0378
\end{aligned}
$$

$\text{bp}_2(S_1) = S_1$ (came from S1)

For state $S_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(S_2) &= \max\left[V_1(S_1) \cdot A_{12}, \quad V_1(S_2) \cdot A_{22}\right] \cdot B_{S_2}(B) \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.54 \times 0.3, \quad 0.12 \times 0.6\right] \times 0.7 \\[4pt]
&= \max\left[0.162, \quad 0.072\right] \times 0.7 \\[4pt]
&= 0.162 \times 0.7 = 0.1134
\end{aligned}
$$

$\text{bp}_2(S_2) = S_1$ (came from S1)

**Step 3 — Termination**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max\left[V_2(S_1), V_2(S_2)\right] &= \max[0.0378, 0.1134] = 0.1134
\end{aligned}
$$

The best final state is $S_2$.

**Step 4 — Backtrace**

$\text{Final} = S_2 \xleftarrow{\text{bp}_2} S_1 \xleftarrow{\text{bp}_1} \text{Start}$

**Best state sequence:** $S_1 \rightarrow S_2$

Probability of best path: $\mathbf{0.1134}$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-night KTU prep):**

- **N-gram model**: N-gram = sequence of N words. Unigram (N=1), Bigram (N=2), Trigram (N=3). Higher N = more context, but sparser counts.

- **Chain rule**: $P(A,B,C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A,B)$ — always exact.

- **Markov assumption**: Approximates the full history with the last $n-1$ words — what makes n-gram models *practical*.

- **MLE formula**: $P(w_n \mid w_{n-1}) = \frac{C(w_{n-1}, w_n)}{C(w_{n-1})}$ — the simplest estimator; can yield zero for unseen events.

- **Zero-probability problem**: MLE assigns 0 to unseen n-grams, which makes entire sentence probability 0. **Smoothing fixes this.**

- **Laplace (Add-1)**: Add 1 to every count; divide by $N + V$. Quick but distorts high-frequency events.

- **Add-k**: Same as Add-1 but with tunable $k$ (typically 0.01 to 0.1) — more nuanced.

- **Good-Turing**: Discount seen counts using count-of-counts; reallocate to unseen. Core idea: $c^* = (c+1) N_{c+1} / N_c$.

- **Kneser-Ney**: Industry standard. Combines **discounting** with **continuation probability** (a word's likelihood to appear in *diverse contexts*).

- **Perplexity**: $\text{PP}(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$. **Lower is better.** Equivalent to branching factor.

- **Entropy**: $H = -\sum P(x) \log_2 P(x)$. Average bits per symbol. **Cross-entropy** = $-\frac{1}{N} \sum \log_2 Q(w_i)$.

- **HMM parameters**: $A$ (transitions), $B$ (emissions), $\pi$ (initial distribution). $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.

- **HMM Problem 1 (Evaluation)**: $\rightarrow$ **Forward algorithm**. Computes $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

- **HMM Problem 2 (Decoding)**: $\rightarrow$ **Viterbi algorithm** (max-product + backpointers). Finds $\arg\max P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$.

- **HMM Problem 3 (Learning)**: $\rightarrow$ **Baum-Welch** (special case of EM). Updates $A, B, \pi$ iteratively.

- **Forward recursion**: $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_j(o_t)$.

- **Viterbi recursion**: $V_t(j) = \max_i \left[V_{t-1}(i) A_{ij}\right] B_j(o_t)$.

- **Backward recursion**: $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j A_{ij} B_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$.

- **Use $\langle s \rangle$ and $\langle /s \rangle$** in all n-gram chain-rule calculations — forgetting these loses 2+ marks.

- **Vocabulary size $V$** = number of *unique* types, not total corpus tokens $N$ — a very common error.

- **PCFGs** extend CFGs by assigning probabilities to each rule; inside-outside algorithm does HMM-style learning for trees.

- **Applications to recall for KTU viva**: speech recognition, statistical MT, POS tagging, NER, autocomplete, spelling correction (noisy channel), bioinformatics (gene finding).

- **Log-space arithmetic**: Always work in log-probabilities to avoid underflow for long sentences. (KTU tip: add $\log P$ values, then exponentiate at the end.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Context Free Grammar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Context Free Grammar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: PROBABILISTIC CONTEXT FREE GRAMMAR (PCFG)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Probabilistic Context Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a Context Free Grammar (CFG) in which every production rule is augmented with a probability (or likelihood) that indicates how likely that rule is to be used in any derivation of a string from a given non-terminal. It is formally defined as a 5-tuple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$$

Where the components are:
- $N$ = A finite set of **Non-Terminal symbols** (grammatical categories like Noun, Verb, NP, VP).
- $\Sigma$ = A finite set of **Terminal symbols** (actual words in the language), where $N \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$.
- $R$ = A finite set of **Production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^{*}$.
- $S$ = A designated **Start symbol** belonging to $N$.
- $P$ = A function that assigns a probability to every rule in $R$ such that for every non-terminal $A \in N$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

This is the **Probability Constraint** (a hard requirement of KTU's expected definition). The sum of probabilities of all rules that expand any given non-terminal must always equal 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Every PCFG production rule takes the augmented form $A \rightarrow \beta \ [p]$, where $p$ is a real number in the closed interval $[0, 1]$. A derivation tree is considered valid only if it obeys the probability sum constraint for all its non-terminal nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an English teacher correcting essays. You see the phrase "**the cat sat**". A standard CFG gives you a single rule-based structure (NP + VP), but it cannot tell you *which* structure is more common in real language. Now, imagine a smarter teacher who, based on observing **10,000 sentences**, knows that:
- Singular Noun Phrases followed by Singular Verbs occur in 70% of cases.
- Pronoun subjects occur in 30% of cases.

A **PCFG** is exactly this "smarter teacher". It attaches a *statistical confidence* to every grammatical rule. When the same string can be parsed in two different ways (a phenomenon called **structural ambiguity**), PCFG automatically chooses the parse that is mathematically *more likely* to occur in natural English. In essence, PCFG = **Context Free Grammar + Statistics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** PCFGs are the mathematical backbone of statistical parsers used in search engines (Google), voice assistants (Siri, Alexa), machine translation systems (Google Translate), and grammar checkers (Grammarly). They allow machines to choose between multiple grammatical interpretations of a sentence.

### 1.3 Illustrative Example

Consider the following PCFG fragment for the sentence "astronomers saw stars with ears":

| **Production Rule** | **Probability** |
| :--- | :---: |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | **0.70** |
| $VP \rightarrow VP\ PP$ | **0.30** |
| $PP \rightarrow P\ NP$ | **1.00** |
| $NP \rightarrow astronomers$ | **0.10** |
| $NP \rightarrow ears$ | **0.10** |
| $NP \rightarrow saw$ | **0.04** |
| $NP \rightarrow stars$ | **0.18** |
| $NP \rightarrow NP\ PP$ | **0.18** |
| $NP \rightarrow NP\ NP$ | **0.10** |
| $P \rightarrow with$ | **1.00** |
| $V \rightarrow saw$ | **1.00** |

For the start symbol $S$, observe that the probabilities of all rules with $S$ on the LHS sum to **1.0**. Similarly, for $NP$, the sum of all 6 production probabilities equals $0.10 + 0.10 + 0.04 + 0.18 + 0.18 + 0.10 = 0.70 + 0.18 + 0.04 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 = ...$ (only 4 rules listed here, all summing to 1.0).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a PCFG: The Underlying Logic

A PCFG operates on three core principles that govern how sentences are constructed and evaluated:

1. **Lexical Rule Generation:** A non-terminal can rewrite into a terminal (a word). For example, $N \rightarrow \text{astronomer}$ has a probability that reflects the word's frequency in a training corpus.
2. **Syntactic Rule Expansion:** Non-terminals rewrite into combinations of other non-terminals (e.g., $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$).
3. **Probabilistic Independence Assumption:** The probability of expanding a non-terminal $A$ into $\beta$ is **independent** of the surrounding context (host node in the parse tree). This is why it is called *Context-Free* with probabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind PCFG:** Plain CFGs suffer from structural ambiguity. A single sentence like "I saw the man with the telescope" can be parsed in two completely different ways. CFGs treat both parses as equally valid. PCFGs resolve this by computing the *probability* of each parse tree and choosing the most likely one.

## 2.2 Probability of a Parse Tree and a String

### Probability of a Parse Tree $T$ for a string $s$:

The probability of a complete parse tree $T$ is the **product** of the probabilities of all rules used at every node in the tree:

$$P(T, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$$

where $R_i$ are the $n$ production rules used in the derivation of tree $T$.

### Probability of a String $s$:

The probability of a string $s$ is the **sum** of the probabilities of *all possible* parse trees that derive $s$:

$$P(s) = \sum_{T: \text{yield}(T) = s} P(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** If a string has only one parse tree, $P(s)$ equals the product of that single tree's rule probabilities. If it has multiple trees, summation is required (computed efficiently using the **Inside Algorithm**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Property** | **Purpose / Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PCFG Tuple | $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ | Formal 5-tuple definition |
| Probability Constraint | $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ | For every non-terminal $A$ |
| Parse Tree Probability | $P(T, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$ | Product of all rules in tree $T$ |
| String Probability | $P(s) = \sum_{T} P(T)$ | Sum over all valid parse trees $T$ |
| Inside Probability $\alpha(i, j, A)$ | Probability that $A$ generates $w_i \ldots w_j$ | Used to compute $P(s)$ via DP |
| Inside Base Case | $\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ | For terminal rewrites |
| Inside Recursive Case | $\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{B,C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$ | For non-terminal splits |
| Viterbi Probability $\beta(i, j, A)$ | Max probability of any subtree for $w_i \ldots w_j$ | Used to find best parse |
| Viterbi Base Case | $\beta(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$ | For terminal rewrites |
| Viterbi Recursive Case | $\beta(i, j, A) = \max_{B, C, k} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \beta(i, k, B) \cdot \beta(k+1, j, C)$ | For non-terminal splits |
| Chomsky Normal Form | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Required input format for CYK |
| Time Complexity (Inside/Viterbi) | $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ | For string of length $n$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCFGs and their associated dynamic programming algorithms are foundational in:

- **Statistical Machine Translation (SMT):** Aligning syntactic structures across languages.
- **Speech Recognition:** Disambiguating phonetically identical word sequences (e.g., "ice cream" vs. "I scream").
- **Information Extraction:** Identifying noun-phrase boundaries for entity recognition.
- **Grammar Checking & Autocorrection:** Selecting the most probable grammatical structure.
- **Bioinformatics:** Modeling RNA secondary structures and protein folding patterns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of a Sentence Using a Parse Tree

### Setup:
Consider a PCFG with the start symbol $S$ and the sentence $s = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$. Suppose there exist $k$ distinct parse trees $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$ that derive $s$. Each tree $T_i$ uses a sequence of production rules $R_{i,1}, R_{i,2}, \ldots, R_{i, m_i}$.

### Step 1: Define the probability of a single parse tree
The probability of a parse tree is the product of the probabilities of all rules used in its derivation. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_i, s) &= \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} P(R_{i,j}) \\
&= P(R_{i,1}) \cdot P(R_{i,2}) \cdot \ldots \cdot P(R_{i, m_i})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Since PCFG assumes context-free independence, the choice of rule at any node is statistically independent of the choices at other nodes. Therefore, the joint probability factorizes into a product of individual rule probabilities.

### Step 2: Aggregate over all parse trees
A string $s$ is considered generated by the grammar if **at least one** parse tree derives it. Since the parse trees represent mutually exclusive events (a single derivation cannot be two different trees), we use the **sum rule of probability**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s) &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} P(T_i, s) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \prod_{j=1}^{m_i} P(R_{i,j})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the marginal probability of the string over the latent variable (the parse tree structure). The summation integrates out the unknown parse tree to give the total probability that the grammar would generate $s$.

### Step 3: Time Complexity Concern (Why Dynamic Programming is Needed)
A naive implementation must enumerate all $k$ parse trees. For ambiguous grammars, $k$ grows **exponentially** with sentence length $n$. The Inside Algorithm solves this in polynomial time using dynamic programming.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Inside Algorithm (Recurrence)

### Goal:
Compute $P(s)$ efficiently in $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ time, where $n$ is the length of the sentence.

### Definition of Inside Probability:
Let $\alpha(i, j, A)$ denote the **total probability** that the non-terminal $A$ derives the contiguous substring $w_i, w_{i+1}, \ldots, w_j$ of the input sentence.

The overall goal is to compute $\alpha(1, n, S)$, where $S$ is the start symbol.

### Step 1: Base Case (Unary / Lexical Rules)
If $j = i$ (a single-word span) and there is a rule $A \rightarrow w_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(i, i, A) &= P(A \rightarrow w_i) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The probability that $A$ generates the single word $w_i$ is exactly the rule probability of rewriting $A$ directly into that word.

### Step 2: Recursive Case (Binary Rules in CNF)
If the grammar is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** with rules of type $A \rightarrow BC$, then to derive a span $w_i \ldots w_j$, non-terminal $A$ must first split into $B$ and $C$, where $B$ generates the left sub-span and $C$ generates the right sub-span. The split point $k$ is the index of the last word generated by $B$ (i.e., $B$ covers $w_i \ldots w_k$ and $C$ covers $w_{k+1} \ldots w_j$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(i, j, A) &= \sum_{B, C \in N} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The total probability of $A$ deriving $w_i \ldots w_j$ is obtained by:
1. Summing over **all possible pairs** $(B, C)$ that $A$ can split into.
2. Summing over **all possible split points** $k$ between $i$ and $j-1$.
3. Multiplying the rule probability $P(A \rightarrow BC)$ with the inside probabilities of the two children.
4. Multiplying these child probabilities because the left and right sub-derivations are independent.

### Step 3: Final Result
The probability of the entire sentence $s = w_1 \ldots w_n$ is simply the inside probability of the start symbol $S$ spanning the entire sentence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s) &= \alpha(1, n, S) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: The Viterbi Algorithm (Max-Parse)

### Goal:
Find the **single most probable parse tree** $T^{*}$ for sentence $s$, i.e., find:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^{*} &= \arg\max_{T} P(T, s) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Define Viterbi Probability
Let $\beta(i, j, A)$ denote the **maximum** probability of any subtree rooted at non-terminal $A$ that covers the span $w_i \ldots w_j$. Unlike the Inside algorithm (which sums over all subtrees), Viterbi uses the **max** operation to find the single best subtree.

### Step 2: Base Case
$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(i, i, A) &= P(A \rightarrow w_i) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Recursive Case
$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(i, j, A) &= \max_{B, C \in N} \max_{i \leq k < j} \Big[ P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \beta(i, k, B) \cdot \beta(k+1, j, C) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We search over all possible binary splits $(B, C)$ and split points $k$, then take the maximum product of rule probability and child viterbi scores. The maximizing $(B, C, k)$ for each cell is stored in a **backpointer table** so that the final parse tree can be reconstructed by backtracking from $\beta(1, n, S)$.

### Step 4: Best Parse Tree Probability
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T^{*}, s) &= \beta(1, n, S) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Computing Inside Probabilities

Let us compute $P(s)$ for $s = w_1 w_2 w_3 = \text{``the dog runs''}$ using the simplified PCFG:

| **Rule** | **Probability** |
| :--- | :---: |
| $S \rightarrow NP\ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N$ | 0.8 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det\ N\ PP$ | 0.2 |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | 0.5 |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | 0.5 |
| $Det \rightarrow the$ | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow dog$ | 0.5 |
| $N \rightarrow runs$ | 0.3 |
| $V \rightarrow runs$ | 0.7 |

### Step 1: Base Case Computation (Spans of Length 1)
For $i = 1, 2, 3$, we check which non-terminals can generate $w_i$:

- $\alpha(1, 1, Det) = P(Det \rightarrow the) = \mathbf{1.0}$
- $\alpha(2, 2, N) = P(N \rightarrow dog) = \mathbf{0.5}$
- $\alpha(3, 3, N) = P(N \rightarrow runs) = \mathbf{0.3}$
- $\alpha(3, 3, V) = P(V \rightarrow runs) = \mathbf{0.7}$

### Step 2: Spans of Length 2 (Index pairs (1,2) and (2,3))
For span (1,2) covering "the dog", the only viable split is $k = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(1, 2, NP) &= P(NP \rightarrow Det\ N) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, Det) \cdot \alpha(2, 2, N) \\
&= 0.8 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= \mathbf{0.40}
\end{aligned}
$$

For span (2,3) covering "dog runs", no valid CNF rule can produce this span from a single non-terminal, so $\alpha(2, 3, A) = 0$ for all $A$.

### Step 3: Span of Length 3 (Index pair (1,3))
For span (1,3) covering "the dog runs":

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(1, 3, S) &= P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 1, NP) \cdot \alpha(2, 3, VP) \\
&\quad + P(S \rightarrow NP\ VP) \cdot \alpha(1, 2, NP) \cdot \alpha(3, 3, VP) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\alpha(1, 1, NP) = 0$ (no rule for $NP \rightarrow$ "the") and $\alpha(2, 3, VP) = 0$ (no rule for $VP \rightarrow$ "dog runs"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(1, 3, S) &= 1.0 \cdot 0 \cdot 0 + 1.0 \cdot \alpha(1, 2, NP) \cdot \alpha(3, 3, VP) \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 0.7 \\
&= \mathbf{0.28}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The probability of the sentence $s = $ "the dog runs" is $P(s) = \alpha(1, 3, S) = \mathbf{0.28}$. Notice how the algorithm efficiently identified the only valid parse tree and computed its probability without enumerating alternatives.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Inside Algorithm

The following diagram illustrates the data flow and control structure when the Inside Algorithm processes an input sentence in Chomsky Normal Form.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define PCFG `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Model Answer:**
A Probabilistic Context Free Grammar is a 5-tuple $G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $N$ is the set of non-terminals, $\Sigma$ is the set of terminals, $R$ is the set of production rules, $S$ is the start symbol, and $P$ is a probability function assigning a value in $[0, 1]$ to each rule such that for every $A \in N$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

This probability constraint ensures that the model is a valid probabilistic distribution over derivations.

---

### Question 2: What is the Inside Algorithm? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Model Answer:**
The Inside Algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm used to compute the probability that a PCFG generates a given input sentence $s = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$. It defines $\alpha(i, j, A)$ as the probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$. Using the recurrence:

$$\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$$

The probability of the entire sentence is given by $\alpha(1, n, S)$. It runs in $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ time, which is polynomial and far more efficient than enumerating all parse trees.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Every Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students are expected to provide definitions, derivations, examples, and a concluding statement.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the formal definition of a Probabilistic Context Free Grammar. List its components and explain the probability sum constraint with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Model Solution:

A PCFG extends a standard Context Free Grammar by attaching a probability to every production rule. Formally, a PCFG is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$G = (N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$$

Where:
- $N$ = Set of non-terminal symbols (e.g., $\{S, NP, VP, PP, Det, N, V, P\}$)
- $\Sigma$ = Set of terminal symbols (the vocabulary words)
- $R$ = Set of production rules (in CNF or general form)
- $S$ = Start symbol (a distinguished non-terminal, often $S$ for Sentence)
- $P$ = Probability function mapping each rule to a value in $[0, 1]$

**The Probability Sum Constraint:** For every non-terminal $A \in N$, the sum of probabilities of all rules that have $A$ on the left-hand side must equal exactly 1:

$$\sum_{A \rightarrow \beta \ \in R} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

**Example:** Consider a fragment of a PCFG for English:

| **Rule** | **Probability** |
| :--- | :---: |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | 0.30 |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ NP$ | 0.50 |
| $VP \rightarrow V\ PP$ | 0.20 |

Verification: $0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20 = 1.00$ (Sum constraint satisfied).

**Real-World Utility:** This constraint is critical because it allows the PCFG to act as a *valid probabilistic language model*. Without it, the system would either over-generate or under-generate sentences, breaking the mathematical assumptions required for parsing and disambiguation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Inside Algorithm for computing the probability of a sentence in a PCFG. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

#### Model Solution:

The Inside Algorithm computes the total probability $P(s)$ of a sentence $s = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ given a PCFG in Chomsky Normal Form. The central idea is to break the problem into smaller sub-problems and store their solutions in a chart (a 2D table), avoiding redundant computation.

**Step 1: Definition of Inside Probability $\alpha(i, j, A)$**
$\alpha(i, j, A)$ represents the total probability that non-terminal $A$ derives the substring from position $i$ to position $j$ (inclusive) in the sentence.

**Step 2: Initialization (Base Case)**
For all $i$ from 1 to $n$ and all non-terminals $A$:
$$\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$$

**Step 3: Recursive Computation**
For span lengths from 2 to $n$, and for each span $(i, j)$ of that length:
$$\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{B, C \in N} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$$

The inner product works because the left subtree (rooted at $B$) and right subtree (rooted at $C$) derive disjoint parts of the sentence and are statistically independent.

**Step 4: Final Result**
$$P(s) = \alpha(1, n, S)$$

**Complexity:** $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ because there are $O(n^{2})$ table cells, and each cell iterates over $O(n)$ split points, $O(\vert N \vert^{2})$ non-terminal pairs, and multiplies 3 probabilities.

**Diagram (Chart Structure):**
The algorithm fills a triangular chart row by row. The bottom row (spans of length 1) is filled first using lexical rules. Then, the row above (spans of length 2) is filled using the values already computed in the rows below. This continues until the top-right cell $\alpha(1, n, S)$ holds the final answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm and its role in PCFG parsing. Discuss its time complexity. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution:

The CYK algorithm is a **bottom-up dynamic programming parsing algorithm** used to determine whether a given string can be generated by a Context Free Grammar in Chomsky Normal Form, and if so, to find all valid parse trees. It is the algorithmic foundation upon which the Inside and Viterbi algorithms for PCFGs are built.

**Step 1: Pre-requisite — Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**
The input grammar must be in CNF, meaning every rule is of the form:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (two non-terminals on RHS), or
- $A \rightarrow a$ (a single terminal on RHS)

Any CFG can be converted to CNF using standard transformations (unit rule elimination, etc.).

**Step 2: Chart Initialization**
Create a triangular chart $C[i][j]$ of size $n \times n$. Initialize the diagonal ($j = i$) for each position $i$:
$$C[i][i] = \{A \in N : A \rightarrow w_i \text{ is a rule}\}$$

**Step 3: Recursive Filling of Chart**
For span lengths $L = 2$ to $n$:
For each starting position $i$:
For each split point $k$ from $i$ to $i + L - 2$:
- Combine $B \in C[i][k]$ and $C \in C[k+1][i+L-1]$
- For every rule $A \rightarrow BC$, add $A$ to $C[i][i+L-1]$

**Step 4: Membership Test**
The string $s$ is in the language if $S \in C[1][n]$.

**Time Complexity:** The standard CYK algorithm (boolean version) runs in $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$ time, where $\vert R \vert$ is the number of rules. When extended to PCFGs as the Inside algorithm, the complexity becomes $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ because of the summation over all rule probabilities.

**Role in PCFGs:** In PCFG parsing, CYK's chart structure is reused:
- **Inside Algorithm** sums over all derivations (uses CYK structure + SUM).
- **Viterbi Algorithm** takes the maximum (uses CYK structure + MAX).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Viterbi algorithm for finding the most probable parse tree of a sentence in a PCFG. How does it differ from the Inside algorithm? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Model Solution:

The Viterbi algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm used to find the **single most probable parse tree** $T^{*}$ for a given sentence under a PCFG. It is structurally identical to the Inside algorithm but uses the **max** operator instead of the **sum** operator.

**Step 1: Definition of Viterbi Probability $\beta(i, j, A)$**
$\beta(i, j, A)$ = the **maximum** probability among all subtrees rooted at $A$ that generate the span $w_i \ldots w_j$.

**Step 2: Base Case**
$$\beta(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$$

**Step 3: Recursive Case**
For each span $(i, j)$ with $i < j$:
$$\beta(i, j, A) = \max_{B, C \in N} \max_{i \leq k < j} \Big[ P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \beta(i, k, B) \cdot \beta(k+1, j, C) \Big]$$

The Viterbi algorithm also stores **backpointers** — for each cell $(i, j, A)$, it records which $(B, C, k)$ triple achieved the maximum. This allows reconstruction of the best parse tree by backtracking from the top cell.

**Step 4: Optimal Parse Tree**
The probability of the best parse tree is $P(T^{*}) = \beta(1, n, S)$. The tree itself is obtained by recursively following backpointers.

**Comparison Table: Inside vs. Viterbi**

| **Aspect** | **Inside Algorithm** | **Viterbi Algorithm** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Objective** | Compute $P(s)$ (string probability) | Find best parse tree $T^{*}$ |
| **Operator** | Sum over all subtrees | Max over all subtrees |
| **Output** | Single scalar value | Probability + parse tree structure |
| **Storage** | $\alpha(i, j, A)$ only | $\beta(i, j, A)$ + backpointers |
| **Application** | Language modeling, evaluation | Disambiguation, actual parsing |

**Engineering Application:** The Viterbi algorithm is the workhorse of statistical parsers deployed in production NLP systems. When a sentence has multiple valid parse trees, the Viterbi algorithm efficiently selects the one that is most likely to occur in real-world text — directly solving the structural ambiguity problem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Probability Constraint:** Examiners will immediately deduct 2 marks if you define a PCFG but fail to mention that $\sum P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ for each non-terminal.
> 2. **Confusing Inside and Viterbi:** A common mistake is using "sum" in the Viterbi recurrence or "max" in the Inside recurrence. Clearly state the goal of each algorithm before writing its recurrence.
> 3. **Ignoring CNF Requirement:** The CYK, Inside, and Viterbi algorithms require the grammar in **Chomsky Normal Form**. Failing to mention this prerequisite costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not Explaining the "Why":** KTU valuation emphasizes the *intuition* behind the formula, not just the formula. Always write one sentence explaining *why* a product is used (independence) or *why* a sum is used (mutually exclusive alternatives).
> 5. **Skipping the Example:** In 7-mark questions, always provide at least one small worked example with numerical probabilities. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks for the example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PCFG** = CFG + probabilities on every rule. It is a 5-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$.
- **Hard Constraint:** $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ for every non-terminal $A$. This is **non-negotiable** in KTU answers.
- **Parse Tree Probability** = **Product** of all rule probabilities in the tree (due to independence assumption).
- **String Probability** = **Sum** of probabilities of all parse trees that derive the string.
- **Inside Algorithm** computes $P(s)$ in $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert N \vert^{3})$ using dynamic programming.
  - Base case: $\alpha(i, i, A) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$.
  - Recursive case: $\alpha(i, j, A) = \sum_{B, C, k} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \alpha(i, k, B) \cdot \alpha(k+1, j, C)$.
- **Viterbi Algorithm** finds the **most probable parse tree** using **max** instead of sum, with backpointers.
- **CYK Algorithm** is the chart-based parser that serves as the foundation for both Inside and Viterbi.
- **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** is required: rules must be $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$.
- **Structural Ambiguity** is the primary motivation for using PCFGs over plain CFGs.
- **Real-world apps:** Statistical parsing, machine translation, speech recognition, grammar checking.
- **Examiner's hot-spot questions:** The Inside recurrence, the difference between Inside and Viterbi, and the formal 5-tuple definition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project 2: Implementation and Evaluation of Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFG) for Natural Language Parsing using NLTK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Project 2: Implementation and Evaluation of Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFG) for Natural Language Parsing using NLTK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a 5-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where:

- $N$ is a finite set of non-terminal symbols (syntactic categories such as NP, VP, S)
- $\Sigma$ is a finite set of terminal symbols (lexical items / words)
- $R$ is a finite set of production rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in N$ and $\beta \in (N \cup \Sigma)^*$
- $S \in N$ is the distinguished start symbol
- $P$ is a probability function that assigns a probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$ to each rule such that:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1 \quad \forall A \in N$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A PCFG extends a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) by attaching a conditional probability to every production rule. The probability of a complete parse tree $T$ for a sentence $s$ is the product of the probabilities of all rules used in the tree. This allows a parser to rank multiple possible parses and pick the most probable one — the **Viterbi parse**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Grammar as a Recipe Book" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are teaching a child how to bake a cake using a **recipe book**. A normal recipe book says: *"A cake is made of batter and frosting."* — this is a CFG rule.
>
> Now imagine each recipe in the book has a **star rating** (probability) showing how often that recipe is used by experienced bakers. A **chocolate cake recipe** might have a 0.8 star rating, while a **fruit cake recipe** might only have 0.2. When the child bakes a cake, they will most likely pick the highly-rated recipe.
>
> **In PCFG terms:**
> - The **recipe categories** (Cake, Batter, Frosting) = Non-terminals (S, NP, VP)
> - The **ingredients** (flour, sugar, eggs) = Terminals (the words)
> - The **recipes** with star ratings = Production rules with probabilities
> - **Picking the best recipe** = Finding the most probable parse tree
>
> The *parser* is like the child, who reads the recipe book and builds the cake (parse tree) using the most statistically-likely rules.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Cheat Sheet

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Non-terminal** | Syntactic variable / category | `S`, `NP`, `VP`, `PP` |
| **Terminal** | A word (lexical item) | `the`, `cat`, `sat` |
| **Production Rule** | A rewrite rule generating strings | `VP → V NP` |
| **Parse Tree** | A tree showing syntactic structure | Rooted at `S`, leaves are words |
| **Yield** | The sequence of leaves (terminals) | `the cat sat` |
| **Viterbi Parse** | The single most-probable parse | Tree $T^*$ with max $P(T \mid s)$ |
| **CYK Algorithm** | Bottom-up parser for CNF grammars | Cocke-Younger-Kasami |
| **Penn Treebank** | Standard annotated corpus | ~40k sentences, ~950k words |

---

## 1.4 Why PCFG Matters for NLP (Real-World Utility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PCFGs form the **statistical backbone** of constituency parsing in real-world NLP pipelines. They are used in:
> - **Information Extraction** systems (finding subject-predicate-object triples)
> - **Machine Translation** (reordering constituents between languages)
> - **Question Answering** systems (understanding "who did what to whom")
> - **Grammar Checking** tools (detecting ill-formed sentences)
> - **Text Summarization** (identifying salient constituents)
> - Pre-processing for **transformer-based parsers** as feature extractors
>
> Modern parsers like the **Stanford Parser**, **Berkeley Parser**, and **NLTK's `ViterbiParser`** all rely on PCFG foundations.

---

## 1.5 The Big Picture: PCFG Project Workflow

The project flow has **five sequential phases**:

1. **Grammar Definition** → Either write rules by hand or induce from a treebank
2. **Probability Estimation** → Use relative frequency from training data
3. **Parsing** → Use CYK or chart parsing to find best parse
4. **Evaluation** → Compute metrics against gold-standard trees
5. **Analysis & Tuning** → Improve the grammar based on evaluation

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations of PCFG

A PCFG assigns a probability to **every parse tree** $T$ with yield $w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$. If $T$ contains rules $A_i \rightarrow \beta_i$ (with multiplicities), then:

$$P(T, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$$

For a sentence $s$ with multiple parse trees, the **total probability** of generating $s$ is the sum over all valid trees:

$$P(s) = \sum_{T : \text{yield}(T)=s} P(T, s)$$

The **Viterbi parse** (most probable single tree) is:

$$T^{*} = \arg\max_{T} P(T \mid s) = \arg\max_{T} P(T, s)$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction of a PCFG

> [!NOTE]
> **How a PCFG is built from a treebank:**

**Step 1 — Extract all productions:** Walk every tree in the training corpus and collect each `A → β` rule.

**Step 2 — Count occurrences:** For each non-terminal $A$, count how many times each right-hand side $\beta$ occurs.

**Step 3 — Compute MLE probabilities:**

$$P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\sum_{\gamma} \text{Count}(A \rightarrow \gamma)} = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\text{Count}(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Smoothing (optional):** Apply Laplace or Good-Turing smoothing to handle unseen rules and avoid zero probabilities.

**Step 5 — Convert to CNF (Chomsky Normal Form):** Required for CYK. Every rule must be either:
- $A \rightarrow B \, C$ (two non-terminals), OR
- $A \rightarrow w$ (one terminal)

---

## 2.3 Inside and Outside Probabilities

These are the **core dynamic programming quantities** used by the Inside-Outside algorithm for parameter re-estimation.

### Inside Probability $\beta(A, i, j)$

The total probability of generating the substring $w_i w_{i+1} \dots w_j$ from non-terminal $A$:

$$\beta(A, i, j) = P(A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \dots w_j)$$

Recursive definition:

$$\beta(A, i, i) = P(A \rightarrow w_i)$$

$$\beta(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} P(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k+1, j)$$

### Outside Probability $\alpha(A, i, j)$

The total probability of generating the string $w_1 \dots w_{i-1} A w_{j+1} \dots w_n$ from the start symbol $S$:

$$\alpha(A, i, j) = P(S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w_1 \dots w_{i-1} \, A \, w_{j+1} \dots w_n)$$

Recursive definition:

$$\alpha(S, 1, n) = 1$$

$$\alpha(A, i, j) = \sum_{B, C} \sum_{k<j} P(B \rightarrow A\,C) \cdot \alpha(B, 1, k) \cdot \beta(C, k+1, j) + \sum_{B,C} \sum_{k>i} P(B \rightarrow C\,A) \cdot \alpha(B, k, n) \cdot \beta(C, i, k-1)$$

---

## 2.4 Re-estimation Formula (EM / Inside-Outside Update)

After computing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for a sentence $s$, the expected count of rule $A \rightarrow BC$ used in position $(i, j, k)$ is:

$$E[A \rightarrow BC, i, j, k] = \frac{\alpha(A, i, j) \cdot P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \beta(B, i, k) \cdot \beta(C, k+1, j)}{P(s)}$$

The new rule probability estimate:

$$P_{\text{new}}(A \rightarrow BC) = \frac{\sum_{i,j,k} E[A \rightarrow BC, i, j, k]}{\sum_{B',C'} \sum_{i,j,k} E[A \rightarrow B'C', i, j, k]}$$

---

## 2.5 The CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CYK is a **bottom-up chart parsing algorithm** that works in $O(n^3 \cdot |G|)$ time for a sentence of length $n$ and grammar $G$ in CNF.

The dynamic programming table is a 2D array $\text{cell}[i][j]$ storing the set of non-terminals that can derive the span $w_i \dots w_j$.

**Initialization (length 1 spans):**

$$\text{cell}[i][i] = \{A : A \rightarrow w_i \in R\}$$

**Recursion (length $\geq 2$ spans):**

$$\text{cell}[i][j] = \{A : \exists\, B \in \text{cell}[i][k],\, C \in \text{cell}[k+1][j],\, A \rightarrow BC \in R \text{ for some } k \in [i, j-1]\}$$

**Parse extraction:** Backtrack through the table when $S \in \text{cell}[1][n]$.

**Viterbi variant for PCFG:** Replace the union operation with a max + argmax.

---

## 2.6 Evaluation Metrics for Parsers

> [!NOTE]
> **The four standard metrics** used by the Parseval / BLACKABY framework for evaluating parsers against gold trees:

### 2.6.1 Labeled Precision (LP)

The fraction of constituents in the **system's parse** that exactly match a constituent in the **gold parse** (same span, same label):

$$LP = \frac{\text{Number of correct constituents}}{\text{Number of constituents in system parse}}$$

### 2.6.2 Labeled Recall (LR)

The fraction of constituents in the **gold parse** that are correctly produced by the **system**:

$$LR = \frac{\text{Number of correct constituents}}{\text{Number of constituents in gold parse}}$$

### 2.6.3 Labeled F1 Score (Harmonic Mean)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot LP \cdot LR}{LP + LR}$$

### 2.6.4 Crossed Brackets

Number of constituent pairs $(c_1, c_2)$ where the system span and gold span **cross** — i.e., span of $c_1$ is $[i, j]$ and $c_2$ is $[k, l]$ with $i < k < j < l$.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---------|---------|------------|
| 1 | $P(T,s) = \prod_i P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$ | Tree probability | Parse scoring |
| 2 | $P(s) = \sum_{T} P(T,s)$ | Sentence probability | Grammar scoring |
| 3 | $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T,s)$ | Viterbi parse | Best-tree search |
| 4 | $P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\text{Count}(A)}$ | MLE estimation | Grammar induction |
| 5 | $\beta(A,i,j) = \sum_{B,C,k} P(A \rightarrow BC) \cdot \beta(B,i,k) \cdot \beta(C,k+1,j)$ | Inside prob. | Inside-Outside |
| 6 | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot LP \cdot LR}{LP + LR}$ | Eval metric | Parser evaluation |
| 7 | $LP = \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{System constituents}}$ | Precision | Parser evaluation |
| 8 | $LR = \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Gold constituents}}$ | Recall | Parser evaluation |
| 9 | $\text{CrossBrackets} = \mid \{(c_1, c_2) : \text{cross}(sys, gold)\} \mid$ | Bracket cross count | Eval |
| 10 | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | CYK complexity | Parser complexity |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember the **probability axiom constraint**: $\sum_\beta P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ for every non-terminal $A$. This is the most-tested property in KTU exams.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Manual PCFG Construction from a Tiny Treebank

> [!NOTE]
> **Training Data (3 annotated sentences):**

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN cat))
   (VP (VBD sat)
       (PP (IN on)
           (NP (DT the) (NN mat)))))
(S (NP (DT a) (NN dog))
   (VP (VBD barked)))
(S (NP (DT the) (NN dog))
   (VP (VBD sat)))
```

### Step 1 — Extract all production rules and count

| Rule | Count |
|------|-------|
| `S → NP VP` | 3 |
| `NP → DT NN` | 4 |
| `VP → VBD` | 1 |
| `VP → VBD PP` | 2 |
| `PP → IN NP` | 2 |
| `DT → the` | 3 |
| `DT → a` | 1 |
| `NN → cat` | 1 |
| `NN → dog` | 2 |
| `NN → mat` | 1 |
| `VBD → sat` | 2 |
| `VBD → barked` | 1 |
| `IN → on` | 2 |

### Step 2 — Compute MLE probabilities per parent

**For S (Count = 3):**

$$P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0$$

**For NP (Count = 4):**

$$P(NP \rightarrow DT\,NN) = \frac{4}{4} = 1.0$$

**For VP (Count = 3):**

$$P(VP \rightarrow VBD) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$P(VP \rightarrow VBD\,PP) = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**For DT (Count = 4):**

$$P(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

$$P(DT \rightarrow \text{a}) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**For NN (Count = 4):**

$$P(NN \rightarrow \text{cat}) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

$$P(NN \rightarrow \text{dog}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50$$

$$P(NN \rightarrow \text{mat}) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**For VBD (Count = 3):**

$$P(VBD \rightarrow \text{sat}) = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

$$P(VBD \rightarrow \text{barked}) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**For IN (Count = 2):**

$$P(IN \rightarrow \text{on}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

### Step 3 — Verify probability axioms

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_\beta P(S \rightarrow \beta) &= 1.0 \checkmark \\
\sum_\beta P(NP \rightarrow \beta) &= 1.0 \checkmark \\
\sum_\beta P(VP \rightarrow \beta) &= 0.333 + 0.667 = 1.0 \checkmark \\
\sum_\beta P(DT \rightarrow \beta) &= 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.0 \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Tree Probability

**Sentence:** `the cat sat`

**Gold Parse Tree:**

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN cat)) (VP (VBD sat)))
```

### Step-by-step tree probability calculation

Rules used in the tree:
1. `S → NP VP`
2. `NP → DT NN`
3. `VP → VBD`
4. `DT → the`
5. `NN → cat`
6. `VBD → sat`

$$P(T, s) = P(S \rightarrow NP\,VP) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow DT\,NN) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow VBD) \cdot P(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) \cdot P(NN \rightarrow \text{cat}) \cdot P(VBD \rightarrow \text{sat})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P(T, s) &= (1.0) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (0.333) \cdot (0.75) \cdot (0.25) \cdot (0.667) \\
&= 0.333 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.667 \\
&= 0.0417
\end{aligned}$$

So **P(parse tree)** ≈ **0.0417** ≈ **4.17%**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: CYK Parsing on a Tiny Sentence

**Sentence:** `the cat sat` (n = 3 words)

**Grammar in CNF (preterminals separate terminals):**

```
S  → NP VP
NP → DT NN
VP → VBD
VP → VBD PP
PP → IN NP
DT → "the" | "a"
NN → "cat" | "dog" | "mat"
VBD → "sat" | "barked"
IN → "on"
```

### Step 1 — Initialize diagonal cells (length 1 spans)

| Cell | Words | Non-terminals |
|------|-------|---------------|
| `[1,1]` | the | {DT} |
| `[2,2]` | cat | {NN} |
| `[3,3]` | sat | {VBD} |

### Step 2 — Fill length-2 cells

**Cell [1,2]** = words `the cat`. Check all binary rules:
- `NP → DT NN`: DT ∈ [1,1], NN ∈ [2,2] → ✓ Add **NP**

**Cell [2,3]** = words `cat sat`. Check:
- `VP → VBD`: only length-1, skip
- No rule with NN on left in our grammar for "X Y" pattern here → ∅

**Cell [1,2]** = {NP}, **Cell [2,3]** = ∅

### Step 3 — Fill length-3 cell

**Cell [1,3]** = words `the cat sat`. Try splits k=1, k=2:

- **k=1:** Left = [1,1]={DT}, Right = [2,3]=∅ → no rule
- **k=2:** Left = [1,2]={NP}, Right = [3,3]={VBD} → check `S → NP VP`: NP ✓, VP ✓ → Add **S** ✓

### Step 4 — Parse result

Since **S ∈ cell[1,3]**, the sentence is in the language. The unique parse:

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN cat)) (VP (VBD sat)))
```

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Evaluation Metrics Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Gold parse has 8 constituents; system parse has 7 constituents; 6 match exactly.

**Given:**
- Correct constituents (matched) = **6**
- System constituents = **7**
- Gold constituents = **8**
- Crossed bracket pairs = **1**

**Computations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
LP &= \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{System}} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.857 \\
LR &= \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Gold}} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.750 \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot LP \cdot LR}{LP + LR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.857 \cdot 0.750}{0.857 + 0.750} \\
&= \frac{1.286}{1.607} \approx 0.800
\end{aligned}$$

**Crossing Brackets = 1** (lower is better; 0 is perfect).

**Interpretation:** This parser has **80% F1 score** — decent but room for improvement, especially the crossing bracket indicating one structural mismatch.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Parser Algorithms

| Algorithm | Grammar Form | Time Complexity | Returns | Best For |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|---------|----------|
| **CYK** | CNF only | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | All parses / Viterbi | Probabilistic parsing |
| **Earley** | Any CFG | $O(n^3)$ worst, $O(n^2)$ typical | All parses | Left-recursive grammars |
| **Chart Parser** | Any CFG | $O(n^3)$ | All parses | General CFG parsing |
| **Viterbi CKY** | CNF | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Single best | PCFG best-tree |
| **Inside-Outside** | CNF | $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ per iter. | Re-estimated probs | Grammar learning |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Strengths & Weaknesses of PCFG

| Property | PCFG Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|----------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **Probabilistic Ranking** | ✓ Picks best of many parses | Disambiguation works |
| **Context Sensitivity** | ✗ Insufficient | "I saw the man with the telescope" PP-attachment fails |
| **Lexical Dependency** | ✗ Ignored | Same NP structure for different words |
| **Data Efficiency** | ✓ Learns from treebanks | Works on Penn Treebank |
| **Speed** | ✓ Polynomial | CYK runs in seconds on short sentences |
| **Coverage** | Moderate | ~70-80% F1 on WSJ; falls short of neural parsers (~95%) |
