# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: Define a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG). State the two key probability axioms that every PCFG must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** is a 5-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $P$ assigns a probability to each rule $A \rightarrow \beta$.

**Axiom 1 (Rule Probability Constraint):** For every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

**Axiom 2 (Tree Probability):** The probability of a parse tree $T$ with rules $A_i \rightarrow \beta_i$ is:

$$P(T, s) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between CFG and PCFG. Why do we need probabilities in grammars?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CFG | PCFG |
|--------|-----|------|
| Rule format | $A \rightarrow \beta$ | $A \rightarrow \beta$ [with probability $p$] |
| Ambiguity handling | Cannot rank parses | Ranks parses by probability |
| Sentence acceptance | Boolean (yes/no) | Probabilistic (graded) |
| Disambiguation | Requires extra heuristics | Built-in via probabilities |
| Learning | Manual or grammar induction | Statistical from treebanks |

**Why probabilities?** Natural language is highly **ambiguous**. A single sentence can have thousands of valid parses. CFG only tells us *whether* a parse is valid; PCFG tells us *how likely* it is — enabling us to pick the **Viterbi parse** (best parse).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the components of a PCFG. Describe with an example how rule probabilities are estimated from a treebank using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a PCFG in NLTK to parse the sentence *"the cat sat on the mat"*. Show the parser output and the probability of the resulting parse tree. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm** for parsing with a PCFG. Show its application on the sentence *"the dog barked"* using a sample CNF grammar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four standard evaluation metrics used to measure parser performance: **Labeled Precision, Labeled Recall, F1 Score, and Crossing Brackets**. Compute all four metrics for a hypothetical parse where correct constituents = 8, system constituents = 10, gold constituents = 12, and crossed brackets = 2. **(7 marks)**

---

### **5.2.1 Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — PCFG Components & MLE Estimation

**Components of a PCFG:**

1. **$N$ (Non-terminals):** Syntactic categories — `S, NP, VP, PP, DT, NN, VBD, IN`
2. **$\Sigma$ (Terminals):** Lexical items — `the, cat, sat, on, mat, ...`
3. **$R$ (Rules):** Rewrite rules — `S → NP VP`, `NP → DT NN`
4. **$S$ (Start symbol):** The distinguished root — typically `S` for sentence
5. **$P$ (Probability function):** $P: R \rightarrow [0, 1]$

**MLE Estimation Example:**

Given treebank sentences:

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN cat)) (VP (VBD sat)))
(S (NP (DT a) (NN dog)) (VP (VBD barked)))
```

**Count rules:**

$$\text{Count}(VP) = 2, \quad \text{Count}(VP \rightarrow VBD) = 2$$

$$P(VP \rightarrow VBD) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

$$\text{Count}(DT) = 2, \quad \text{Count}(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) = 1, \quad \text{Count}(DT \rightarrow \text{a}) = 1$$

$$P(DT \rightarrow \text{the}) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5, \quad P(DT \rightarrow \text{a}) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**General MLE Formula:**

$$P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\sum_{\gamma} \text{Count}(A \rightarrow \gamma)}$$

#### Part (b) — NLTK Implementation

```python
import nltk
from nltk import PCFG, ViterbiParser

# Step 1: Define the PCFG with probabilities
grammar = PCFG.fromstring("""
    S   -> NP VP          [1.0]
    NP  -> DT NN          [1.0]
    VP  -> VBD            [0.4]
    VP  -> VBD PP         [0.6]
    PP  -> IN NP          [1.0]
    DT  -> 'the'          [0.7]
    DT  -> 'a'            [0.3]
    NN  -> 'cat'          [0.3]
    NN  -> 'mat'          [0.3]
    NN  -> 'dog'          [0.4]
    VBD -> 'sat'          [0.6]
    VBD  -> 'barked'      [0.4]
    IN  -> 'on'           [1.0]
""")

# Step 2: Initialize Viterbi parser
parser = ViterbiParser(grammar)

# Step 3: Parse the sentence
sentence = "the cat sat on the mat".split()

# Step 4: Get best parse
for tree in parser.parse(sentence):
    print(tree)
    tree.pretty_print()
    print(f"\nProbability of parse tree: {tree.prob():.6f}")
    print(f"Log probability: {tree.logprob():.4f}")
```

**Expected Output:**

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN cat))
   (VP (VBD sat)
       (PP (IN on)
           (NP (DT the) (NN mat))))) (p=0.008122)

Probability of parse tree: 0.008122
Log probability: -4.8132
```

**Probability computation:**

$$P = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.3 \approx 0.00812$$

---

### **5.2.2 Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — CYK Algorithm

**CYK Steps:**

1. **Convert grammar to CNF:** Every rule must be $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$
2. **Build the chart:** Triangular table of size $n \times n$
3. **Initialize diagonal** with preterminals for each word
4. **Fill upward:** For length $\ell = 2$ to $n$, combine cells from $\ell-1$ splits
5. **Check root cell:** $S$ in cell[1][n] means parse succeeded

**Sample CNF Grammar:**

```
S  -> NP VP
NP -> DT NN
VP -> VBD
VP -> VBD PP
PP -> IN NP
DT -> 'the' | 'a'
NN -> 'dog'
VBD -> 'barked'
IN -> 'on'
```

**Sentence:** `the dog barked`

**CYK Table:**

| Span | Words | Non-terminals |
|------|-------|---------------|
| [1,1] | the | {DT} |
| [2,2] | dog | {NN} |
| [3,3] | barked | {VBD} |
| [1,2] | the dog | {NP} (from DT NN) |
| [2,3] | dog barked | {VP} (from NN VBD? No — only VBD PP) → ∅ ... or VP from `VP → VBD` doesn't apply since VBD is unary... actually |

> For this exact grammar, the parse requires **unary chaining**: `VP → VBD` is a unary rule. In strict CNF, unary chains need to be collapsed.

After collapsing unary chains (VP becomes synonymous with VBD):

| Span | Words | Non-terminals |
|------|-------|---------------|
| [3,3] | barked | {VBD, VP} |
| [2,3] | dog barked | — (no rule uses NN VBD) |
| [1,3] | the dog barked | S if NP in [1,2] and VP in [3,3] ✓ |

**Final parse:**

```
(S (NP (DT the) (NN dog)) (VP (VBD barked)))
```

#### Part (b) — Evaluation Metrics Computation

**Given:** Correct = 8, System = 10, Gold = 12, Crossed = 2

**Labeled Precision (LP):**

$$LP = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Labeled Recall (LR):**

$$LR = \frac{8}{12} = 0.6\overline{6} = 66.67\%$$

**F1 Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot LP \cdot LR}{LP + LR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.667}{0.80 + 0.667} = \frac{1.0667}{1.467} \approx 0.7272 = 72.72\%$$

**Crossing Brackets = 2** (out of $\binom{10}{2} = 45$ pairs; lower is better).

**Interpretation:** The parser achieves **72.72% F1** — moderate performance. The 2 crossed brackets indicate structural disagreement on PP-attachment or coordination.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the probability axiom check.** Many students compute individual rule probabilities but forget to verify $\sum_\beta P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$. Always show this verification — it's a **2-mark differentiator**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $P(T \mid s)$ with $P(T, s)$.** They are equal when $P(s)$ is constant, but students should state this assumption explicitly.
>
> 3. **Using CYK without CNF conversion.** If your grammar has rules like $A \rightarrow B\, C\, D$ or $A \rightarrow w_1 w_2$, CYK will fail silently. Always show the CNF conversion step.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall.** Precision uses **system** as denominator; Recall uses **gold** as denominator. Mnemonic: *"P-rediction-iction = system; R-ecall = gold"*.
>
> 5. **In NLTK, forgetting `.split()` on sentences.** Passing a string to `parser.parse()` throws an error. Always tokenize first.
>
> 6. **Not handling unknown words.** A test sentence with a word not in the grammar causes CYK to return no parse. Always include a fallback or unknown-word rule.
>
> 7. **Computing F1 incorrectly.** Use the harmonic mean — not arithmetic mean. Arithmetic mean would give $(0.8 + 0.667)/2 = 0.733$, which is **wrong**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **PCFG** = CFG + probability per rule. Five-tuple $(N, \Sigma, R, S, P)$.
> - **Probability Axiom:** $\sum_\beta P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ for every non-terminal $A$.
> - **Tree Probability:** $P(T, s) = \prod_i P(A_i \rightarrow \beta_i)$.
> - **Viterbi Parse:** $T^* = \arg\max_T P(T, s)$ — the single best parse.
> - **MLE Estimation:** $P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \frac{\text{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\text{Count}(A)}$.
> - **CYK Algorithm:** Bottom-up, requires CNF, $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$, returns all parses or best.
> - **CNF:** Rules are either $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (one terminal).
> - **Inside Probability** $\beta(A, i, j)$ = prob. of generating span $w_i \dots w_j$ from $A$.
> - **Outside Probability** $\alpha(A, i, j)$ = prob. of $S$ deriving $w_1 \dots A \dots w_n$.
> - **Inside-Outside Algorithm** uses $\alpha, \beta$ for EM-style re-estimation of rule probabilities.
> - **Evaluation Metrics:** Labeled Precision (system), Labeled Recall (gold), F1 (harmonic mean), Crossed Brackets.
> - **NLTK classes to know:** `PCFG.fromstring()`, `ViterbiParser()`, `InsideChartParser()`, `tree.prob()`, `tree.logprob()`.
> - **Penn Treebank** is the standard training corpus (~40K sentences, WSJ section).
> - **Real-world parsers:** Stanford Parser, Berkeley Parser, NLTK's built-in parsers.
> - **Limitation:** Vanilla PCFGs cannot model lexical dependencies (e.g., PP-attachment, coordination) — modern extensions use lexicalized PCFGs (Collins, Charniak).

---

## 5.5 Complete NLTK Project Code Skeleton (For Reference)

```python
"""
PCFG Implementation and Evaluation using NLTK
Project 2 - PEADT635 NLP - KTU 2024 Scheme
"""

import nltk
from nltk import PCFG, ViterbiParser, InsideChartParser
from nltk.corpus import treebank
from nltk.parse import pchart
from nltk.metrics import precision, recall, f_measure

# ---- STEP 1: Load treebank data ----
sentences = treebank.tagged_sents()[:100]
test_sents = treebank.tagged_sents()[100:200]

# ---- STEP 2: Extract productions and build PCFG ----
productions = []
for sent in treebank.parsed_sents()[:100]:
    productions += sent.productions()

# ---- STEP 3: Induce PCFG from productions ----
grammar = nltk.induce_pcfg(Nonterminal('S'), productions)

# ---- STEP 4: Initialize parser ----
parser = ViterbiParser(grammar)

# ---- STEP 5: Parse and evaluate ----
correct = system = gold = 0
crossed = 0

for i, sent in enumerate(test_sents):
    tokens = [w for (w, t) in sent]
    gold_tree = treebank.parsed_sents()[100 + i]
    
    sys_parses = list(parser.parse(tokens))
    if not sys_parses:
        continue
    sys_tree = sys_parses[0]
    
    # Extract constituents as (label, start, end) tuples
    gold_cons = set(gold_tree.constituents())
    sys_cons = set(sys_tree.constituents())
    
    correct += len(gold_cons & sys_cons)
    system += len(sys_cons)
    gold += len(gold_cons)
    
    # Count crossed brackets (simplified)
    for gc in gold_cons:
        for sc in sys_cons:
            if cross(gc, sc):
                crossed += 1

# ---- STEP 6: Compute metrics ----
LP = correct / system if system else 0
LR = correct / gold if gold else 0
F1 = 2 * LP * LR / (LP + LR) if (LP + LR) else 0

print(f"Labeled Precision: {LP:.4f}")
print(f"Labeled Recall:    {LR:.4f}")
print(f"F1 Score:          {F1:.4f}")
print(f"Crossed Brackets:  {crossed}")
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key NLTK Function Quick Reference:**
>
> | Function | Purpose |
> |----------|---------|
> | `PCFG.fromstring(rules)` | Build PCFG from a string of rules |
> | `ViterbiParser(grammar)` | Returns the single best parse |
> | `InsideChartParser(grammar)` | Returns all parses with inside probabilities |
> | `tree.prob()` | Returns tree probability |
> | `tree.logprob()` | Returns log probability |
> | `nltk.induce_pcfg(start, prods)` | Induce PCFG from productions |
> | `tree.constituents()` | Returns set of `(label, start, end)` tuples |
> | `treebank.parsed_sents()` | Returns parsed sentences from Penn Treebank |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantics and Logical Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semantics and Logical Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Semantics in NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantics** is the branch of linguistics and NLP concerned with the *literal, context-independent meaning* of linguistic expressions — words, phrases, sentences, and entire discourses. It maps syntactic structures (parse trees) to **logical representations** (logical forms) that can be evaluated against a world model to determine truth conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, semantics is the third tier of linguistic analysis, sitting between **Syntax** (structural arrangement) and **Pragmatics** (speaker intent / conversational context).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Logical Form (LF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Form (LF)** is the meaning representation language — typically a variant of **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)**, **Lambda Calculus**, or **Discourse Representation Structures (DRS)** — into which a natural language sentence is translated after syntactic parsing, so that a reasoning engine or model-theoretic interpreter can manipulate it.

LF strips away the surface ambiguity of natural language and exposes the underlying *propositional skeleton* that a sentence asserts about the world.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of semantics as **translating a WhatsApp message from Malayalam to a universally understood "thinking language."** You don't need to know Malayalam to understand the *meaning* of the message once it's been translated.

- **Syntax** is the *grammar check* that tells you the message is well-formed ("Mwan aache" → "Man ache" with correct subject-verb order).
- **Semantics** is the *translator* that converts the well-formed sentence into pure logic: `∃x (Man(x) ∧ Ache(x))` — "There exists some x such that x is a man and x is good."
- **Pragmatics** is the *friend who interprets sarcasm* — figuring out whether "great job" means genuinely good or passive-aggressive.

The **Logical Form** is the final translated statement — a clean, unambiguous, mathematical snapshot of what the sentence asserts, ready to be checked against the real world.

## 1.4 The Three-Tier Linguistic Stack

| Tier | Concern | Example for "All students sleep." |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | Structure | `[S [NP All students] [VP sleep]]` |
| **Semantics** | Literal meaning | `∀x (Student(x) → Sleep(x))` |
| **Pragmatics** | Speaker intent | "S1 is telling S2 to stop making noise" |

## 1.5 Why Semantics and LF Matter for Engineers

- **Information Retrieval**: Google translates your query to LF to match documents by meaning, not just keywords.
- **Question Answering**: IBM Watson parses "Who founded Apple?" into `∃x (Found(x, Apple))` and queries a knowledge base.
- **Machine Translation**: Google Translate uses semantic intermediates to bridge languages.
- **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants**: Alexa maps "Set alarm for 7 AM" to `SetAlarm(07:00)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight**: The 2024 Scheme emphasises that students must understand the *pipeline*: **Sentence → Parse Tree → Logical Form → Model-theoretic Interpretation → Truth Value**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Semantics Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The computational process of converting English text to a meaningful logical form follows a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Tokenization & Lemmatization** — The input sentence is broken into canonical word forms (e.g., "ran" → "run").
2. **Syntactic Parsing** — A CFG parser (e.g., CKY, Earley, or a PCFG) produces a parse tree. The parser uses grammar rules of the form `S → NP VP`.
3. **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)** — For each constituent, identify the predicate and its arguments (Agent, Patient, Instrument, Goal, etc.).
4. **Lexical Semantics Mapping** — Each terminal word is mapped to a semantic primitive or predicate symbol using a **lexicon** (e.g., "give" → `GIVE(x, y, z)`).
5. **Compositionality Application** — The semantics of larger constituents is built from the semantics of smaller ones using **lambda calculus** and **functional application**.
6. **Quantifier Scoping** — Resolve the scope of quantifiers (`∀`, `∃`) using rules like *Cooper Storage* or *Hobbs-Shieber*.
7. **Logical Form Output** — The final well-formed formula in FOPL or a similar language is emitted as the LF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters for KTU exams**: Question setters love asking "Trace the semantic interpretation of a sentence step by step." Marks are awarded at *each* stage.

## 2.2 Compositionality Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frege's Principle of Compositionality (1892)**: *The meaning of a complex expression is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.*

This single principle is the **backbone of formal semantics**. It is the reason we can mechanically build the meaning of "every tall student" from the meanings of "every," "tall," and "student."

## 2.3 Lexical Semantics: From Words to Predicates

Every content word (noun, verb, adjective, adverb, preposition) is assigned a **semantic type** in the typed lambda calculus:

| Word Class | Semantic Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Common Noun (CN) | `e → t` | `dog` → λx. Dog(x) |
| Proper Noun (PN) | `e` | `John` → j |
| Intransitive Verb (IV) | `e → t` | `run` → λx. Run(x) |
| Transitive Verb (TV) | `(e → t) → e → t` | `love` → λy.λx. Love(x, y) |
| Adjective (Adj) | `(e → t) → (e → t)` | `red` → λP.λx. P(x) ∧ Red(x) |

Where:
- `e` = entity type
- `t` = truth value type (True/False)

## 2.4 Lambda Calculus — The Engine of Composition

**Lambda notation** (invented by Alonzo Church, 1936) is the formal mechanism for building complex meanings from simple ones.

### 2.4.1 Lambda Abstraction

$$\lambda x . P(x)$$

Reads as: *"the function that takes an x and returns the property that x has P."*

### 2.4.2 Function Application

If `f = λx. P(x)` and `a` is an entity, then `f(a) = P(a)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application is written `f(a)` and is evaluated by β-reduction**: replace every bound occurrence of the variable in the body with the argument. This is the **only computation rule** in pure lambda calculus.

### 2.4.3 β-Reduction (Beta Reduction)

$$(\lambda x . M) N \;\Rightarrow_{\beta}\; M[x := N]$$

Read: *"The application of the function λx.M to the argument N reduces to M with every free x replaced by N."*

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **every formula/rule** you need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Notation / Rule | Formal Form | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lexicon for Intransitive Verb | `[[run]] = λx. Run(x)` | Type: `e → t` |
| 2 | Lexicon for Transitive Verb | `[[love]] = λy.λx. Love(x,y)` | Type: `(e→t) → e → t` |
| 3 | Lexicon for Common Noun | `[[dog]] = λx. Dog(x)` | Type: `e → t` |
| 4 | Lexicon for Proper Noun | `[[John]] = j` | Type: `e` |
| 5 | Lexicon for Adjective | `[[red]] = λP.λx. P(x) ∧ Red(x)` | Type: `(e→t) → (e→t)` |
| 6 | Subject NP Rule | `[[NP Det N]] = [[Det]]([[N]])` | Combine determiner with noun |
| 7 | VP Rule (Intransitive) | `[[VP V]] = [[V]]` | Direct application |
| 8 | VP Rule (Transitive) | `[[VP V NP]] = [[V]]([[NP]])` | Apply verb to its object |
| 9 | S Rule | `[[S NP VP]] = [[VP]]([[NP]])` | Apply VP's function to subject |
| 10 | Quantifier ∀ | `[[every]] = λP.λQ. ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))` | Universal quantifier |
| 11 | Quantifier ∃ | `[[a]] = λP.λQ. ∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(x))` | Existential quantifier |
| 12 | β-Reduction | `(λx. M) N ⇒β M[x := N]` | Function application |
| 13 | α-Conversion | `λx. M = λy. M[x := y]` | Rename bound variables |
| 14 | Truth Condition | `[[S]]^M = 1` iff `M ⊨ [[S]]` | Model-theoretic evaluation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap**: The vertical pipe `|` is **forbidden inside markdown table cells** by the engine. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., `P \vert x` instead of `P \vert x`) when writing set/cardinality notation in tables.

## 2.6 Model-Theoretic Semantics

A **model** `M = ⟨D, I⟩` consists of:
- `D` = the **domain of discourse** (a non-empty set of entities being talked about)
- `I` = the **interpretation function** that maps:
  - Each constant `c` to an element `I(c) ∈ D`
  - Each n-ary predicate `P` to a subset `I(P) ⊆ D^n`

> [!NOTE]
> A sentence is **true in M** (written `M ⊨ S`) iff its logical form evaluates to **1** under the interpretation `I`. A sentence is **entailed** by a knowledge base KB iff every model that satisfies KB also satisfies S.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Semantics & LF |
|---|---|
| **Database Querying (NLIDB)** | "Show all employees earning more than 50k" → `SELECT * FROM Emp WHERE Salary > 50000` via LF translation |
| **Robotics** | "Pick up the red ball" → `∃x (Ball(x) ∧ Red(x) ∧ PickUp(Robot, x))` |
| **Legal AI (e.g., Indian Kanoon)** | Case summaries translated to LF for case-based reasoning |
| **Medical NLP (ICD coding)** | Discharge summaries mapped to formal medical predicates for diagnosis |
| **Cybersecurity (Phishing Detection)** | Email body LF checked against attack-pattern predicates |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Simple Sentence LF

**Input:** "John runs."

**Step 1 — Lexical Lookup:**

$$[[\text{runs}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Run}(x)$$

$$[[\text{John}]] = j$$

**Step 2 — Syntactic Combination (S = NP VP):**

$$[[S]] = [[VP]]([[NP]])$$

**Step 3 — Apply VP rule to intransitive V:**

$$[[\text{runs John}]] = [[\text{runs}]]([[\text{John}]])$$

$$= (\lambda x.\; \text{Run}(x))(j)$$

**Step 4 — β-Reduction:** Replace `x` with `j` in the body:

$$(\lambda x.\; \text{Run}(x))(j) \;\Rightarrow_{\beta}\; \text{Run}(j)$$

**Step 5 — Final Logical Form:**

$$\boxed{\text{Run}(j)}$$

This reads: *"The property 'Run' holds of the individual j (John)."*

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Transitive Sentence with Quantifier

**Input:** "Every student loves a book."

**Step 1 — Lexicon:**

$$[[\text{student}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Student}(x)$$

$$[[\text{book}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Book}(x)$$

$$[[\text{loves}]] = \lambda y.\lambda x.\; \text{Loves}(x, y)$$

$$[[\text{every}]] = \lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$$

$$[[\text{a}]] = \lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (P(x) \wedge Q(x))$$

**Step 2 — Compose the Noun Phrases (Object NP first):**

$$[[\text{a book}]] = [[\text{a}]]([[\text{book}]])$$

$$= (\lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (P(x) \wedge Q(x)))\; (\lambda x.\; \text{Book}(x))$$

**Step 3 — β-Reduction (substitute the book-predicate for P):**

$$= \lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (\text{Book}(x) \wedge Q(x))$$

This is now a function waiting for a verb phrase's meaning.

**Step 4 — Compose the Verb Phrase with the object NP:**

$$[[\text{loves a book}]] = [[\text{loves}]]([[\text{a book}]])$$

$$= (\lambda y.\lambda x.\; \text{Loves}(x, y))\; (\lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (\text{Book}(x) \wedge Q(x)))$$

**Step 5 — β-Reduction:** Substitute `λQ. ∃x (Book(x) ∧ Q(x))` for `y`:

$$= \lambda x.\; \text{Loves}\bigl(x,\; \lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (\text{Book}(x) \wedge Q(x))\bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Build Subject NP:**

$$[[\text{every student}]] = [[\text{every}]]([[\text{student}]])$$

$$= (\lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)))\; (\lambda x.\; \text{Student}(x))$$

**Step 7 — β-Reduction:**

$$= \lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$$

**Step 8 — Apply S-rule: `[[S]] = [[VP]]([[NP]])`**

$$[[S]] = (\lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow Q(x)))\; (\lambda x.\; \text{Loves}(x, \lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (\text{Book}(x) \wedge Q(x))))$$

**Step 9 — Final β-Reduction:** Substitute the loves-predicate for `Q`:

$$= \forall x\; \bigl(\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Loves}\bigl(x,\; \lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (\text{Book}(x) \wedge Q(x))\bigr)\bigr)$$

**Step 10 — Final cleanup of the inner lambda (apply to the identity of being-loved):**

$$\boxed{\forall x\; \bigl(\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y\; (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Loves}(x, y))\bigr)}$$

**Reading**: *"For every x, if x is a student, then there exists a y such that y is a book and x loves y."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Conjunction and Negation

**Input:** "Sita does not sing and dance."

**Lexicon:**

$$[[\text{not}]] = \lambda p.\; \neg p$$

$$[[\text{and}]] = \lambda p.\lambda q.\; p \wedge q$$

$$[[\text{sing}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x)$$

$$[[\text{dance}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Dance}(x)$$

**Step 1 — Combine `sing` and `dance` with `and`:**

$$[[\text{sing and dance}]] = [[\text{and}]]([[\text{sing}]])([[\text{dance}]])$$

$$= (\lambda p.\lambda q.\; p \wedge q)(\lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x))(\lambda x.\; \text{Dance}(x))$$

**Step 2 — Sequential β-reductions:**

$$= (\lambda q.\; (\lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x)) \wedge q)(\lambda x.\; \text{Dance}(x))$$

$$= (\lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x)) \wedge (\lambda x.\; \text{Dance}(x))$$

$$= \lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x) \wedge \text{Dance}(x)$$

**Step 3 — Apply negation:**

$$[[\text{not (sing and dance)}]] = [[\text{not}]]([[\text{sing and dance}]])$$

$$= (\lambda p.\; \neg p)(\lambda x.\; \text{Sing}(x) \wedge \text{Dance}(x))$$

**Step 4 — Final β-reduction:**

$$\boxed{\neg(\text{Sing}(s) \wedge \text{Dance}(s))}$$

**Reading**: *"It is not the case that Sita sings and Sita dances."*

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, you will be awarded **partial credit for showing the intermediate β-reduction steps** even if your final substitution contains a minor variable-clash error. Always show the *full chain* of `(λx. M)N ⇒β M[x:=N]` rewrites.

## 3.4 Scope Ambiguity — A Critical KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sentence **"Every student read a book."** has **two valid logical forms** depending on quantifier scope:

### Scope Reading 1: `∀ > ∃` (Universal Wide Scope)

$$\forall x\; (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y\; (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x, y)))$$

**Reading**: *"Every student read some book (possibly different for each)."* — The existential quantifier falls *inside* the universal.

### Scope Reading 2: `∃ > ∀` (Existential Wide Scope)

$$\exists y\; (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x\; (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x, y)))$$

**Reading**: *"There is one specific book that every student read."* — The existential quantifier *takes over* the entire sentence.

> [!WARNING]
> Both are grammatically valid. Resolving which one is intended requires **pragmatics** (e.g., world knowledge, stress, context) — semantics alone is *underspecified*.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Truth Conditions

| LF Formula | True When... | False When... |
|---|---|---|
| `Run(j)` | John is in the running-set of the model | John is not running |
| `∀x (Student(x) → Sleep(x))` | Every entity in the domain that is a student also sleeps | At least one student is not sleeping |
| `∃x (Student(x) ∧ Smart(x))` | At least one entity is *both* a student and smart | No such entity exists |
| `¬P ∨ Q` | P is false OR Q is true (or both) | P is true AND Q is false |
| `P → Q` | P is false OR Q is true | P is true AND Q is false |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Semantics Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram depicts the full computational pipeline from raw English text to evaluated truth conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw English Text] --> B[Tokenizer and Lemmatizer]
    B --> C[POS Tagger]
    C --> D[CFG or PCFG Parser]
    D --> E[Parse Tree]
    E --> F[Lexicon Lookup]
    F --> G[Semantic Composition via Lambda Calculus]
    G --> H[Quantifier Scope Resolution]
    H --> I[Logical Form in FOPL]
    I --> J[Model Theoretic Interpreter]
    K[Domain D and Interpretation I] --> J
    J --> L[Truth Value True or False]

    nodeA[Input]:::in
    nodeL[Output]:::out

    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class A nodeA
    class L nodeL
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K mid
```

## 4.2 Parse Tree to Logical Form Mapping

The diagram below shows how each node in a syntactic parse tree maps to a typed lambda expression, and how functional application at the top yields the final LF.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nS[S] --> nNP1[NP Subject]
    nS --> nVP[VP]

    nNP1 --> nDet1[Det: every]
    nNP1 --> nN1[N: student]

    nVP --> nV[V: loves]
    nVP --> nNP2[NP Object]

    nNP2 --> nDet2[Det: a]
    nNP2 --> nN2[N: book]

    nDet1 -.lex.-> s1[lambda P lambda Q forall x Px implies Qx]
    nN1 -.lex.-> s2[lambda x Student x]
    nDet2 -.lex.-> s3[lambda P lambda Q exists x Px and Qx]
    nN2 -.lex.-> s4[lambda x Book x]
    nV -.lex.-> s5[lambda y lambda x Loves x y]

    nNP1 -.compose.-> s6[lambda Q forall x Student x implies Qx]
    nNP2 -.compose.-> s7[lambda Q exists x Book x and Qx]
    nVP -.compose.-> s8[lambda x Loves x and lambda Q exists x Book x and Qx]
    nS -.compose.-> s9[forall x Student x implies exists y Book y and Loves x y]

    classDef treeNode fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef lexNode fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef compNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef finalNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000

    class nS,nNP1,nVP,nDet1,nN1,nV,nNP2,nDet2,nN2 treeNode
    class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5 lexNode
    class s6,s7,s8 compNode
    class s9 finalNode
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Meaning Representation

This diagram shows the abstraction hierarchy — the higher you go, the more abstract and computationally tractable the meaning becomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Surface Text: Every student loves a book] --> L1[Tokenized: every student loves a book]
    L1 --> L2[POS Tagged: every DT student NN loves VBZ a DT book NN]
    L2 --> L3[Parse Tree: S above NP VP structure]
    L3 --> L4[Lambda Expression: lambda Q forall x Student x implies Qx applied to loves predicate]
    L4 --> L5[Logical Form: forall x Student x implies exists y Book y and Loves x y]
    L5 --> L6[Model Checked Truth: True or False in World M]

    classDef lvl0 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000000
    classDef lvl3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef lvl4 fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    classDef lvl5 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,color:#000000
    classDef lvl6 fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000000

    class L0 lvl0
    class L1 lvl1
    class L2 lvl2
    class L3 lvl3
    class L4 lvl4
    class L5 lvl5
    class L6 lvl6
```
