# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A carries 3 marks, Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two questions. The mark split within Part B sub-parts is 7 + 7.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **Syntax, Semantics, and Pragmatics** with one example sentence for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Concern | Example for "Can you pass the salt?" |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | Grammatical structure | Subject "you", auxiliary "can", verb "pass", object "the salt" — well-formed |
| **Semantics** | Literal meaning | *Is the listener physically able to pass the salt?* — a yes/no question about ability |
| **Pragmatics** | Speaker intent | A polite *request* to hand over the salt, not an inquiry about physical capability |

**Awarding marks:** 1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for distinctions, 1 mark for the cohesive example.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q:** Define **Principle of Compositionality** and state its significance in NLP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Compositionality (Frege, 1892)**: *The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them.*

**Significance in NLP** (2 marks):
1. Allows **mechanical, bottom-up construction** of meaning from a parse tree using lambda calculus.
2. Makes semantic interpretation **algorithmic and tractable** — essential for chatbots, QA systems, and machine translation.
3. Provides a **mathematical foundation** (typed lambda calculus, model theory) that connects linguistics to formal logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **typed lambda calculus approach to compositional semantics** with suitable lexicon rules for common nouns, proper nouns, intransitive verbs, transitive verbs, adjectives, and quantifiers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the following English sentences into **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** logical forms:
  1. "Every dog barks."
  2. "Some boy gave a book to Mary."
  3. "Ravi did not eat the mango."
  4. "If it rains, the match is cancelled." **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **typed lambda calculus** is a formal language for representing meaning in which every expression is assigned a **type** (most commonly `e` for entities and `t` for truth values). Lexicon rules assign typed semantic values to terminal symbols:

**1. Proper Noun (Type: e)**

$$[[\text{John}]] = j$$

**2. Common Noun (Type: e → t)**

$$[[\text{dog}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Dog}(x)$$

**3. Intransitive Verb (Type: e → t)**

$$[[\text{barks}]] = \lambda x.\; \text{Bark}(x)$$

**4. Transitive Verb (Type: e → (e → t))**

$$[[\text{loves}]] = \lambda y.\lambda x.\; \text{Loves}(x, y)$$

**5. Adjective (Type: (e → t) → (e → t))**

$$[[\text{red}]] = \lambda P.\lambda x.\; P(x) \wedge \text{Red}(x)$$

**6. Universal Quantifier (Type: (e → t) → (e → t) → t)**

$$[[\text{every}]] = \lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$$

**7. Existential Quantifier (Type: (e → t) → (e → t) → t)**

$$[[\text{some}]] = \lambda P.\lambda Q.\; \exists x\; (P(x) \wedge Q(x))$$

**Composition Rules:**

$$[[S \rightarrow NP\; VP]] \;:\; [[S]] = [[VP]]([[NP]])$$

$$[[VP \rightarrow V\; NP]] \;:\; [[VP]] = [[V]]([[NP]])$$

$$[[NP \rightarrow Det\; N]] \;:\; [[NP]] = [[Det]]([[N]])$$

These rules are applied **bottom-up** from the leaves of the parse tree to yield a fully composed logical form. The **β-reduction** `(λx. M)N ⇒β M[x:=N]` performs the function-application step.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Sentence 1: "Every dog barks."**

Lexical: `[[every]] = λP.λQ. ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`, `[[dog]] = λx. Dog(x)`, `[[barks]] = λx. Bark(x)`

Build `[[every dog]]`:

$$= \lambda Q.\; \forall x\; (\text{Dog}(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$$

Apply to `[[barks]]` via `[[S]] = [[VP]]([[NP])` and β-reduce:

$$\boxed{\forall x\; (\text{Dog}(x) \rightarrow \text{Bark}(x))}$$

*Reading: For every x, if x is a dog then x barks.*

---

**Sentence 2: "Some boy gave a book to Mary."**

Treat "give" as a 3-place predicate `Give(x, y, z)` = x gave y to z. Prepositional phrase is converted to a third argument.

$$\boxed{\exists x\; (\text{Boy}(x) \wedge \exists y\; (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Give}(x, y, m)))\ }$$

*Reading: There is some x who is a boy and some y which is a book such that x gave y to Mary.*

---

**Sentence 3: "Ravi did not eat the mango."**

Treat "the" as a unique-existential presupposition. `[[not]] = λp. ¬p`.

$$\boxed{\neg \text{Eat}(r, m)}$$

where `r` = Ravi and `m` = the unique mango. (Full lexical form: `¬(Eat(r, m))`.)

---

**Sentence 4: "If it rains, the match is cancelled."**

Treat "if P, then Q" as `P → Q` (material conditional).

$$\boxed{\text{Rain} \rightarrow \text{Cancelled}(\text{match})}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** What is a **model** in model-theoretic semantics? Explain the **interpretation function** and the **truth-conditional evaluation** of the sentence "Every bird flies" with respect to a sample domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **scope ambiguity** in the sentence "Every farmer owns a donkey." Show the two possible logical forms and explain which pragmatic factors resolve the ambiguity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of a Model:**

> A model in formal semantics is a mathematical structure `M = ⟨D, I⟩` that provides a concrete "world" against which logical forms are evaluated for truth.

- `D` is the **Domain of Discourse** — a non-empty set of all entities being talked about.
- `I` is the **Interpretation Function** — a mapping:
  - Each constant `c` → an element `I(c) ∈ D`
  - Each n-ary predicate `P` → a subset `I(P) ⊆ D^n`
  - Each function symbol `f` → a function `I(f) : D^n → D`

**Sample Domain for "Every bird flies":**

Let `D = {Tweety, Woody, Eagle1, Rock1}` (where Rock1 is in the domain but is not a bird).

Interpretation:
- `I(Bird) = {Tweety, Woody, Eagle1}`
- `I(Fly) = {Tweety, Woody}` (note: Eagle1 does not fly, Rock1 does not fly)

**Logical Form of the sentence:**

$$[[\text{Every bird flies}]] = \forall x\; (\text{Bird}(x) \rightarrow \text{Fly}(x))$$

**Truth-Conditional Evaluation:**

For `M ⊨ ∀x (Bird(x) → Fly(x))` to be true, **for every element `d ∈ D`**, the implication `Bird(d) → Fly(d)` must be true:

- `d = Tweety`: `Bird(Tweety) = T`, `Fly(Tweety) = T` → `T → T = T` ✓
- `d = Woody`: `Bird(Woody) = T`, `Fly(Woody) = T` → `T → T = T` ✓
- `d = Eagle1`: `Bird(Eagle1) = T`, `Fly(Eagle1) = F` → `T → F = F` ✗
- `d = Rock1`: `Bird(Rock1) = F`, `Fly(Rock1) = F` → `F → F = T` ✓

**Conclusion:** `M ⊭ ∀x (Bird(x) → Fly(x))` because Eagle1 is a bird that does not fly. The sentence is **FALSE** in model M.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The Ambiguity:**

The sentence "Every farmer owns a donkey" admits **two scope readings** depending on which quantifier has wider scope.

**Reading 1: `∀ > ∃` (Universal-Wide-Scope — distributive)**

$$\forall x\; (\text{Farmer}(x) \rightarrow \exists y\; (\text{Donkey}(y) \wedge \text{Own}(x, y)))$$

*Reading: For every farmer x, there exists *some* donkey y (potentially different for each x) such that x owns y.*

**Reading 2: `∃ > ∀` (Existential-Wide-Scope — collective)**

$$\exists y\; (\text{Donkey}(y) \wedge \forall x\; (\text{Farmer}(x) \rightarrow \text{Own}(x, y)))$$

*Reading: There is a single specific donkey y such that every farmer x owns that same y.*

**Pragmatic Resolution:**

1. **Grice's Maxim of Quantity / Common Sense**: Without further context, the *distributive* reading (1) is strongly preferred because in the real world each farmer has a *different* donkey.
2. **Indefinite Article "a"**: In English, the indefinite article typically introduces a *non-specific* entity, which favours the bound-variable reading (1).
3. **Stress and Focus**: If the speaker emphasizes "**a** donkey" (with stress), the collective reading (2) becomes more salient — they are highlighting the existence of one shared donkey.
4. **Discourse Context**: In a story about "the village donkey," reading (2) is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often present only ONE reading and assume it is the "correct" one. Both are *grammatically* and *semantically* valid logical forms. **Full marks require explicit presentation of BOTH readings**, plus at least 2 pragmatic factors. Deduct 3 marks if only one LF is given.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Semantics & LF Questions (KTU 2024 ESE)**:
> 
> 1. **Skipping β-reduction steps**: Examiners award 1 mark for each visible `(λx. M) N ⇒β M[x:=N]` rewrite. If you write only the final LF, you lose up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing types**: Writing `[[loves]] = λx.λy. Loves(x, y)` (Type: `e → e → t`) is acceptable, but writing `[[loves]] = λx. Loves(x)` (missing the y) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring scope ambiguity**: Always mention that natural-language sentences often have multiple valid LFs.
> 4. **Forgetting the model**: A logical form is *meaningless* without a model-theoretic interpretation. For 14-mark questions, always include the domain `D` and the interpretation function `I`.
> 5. **Wrong quantifier semantics**: `∀x (P(x) ∧ Q(x))` is **NOT** the meaning of "every P Q" — it should be `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`. This single error loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up types**: An adjective takes type `(e → t) → (e → t)`, NOT `e → t`. Get this right.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam)**

- ✅ **Semantics** = literal, context-independent meaning; sits between Syntax and Pragmatics.
- ✅ **Logical Form (LF)** = a formal language (usually FOPL or typed lambda calculus) representation of sentence meaning.
- ✅ **Compositionality** (Frege, 1892): Meaning of the whole = function of meanings of the parts.
- ✅ **Typed Lambda Calculus Types**: `e` (entity), `t` (truth value), `e → t` (property/predicate), `(e → t) → (e → t)` (modifier/adjective).
- ✅ **β-reduction rule**: `(λx. M) N ⇒β M[x := N]` — single computation step.
- ✅ **Lexicon Entries to Memorize**:
  - `[[every]] = λP.λQ. ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`
  - `[[a/some]] = λP.λQ. ∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(x))`
  - `[[not]] = λp. ¬p`
  - `[[and]] = λp.λq. p ∧ q`
  - `[[or]] = λp.λq. p ∨ q`
  - `[[if]] = λp.λq. p → q`
- ✅ **Composition Rules (Bottom-Up)**: Apply NP rule first, then VP, then S.
- ✅ **Model** = `⟨D, I⟩` where `D` = domain (set of entities), `I` = interpretation function.
- ✅ **Truth Condition**: `M ⊨ S` iff evaluating the LF in model M yields True (1).
- ✅ **Scope Ambiguity**: Multiple LFs from one sentence — resolved by pragmatics, not syntax.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications**: NLIDB, QA, MT, robotics, legal AI, medical NLP.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Topic**: Tracing a sentence from raw text → LF via λ-calculus (always show all β-reductions).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linking Syntax and Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linking Syntax and Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Linking Syntax and Semantics** is the sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) concerned with establishing a systematic, compositional mapping between **syntactic structures** (parse trees, phrase-structure rules, dependency arcs) and **semantic representations** (predicate–argument structures, logical forms, semantic role labels, knowledge-graph relations). The discipline is grounded in the **Principle of Compositionality** of Frege: *"The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, this topic spans three intertwined problems:
1. **Predicate–Argument Identification** – determining *who did what to whom*.
2. **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)** – labelling constituents with their thematic function.
3. **Lexical-Syntactic Interface** – integrating lexicon, grammar, and ontological knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEADT635 / Module 3):** Linking is treated as the *bridge* between shallow parsing and deep semantic interpretation. Students are expected to know PropBank, VerbNet, Selectional Restrictions, CCG (Combinatory Categorial Grammar), and Abductive Inference.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Movie Script" View**
> 
> Imagine a **movie script** (the *syntax* — who stands where, in what order, with what props) versus the **actual film** (the *semantics* — what is really happening emotionally and causally). 
> 
> - A parse tree tells us: *"Subi ran quickly to the store" → (NP Subi) (VP ran quickly) (PP to the store)*. 
> - Linking tells us: **Subi** is the *Agent* (runner), **the store** is the *Goal* (destination), and *ran* is the *Predicate*.
> 
> Just as a director *links* blocking (syntax) to dramatic intent (semantics), a computational linguist *links* the parse tree to meaning using **semantic role labels** and **lexical resources** (PropBank, VerbNet, WordNet).

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Predicate** | The event/action denoted by a verb (e.g., *give*, *break*). |
| **Argument** | A participant in the predicate's event (e.g., giver, given, recipient). |
| **Agent (ARG0)** | The deliberate initiator of an action. |
| **Patient / Theme (ARG1)** | The entity undergoing the action or change of state. |
| **Adjunct** | Non-core modifiers (time, location, manner, reason). |
| **Compositionality** | Whole meaning is a function of part meanings + combination rules. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Linking Is Hard — The Core Problem

Natural language sentences contain:
- **Argument movement** (passivisation, raising, wh-extraction): *"The book was read by John."*
- **Implicit arguments** (*pro-drop*): *"John ate."* (what did he eat?)
- **Metonymy / world knowledge**: *"The ham sandwich ordered a beer."* (the *customer*, not the sandwich)
- **Selectional mismatches**: *"The idea sleeps."* (abstract noun as patient of a normally animate verb)

Linking theory addresses these by employing:
1. **Lexical resources** (PropBank, VerbNet, WordNet, FrameNet).
2. **Selectional restrictions** — semantic type constraints on arguments.
3. **Statistical preference models** — soft probabilistic biases.
4. **Abductive inference** — plausibility-based argument reconstruction.

## 2.2 The Linking Pipeline (Operational Concept)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects the following six-step pipeline:

1. **Tokenisation & POS Tagging** — surface preparation.
2. **Parsing** — produce constituency or dependency tree.
3. **Predicate Identification** — locate verbs and light verb constructions.
4. **Argument Identification** — locate candidate constituents (NP, PP, SBAR, S).
5. **Argument Classification** — assign a semantic role label.
6. **Logical-Form Assembly** — construct the predicate–argument logical form (LF).

## 2.3 Theoretical Components Explained

### 2.3.1 Thematic Roles (Fillmore's Case Grammar)
The classical theory assigns atomic semantic roles. Modern PropBank-style systems refine this to **numbered arguments** (ARG0…ARG5) whose meaning is *verb-specific*.

### 2.3.2 PropBank (Palmer et al., 2005)
- A **predicate-specific** annotation scheme.
- ARG0 is typically the *Agent* of a transitive verb, but for *sell*, ARG0 = seller, ARG1 = thing sold, ARG2 = buyer.
- Uses *framesets* per verb sense.

### 2.3.3 VerbNet (Kipper et al., 2000)
- A **class-based** hierarchical verb lexicon (≈ 270 Levin's classes).
- Each class shares **syntactic frames** *and* **selectional restrictions**.
- E.g., *give-13.1* class: *give, donate, hand, lend, pass, sell, serve, ...*

### 2.3.4 Selectional Restrictions (Katz & Fodor 1963)
Hard binary constraints:
$$ \text{eat} \rightarrow \text{ARG1} \in \text{[ANIMATE, EDIBLE]} $$
A sentence is **ungrammatical at the semantic level** if the constraints are violated (*"The chair ate the apple"*).

### 2.3.5 Selectional Preferences (Resnik 1996)
Soft, probabilistic strength based on **distributional similarity**:
$$ S(r) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{KL}\!\left( P(c \mid w_i) \,\big\vert\vert\, P(c) \right) $$

where $r$ is a selectional relation, $w_i$ are words filling the relation, $c$ ranges over WordNet concepts.

### 2.3.6 Abduction (Hobbs et al. 1993)
Given an observation $O$ and background knowledge $K$, find the **best minimal explanation** $E$:
$$ E = \arg\min_{E} \left[ \text{cost}(E) - \log P(E \mid K) \right] \quad \text{s.t. } K \cup E \models O $$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compositionality | $\text{meaning}(A B) = f(\text{meaning}(A), \text{meaning}(B))$ | Foundation of linking |
| 2 | Selectional Association | $S(r) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i} \mathrm{KL}\!\left( P(c \mid w_i) \,\big\vert\vert\, P(c) \right)$ | Resnik's selectional preference |
| 3 | Abduction cost | $\text{cost}(E) = -\log P(E \mid K)$ | Hobbs' abductive inference |
| 4 | Predicate-Argument LF | $\lambda x.\, \text{predicate}(x, y)$ | Lambda-calculus LF |
| 5 | Frame matching score | $\text{score}(f,s) = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot \phi_i(f,s)$ | Linear SRL model |
| 6 | VerbNet class mapping | $v \mapsto C_v \in \text{VerbNet}$ | Class-based linking |
| 7 | PropBank roleset | $v.\text{nn} \rightarrow \langle \text{ARG0},\dots,\text{ARGM} \rangle$ | Predicate-specific role defs |
| 8 | SRL F1 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Evaluation metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linking syntax and semantics underpins **Information Extraction**, **Question Answering**, **Machine Translation quality estimation**, **Sentiment Analysis**, and **Biomedical Text Mining** (e.g., extracting drug–disease relations from clinical reports).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Logical Form

Consider the sentence:

> *"Subi gave a book to Kavya yesterday."*

We will derive its **semantic representation** step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Surface Form & Lexical Semantics

Assign each content word a *meaning representation* (lambda expression):

| Word | Lexical Meaning |
|---|---|
| Subi | $\mathbf{Subi}$ |
| Kavya | $\mathbf{Kavya}$ |
| yesterday | $\mathbf{Yesterday}$ |
| book | $\mathbf{Book}(x)$ |
| give | $\lambda y.\,\lambda z.\,\lambda w.\,\mathbf{give}(y, z, w)$ |
| a | (indefinite article, $\lambda P.\,\lambda Q.\,\exists x\, [P(x) \wedge Q(x)]$) |

### Step 2 — Syntactic Parse (Constituency)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S} &\rightarrow \text{NP}_1\ \text{VP} \\
\text{VP} &\rightarrow \text{V}\ \text{NP}_2\ \text{PP} \\
\text{PP} &\rightarrow \text{P}\ \text{NP}_3 \\
\text{NP}_1 &\rightarrow \text{Subi} \\
\text{NP}_2 &\rightarrow \text{Det}\ \text{N} \\
\text{NP}_3 &\rightarrow \text{Kavya} \\
\text{PP} &\rightarrow \text{P}\ \text{NP}_{temporal} \\
\text{NP}_{temporal} &\rightarrow \text{yesterday}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Application of $\beta$-Reduction (Compositional Linking)

We apply function application from **right-to-left** inside the VP, then bind the subject NP.

- VP: $\mathbf{give}(y, z, w)$ is a 3-argument verb. Apply **yesterday** as adjunct $\mathbf{Yesterday}(e)$ (the event time).
- Apply NP$_3$ = **Kavya** as the **Goal** ($w$).
- Apply NP$_2$ = **a book** as the **Theme** ($z$).
- Apply NP$_1$ = **Subi** as the **Agent** ($y$).

Resulting logical form:

$$
\exists e\, \big[\mathbf{give}(e) \wedge \mathbf{Agent}(e, \mathbf{Subi}) \wedge \mathbf{Theme}(e, \mathbf{Book}(x)) \wedge \mathbf{Goal}(e, \mathbf{Kavya}) \wedge \mathbf{Time}(e, \mathbf{Yesterday})\big]
$$

### Step 4 — PropBank Mapping

In PropBank notation: roleset **give.01** assigns
- $\text{ARG0} = \text{Subi}$ (giver)
- $\text{ARG1} = \text{the book}$ (thing given)
- $\text{ARG2} = \text{Kavya}$ (recipient)
- $\text{ARGM-TMP} = \text{yesterday}$ (temporal modifier)

### Step 5 — VerbNet Cross-Check

*give* belongs to class **13.1** (transfer-of-possession), whose members share the *NP V NP PP.to* syntactic frame. The parse matches → the class-based assignment is consistent.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Algorithms Compared

| Method | Knowledge Source | Strength | Weakness | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-based (HPSG, LFG)** | Hand-built grammar | Precise, linguistically motivated | Brittle, expensive to maintain | High |
| **PropBank SRL** | Annotated corpora | Verb-specific accuracy | Limited generalisation | Very High |
| **VerbNet class-based** | Lexical classes | Cross-verb generalisation | Class boundaries coarse | High |
| **Statistical (Resnik)** | Distributional | Handles soft preferences | Needs huge corpora | Medium |
| **Neural SRL (deep)** | End-to-end models | State-of-the-art accuracy | Black-box, data-hungry | High (modern) |
| **Abductive (Hobbs)** | World knowledge | Fills implicit arguments | Computationally expensive | Medium |

## 3.3 Derivation — Resnik's Selectional Association

Given a selectional relation $r$ (e.g., *direct-object of eat*) and observed fillers $w_1, \dots, w_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(r) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} D\!\left( P(c \mid w_i) \,\big\vert\vert\, P(c) \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{c \in C} P(c \mid w_i) \log \frac{P(c \mid w_i)}{P(c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $D(\cdot \,\big\vert\vert\, \cdot)$ is the Kullback–Leibler divergence,
- $P(c \mid w_i)$ is the probability of WordNet concept $c$ given word $w_i$,
- $P(c)$ is the prior probability of concept $c$ in the corpus.

**Interpretation:** A high $S(r)$ means the relation $r$ is semantically coherent (its fillers belong to a tight conceptual class). A low $S(r)$ signals a selectional violation or metaphor.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Selectional Association (Toy Corpus)

Suppose a tiny toy corpus and the relation *object-of-eat* has fillers: *apple, pizza, bread, chair, idea*.

Conceptual vocabulary $C = \{$ **Food**, **Furniture**, **AbstractIdea** $\}$.

| Word $w_i$ | $P(\text{Food} \mid w_i)$ | $P(\text{Furniture} \mid w_i)$ | $P(\text{AbstractIdea} \mid w_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| apple | 0.95 | 0.03 | 0.02 |
| pizza | 0.97 | 0.01 | 0.02 |
| bread | 0.99 | 0.00 | 0.01 |
| chair | 0.05 | 0.90 | 0.05 |
| idea | 0.02 | 0.00 | 0.98 |

Priors: $P(\text{Food}) = 0.60$, $P(\text{Furniture}) = 0.20$, $P(\text{AbstractIdea}) = 0.20$.

For *apple*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(\cdot \,\big\vert\vert\, \cdot)_{\text{apple}} &= 0.95 \log \frac{0.95}{0.60} + 0.03 \log \frac{0.03}{0.20} + 0.02 \log \frac{0.02}{0.20} \\
&\approx 0.95(0.459) + 0.03(-1.897) + 0.02(-2.303) \\
&\approx 0.436 - 0.057 - 0.046 \\
&\approx 0.333 \text{ nats}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly compute for the other four words, then average — a *Food-dominated* class will give a high $S(r)$ for the noun *apple, pizza, bread*; mixing in *chair* and *idea* lowers the average, exposing the selectional anomaly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipeline Block Diagram — Syntax → Semantics Linker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Text Input] --> A2[Tokeniser and POS Tagger]
    A2 --> A3[Syntactic Parser Constituency or Dependency]
    A3 --> A4{Predicate Identification Verb Light Verb}
    A4 -->|Found| A5[Argument Boundary Detection]
    A4 -->|Not Found| A6[Skip — No Predicate]
    A5 --> A7[Argument Classification PropBank VerbNet]
    A7 --> A8[Selectional Constraint Checker]
    A8 -->|Pass| A9[Logical Form Constructor Lambda Calculus]
    A8 -->|Fail| A10[Abductive Repair Hobbs Algorithm]
    A10 --> A9
    A9 --> A11[Predicate Argument Semantic Representation]
    A11 --> A12[Downstream Task IE QA MT Sentiment]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Linking Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Lexicon Level] --> B2[Verb Sense Disambiguation]
    B2 --> B3[VerbNet Class Lookup]
    B3 --> B4[PropBank Roleset Selection]
    B4 --> B5[Selectional Restrictions]
    B4 --> B6[Selectional Preferences]
    B5 --> B7[Argument Role Assignment]
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Final Predicate Argument Structure]
```

## 4.3 CCG Combinator Flow (Lexicalised Linking)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Lexicon Entry give NP slash NP slash PP TO] --> C2[Lexicon Entry book N]
    C3[Lexicon Entry to PP slash NP] --> C4[Lexicon Entry Kavya NP]
    C2 --> D1[Forward Application]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Give Book as Function over PP]
    E1 --> D2[Forward Composition]
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D2
    D2 --> F1[Complete VP Meaning: lambda w give book w]
    F1 --> G1[Attach Subject Subi NP]
    G1 --> H1[Final Sentence Meaning: give subi book kavya]
```

## 4.4 Abductive Repair Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Observed Sentence] --> O2[Parse Tree Incomplete]
    O2 --> O3[Background Knowledge Base KB]
    O3 --> O4[Generate Candidate Hypotheses H1 H2 H3]
    O4 --> O5[Cost Function cost H]
    O5 --> O6[Argmin Select Best H]
    O6 --> O7[Augmented Logical Form]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme examination, students are expected to be able to draw a labelled **pipeline diagram** (similar to Section 4.1) and identify at which stage each lexical resource (PropBank / VerbNet / Selectional Restrictions) is consulted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define semantic role labeling. List any four semantic roles. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) is the NLP task of identifying the **predicate–argument structure** of a sentence and assigning a semantic role label to each argument of the predicate. The four semantic roles are:
1. **Agent** — the deliberate initiator of an action.
2. **Theme / Patient** — the entity undergoing the action.
3. **Instrument** — the means by which the action is performed.
4. **Goal / Recipient** — the destination of a transferred object.

*Example:* In *"Ravi broke the vase with a hammer"*, *Ravi* = Agent, *the vase* = Theme, *a hammer* = Instrument.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between PropBank and VerbNet in the context of linking syntax and semantics. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PropBank | VerbNet |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Predicate-specific | Class-based |
| Role Labels | ARG0, ARG1, …, ARGM | Thematic roles (Agent, Theme, etc.) per class |
| Coverage | ~3,000 verb senses | ~270 Levin's classes |
| Strength | High verb-specific precision | Cross-verb generalisation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Principle of Compositionality** and its role in linking syntactic constituents to semantic representations. Use the sentence *"Aswathi opened the door with the key"* to construct a logical form and label all arguments using PropBank roleset **open.01**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **PropBank annotation scheme** in detail. Explain how ARG0, ARG1, ARG2 and ARGM are assigned for the verbs *give*, *buy*, and *sell*. Highlight the role of **VerbNet** in cross-verb generalisation. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Compositionality Principle (Frege 1892):**
The meaning of a complex expression is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.

**Step 2 — Lexical Assignments:**

| Word | Lexicon Entry |
|---|---|
| Aswathi | NP → **Aswathi** |
| the key | NP → **key** |
| the door | NP → **door** |
| open | VP/NP/PP-with → $\lambda y.\,\lambda z.\,\mathbf{open}(y, z)$ |
| with | PP/(NP) → instrument marker |

**Step 3 — Parse:**
$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{NP (Aswathi)}\ \text{VP};\ \ \text{VP} \rightarrow \text{V (open)}\ \text{NP (door)}\ \text{PP (with the key)}$.

**Step 4 — Compositional Build-Up:**
- The VP combines with the subject NP via **Function Application** to produce $\mathbf{open}(\mathbf{Aswathi}, \mathbf{door})$.
- The PP attaches as an **Instrumental adjunct**: $\mathbf{Instrument}(e, \mathbf{key})$.

**Step 5 — Final Logical Form:**

$$
\exists e\, \big[\mathbf{open}(e) \wedge \mathbf{ARG0}(e, \mathbf{Aswathi}) \wedge \mathbf{ARG1}(e, \mathbf{door}) \wedge \mathbf{ARGM-INSTR}(e, \mathbf{key})\big]
$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**PropBank Rolesets:**

| Verb | Roleset | ARG0 | ARG1 | ARG2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| *give.01* | give.01 | giver | thing given | recipient |
| *buy.01* | buy.01 | buyer | thing bought | seller |
| *sell.01* | sell.01 | seller | thing sold | buyer |

**Key Insight:** Notice that *give* and *sell* swap ARG0/ARG2 in semantic terms. PropBank captures this by **predicate-specific** rolesets rather than fixed thematic labels.

**VerbNet Contribution:**
VerbNet groups *give, donate, hand, lend, pass, sell, serve* into class **13.1 — Transfer-of-possession**, all of which share the *NP V NP PP.to* syntactic frame and the same thematic skeleton. This enables **cross-verb generalisation** — a parser trained on *give* can be applied to *donate* via the class link.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **Selectional Restrictions** and **Selectional Preferences**. Derive the formula for Resnik's **Selectional Association Strength** and interpret its components. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Hobbs' Abduction Algorithm** for filling implicit arguments. How does it address sentences like *"Subi ate."* where the Theme is syntactically absent? Show a cost computation for at least one abductive hypothesis. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Selectional Restrictions** are *hard* binary semantic-type constraints imposed by a predicate on its arguments (Katz & Fodor, 1963). E.g., *eat* requires its object to be $[+ \text{edible}]$.

**Selectional Preferences** are *soft*, probabilistic biases computed from corpus statistics (Resnik, 1996).

**Derivation of Resnik's Formula:**

We want to measure how semantically coherent a relation $r$ is, given observed fillers $w_1, \dots, w_n$. The selectional association is the **average KL-divergence** between the conditional concept distribution $P(c \mid w_i)$ and the marginal $P(c)$:

$$
S(r) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} D\!\left( P(c \mid w_i) \,\big\vert\vert\, P(c) \right) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{c \in C} P(c \mid w_i) \log \frac{P(c \mid w_i)}{P(c)}
$$

**Interpretation of Components:**
- $P(c \mid w_i)$ — probability of WordNet concept $c$ given the word $w_i$; estimated as the relative frequency of $c$ in a parsed corpus.
- $P(c)$ — prior probability of concept $c$.
- $\log \frac{P(c \mid w_i)}{P(c)}$ — the **information gain** of $c$ under $w_i$.
- Higher $S(r)$ ⇒ the fillers cluster in a few concepts ⇒ coherent selectional class.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Hobbs' Abduction Algorithm (1993):**
Given an observed sentence $O$ and a knowledge base $K$, find the **cheapest proof** of $O$ by hypothesising unobserved literals $E$:

$$
\hat{E} = \arg\min_{E}\, \left[-\log P(E) \;\Big|\; K \cup E \models O \right]
$$

The cost of an assumed proposition is $-\log P(E)$ — the rarer the assumption, the higher the cost.

**Application to *"Subi ate."*:**

- **Observed:** $\mathbf{Agent}(e, \mathbf{Subi}) \wedge \mathbf{eat}(e)$.
- **Missing:** the Theme of *eat*.
- **Candidates from KB:**
  1. $H_1$: $\exists x\,[\mathbf{Food}(x) \wedge \mathbf{Theme}(e,x)]$ — Subi ate some food.
  2. $H_2$: $\exists x\,[\mathbf{Medicine}(x) \wedge \mathbf{Theme}(e,x)]$ — Subi ate some medicine.

**Cost Computation (toy):** Suppose $P(H_1) = 0.7$ and $P(H_2) = 0.1$ based on world priors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(H_1) &= -\log(0.7) = 0.357 \\
\text{cost}(H_2) &= -\log(0.1) = 2.303
\end{aligned}
$$

The minimal cost is $\hat{E} = H_1$. The abductive system therefore fills the implicit argument as $\exists x\,[\mathbf{Food}(x) \wedge \mathbf{Theme}(e,x)]$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points**
> 1. **Confusing ARG0 with Agent universally:** In PropBank, ARG0 is the *most agentive* argument of a specific verb, but for verbs like *receive* the ARG0 is the *recipient*, not the giver. Read the roleset file!
> 2. **Forgetting adjuncts:** Many students drop ARGM-TMP, ARGM-LOC, ARGM-MNR. They are *not* required, but their omission in long sentences often loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing FrameNet and PropBank labels:** FrameNet uses frame-specific role names (Buyer, Seller); PropBank uses ARG0…ARG5. Examiners are strict on consistency.
> 4. **Skipping the parse step:** When asked to "build a logical form", you *must* show the parse tree before the lambda-reduction. Jumping directly to the LF loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting quantifier scope:** In *"Every student read a book"*, the relative scope of $\forall$ and $\exists$ matters. Always state the scope explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compositionality** is the foundational assumption: $\text{meaning}(\text{whole}) = f(\text{meaning}(\text{parts}))$.
- **Thematic roles** (Agent, Theme, Goal, Instrument, Experiencer, Beneficiary) are the classical inventory; **PropBank** generalises them into **predicate-specific** numbered arguments.
- **VerbNet** provides *class-based* generalisation — verbs in the same class share syntactic frames and selectional restrictions.
- **Selectional Restrictions** = hard, symbolic, type-based constraints; **Selectional Preferences** = soft, distributional, corpus-based.
- **Resnik's Selectional Association** uses KL-divergence to measure the semantic coherence of a relation.
- **Hobbs' Abduction** handles implicit arguments by finding the cheapest proof using world knowledge and $-\log P(E)$ cost.
- **CCG (Combinatory Categorial Grammar)** is a lexicalised grammar where every lexical entry carries both a syntactic category and a semantic expression, enabling tight syntax–semantics coupling.
- **Logical Forms** in modern NLP are typically expressed as **predicate–argument** structures; quantifier scope must be made explicit.
- **End-to-end neural SRL** (BiLSTM, BERT-based) currently dominates CoNLL-2005 benchmarks but is data-hungry.
- For KTU exams: **always pair** a PropBank-style role with a VerbNet class mention when explaining verb semantics — it shows cross-resource understanding.
- **Evaluation metric** is **F1-score on labelled arguments** (CoNLL shared task standard).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ambiguity Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ambiguity Resolution in NLP** is the computational process of identifying, classifying, and eliminating multiple possible interpretations of a linguistic expression (word, phrase, sentence, or discourse) by selecting the interpretation that is most plausible given the syntactic, semantic, pragmatic, and contextual evidence available in the input.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEADT635 – Natural Language Processing*, **Ambiguity Resolution** is treated as a foundational sub-task of natural language understanding. It is formally defined as the act of mapping an **ambiguous linguistic input** $L$ to a **single, well-formed meaning representation** $M$ chosen from a set of candidates $M = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\}$ such that the chosen meaning $m^*$ maximizes some scoring function:

$$m^{*} = \arg\max_{m_i \in M} \; \text{Score}(L, m_i)$$

The scoring function may be **probabilistic**, **rule-based**, **distributional**, or **learned via neural networks**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a friend texts you: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* Now, did *you* use a telescope to see the man, or did the *man* have a telescope? Both readings are valid English — your brain instantly disambiguates based on world knowledge, prior context, and grammar. **Ambiguity Resolution in NLP is the engineered equivalent of teaching a machine to do what your brain did unconsciously in under a second.**

A machine, however, has no intuition. It must be explicitly shown the syntactic tree, the co-occurring words, the discourse context, and statistical distributions before it can choose one interpretation. That is precisely what ambiguity resolution techniques — such as **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**, **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)**, and **Anaphora Resolution Algorithms** — are designed to do.

## 1.3 Why Ambiguity is the Central Bottleneck of NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single English sentence has, on average, **between 2 and 8 valid syntactic parses** and each verb can have **3–12 distinct senses** in a standard lexicon like **WordNet** (containing **~117,000 synsets**). Without ambiguity resolution, downstream tasks such as **Machine Translation, Sentiment Analysis, Question Answering, and Information Retrieval** collapse in accuracy.

## 1.4 The Five Canonical Layers of Linguistic Ambiguity

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly identifies five types of ambiguity that any complete NLP pipeline must address:

| # | Type of Ambiguity | Unit of Ambiguity | Classic Example |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Lexical Ambiguity** | A single word | *Bank* → river bank / financial bank |
| 2 | **Syntactic (Structural) Ambiguity** | A phrase or sentence | *"I saw the man with the telescope."* |
| 3 | **Semantic Ambiguity** | Sentence meaning | *"The chicken is ready to eat."* (chicken will eat / chicken will be eaten) |
| 4 | **Anaphoric Ambiguity** | Pronoun / reference | *"John told Bill he was right."* (who is "he"?) |
| 5 | **Pragmatic Ambiguity** | Intent / speech act | *"Can you pass the salt?"* (literal ability vs. polite request) |

A **sixth**, sometimes explicitly listed type, is **Scope Ambiguity**, which deals with quantifier binding — e.g., *"Every student read a book."* — does each student read the *same* book, or *possibly different* ones?
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lexical Ambiguity & Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)

**Lexical ambiguity** arises when a single word-form belongs to multiple **synsets (synonym sets)** in a lexicon. The disambiguation task is called **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (WSD):** Given a word $w_i$ occurring in a context $C = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$, determine which sense $s \in S(w_i)$ is intended, where $S(w_i)$ is the set of all known senses of $w_i$ in a knowledge source (e.g., **WordNet**).

### 2.1.1 Two Evaluation Variants of WSD

1. **Lex Sample Task** — A small list of target words is pre-selected; systems disambiguate only these.
2. **All-Words Task** — Every content word (noun, verb, adjective, adverb) in a running text must be disambiguated.

### 2.1.2 Lesk Algorithm (Knowledge-Based WSD)

The **original Lesk algorithm (1986)** is the classical dictionary-gloss overlap method. For a target word $w$ with senses $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ and context words $C$, the chosen sense is the one whose dictionary **definition (gloss)** shares the maximum number of words with the context.

**Formal scoring equation:**

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \;\big\vert \; \text{Gloss}(s_i) \;\cap\; \text{Context}(w) \;\big\vert$$

> **Significance:** The Lesk algorithm is **explainable, deterministic, and resource-light**, making it a KTU board-exam favourite. Its variant, **Adapted/Extended Lesk**, enriches glosses with hypernyms, hyponyms, and examples from WordNet.

### 2.1.3 Supervised WSD — Naive Bayes Formulation

Given labeled training data $\{(C_j, s_j)\}_{j=1}^{N}$, the most likely sense for a new context $C$ is:

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} \; P(s \mid C) = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} \; P(s) \prod_{c \in C} P(c \mid s)$$

The second equality applies **Bayes' rule with the naive independence assumption** that context words are conditionally independent given the sense. This produces the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate.

## 2.2 Syntactic Ambiguity Resolution

**Syntactic ambiguity** occurs when a sentence admits more than one valid **parse tree** under a Context-Free Grammar (CFG). Modern parsers use **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)** to choose the most likely parse.

**PCFG scoring rule:**

$$T^{*} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} \; P(T)$$

where the probability of a parse tree is the product of the probabilities of all rules used:

$$P(T) = \prod_{r \in \text{rules}(T)} P(r)$$

**Disambiguation by attachment preferences** — three classical heuristics taught at KTU level:

| Heuristic | Description | Example Application |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Low Attachment** | Attach new constituents to the lowest possible node | *PP attachment* in *"I ate pizza with olives"* |
| **Right Association** | Attach to the most recent valid node | Reduces garden-path errors |
| **Minimal Attachment** | Use the tree with the fewest nodes | Avoids over-structuring |

## 2.3 Semantic Ambiguity — Selectional Restrictions

For sentences like *"The chicken is ready to eat"*, semantic ambiguity is resolved using **selectional restrictions** — semantic constraints verbs place on their arguments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selectional Restriction Violation (SRV):** A sentence is judged implausible if a verb's argument violates its expected semantic class. E.g., the verb *eat* requires its object to be **+edible**; thus *"The chicken is ready to eat"* resolves to *the chicken will be eaten* when the subject is **+animate** and the object is the grain.

## 2.4 Anaphoric (Pronoun) Ambiguity Resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anaphora Resolution** is the task of linking a **pronoun (anaphor)** to its **antecedent** — the noun phrase it refers to. KTU 2024 syllabus typically covers rule-based and preference-based approaches.

**Hobbs' Algorithm (1978)** — a classic **syntactic, left-to-right tree-walk algorithm** that traverses the parse tree in preorder, giving male pronouns priority over female, and singular over plural.

**Centering Theory (Grosz, Joshi & Weinstein, 1995):** Models local coherence by tracking the **forward-looking centers** $C_f$ in each utterance and the **preferred (backward-looking) center** $C_b$. The ranking of preferred antecedents is determined by the **Cb-Cf relationship**.

## 2.5 Pragmatic Ambiguity & Speech Acts

Pragmatic ambiguity is resolved using **Speech Act Theory (Austin 1962, Searle 1969)**. The five categories are:

- **Representatives** — assert a fact (*"It is raining."*)
- **Directives** — try to get the hearer to do something (*"Close the door."*)
- **Commissives** — commit the speaker (*"I will come."*)
- **Expressives** — express a psychological state (*"Thank you."*)
- **Declarations** — change the world (*"I now pronounce you..."*)

The literal vs. intended meaning gap is bridged by **Grice's Maxims** and the principle of **Cooperative Principle**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all equations, metrics, and decision rules** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Ambiguity Resolution question.

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Key Variables | Domain |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|--------|
| **MAP Sense Selection** | $s^{*} = \arg\max_s P(s \mid C)$ | $s$: sense, $C$: context | Lexical WSD |
| **Naive Bayes (NB) for WSD** | $s^{*} = \arg\max_s P(s) \prod_{c \in C} P(c \mid s)$ | $P(c \vert s)$: word-given-sense probability | Supervised WSD |
| **Lesk Score** | $\text{Score}(s_i) = \vert \text{Gloss}(s_i) \cap \text{Context} \vert$ | $\text{Gloss}$: WordNet definition | Knowledge-based WSD |
| **PCFG Tree Probability** | $P(T) = \prod_{r \in T} P(r)$ | $r$: CFG rule | Syntactic disambiguation |
| **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(s \mid C) = \dfrac{P(C \mid s) \cdot P(s)}{P(C)}$ | Foundation of NB & HMM | All probabilistic NLP |
| **Mutual Information (collocation)** | $MI(x, y) = \log \dfrac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}$ | Detects preferred attachments | Selectional preference |
| **Precision / Recall** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP},\; R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | WSD evaluation metric | Evaluation |
| **F1 Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric | Evaluation |
| **Cosine Similarity (vector WSD)** | $\cos(\vec{v}_w, \vec{v}_c) = \dfrac{\vec{v}_w \cdot \vec{v}_c}{\Vert \vec{v}_w \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v}_c \Vert}$ | Distributional semantics | Neural WSD |
| **Hobbs Distance** | Preorder tree walk distance | Anaphora preference | Pronoun resolution |
| **Selectional Restriction Strength** | $S(v, c) = P(c \mid v) = \dfrac{\text{count}(v, c)}{\text{count}(v)}$ | $v$: verb, $c$: class | Semantic disambiguation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Naive Bayes Classifier for WSD

**Problem Setup.** We are given a polysemous word $w$ (e.g., *bank*) with senses $S(w) = \{s_1, s_2\}$ — financial institution vs. river edge. We are also given an unlabeled context sentence $C = (c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ containing $w$. Our goal is to compute $P(s_i \mid C)$ for each sense and pick the maximum.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$P(s_i \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)}{P(C)}$$

**Why this step?** Bayes' theorem inverts the conditional: it lets us compute the posterior probability of a sense given the observable context words, from the prior of the sense and the likelihood of the context under that sense.

**Step 2 — Drop the denominator $P(C)$ for argmax purposes.**

Since $P(C)$ is constant across all senses $s_i \in S(w)$, the sense that maximizes $P(s_i \mid C)$ is the same sense that maximizes the numerator $P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$. Therefore:

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \; P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$$

**Step 3 — Expand $P(C \mid s_i)$ using the chain rule.**

$$P(C \mid s_i) = P(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n \mid s_i)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption.**

We assume that the occurrence of each context word $c_j$ is conditionally independent of every other context word, given the sense $s_i$. Mathematically:

$$P(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n \mid s_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid s_i)$$

**Why this assumption?** It is naive because real contexts exhibit strong dependencies (e.g., *river* and *water* are highly correlated), but it dramatically simplifies computation and is empirically a strong baseline for WSD.

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the final NB classifier.**

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \; P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid s_i)$$

**Step 6 — Apply log-transformation for numerical stability.**

Taking the natural log turns the product into a sum, preventing underflow for large $n$:

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \left[\log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(c_j \mid s_i)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Estimate the parameters from training data.**

The two probabilities are estimated by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** with **Laplace (add-one) smoothing** to handle unseen words:

$$P(s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(s_i) + 1}{N + \vert S(w) \vert}, \qquad P(c_j \mid s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(c_j, s_i) + 1}{\text{count}(s_i) + V}$$

where $N$ is the total number of training instances, $V$ is the vocabulary size, and $\text{count}(c_j, s_i)$ is the number of times $c_j$ appeared with sense $s_i$ in training.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Lesk Algorithm

**Problem.** Disambiguate the word *bank* in the sentence: *"He deposited cash in the bank."* WordNet lists two primary senses:
- $s_1$ = *financial institution* (gloss: *"a financial institution that accepts deposits and channels those deposits into lending activities"*)
- $s_2$ = *sloping land beside a body of water* (gloss: *"sloping land (especially the slope beside a body of water)"*)

**Context words** (after stop-word removal): $\{ \text{deposited, cash} \}$

**Step 1 — Tokenize gloss of $s_1$.**

$$\text{Gloss}(s_1) = \{\text{financial, institution, accepts, deposits, channels, lending, activities}\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute intersection with context.**

$$\text{Gloss}(s_1) \cap \{\text{deposited, cash}\} = \emptyset \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Score}(s_1) = 0$$

(The match is exact-stem — *deposited* and *deposits* differ; hence the original Lesk often fails. Adapted Lesk would stem them.)

**Step 3 — Tokenize gloss of $s_2$.**

$$\text{Gloss}(s_2) = \{\text{sloping, land, especially, slope, beside, body, water}\}$$

**Step 4 — Compute intersection with context.**

$$\text{Gloss}(s_2) \cap \{\text{deposited, cash}\} = \emptyset \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Score}(s_2) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Extended Lesk with hypernyms and examples.**

The **Extended Lesk algorithm** enriches each gloss with the glosses of related synsets (hypernyms, hyponyms, meronyms, examples). For $s_1$, the *depository_financial_institution* hypernym gloss contains words like *money, account, loan*. The intersection with *cash* is non-empty:

$$\text{EnrichedGloss}(s_1) \cap \{\text{deposited, cash}\} = \{\text{cash}\} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Score}(s_1) = 1$$

**Step 6 — Choose the highest-scoring sense.**

$$s^{*} = s_1 = \text{financial institution}$$

This is the **correct human interpretation**, validating the algorithm.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Resolution Technique

| Ambiguity Type | Best Statistical Method | Best Rule-Based Method | Best Neural Method | KTU Favourite |
|----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Lexical | Naive Bayes / SVM WSD | Original / Extended Lesk | BERT-based WSD | **Lesk Algorithm** |
| Syntactic | PCFG parsing | Attachment heuristics | Chart parsing + NN | **PCFG derivation** |
| Semantic | Selectional Preference | Selectional Restrictions | Sense-embedding dot product | **Selectional restrictions example** |
| Anaphoric | Decision-tree preference | Hobbs' Algorithm | Coref neural models (e.g., Lee et al.) | **Hobbs' algorithm steps** |
| Pragmatic | MaxEnt classifiers | Speech-act rule sets | Transformer intent detection | **Speech Act theory** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — PCFG Parse Selection

**Grammar $G$:**

| Rule | Probability |
|------|-------------|
| $S \to NP \; VP$ | **0.8** |
| $S \to NP \; VP \; PP$ | 0.2 |
| $VP \to V \; NP$ | **0.7** |
| $VP \to V \; NP \; PP$ | 0.3 |
| $PP \to P \; NP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \to \text{Det} \; N$ | 1.0 |

**Sentence:** *"I saw the man with the telescope."*

**Parse Tree 1** (PP attaches to VP — *telescope is the instrument*):

$$P(T_1) = P(S \to NP \, VP) \cdot P(VP \to V \, NP \, PP) \cdot \dots = 0.8 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 = 0.24$$

**Parse Tree 2** (PP attaches to NP — *man has the telescope*):

$$P(T_2) = P(S \to NP \, VP \, PP) \cdot P(VP \to V \, NP) \cdot \dots = 0.2 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 = 0.14$$

**Decision:** $P(T_1) = 0.24 > P(T_2) = 0.14$, so the parser selects **Tree 1** (instrument reading). The model is correctly biased toward low attachment in this case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Ambiguity Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Text or Sentence] --> B[Linguistic Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B --> B2[POS Tagging]
    B --> B3[Stopword Removal]
    B1 --> C[Ambiguity Detector]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Ambiguity Type Classifier}
    D -->|Lexical| E1[Word Sense Disambiguation Module]
    D -->|Syntactic| E2[Probabilistic Parser PCFG]
    D -->|Semantic| E3[Selectional Restriction Engine]
    D -->|Anaphoric| E4[Pronoun Resolution Module]
    D -->|Pragmatic| E5[Speech Act Classifier]
    E1 --> F[Score Aggregator]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Argmax Decision]
    G --> H[Disambiguated Meaning Representation]
    H --> I[Downstream NLP Task]
    I --> I1[Machine Translation]
    I --> I2[Sentiment Analysis]
    I --> I3[Question Answering]
    H --> J[Evaluation Module]
    J --> J1[Precision]
    J --> J2[Recall]
    J --> J3[F1 Score]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Lesk Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start] --> A1[Read target word w and context C]
    A1 --> A2[Retrieve all senses of w from WordNet]
    A2 --> A3[For each sense s initialize Score s to zero]
    A3 --> A4[For each sense s in S of w]
    A4 --> A5[Tokenize gloss of s into word set Gs]
    A5 --> A6[Tokenize context C into word set Wc]
    A6 --> A7[Compute intersection of Gs and Wc]
    A7 --> A8[Set Score s to size of intersection]
    A8 --> A9[Optional Enrichment via hypernyms and examples]
    A9 --> A10[Append to candidate list]
    A10 --> A11[Pick sense with maximum score]
    A11 --> A12[Return chosen sense as disambiguated output]
    A12 --> A13[End]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Hobbs' Anaphora Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Input: Parse Tree with Pronoun P] --> B1[Begin preorder traversal from root]
    B1 --> B2[Visit each node left to right and bottom up]
    B2 --> B3{Is node a candidate antecedent}
    B3 -->|No| B2
    B3 -->|Yes| B4[Check agreement: gender number person]
    B4 --> B5{Agreement passes}
    B5 -->|No| B2
    B5 -->|Yes| B6[Record candidate with priority score]
    B6 --> B7{Male singular pronoun}
    B7 -->|Yes| B8[Give highest priority to first matching NP]
    B7 -->|No| B9[Apply default priority rules]
    B8 --> B10[Continue traversal]
    B9 --> B10
    B10 --> B11{Traversal complete}
    B11 -->|No| B2
    B11 -->|Yes| B12[Return highest priority candidate as antecedent]
    B12 --> B13[End]
```

## 4.4 PCFG-Based Syntactic Disambiguation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Input Sentence S] --> C1[Lexicalize and POS-tag tokens]
    C1 --> C2[Apply CYK or Chart Parser]
    C2 --> C3[Generate all valid parse trees T1 T2 Tn]
    C3 --> C4[For each tree Ti compute probability Pi]
    C4 --> C5{Pi is maximum}
    C5 -->|Yes| C6[Mark Ti as best parse]
    C5 -->|No| C4
    C6 --> C7[Output best parse tree as resolved structure]
    C7 --> C8[Pass to downstream semantic interpreter]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define ambiguity in NLP. List and briefly explain any three types of linguistic ambiguity with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Ambiguity in NLP refers to the phenomenon where a single linguistic expression (word, phrase, sentence, or discourse) can be interpreted in more than one valid way. It is a fundamental challenge because natural language is highly context-dependent and human languages are inherently multifunctional.

**Three major types of ambiguity:**

1. **Lexical Ambiguity** — A single word has multiple meanings. For example, the word *bank* can mean a *financial institution* or the *edge of a river*. Disambiguation here is called **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**.

2. **Syntactic (Structural) Ambiguity** — A sentence has more than one valid parse tree. For example, *"I saw the man with the telescope"* can be parsed in two ways: the telescope is the instrument of seeing, or the man possesses the telescope.

3. **Anaphoric Ambiguity** — A pronoun or reference can plausibly point to more than one antecedent. For example, in *"John told Bill that he was late,"* the pronoun *he* could refer to either John or Bill.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**What is the Lesk Algorithm? State its limitation and explain how Extended Lesk addresses it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lesk Algorithm**, proposed by Michael Lesk in 1986, is a **dictionary-based Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** technique. For a polysemous target word $w$, the algorithm computes the **overlap between the dictionary gloss (definition) of each sense** of $w$ and the **context words** surrounding $w$. The sense with the maximum overlap is selected as the correct sense.

**Formal expression:**

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \;\big\vert \; \text{Gloss}(s_i) \;\cap\; \text{Context}(w) \;\big\vert$$

**Limitation of Original Lesk:**

- Glosses in dictionaries are often **short and contain few content words**, leading to **zero or low overlap** for many contexts.
- It cannot match **morphological variants** (e.g., *deposited* vs. *deposits*) or **synonyms**.
- It is **purely lexical** and ignores **semantic relations** in the lexicon.

**How Extended Lesk Addresses It:**

The **Extended (Adapted) Lesk algorithm** enriches each sense's gloss with the glosses of its **hypernyms, hyponyms, holonyms, meronyms, and example sentences** from WordNet. It also applies **stemming and stop-word removal** to maximize the surface-form match. As a result, the effective definition set becomes much larger, the intersection with context grows, and disambiguation accuracy improves significantly.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the different types of ambiguity that occur at various linguistic levels. Provide at least one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Ambiguity can occur at every level of linguistic analysis — from the smallest unit (a word) to the largest (a discourse). The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes the following types:

1. **Lexical Ambiguity:** A single word has multiple meanings. The word *bat* can be a flying mammal or a piece of sports equipment. WSD aims to pick the correct sense from the dictionary definitions.

2. **Syntactic (Structural) Ambiguity:** A sentence has multiple valid parse trees. The classic example is *"I saw the man with the telescope,"* which can be parsed as either the **PP-attachment to the VP** (instrument reading) or **PP-attachment to the NP** (possessive reading).

3. **Semantic Ambiguity:** A sentence has multiple meanings even after the syntactic structure is determined. For instance, *"The chicken is ready to eat"* is semantically ambiguous because the verb *eat* can have either the chicken as the subject (eater) or as the object (eatee). This is resolved through **selectional restrictions**.

4. **Anaphoric Ambiguity:** A pronoun or a referring expression can refer to multiple possible antecedents. In *"Sara told Maria that she had won,"* the pronoun *she* can refer to either *Sara* or *Maria*. **Hobbs' algorithm** and **Centering Theory** are used to resolve this.

5. **Pragmatic Ambiguity:** A speaker's intention is not directly conveyed by the literal meaning. The sentence *"Can you pass the salt?"* is a request, not a question about physical ability. **Speech Act Theory** by Searle classifies such utterances into directives, representatives, commissives, expressives, and declarations.

6. **Scope Ambiguity:** Quantifiers can bind to different constituents, altering meaning. *"Every student read a book"* could mean every student read **the same** book, or **a possibly different** book for each. This is analyzed using **Montague Grammar** and **type-logical semantics**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the Hobbs' algorithm for anaphora (pronoun) resolution. Apply it to a sample sentence and show the steps.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hobbs' Algorithm (1978)** is a classic, purely syntactic algorithm for resolving **anaphoric pronouns** (he, she, it, they, him, her, them). It traverses the parse tree of the sentence containing the pronoun in **preorder** (left-to-right, depth-first) and selects the first noun phrase (NP) that agrees with the pronoun in **gender, number, and person**.

**Steps of Hobbs' Algorithm:**

1. Begin at the **root node** of the parse tree.
2. Do a **left-to-right breadth-first (preorder) traversal** of the tree.
3. Traverse the tree **from left to right and from bottom to top**.
4. For every NP node encountered, check whether it agrees with the pronoun in gender, number, and person.
5. If agreement holds, select this NP as the **antecedent**.
6. **Priority Rules:** If multiple candidates exist, give priority to NPs in the **current sentence** before considering previous sentences. Among candidates in the current sentence, the one encountered **first in the preorder traversal** is preferred.
7. **Gender priority:** For the pronoun *he*, give priority to NPs that are likely to refer to a male entity.
8. Return the chosen NP as the antecedent of the pronoun.

**Application Example:**

**Sentence:** *"John told Bill that he had won the match."*

Let the pronoun be *he*. We assume the parse tree has NPs in the order: [John] (NP1), [Bill] (NP2), [he] (pronoun, P).

**Step 1:** Start preorder traversal at the root.
**Step 2:** Visit NP1 (*John*) — does it agree with *he* in gender, number, person? *John* is masculine, singular, third person. **Yes — agreement holds.**
**Step 3:** Per Hobbs' rule, the **first matching NP is selected**.
**Step 4:** Antecedent = *John*. Resolution: *John had won the match.*

If the order in the parse tree were [*Bill*, *John*, *he*], then **Bill** would be the first matching NP and would be selected as the antecedent.

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Naive Bayes approach to Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD). Derive the classification rule and describe how the parameters are estimated.**

**Model Answer:**

**Naive Bayes for WSD** is a **supervised, probabilistic** disambiguation method. It requires a labeled training corpus where each occurrence of a polysemous word is annotated with its correct sense.

**Derivation of the Classification Rule:**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement.** Given a context $C = (c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ and a word $w$ with senses $S(w) = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$, find the most likely sense:

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} P(s_i \mid C)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$P(s_i \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)}{P(C)}$$

**Step 3 — Drop the denominator.** Since $P(C)$ is constant for all senses, the argmax depends only on the numerator:

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} P(C \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Naive Conditional Independence Assumption.** Each context word $c_j$ is conditionally independent of the others, given the sense:

$$P(C \mid s_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid s_i)$$

**Step 5 — Final Classification Rule.**

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(c_j \mid s_i)$$

**Step 6 — Log transformation for numerical stability.**

$$s^{*} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S(w)} \left[\log P(s_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log P(c_j \mid s_i)\right]$$

**Parameter Estimation (with Laplace Smoothing):**

- **Prior probability of a sense:**

$$P(s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(s_i) + 1}{N + \vert S(w) \vert}$$

- **Likelihood of a context word given a sense:**

$$P(c_j \mid s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(c_j, s_i) + 1}{\text{count}(s_i) + V}$$

where $N$ is the number of training instances, $V$ is the vocabulary size, $\text{count}(c_j, s_i)$ is the number of times $c_j$ occurred with sense $s_i$, and **+1 is the Laplace add-one smoothing constant** to prevent zero probabilities for unseen words.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Illustrate with a numerical example how a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) resolves syntactic ambiguity in a sentence with a prepositional phrase (PP) attachment.**

**Model Answer:**

**Problem.** The sentence *"I ate spaghetti with chopsticks"* has two parse trees: (1) the **PP *with chopsticks* attaches to the VP** (instrument reading — chopsticks are the tool used for eating), or (2) the **PP attaches to the NP *spaghetti*** (modifier reading — spaghetti that contains chopsticks, which is implausible but grammatically valid).

**PCFG rules and their probabilities (illustrative):**

| Rule | Probability |
|------|-------------|
| $S \to NP \; VP$ | 0.70 |
| $S \to NP \; VP \; PP$ | 0.30 |
| $VP \to V \; NP$ | 0.60 |
| $VP \to V \; NP \; PP$ | 0.40 |
| $PP \to P \; NP$ | 1.00 |
| $NP \to \text{Det} \; N$ | 1.00 |

**Parse Tree 1 (PP attaches to VP — instrument reading):**

The required rules are: $S \to NP \; VP$, $VP \to V \; NP \; PP$, $PP \to P \; NP$, plus lexical rules (each probability 1.0). Thus:

$$P(T_1) = 0.70 \times 0.40 \times 1.00 = 0.28$$

**Parse Tree 2 (PP attaches to NP — modifier reading):**

The required rules are: $S \to NP \; VP \; PP$, $VP \to V \; NP$, $PP \to P \; NP$. Thus:

$$P(T_2) = 0.30 \times 0.60 \times 1.00 = 0.18$$

**Decision:**

$$P(T_1) = 0.28 > P(T_2) = 0.18 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T^{*} = T_1$$

**Conclusion:** The PCFG prefers the instrument reading, which matches human intuition. This demonstrates how the **right-association** and **low-attachment** biases, when encoded as rule probabilities, lead to correct syntactic disambiguation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the independence assumption in Naive Bayes** — students often write the joint probability $P(C \mid s_i)$ without decomposing it into a product. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing "Lesk Score" with simple word count** — examiners expect the gloss-vs-context intersection explicitly written, not just "Lesk picks the most common sense."
> 3. **Applying Hobbs' algorithm to the wrong tree** — the algorithm requires a **preorder traversal**, not a postorder or inorder traversal. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting Laplace smoothing in WSD** — the formula must include the `+1` and `+V` terms; an unsmoothed MLE formula is incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing up types of ambiguity** — lexical vs. syntactic vs. semantic distinctions are not interchangeable; examiners award partial credit only if the type is correctly identified.
> 6. **Forgetting the log transformation** in NB derivations — a minor loss, but frequent. Use $\log$ on long products.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ambiguity Resolution (PEADT635 Module 3)**

- **Ambiguity** is a property of natural language where one form maps to many meanings; **resolution** is the process of selecting the correct one.
- **Five (sometimes six) types** to memorize: **Lexical, Syntactic, Semantic, Anaphoric, Pragmatic, and Scope.**
- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is the sub-task dedicated to resolving **lexical ambiguity.**
- **Lesk Algorithm** = gloss overlap; **Extended Lesk** = gloss + hypernyms + examples + stemming.
- **Naive Bayes WSD formula:** $s^{*} = \arg\max_s P(s) \prod_j P(c_j \mid s)$ — must include **Laplace smoothing**.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation of nearly all probabilistic ambiguity-resolution methods.
- **PCFG disambiguation** is done by computing $P(T) = \prod_r P(r)$ for every valid parse tree and choosing the maximum.
- **Hobbs' Algorithm** uses **preorder traversal** of the parse tree to find the antecedent of a pronoun.
- **Selectional Restrictions** are used to resolve **semantic ambiguity** by checking the semantic class of arguments against a verb's expectations.
- **Speech Act Theory** (Searle) classifies utterances into **Representatives, Directives, Commissives, Expressives, Declarations** — used for pragmatic disambiguation.
- **Centering Theory** tracks $C_b$ (backward-looking center) and $C_f$ (forward-looking centers) for **discourse coherence** and anaphora.
- **Evaluation metrics:** Precision, Recall, **F1 Score** are mandatory; report all three in WSD experiments.
- **Mutual Information (MI)** detects preferred word co-occurrences; useful for **selectional preference** modeling.
- **Modern neural methods** like **BERT, ELMo, and GPT** use contextual embeddings to resolve ambiguity with much higher accuracy, but **classical Lesk and Naive Bayes are still exam-favourites.**
- **Remember the cooperative principle and Grice's Maxims** (Quantity, Quality, Relevance, Manner) for pragmatic ambiguity.
- **Exam tip:** Always show the derivation, not just the final formula. KTU examiners reward logical progression.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Strategies for Semantic Interpretation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Semantic Interpretation** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) refers to the systematic process of mapping natural language expressions (sentences, phrases, utterances) into a structured, machine-readable meaning representation (such as First-Order Logic formulae, semantic frames, conceptual graphs, or ontologically-typed predicate-argument structures).

Within the taxonomy of NLP pipelines, the phrase **"Other Strategies for Semantic Interpretation"** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3) denotes the **non-compositional / lexicalist / ontology-driven / frame-based / network-based approaches** that supplement (or stand as alternatives to) classical Montague-style lambda calculus composition. These strategies rely on:

- Lexical resource lookup (WordNet, FrameNet, VerbNet),
- Conceptual abstractions (Scripts, Conceptual Dependency, Schank's CD theory),
- Constraint propagation (Selectional Restrictions),
- Structured inheritance graphs (Semantic Networks, KL-ONE-style ontologies),
- Case-role assignment (Fillmore's Case Grammar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** These strategies are positioned as **Module 3** content in PEADT635, sitting between basic compositional semantics and advanced pragmatics/discourse. Students must understand *why* pure first-order logic composition fails on open-domain text and *how* each "other" strategy patches that failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Records System**
>
> Imagine a hospital where every patient (a word/concept) is not described from scratch each time. Instead, the hospital has:
> - **A master disease encyclopedia** (WordNet/Ontology) — generic knowledge like "fever is a symptom",
> - **Pre-printed intake forms** (Frames) — slots for *Patient*, *Doctor*, *Diagnosis*, *Treatment*,
> - **A librarian's "goes-with" rules** (Selectional Restrictions) — *"The surgery requires a surgeon, not a banana"*.
>
> When a new patient walks in carrying a sentence, the staff does NOT invent the meaning. They *look up* the relevant form, *fill in the slots* using constraints, and *reference* the encyclopedia. This is **exactly** what "other strategies" do for language — they bypass building meaning compositionally from primitives and instead leverage **pre-existing structured knowledge**.

## 1.3 The Core Problem Being Solved

Classical predicate-argument composition (λ-calculus) suffers from three fatal issues on real text:

1. **Lexical Ambiguity**: "bank" has 18+ senses — pure logic cannot disambiguate without external constraints.
2. **Selectional Infelicity**: `eat a piano` is grammatical but semantically absurd.
3. **Background Knowledge Gap**: `John kicked the bucket.` — literal vs. idiomatic (`he died`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "other strategies" collectively address these issues by **injecting lexical world-knowledge** and **structured constraints** into the interpretation pipeline.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Selectional Restriction** | A typed constraint a predicate imposes on its arguments (e.g., `eat` requires `[+edible]` theme). |
| **Semantic Network** | A directed graph of concepts linked by labeled edges (`is-a`, `has-part`, `causes`). |
| **Conceptual Dependency (CD)** | Schank's theory using a small set of primitive acts (`PTRANS`, `ATRANS`, `INGEST`). |
| **Frame** | A data structure with slots and fillers representing a stereotyped situation (Minsky, 1974). |
| **FrameNet** | A computational lexicon of ~1,200 frames, each annotated on real sentences (Fillmore, Baker et al.). |
| **WordNet** | A large lexical database of English grouping words into **synsets** linked by hypernymy, hyponymy, etc. |
| **Case Role** | A semantic relation (Agent, Patient, Instrument, Location…) tying a noun phrase to a verb (Fillmore). |
| **Script** | A stereotyped sequence of events for familiar situations (restaurant, airport). |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Major "Other Strategies"

### 2.1.1 Selectional Restrictions
A predicate P places type constraints on each of its argument slots. Interpretation = matching the actual argument's semantic type against the expected type.

- `eat(Agent, Theme)` ⟹ `Agent ∈ [+animate]`, `Theme ∈ [+edible]`
- Violations → **Selectional Preference Violation** (Wilks, Preference Semantics).

**Mechanism:** Type checking on a typed-feature system or typed lambda calculus (λ-typed semantics).

### 2.1.2 Semantic Networks (Quillian, 1968)
Concepts are **nodes**; relations are **labeled directed edges**. Inheritance flows up via `is-a` edges, enabling **default reasoning** (if not contradicted at a child node).

```
    Animal            <-- root node (most general)
      |
    Mammal
      |
    Dog  <-- inherits "can-bark" from Mammal→Animal unless overridden
      |
    Fido  <-- specific instance
```

### 2.1.3 Conceptual Dependency (CD) — Schank, 1972
A *primitive-based* theory claiming all semantic meaning can be expressed via ~11 **primitive conceptualizations** (conceptual primitives) plus rules:

| Primitive | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `PTRANS`  | Physical transfer of location |
| `ATRANS`  | Abstract transfer (possession) |
| `MTRANS`  | Mental transfer (information) |
| `MBUILD`  | Mental construction of new info |
| `INGEST`  | Take in (eat/drink/breathe) |
| `EXPEL`   | Push out |
| `PROPEL`  | Apply physical force |
| `MOVE`    | Move a body part |
| `GRASP`   | Grasp an object |
| `ATTEND`  | Focus a sense organ |
| `SPEAK`   | Produce a sound |

> **Example:** `John ate the cake.` 
> $$\text{John} \xrightarrow{\text{INGEST}} \text{cake} \quad \text{(direction: FROM mouth TO stomach of John)}$$

### 2.1.4 Case Grammar (Fillmore, 1968)
Every sentence has an underlying **deep case structure**, independent of surface syntactic roles. Six original cases: **Agentive, Instrumental, Dative, Factitive, Locative, Objective**.

- Deep structure example: `The key opened the door.`
  - `Instrument(key) — open — Objective(door)`
- Unlike thematic roles, cases are **syntactico-semantic universals**, strictly numbered.

### 2.1.5 Frame Semantics & FrameNet (Fillmore, Baker et al., 1998+)
A **Frame** = a unified description of a stereotyped situation (e.g., *Commerce_buy*, *Apply_heat*, *Cognition*). It has:
- **Frame Elements (FEs)**: roles like `Buyer`, `Seller`, `Goods`, `Money`.
- **Lexical Units (LUs)**: words/phrases that evoke the frame.
- **Valence patterns**: how FEs map to syntactic positions.

> Example: `Commerce_buy` frame
> $$\text{Buyer} \xrightarrow{\text{pays Money}} \text{Seller} \xleftarrow{\text{transfers}} \text{Goods}$$

### 2.1.6 WordNet (Miller, Fellbaum, 1990+)
A manually curated **lexical ontology** of English. Structure:
- **Synset**: a set of cognitive synonyms representing one concept.
- **Relations**: hypernymy (`is-a`), hyponymy, meronymy (`part-of`), holonymy, entailment, similarity, attribute.
- **Polysemy count** = number of synsets a word belongs to.

### 2.1.7 Scripts (Schank & Abelson, 1977)
Pre-defined **stereotyped event sequences** for familiar scenarios. A script has:
- **Entry conditions**, **Scenes**, **Props**, **Roles**, **Results**.

> Restaurant Script:
> 1. Enter → 2. Sit → 3. Read Menu → 4. Order → 5. Eat → 6. Pay → 7. Leave

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Strategy | Representation Form | Core Operation | Failure Handling |
|---|----------|--------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Selectional Restrictions | Type signature `P : T1 × T2 → T` | Type unification / match | Soft constraints via probability |
| 2 | Semantic Network | Graph $G = (V, E, \text{label})$ | Inheritance + propagation | Default inheritance with overrides |
| 3 | Conceptual Dependency | Primitive-act tuples $(Actor, Act, Object, Direction, ...)$ | Reduction to 11 primitives | N/A — primitives are exhaustive by claim |
| 4 | Case Grammar | $(Predicate, \{Case_i : NP_i\})$ | Case role assignment | Re-analyze with different surface parse |
| 5 | FrameNet | $(Frame, \{FE_i : Lex_i\})$ | Frame activation + FE filling | Choose alternate LU → alternate frame |
| 6 | WordNet | $(\text{Synset}, R, \text{Gloss})$ | Synset lookup + graph traversal | Multiple synsets → sense disambiguation (Lesk, Wu-Palmer) |
| 7 | Scripts | $(\text{Script}, \{Scene_j : \text{Sequence}(E_k)\})$ | Scene matching + slot-filling | Mismatch → script failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit / Symbol Conventions used in NLP literature:**
> - $G = (V, E)$ — Graph with nodes $V$ and edges $E$
> - $|S|$ — number of synsets for a word $S$ (polysemy count)
> - $sim(w_1, w_2)$ — semantic similarity (range $[0, 1]$ for normalized metrics)
> - $d(w_1, w_2)$ — semantic distance, $d = \frac{1}{sim}$ in some formulations
> - **Wu-Palmer similarity:** $sim_{WP}(c_1, c_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(LCS(c_1, c_2))}{\text{depth}(c_1) + \text{depth}(c_2)}$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Strategy | Industry Use Case |
|----------|-------------------|
| **FrameNet** | PropBank / SRL systems, Google's AMR parsing, IBM Watson question-answering |
| **WordNet** | Search engines (Google's original "synonym expansion"), WSD in sentiment analysis |
| **CD Primitives** | Early information extraction, news event triplification (Reuters TAC-KBP) |
| **Case Grammar** | Modern Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) uses ~100 roles inspired by case grammar |
| **Semantic Networks** | Medical ontologies (SNOMED-CT), product taxonomies (Amazon, eBay), Google Knowledge Graph |
| **Scripts** | Dialogue state tracking in chatbots, smart-home intent prediction |
| **Selectional Restrictions** | Pre-training filters in LLMs, hallucination detection, plausibility scoring |
