# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Walkthrough 1 — Selectional Restriction Violation Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Sentence:** `The fridge drank the milk.`
> **Goal:** Detect whether the sentence violates selectional restrictions.

### Step 1: Lexical Retrieval
For the predicate `drink`, consult a lexicon (e.g., VerbNet or PropBank) and retrieve its argument structure and selectional preferences:

$$\text{drink}(Arg_1, Arg_2) \quad ; \quad Arg_1 \in [+animate], \quad Arg_2 \in [+liquid]$$

### Step 2: Identify Surface Arguments
- Surface subject: `The fridge` → Noun phrase referring to entity $e_1$.
- Surface object: `the milk` → Noun phrase referring to entity $e_2$.

### Step 3: Retrieve Semantic Types of Arguments
Using WordNet hypernym traversal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{fridge} &\rightarrow \text{fridge, n.1} \rightarrow \text{hyponym of} \rightarrow \text{appliance} \rightarrow \text{hyponym of} \rightarrow \text{artifact} \\
\text{milk} &\rightarrow \text{milk, n.1} \rightarrow \text{hyponym of} \rightarrow \text{beverage} \rightarrow \text{hyponym of} \rightarrow \text{food} \rightarrow \text{hyponym of} \rightarrow \text{substance} \rightarrow \text{liquid}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Type-Match Against Selectional Preferences

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type}(e_1) &= \text{artifact} \not\Rightarrow [+animate] \quad &\Rightarrow \text{VIOLATION on } Arg_1 \\
\text{type}(e_2) &= \text{liquid} \Rightarrow [+liquid] \quad &\Rightarrow \text{OK on } Arg_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Violation Severity Score (Wilks-style)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Severity}(e_1) &= \frac{\text{min\_distance}(\text{type}(e_1), [+animate])}{\text{max\_depth}} \\
&= \frac{\text{dist}(\text{artifact}, \text{animate})}{\text{max\_depth}} \\
&= \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Threshold } 0.4 \text{ crossed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Decision

$$
\text{Verdict} = \begin{cases}
\text{Normal} & \text{if Severity} < \tau \\
\text{Violated (anomalous/metaphorical)} & \text{if Severity} \geq \tau
\end{cases}
$$

Final verdict: **VIOLATED** → sentence is flagged as anomalous or metaphorical.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough 2 — Wu-Palmer Similarity Between Two Synsets

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Compute $sim_{WP}(\text{tiger}, \text{cat})$ using WordNet hierarchy.

### Step 1: Locate Synsets
- $\text{tiger} \rightarrow synset = \text{tiger.n.01}$
- $\text{cat} \rightarrow synset = \text{cat.n.01}$

### Step 2: Compute Depths

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{depth}(\text{tiger.n.01}) &= 14 \\
\text{depth}(\text{cat.n.01}) &= 13
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Locate Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS)
Walk up hypernym chains from both synsets until a common ancestor is found.

$$
\text{LCS}(\text{tiger.n.01}, \text{cat.n.01}) = \text{feline.n.01} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{depth}(\text{LCS}) = 12
$$

### Step 4: Apply Wu-Palmer Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{WP}(tiger, cat) &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(LCS)}{\text{depth}(tiger) + \text{depth}(cat)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 12}{14 + 13} \\
&= \frac{24}{27} \\
&= 0.8889
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpret

$sim_{WP} \approx 0.89$ → very high semantic similarity. ✓ Correct, since both are felines.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Walkthrough 3 — Conceptual Dependency Representation

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Sentence:** `John gave Mary a book.`

### Step 1: Identify Primitive Acts
Two primitive acts are involved:
- `ATRANS` (Abstract transfer — possession)
- `MTRANS` (Mental transfer — informing; optional)

### Step 2: Identify Conceptual Cases
- Actor: John
- Act: ATRANS
- Object: book (the thing transferred)
- Source: John (FROM)
- Recipient: Mary (TO)
- Direction: TO

### Step 3: Build CD Structure

$$
\text{John} \xrightarrow{\text{ATRANS}} \text{book}
\quad \text{with} \quad \text{FROM} = \text{John}, \quad \text{TO} = \text{Mary}
$$

The full graph:

```
       [John] ---ATRANS---> [book]
         |                    |
        FROM                 TO
         |                    |
         +----> [Mary] <------+
```

### Step 4: Verify Primitive Sufficiency
Compare against the 11 CD primitives — yes, ATRANS is canonical for transfer-of-possession.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection by Task

| Task | Best Strategy | Justification |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| Sense disambiguation in search | WordNet + Lesk algorithm | Synset lookup is fast, well-curated |
| Event extraction in news | Conceptual Dependency | Primitive acts are flat & easy to template |
| Plausibility scoring | Selectional Restrictions | Direct type-check |
| Dialogue state tracking | Scripts | Predefined scenarios match dialogue |
| Question answering (deep) | FrameNet | Frames give direct slot-question mappings |
| Knowledge graph completion | Semantic Network | Graph traversal is native |
| AMR parsing | Case Grammar (modern variant) | PropBank/AMR roles are descendants of cases |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Q: Briefly explain Selectional Restrictions with an example. List two ways a system can handle violations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Selectional Restrictions are typed constraints imposed by a predicate on its arguments, ensuring semantic plausibility. For instance, the verb `eat` requires its theme argument to be `[+edible]`. Thus, `John ate an apple` is licensed, while `John ate a rock` is violated.
**Handling violations:**
1. Reject the sentence as ill-formed (strict mode).
2. Assign a low plausibility score and allow downstream modules to decide (Wilks' preference semantics).

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark example, 1 mark handling.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Q: Define "Frame" in Fillmore's Frame Semantics. Name two frames and their frame elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Frame** is a structured representation of a stereotyped situation, consisting of **Frame Elements (FEs)** which are semantic roles and **Lexical Units (LUs)** which are words evoking the frame.
- *Commerce_buy*: FE = Buyer, Seller, Goods, Money.
- *Apply_heat*: FE = Cook, Food, Heating_instrument, Temperature.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for two frame examples with FEs.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain **Conceptual Dependency (CD) theory** proposed by Schank. List all primitive ACTs in CD with their meanings. Represent the sentence *"John ate the cake using a fork."* using a CD graph. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Semantic Networks** and **Conceptual Dependency**. Show how inheritance works in a semantic network with an example involving at least 5 nodes. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Solution to (a):

**Conceptual Dependency (CD)** is a knowledge representation scheme proposed by **Roger Schank (1972)** which claims that actions in natural language can be represented using a small set (~11) of primitive conceptualizations (ACTs), independent of the surface linguistic form. CD uses conceptual cases — ACTOR, ACT, OBJECT, SOURCE, RECIPIENT, INSTRUMENT, DIRECTION, STATE — that are language-universal.

**Primitives (11 ACTs):**

1. `PTRANS` — Physical transfer (move location)
2. `ATRANS` — Abstract transfer (change possession)
3. `MTRANS` — Mental transfer (communicate info)
4. `MBUILD` — Mental construction (form new idea)
5. `INGEST` — Take in (eat/drink/breathe)
6. `EXPEL` — Push out (cry/sweat)
7. `PROPEL` — Apply physical force
8. `MOVE` — Move body part
9. `GRASP` — Grasp object
10. `ATTEND` — Focus sense organ
11. `SPEAK` — Produce sound

**CD Representation of *"John ate the cake using a fork."*:**

The sentence decomposes into two primitive acts:

- `INGEST`: John consumes cake.
- `GRASP` (or `PROPEL`): of fork, to enable ingest.

CD graph:

```
   John ---INGEST---> cake
     |                  |
    INSTRUMENT          TO
     |                  |
   fork ----GRASP------+
                       |
                     cake (object)
```

Full formal CD structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&ACT_1 = \langle ACTOR = \text{John},\ ACT = \text{INGEST},\ OBJECT = \text{cake},\ INSTRUMENT = \text{fork},\ DIRECTION = TO \rangle \\
&ACT_2 = \langle ACTOR = \text{John},\ ACT = \text{GRASP},\ OBJECT = \text{fork},\ DIRECTION = TO \rangle \\
&\text{Dependency: } ACT_2 \text{ enables } ACT_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):** 2 marks theory, 3 marks listing primitives, 2 marks for CD graph of the given sentence.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Semantic Network | Conceptual Dependency |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| Primitive count | No fixed primitive set; nodes are arbitrary concepts | Fixed 11 ACTs only |
| Relation type | Generic labeled edges (`is-a`, `has-part`, `causes`) | Strict conceptual cases |
| Inheritance | Yes, via `is-a` | No; explicit dependency only |
| Application | Static ontology / world knowledge | Event / action representation |
| Reasoning | Default inheritance | Causal/temporal dependencies between acts |

**Example: Inheritance in Semantic Network (5+ nodes):**

Consider nodes: `Animal → Mammal → Dog → Labrador → Fido (instance)`.

Define edge `is-a` between parent and child, and define property `has-fur: TRUE` at node `Mammal`.

```
Animal  ---is-a--->  Mammal  ---is-a--->  Dog  ---is-a--->  Labrador  ---is-a--->  Fido
   |                     |                |                 |                  |
 (root)           has-fur:TRUE     has-4-legs:TRUE    color:Yellow      age:5
                     (inherited)   (inherited)        (overridden)
```

**Reasoning (default inheritance):**
1. `Fido` inherits `has-fur: TRUE` from `Mammal` through the chain.
2. `Fido` inherits `has-4-legs: TRUE` from `Dog` unless overridden.
3. If at `Labrador` we set `has-fur: FALSE` (e.g., hypothetical hairless breed), then Fido gets FALSE for that single property (overriding default).

Formally, for any property $p$ and node $n$:

$$
\text{value}(p, n) = \begin{cases}
\text{local}(p, n) & \text{if defined at } n \\
\text{value}(p, parent(n)) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):** 3 marks comparison, 2 marks inheritance explanation, 2 marks for 5-node worked example.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall):**
> - Students often confuse **Thematic Roles** (Agent, Patient) with **Fillmore's Deep Cases** (Agentive, Instrumental, Dative, Factitive, Locative, Objective). They are **not identical** — Fillmore's cases are syntactico-semantic universals, distinct from the 6/8/35 thematic role lists.
> - In CD questions, students forget that **dependency between acts** (e.g., GRASP enables INGEST) must be drawn explicitly — not just two isolated primitive acts.
> - For Wu-Palmer questions, forgetting to take the **Lowest** Common Subsumer (LCS) is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain **Fillmore's Case Grammar**. How does it differ from traditional Subject-Predicate grammar? Represent the case structure of *"The hammer broke the window."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the structure of **WordNet**. Define **synset**, **hypernymy**, **meronymy**, and **polysemy**. Compute the **Wu-Palmer similarity** between synsets `dog.n.01` and `wolf.n.01` given:
- depth(`dog.n.01`) = 12
- depth(`wolf.n.01`) = 13
- depth(LCS) = 11
**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question B

#### Solution to (a):

**Fillmore's Case Grammar (1968)** rejects the view that syntax alone determines sentence meaning. It postulates that every sentence has an underlying **deep structure** composed of a **modality** and a **proposition**, where the proposition contains a **predicate** and one or more **deep cases** (semantic roles tied to noun phrases).

**Original six cases:**
1. **Agentive (A)** — animate instigator of action.
2. **Instrumental (I)** — inanimate cause / tool.
3. **Dative (D)** — animate being affected.
4. **Factitive (F)** — object resulting from action.
5. **Locative (L)** — location of action.
6. **Objective (O)** — semantically neutral patient.

**Difference from Subject-Predicate grammar:**

| Aspect | Traditional Grammar | Case Grammar |
|--------|--------------------|--------------|
| Atomic unit | NP, VP, S | Modality + Proposition (predicate + cases) |
| Case assignment | By syntactic position | By deep semantic role |
| Surfaceness | Tied to syntax | Independent of syntax |
| Example | `The key opened the door.` → S → NP VP | `key` is Instrumental, not Subject-as-Agent |

**Case Structure of *"The hammer broke the window."*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Proposition} &= \langle \text{Predicate} = \text{BREAK},\ \text{Instrumental} = \text{hammer},\ \text{Objective} = \text{window} \rangle \\
\text{Modality} &= \text{Past Indicative}
\end{aligned}
$$

Surface `The hammer` is syntactically the subject but semantically fills the **Instrumental** case, not Agentive. This is the central insight of Case Grammar.

**Mark split (7 marks):** 3 marks for theory with 6 cases listed, 2 marks for differences table, 2 marks for case structure of given sentence.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**WordNet Structure:**
WordNet is a large lexical database of English, organized around **synsets** (sets of cognitive synonyms). Each synset represents one distinct concept and is linked to others through semantic relations.

- **Synset:** A group of synonymous words sharing one meaning, e.g., `{car, auto, automobile, machine, motorcar}`.
- **Hypernymy (`is-a`):** Y is hypernym of X if X is a kind of Y. e.g., `dog` is-a `canine`, `canine` is-a `mammal`.
- **Meronymy (`part-of`):** Y is meronym of X if Y is part of X. e.g., `wheel` is part-of `car`.
- **Polysemy:** The number of distinct synsets (= senses) a word belongs to. e.g., `bank` has ~18 senses; polysemy count = 18.

**Wu-Palmer Similarity Calculation:**

Given:
- depth(`dog.n.01`) = 12
- depth(`wolf.n.01`) = 13
- depth(LCS) = 11

Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{WP}(dog, wolf) &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(LCS)}{\text{depth}(dog) + \text{depth}(wolf)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 11}{12 + 13} \\
&= \frac{22}{25} \\
&= 0.88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Similarity = 0.88 (high) — semantically very close, which is correct since both are canines (LCS = canine).

**Mark split (7 marks):** 2 marks WordNet structure, 2 marks for four-term definitions, 3 marks for Wu-Palmer computation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In **Wu-Palmer questions**, students sometimes use the **highest** common subsumer — but the correct one is the **Lowest** (most specific) Common Subsumer (LCS). Always walk up from BOTH synsets until they meet at the deepest possible shared ancestor.
> - In **Case Grammar questions**, do **NOT** confuse Fillmore's original 6 cases with thematic roles. Fillmores **Agentive ≠ Agent** in all contexts. The **Instrumental** case is filled by an inanimate cause, while an animate cause in some later treatments becomes Agent.
> - In **CD questions**, missing the **INSTRUMENT case** in a sentence containing *"with"* or *"using"* leads to −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Other Strategies for Semantic Interpretation**

### 📌 Core Strategy Inventory (MUST remember all 7)
- ☐ **Selectional Restrictions** — Type constraints on predicate arguments.
- ☐ **Semantic Networks** — Graph of concepts with `is-a` inheritance.
- ☐ **Conceptual Dependency (CD)** — Schank; 11 ACTs.
- ☐ **Case Grammar** — Fillmore; 6 deep cases; modality + proposition.
- ☐ **Frame Semantics / FrameNet** — Frames with FEs and LUs.
- ☐ **WordNet** — Synsets + hypernymy / hyponymy / meronymy / polysemy.
- ☐ **Scripts** — Schank & Abelson; stereotyped event sequences.

### 📌 Memorization Anchors
- ☐ **CD primitives (11)**: PTRANS, ATRANS, MTRANS, MBUILD, INGEST, EXPEL, PROPEL, MOVE, GRASP, ATTEND, SPEAK.
- ☐ **Fillmore's 6 cases**: Agentive, Instrumental, Dative, Factitive, Locative, Objective.
- ☐ **Wu-Palmer formula**: $sim_{WP} = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(c_1) + depth(c_2)}$.
- ☐ **FrameNet components**: Frame + Frame Elements + Lexical Units.

### 📌 Exam Hot-Spots
- ☐ Distinguish **Thematic Roles** vs **Fillmore's Deep Cases** — common 14-mark question.
- ☐ Wu-Palmer computation with given depths — frequently appears in Part B.
- ☐ CD representation of sentences involving two acts + an INSTRUMENT dependency.
- ☐ Compare/contrast strategy pairs (Semantic Network vs CD, FrameNet vs Case Grammar).
- ☐ Define + illustrate 4 WordNet relations in one answer.

### 📌 Frequent Pitfalls
- ☐ Confusing `hypernym` direction (parent vs child).
- ☐ Using the **highest** common subsumer instead of **lowest**.
- ☐ Forgetting INSTRUMENT case in CD.
- ☐ Saying "Agent" instead of "Agentive case" in Fillmore answers.
- ☐ Writing selectional restriction examples without specifying the type feature (`[+animate]`, `[+edible]`).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scoping and the Interpretation of Noun Phrases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scoping and the Interpretation of Noun Phrases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scoping — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scoping (Quantifier Scope / Operator Scope):** Scoping is the process of determining the *extent of influence* of a logical operator (quantifier, negation, modal) over the syntactic constituents of a sentence. It answers the question: *"Which parts of the sentence does this operator govern?"*

In formal semantics, every quantified expression (e.g., *every*, *some*, *no*, *most*) carries a **scope** that defines the set of entities over which the quantification is evaluated. When a sentence contains more than one quantifier, the relative ordering of their scopes often produces **scope ambiguities**.

Mathematically, scope can be expressed as a dominance relation in the syntactic tree:

$$
\text{scope}(\text{Op}) = \{ x \in \text{Tree} \mid \text{Op} \text{ c-commands } x \}
$$

### 1.2 Interpretation of Noun Phrases — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **NP Interpretation:** The semantic act of mapping a *Noun Phrase* (NP) to a discourse referent or to a set of entities in the world/model. Different NP types (definite, indefinite, quantified, generic) require *different interpretation rules*.

The interpretation process involves three sub-tasks:
1. **Reference Resolution** — binding the NP to a real-world entity.
2. **Binding** — linking pronouns/reflexives to their antecedents.
3. **Co-reference** — deciding whether two NPs refer to the same entity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Scope as a "Spotlight on a Stage":**
> Imagine a theatre with two spotlights (operators `every` and `some`). The **beam of `every`** lights up a set of actors (entities), and the **beam of `some`** lights up a possibly overlapping set. The **scope** of each operator is the *area its spotlight illuminates*. When the spotlights overlap, the audience sees *different "shows"* depending on the order — this is exactly what a *scope ambiguity* is.

> For **NP Interpretation**, picture a **name tag at a conference**: a *definite NP* ("the professor") assumes a unique person already in the room; an *indefinite NP* ("a professor") introduces a new, possibly unfamiliar person; a *quantified NP* ("every professor") refers to the entire group. Each tag requires a different rule to find its wearer.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Binding Condition C** — An R-expression must be free everywhere (Principle C, Chomsky 1981).
- **c-command** — *Constituent command*: Node A c-commands node B iff the smallest subtree containing A also contains B, and A does not dominate B and B does not dominate A.
- **Domain of a Quantifier (D)** — the set of entities the quantifier ranges over.
- **Extension (‖·‖ᴹ)** — the semantic value of an expression in model M.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scope of Quantifiers — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify Quantifiers (Q):** Locate all quantified NPs (*every, some, no, most, each, all*).
2. **Build Scope Trees:** Assign each quantifier a potential scope position in the parse tree.
3. **Apply C-Command:** A quantifier Q₁ has *wide scope* over Q₂ if the node of Q₁ c-commands the node of Q₂.
4. **Resolve Ambiguity:** Use pragmatics, word order, and focus to choose the preferred scope.
5. **Evaluate Truth Conditions:** Compute truth under both readings; pick the contextually salient one.

### 2.2 Scope Ambiguity — The Core "Why" and "How"

**Example sentence:**
*Every student read a book.*

| Scope Reading | Logical Form | Truth Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Wide scope of *every* | $\forall x [\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y [\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x,y)]]$ | Each student read *some* book (possibly different). |
| Wide scope of *a* | $\exists y [\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x [\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y)]]$ | There is a *single* book that all students read. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this ambiguous?** Because natural language syntax does not fix the relative scope of quantifiers — both readings are grammatically valid. Only context/discourse narrows it down.

### 2.3 Interpretation of Different NP Types

| NP Type | Example | Interpretation Rule | Set-Theoretic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proper Noun** | *Kerala* | Direct reference (rigid designator) | $\text{Kerala}^M \in D_e$ |
| **Definite NP** | *the prime minister* | Uniqueness presupposition (Russellian definite) | $\iota x.\,\text{PM}(x)$ |
| **Indefinite NP** | *a man* | Existential introduction (∃) | $\lambda P.\,\exists x.\,\text{Man}(x) \wedge P(x)$ |
| **Quantified NP** | *every linguist* | Generalized quantifier | $\lambda P.\,\forall x[\text{Ling}(x) \rightarrow P(x)]$ |
| **Pronoun** | *he / she / it* | Bound variable / free variable | $x_i$ (indexed) |
| **Generic NP** | *tigers are striped* | Habitual / generic operator (Gen) | $\text{Gen}_{x}(\text{Tiger}(x),\text{Striped}(x))$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "$\iota$" (iota) operator** is the *definite description* operator: $\iota x.\,\phi(x)$ denotes the *unique* $x$ satisfying $\phi$. It is used to formalize the meaning of *the*.

### 2.4 Binding Theory (Chomsky 1981)

Binding principles govern the relationship between an NP and its coindexed antecedent.

| Principle | Statement | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle A** | An anaphor (*himself*) must be bound in its governing category. | *John$_i$ saw himself$_i$.* ✓ |
| **Principle B** | A pronoun must be free in its governing category. | *John$_i$ saw him$_j$ ($j \neq i$).* ✓ |
| **Principle C** | An R-expression must be free everywhere. | *He$_i$ saw John$_i$.* ✗ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Governing Category** = the smallest IP/CP/TP containing the NP and a coindexed subject. Students often confuse *governing category* with *clause*.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\forall x\,\phi$ | Universal quantification | Generalized quantifier |
| $\exists x\,\phi$ | Existential quantification | Indefinite NP meaning |
| $\iota x.\,\phi$ | Definite description (the unique x) | *the* |
| $\lambda x.\,\phi$ | Lambda abstraction | Function from entities to truth |
| $\text{Gen}(\phi, \psi)$ | Generic operator | Habituals, generics |
| $c$-command | Constituent command relation | Scope, binding |
| $\text{Bind}(\alpha, \beta)$ | α binds β iff α c-commands β & coindexed | Binding theory |
| $\Vert \alpha \Vert^M$ | Extension of α in model M | Model-theoretic semantics |
| $\text{SVAR}(\alpha)$ | Syntactic variation set (scopal readings) | Scope ambiguity |
| $\text{DRS}$ | Discourse Representation Structure | DRT (Kamp & Reyle) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Information Extraction (IE):** Quantifier scope resolution is critical in biomedical NLP (*"Every patient received 5mg of Drug A"*).
- **Machine Translation (MT):** Incorrect scope reordering causes translation errors, especially between SOV and SVO languages.
- **Question Answering (QA):** Resolving "*the first president of the US*" requires $\iota$-operator logic.
- **Legal NLP:** Contracts hinge on scope of "*all*", "*any*", "*each*".
- **Dialogue Systems:** Pronoun resolution is essential for coherent multi-turn conversations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Semantics of *Every Student Read a Book*

We compute truth conditions under both scope readings using **Montague Grammar** style compositional semantics.

#### Step 1 — Lexical Entries

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{every} &\;\mapsto\; \lambda P.\,\lambda Q.\,\forall x\,[P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)] \\
\text{a} &\;\mapsto\; \lambda P.\,\lambda Q.\,\exists x\,[P(x) \wedge Q(x)] \\
\text{student} &\;\mapsto\; \lambda y.\,\text{Student}(y) \\
\text{book} &\;\mapsto\; \lambda y.\,\text{Book}(y) \\
\text{read} &\;\mapsto\; \lambda y.\,\lambda x.\,\text{Read}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Syntactic Parse Tree (Simplified)

$$
\text{[S [NP Every student] [VP [V read] [NP a book]]]}
$$

#### Step 3 — Reading I: *Every* has wide scope over *a* (surface scope)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{``a book''} &\mapsto \lambda Q.\,\exists y\,[\text{Book}(y) \wedge Q(y)] \\
\text{VP} &\mapsto \lambda x.\,\exists y\,[\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x,y)] \\
\text{``Every student''} &\mapsto \lambda P.\,\forall x\,[\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow P(x)] \\
\text{S}_{\text{I}} &\mapsto \forall x\,[\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y\,[\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x,y)]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain English:** "For every student $x$, there exists a book $y$ that $x$ read." Books may differ per student.

#### Step 4 — Reading II: *A* takes wide scope (inverse scope)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VP application (intermediate)} &\mapsto \lambda y.\,\forall x\,[\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y)] \\
\text{Combine with `a book'} &\mapsto \exists y\,[\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x\,[\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y)]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain English:** "There is a particular book $y$ such that every student read $y$."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope inversion** is allowed in natural language even when it violates surface structure. This is a key argument for using **Logical Form (LF)** in syntax–semantics interface.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Russellian Analysis of *The King of France is Bald*

This is the famous **Russell (1905)** example showing how definite descriptions encode an existence + uniqueness presupposition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{``The King of France''} &\mapsto \iota x.\,\text{KingOfFrance}(x) \\
\text{``is bald''} &\mapsto \lambda x.\,\text{Bald}(x) \\
\text{Composite} &\mapsto \text{Bald}(\iota x.\,\text{KingOfFrance}(x))
\end{aligned}
$$

Russell proposed *three* propositions are actually asserted:
1. $\exists x\,[\text{KingOfFrance}(x)]$ — Existence
2. $\forall x\,\forall y\,[(\text{KingOfFrance}(x) \wedge \text{KingOfFrance}(y)) \rightarrow x = y]$ — Uniqueness
3. $\forall x\,[\text{KingOfFrance}(x) \rightarrow \text{Bald}(x)]$ — Predication

> [!TIP]
> This is the origin of **Presupposition Projection** — definite NPs *presuppose* existence and uniqueness of the referent.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Scope Resolution Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Example Sentence | Preferred Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Scope** | Use linear order of quantifiers | *Every boy ate a sandwich.* | $\forall > \exists$ |
| **Inverse Scope** | QP in object position takes wide scope | *A sandwich was eaten by every boy.* | $\exists > \forall$ |
| **Raising / Quantifier Raising (QR)** | Move QP covertly to adjoin to S | *A linguist admires every novelist.* | $\exists > \forall$ (common) |
| **Focus-Driven Scope** | Focused constituent takes narrow scope | *Even MARY ordered SALAD.* | Salient focus |
| **Discourse-Linked Scope** | Coherent with preceding context | *A man walked in. He sat down.* | Anaphoric binding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantifier Raising (QR)** — proposed by May (1977, 1985) — covertly moves a QP to a position where it c-commands the rest of the clause, deriving scope.

### 3.4 Worked Example — *Donkey Sentences*

**Sentence:** *Every farmer who owns a donkey beats it.*

Naïve reading produces a problem: the indefinite *a donkey* is inside a relative clause, but the pronoun *it* is outside.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial LF (problematic):} \quad & \forall x\,[\text{Farmer}(x) \wedge \exists y\,[\text{Donkey}(y) \wedge \text{Owns}(x,y)] \rightarrow \text{Beats}(x,y)] \\
\text{Problem:} \quad & y \text{ is not in scope of } x \text{ at the point of `it'.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution via Discourse Representation Theory (DRT — Kamp & Reyle 1993):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRS:}\quad & [x,y \mid \text{Farmer}(x),\ \text{Donkey}(y),\ \text{Owns}(x,y)] \\
& \rightarrow \text{Beats}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Both variables are *universe-bound* by the DRS, so *it* is coindexed with the donkey $y$ inside the restrictor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Scope Resolution Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Sentence] --> B[Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    B --> C[Parser Generates Syntax Tree]
    C --> D[Identify Quantifiers NPs Pronouns]
    D --> E[Compute c command Relations]
    E --> F{Quantifier Raising Required?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Covert QR May 1985]
    F -- No --> H[Assign Surface Scope]
    G --> I[Generate All Scopal LF Readings]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Filter by Pragmatic Constraints]
    J --> K[Discourse Model Lookup]
    K --> L[Resolve Anaphora and Co reference]
    L --> M[Final Logical Form with Scopal Interpretation]
    M --> N[Semantic Representation in Model M]
```

### 4.2 Noun Phrase Interpretation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Input NP] --> T{NP Type Classification}
    T -- Proper Noun --> P1[Direct Reference via Model M]
    T -- Definite NP the --> P2[Apply iota Operator Uniqueness Check]
    T -- Indefinite NP a an --> P3[Existential Introduction new referent]
    T -- Quantified NP every some --> P4[Generalized Quantifier lambda Abstraction]
    T -- Pronoun he she it --> P5{Bind to Antecedent?}
    P5 -- Yes Local Antecedent --> P6[Principle A or B Apply]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Free Variable Deixis or Exophora]
    T -- Generic NP --> P8[Apply Generic Operator Gen]
    P2 --> R[Output NP Interpretation]
    P3 --> R
    P1 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P6 --> R
    P7 --> R
    P8 --> R
```

### 4.3 Binding Theory Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Principle A Anaphor] --> A1[himself herself]
    A1 --> A2[Must be bound in Governing Category]
    B[Principle B Pronoun] --> B1[he she it them]
    B1 --> B2[Must be FREE in Governing Category]
    C[Principle C R expression] --> C1[John Mary the man]
    C1 --> C2[Must be FREE EVERYWHERE]
    A2 --> R[B c commands check]
    B2 --> R
    C2 --> R
    R --> O[Well formedness Judgement]
```

### 4.4 Scope Ambiguity Example — *Every student read a book*

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Sentence Every student read a book] --> S1[Scope Reading 1]
    S0 --> S2[Scope Reading 2]
    S1 --> L1[Every > A]
    S1 --> M1[forall x Student x implies exists y Book y and Read x y]
    S2 --> L2[A > Every]
    S2 --> M2[exists y Book y and forall x Student x implies Read x y]
    M1 --> OUT[Two Distinct Truth Conditions]
    M2 --> OUT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define *scoping* in NLP. Illustrate with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

Scoping is the process of determining the syntactic-semantic domain over which a logical operator (quantifier, negation, modal) has influence. It specifies the constituents that fall within the operator's interpretation.

**Example:** In the sentence *"Every student read a book"*, the quantifier *every* may take scope over the entire sentence (wide scope) or *a book* may take scope over *every student* (inverse scope), producing two distinct truth conditions.

(3 marks: Definition – 1.5, Example – 1.5)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List the three principles of Binding Theory with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Principle A:** An anaphor (reflexive) must be bound in its governing category.
   *e.g.,* John$_i$ hurt himself$_i$. ✓
2. **Principle B:** A pronoun must be free (unbound) in its governing category.
   *e.g.,* John$_i$ hurt him$_j$ ($j \neq i$). ✓
3. **Principle C:** An R-expression (referential NP) must be free everywhere.
   *e.g.,* \*He$_i$ met John$_i$. ✗

(3 marks: 1 mark per principle with example)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of *quantifier scope ambiguity* in natural language. With suitable examples, discuss the difference between **surface scope** and **inverse scope** readings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Quantifier scope ambiguity arises when a sentence contains two or more quantified expressions whose relative scope is not uniquely determined by surface syntax, yielding multiple interpretations.

**Surface Scope:** The quantifier occurring earlier in the linear order takes wider scope.

**Inverse Scope:** A quantifier in a syntactically embedded position takes wider scope than a syntactically higher one, requiring covert movement (Quantifier Raising).

**Example:** *A linguist admires every novelist.*

- **Surface Scope:** $\exists > \forall$ — "There is a particular linguist who admires every novelist."
- **Inverse Scope:** $\forall > \exists$ — "For every novelist, there is some linguist (possibly different) who admires them."

Both readings are grammatical. In English, *inverse scope* is constrained — it is more freely available with *some* than with *every*. The LF (Logical Form) level hosts the scopal relations, while pragmatics selects the contextually salient reading.

(7 marks: Definition – 2, Surface vs inverse – 3, Example LF forms – 2)

---

**(b)** Describe the **Russellian analysis of definite descriptions**. How does it differ from the *referential / direct reference* view? Explain with the example *"The King of France is bald."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Russellian Analysis (1905):** A definite NP of the form *the F* is analyzed as a complex predicate encoding three propositions:

1. **Existence:** $\exists x\,F(x)$
2. **Uniqueness:** $\forall x\,\forall y\,[(F(x) \wedge F(y)) \rightarrow x = y]$
3. **Predication:** $F(x) \rightarrow G(x)$

For *"The King of France is bald"*:

- There exists a King of France.
- There is at most one King of France.
- That unique individual is bald.

If the existence or uniqueness condition fails, the sentence is **false** (Russell) or **truth-valueless** (Strawson's revised view).

**Direct Reference (Kripke / Donnellan):** A definite NP like *the King of France* is a *rigid designator* referring directly to an individual, independent of descriptive content. The sentence's truth depends on whether the referent satisfies the predicate *bald*.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Russellian | Direct Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Description-based | Individual-based |
| Failure of referent | Sentence is false | Presupposition failure |
| Proper names | Abbreviated descriptions | Rigid designators |
| Modal contexts | Fails substitutivity | Preserved |

(7 marks: Russellian analysis – 3, Direct reference – 2, Comparison – 2)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three principles of Binding Theory**. Discuss the role of *c-command* and *governing category* in determining binding. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Binding Theory (Chomsky 1981) regulates the coindexing between an NP and a possible antecedent.

- **Principle A:** *himself* must be bound in its governing category.
- **Principle B:** *he / him* must be free in its governing category.
- **Principle C:** *John* (R-expression) must be free.

**c-command:** Node A c-commands B if the first branching node dominating A also dominates B (and A does not dominate B).

**Governing Category:** The minimal IP/CP/TP containing the NP and a subject coindexed with it.

**Diagrammatic Example:**

```
[IP Subject [I' [VP ... himself ...]]]
```

Here *Subject* c-commands *himself* inside VP, and the IP is the governing category. Therefore *himself* can be bound by *Subject* (Principle A satisfied).

(7 marks: Three principles – 3, c-command & gov. cat. – 4)

---

**(b)** Discuss **Discourse Representation Theory (DRT)**. How does DRT solve the problem of *donkey sentences*? Provide a formal DRS for the sentence *"Every farmer who owns a donkey beats it."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

DRT (Kamp 1981, Kamp & Reyle 1993) represents meaning as a **Discourse Representation Structure (DRS)** consisting of:
- A **universe of discourse variables** $U = \{x_1, x_2, \dots\}$
- A set of **conditions** $C$ (atomic or complex).

A DRS is recursively interpreted: a DRS is true iff there is an embedding of $U$ into the model such that all conditions are satisfied.

**Problem of Donkey Sentences:** In *"Every farmer who owns a donkey beats it"*, the indefinite *a donkey* is syntactically embedded inside the relative clause, yet the pronoun *it* appears to be bound by it. Classical predicate logic cannot capture this.

**DRT Solution:** Variables introduced by an indefinite inside a restrictor are added to the *universe of the embedding DRS*, making them accessible to subsequent pronouns.

**DRS for the sentence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRS}_1 &= [x, y \mid \text{Farmer}(x),\ \text{Donkey}(y),\ \text{Owns}(x,y)] \\
\text{DRS}_2 &= [\, \mid \text{Beats}(x, y)\,] \\
\text{Full DRS} &= [\text{DRS}_1] \rightarrow [\text{DRS}_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

The implication DRS encodes the conditional structure: every farmer–donkey pair satisfying the restrictor is in the beating relation.

(7 marks: DRT basics – 3, Donkey problem + DRS – 4)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly confuse *governing category* with *clause* or *sentence*. It is the *smallest IP/TP containing the NP and a coindexed subject*. **(–1 mark)**
> - In scope questions, forgetting to write the **complete logical form with both quantifiers** loses 2–3 marks.
> - For DRT, students often forget to specify the **universe variables** explicitly — this is mandatory.
> - Mixing up **Principle A** (reflexive — bound) and **Principle B** (pronoun — free) is the single most common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Scoping** determines the *domain* of a quantifier/operator over the sentence.
- **Surface scope** follows linear order; **inverse scope** requires QR.
- **NP interpretation** types: proper noun, definite ($\iota$), indefinite ($\exists$), quantified ($\forall$), pronoun (bound/free), generic (Gen).
- **Binding Theory** has three principles (A, B, C) operating on anaphors, pronouns, and R-expressions respectively.
- **c-command** and **governing category** are the structural primitives of binding.
- **Russell (1905)** analyzed definite descriptions as existence + uniqueness + predication.
- **Kripke / Donnellan** countered with direct reference and rigid designation.
- **Donkey sentences** are handled elegantly by **DRT** (Kamp & Reyle 1993) via DRS boxes.
- **Quantifier Raising (QR)** — covert syntactic movement to derive scopal readings.
- **Generalized Quantifier Theory** (Barwise & Cooper 1981) treats every/some as functions between sets.
- **Presupposition** is triggered by definite NPs, factives, and focus particles.
- Applications: IE, MT, QA, legal NLP, dialogue systems.
- Key notational symbols: $\forall$, $\exists$, $\iota$, $\lambda$, Gen, $\text{Bind}$, $c$-command.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Scoping and the Interpretation of Noun Phrases Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Knowledge Representation and Reasoning — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR&R)** is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Natural Language Processing concerned with how an intelligent agent can encode information about the world in a form that a computer system can utilize to solve complex tasks such as diagnosis, dialogue understanding, question answering, and commonsense inference. It combines a **representation language** (how facts are encoded) with an **inference mechanism** (how new facts are derived).

In the context of **PEADT635 – Natural Language Processing**, KR&R provides the *semantic backbone* that allows NLP systems to move beyond surface-level string matching toward genuine linguistic understanding. Without structured knowledge, an NLP system cannot disambiguate polysemous words, perform coreference resolution across long discourses, or answer reading-comprehension questions that require world knowledge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library & Librarian" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of Knowledge Representation as the *cataloging system* of a vast library, and Reasoning as the *librarian* who uses the catalog to answer questions you have never asked before.
>
> - **Books on shelves** = Raw data and facts in the world.
> - **Dewey Decimal / Card Catalog** = The *representation scheme* (frames, semantic nets, logic).
> - **The Librarian's training** = The *inference engine* (forward chaining, resolution, abduction).
> - **Walking back with a new, related book** = *Derived knowledge* (entailment).

If the catalog is messy, even the smartest librarian fails. If the librarian cannot deduce connections, even a perfect catalog is useless. **KR is the storage; KR+R is the engine that makes storage pay off.**

## 1.3 Why KR&R is Central to Modern NLP

Modern NLP applications that fundamentally rely on KR&R include:

| Application | Role of KR&R |
|---|---|
| **Question Answering (QA)** | Maps natural-language queries to logical form over a knowledge base. |
| **Dialogue Systems** | Maintains dialogue state, user intent, and world context across turns. |
| **Machine Reading Comprehension** | Grounds answers in a structured knowledge graph. |
| **Text Summarization** | Identifies salient entities and relations via ontology-driven salience. |
| **Information Extraction** | Uses ontological constraints to validate extracted triples. |
| **Common-Sense Reasoning** | Leverages resources like ConceptNet, ATOMIC, WordNet+ |

## 1.4 The Five Categories of Knowledge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner will frequently test the student's ability to **classify** an example fact into the correct knowledge category. Memorize the five:

1. **Declarative (Know-What)** — Static facts about the world. *Example:* "Paris is the capital of France."
2. **Procedural (Know-How)** — Action sequences and skills. *Example:* "To boil water, heat it to 100 °C at 1 atm."
3. **Meta-Knowledge (Know-About-Knowledge)** — Knowledge *about* the knowledge base itself. *Example:* "The rule 'X is a bird ⇒ X can fly' is *uncertain*."
4. **Structural (Know-What-with-What)** — Knowledge describing relationships between concepts. *Example:* "A 'hand' is part of a 'body'."
5. **Heuristic (Know-Why / Rule-of-Thumb)** — Empirical shortcuts. *Example:* "If the patient has fever AND cough, suspect infection."

## 1.5 Key Metrics & Standards in KR&R

The following standardized metrics and resources are **mandatory KTU terminology**:

- **W3C Standards:** **RDF** (Resource Description Framework), **RDFS**, **OWL** (Web Ontology Language), **SPARQL** query language.
- **Reasoner Soundness:** A reasoning system is **sound** if every derived fact is logically entailed (no false positives).
- **Reasoner Completeness:** A reasoning system is **complete** if every entailed fact can eventually be derived (no false negatives).
- **Decidability:** Whether the reasoning process is guaranteed to terminate. First-Order Logic (FOL) is **semi-decidable**; Description Logic (the basis of OWL) is often decidable.
- **Ontology Population Density:** Measured in **triples per entity (TPE)** — a benchmark for KG completeness.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Representation Language + Inference Engine

A KR&R system is the union of two artifacts:

$$
\text{KR\&R System} = \langle \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{I} \rangle
$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the **formal representation language** (syntax + semantics) and $\mathcal{I}$ is the **inference engine** that operates on $\mathcal{L}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in NLP:** A chatbot built on **pure regex** has $\mathcal{L} = $ patterns and $\mathcal{I} = $ pattern matching — *extremely weak*. A system built on **FOL + resolution** has $\mathcal{L} = $ first-order sentences and $\mathcal{I} = $ a theorem prover — *expressive but expensive*. A pragmatic NLP pipeline often uses **frames or description logic** as a middle ground.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Choosing a Representation Scheme

When an NLP engineer designs a KR layer, the following decision ladder is followed:

1. **Identify the domain boundaries** — Is the domain open-world (the Web) or closed-world (a medical records DB)?
2. **Quantify expressiveness needed** — Do you need to express *negation*? *Disjunction*? *Existential* and *universal* quantifiers?
3. **Quantify inference speed required** — Is the application real-time (chatbot, $\le$ 100 ms) or batch (offline KG completion)?
4. **Select the language family** — Propositional, First-Order Logic, Horn Logic, Description Logic, Probabilistic (Bayesian Nets), or Neural (Knowledge Graph Embeddings).
5. **Pair with an inference engine** — Forward/Backward chaining, Resolution refutation, Tableau, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, or TransE/DistMult.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Construct | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $\models$ | Entailment (KB $\models \alpha$ means KB entails $\alpha$) | Soundness/completeness proofs |
| $\vdash$ | Derivation (KB $\vdash \alpha$ means $\alpha$ is provable from KB) | Theorem-prover questions |
| $\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$ | Logical connectives | Translating English to FOL |
| $\forall, \exists$ | Universal and existential quantifiers | FOL translation |
| $\text{CNF}$ | Conjunctive Normal Form: $\bigwedge_i \left( \bigvee_j \ell_{ij} \right)$ | Required input for resolution |
| $\text{Ground Instance}$ | A sentence with all variables substituted by constants | Substitution $\theta$ application |
| $\text{Unify}(\alpha, \beta)$ | Most general unifier (MGU) of two literals | Resolution step |
| $\text{Clause}$ | A disjunction of literals | Resolution operates on clauses |
| $\text{Resolvent}$ | Clause obtained by resolving two parent clauses | Core inference rule |
| $\square$ | Empty clause (contradiction) | Termination of refutation |
| $T_{Box}$ | Terminological axioms (class definitions) in DL | Ontology design |
| $A_{Box}$ | Assertional axioms (instance facts) in DL | Ontology population |
| $\text{RDF Triple}$ | $\langle \text{Subject}, \text{Predicate}, \text{Object} \rangle$ | Knowledge Graph storage |
| $\text{Confidence}(h)$ | Probability of hypothesis $h$ given evidence | Probabilistic reasoning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse $\models$ and $\vdash$. Memorize: $\models$ is the *semantic* relation (truth-preserving); $\vdash$ is the *syntactic* relation (derivable by rules). Soundness = $\vdash \Rightarrow \models$; Completeness = $\models \Rightarrow \vdash$.

## 2.4 The Four Classical Reasoning Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU Part A (3-mark) question will almost certainly ask: *Distinguish between deductive, inductive, abductive, and analogical reasoning*. Memorize the canonical examples.

### 2.4.1 Deductive Reasoning (Top-Down, Truth-Preserving)
- **Form:** Premise $\rightarrow$ Conclusion, where the conclusion *must* follow.
- **Example:** All humans are mortal (Premise) $\rightarrow$ Socrates is human (Premise) $\rightarrow$ *Therefore, Socrates is mortal* (Conclusion).
- **Property:** **Monotonic** — adding new premises never invalidates a conclusion.
- **NLP Use:** Theorem provers, FOL question answering, formal verification of NLU pipelines.

### 2.4.2 Inductive Reasoning (Bottom-Up, Generalizing)
- **Form:** Specific observations $\rightarrow$ General rule.
- **Example:** Swan 1 is white, Swan 2 is white, ... $\rightarrow$ *All swans are white* (until a black swan is observed).
- **Property:** **Non-monotonic**, **probabilistic**.
- **NLP Use:** Training statistical and neural models, learning lexical generalizations from corpora.

### 2.4.3 Abductive Reasoning (Inference to Best Explanation)
- **Form:** Observation + World Knowledge $\rightarrow$ Most plausible cause.
- **Example:** The grass is wet $\rightarrow$ *It probably rained last night*.
- **Property:** **Non-monotonic**, produces *plausible* (not certain) hypotheses.
- **NLP Use:** Medical NLU, fault diagnosis, narrative understanding, sarcasm detection.

### 2.4.4 Analogical Reasoning (Structure Mapping)
- **Form:** $A$ is to $B$ as $C$ is to $D$.
- **Example:** Atom is to solar system as solar system is to galaxy.
- **NLP Use:** Metaphor processing, word-embedding analogies (king − man + woman ≈ queen), compositional generalization.

## 2.5 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining

These are the two **production-system inference algorithms** central to KTU module questions.

| Dimension | Forward Chaining (Data-Driven) | Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Facts $\rightarrow$ Conclusions | Goal $\rightarrow$ Sub-goals $\rightarrow$ Facts |
| **Trigger** | New fact added to KB | Query / goal clause presented |
| **Algorithm** | Iterate over rules whose premises are satisfied | Recursively prove antecedents |
| **Strength** | Finds *all* consequences; good for monitoring | Efficient when goal set is small |
| **Weakness** | Can fire irrelevant rules (combinatorial explosion) | Cannot discover unexpected facts |
| **NLP Use** | Real-time event extraction | Targeted question answering |
| **Standard** | Rete algorithm (used in CLIPS, Jess) | Prolog's evaluation strategy |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare NLP (e.g., IBM Watson Health):** Uses description-logic ontologies (SNOMED-CT) + abductive reasoning over patient records.
- **Search Engines (Google Knowledge Graph):** Uses RDF triples + OWL 2 RL reasoning to disambiguate entities.
- **Conversational AI (Siri, Alexa):** Hybrid — intents (forward chaining) + slot-filling (frame-based KR).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Probabilistic KR (Bayesian networks) + temporal logic for scene understanding.
- **Legal-Tech NLP:** Uses **defeasible logic** (non-monotonic) because legal rules have exceptions and precedence.

# Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: FOL → CNF → Resolution Refutation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-valuable worked example** for any KTU 14-mark question on automated reasoning. Practice the steps until mechanical.

### Problem Statement

**KB (in English):**
1. Every student who studies NLP passes the exam.
2. Every student who passes the exam is smart.
3. Ram is a student who studies NLP.
4. **Query:** Is Ram smart?

### Step 1 — Translate English to First-Order Logic

We use the following predicate symbols:
- $\text{Student}(x)$ — $x$ is a student
- $\text{StudiesNLP}(x)$ — $x$ studies NLP
- $\text{Passes}(x)$ — $x$ passes the exam
- $\text{Smart}(x)$ — $x$ is smart
- $\text{Ram}$ — the constant $a$

The KB becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KB}_1 &: \forall x \bigl( \text{Student}(x) \land \text{StudiesNLP}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Passes}(x) \bigr) \\
\text{KB}_2 &: \forall x \bigl( \text{Passes}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Smart}(x) \bigr) \\
\text{KB}_3 &: \text{Student}(\text{Ram}) \\
\text{KB}_4 &: \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram})
\end{aligned}
$$

The query to be proved (negated) is: $\text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$.

### Step 2 — Convert each sentence to Prenex Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)

> Each FOL sentence must be reduced to a conjunction of disjunctions of literals.

**Sentence 1:** $\forall x \bigl( \lnot \text{Student}(x) \lor \lnot \text{StudiesNLP}(x) \lor \text{Passes}(x) \bigr)$

- Eliminate $\Rightarrow$ using $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \lnot P \lor Q$.
- Drop universal quantifier (Skolemization: no existential, so variables remain).

**Sentence 2:** $\forall x \bigl( \lnot \text{Passes}(x) \lor \text{Smart}(x) \bigr)$

**Sentence 3:** Already a unit clause: $\text{Student}(\text{Ram})$.

**Sentence 4:** Already a unit clause: $\text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram})$.

**Negated query:** $\lnot \text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$ — this is what we try to refute.

### Step 3 — Collect All Clauses into the Clause Set

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \lnot \text{Student}(x) \lor \lnot \text{StudiesNLP}(x) \lor \text{Passes}(x) \\
C_2 &: \lnot \text{Passes}(x) \lor \text{Smart}(x) \\
C_3 &: \text{Student}(\text{Ram}) \\
C_4 &: \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram}) \\
C_5 &: \lnot \text{Smart}(\text{Ram}) \quad \text{(negated goal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Resolution Refutation (Step-by-Step)

> The **Resolution Rule:** From clauses $(P \lor \alpha)$ and $(\lnot P \lor \beta)$, infer $(\alpha \lor \beta)$ — provided $P$ and $\lnot P$ unify via a substitution $\theta$.

**Resolution Step 1:** Resolve $C_3$ and $C_1$ on $\text{Student}(x)$.

- $C_3 = \text{Student}(\text{Ram})$ — unifies with $\text{Student}(x)$ using $\theta_1 = \{ x \setminus \text{Ram} \}$.
- Resolvent: $\lnot \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram}) \lor \text{Passes}(\text{Ram})$.

$$
C_6 : \lnot \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram}) \lor \text{Passes}(\text{Ram})
$$

**Resolution Step 2:** Resolve $C_4$ and $C_6$ on $\text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram})$.

- $C_4 = \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram})$ cancels $\lnot \text{StudiesNLP}(\text{Ram})$.
- Resolvent: $\text{Passes}(\text{Ram})$.

$$
C_7 : \text{Passes}(\text{Ram})
$$

**Resolution Step 3:** Resolve $C_7$ and $C_2$ on $\text{Passes}(x)$.

- Unifier $\theta_3 = \{ x \setminus \text{Ram} \}$.
- Resolvent: $\text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$.

$$
C_8 : \text{Smart}(\text{Ram})
$$

**Resolution Step 4:** Resolve $C_8$ and $C_5$ on $\text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$.

- $C_8$ and $\lnot \text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$ are complementary literals.
- Resolvent: **empty clause $\square$**.

$$
C_9 : \square \quad \text{(contradiction)}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The empty clause $\square$ is derived $\Rightarrow$ The KB is *inconsistent* with the negated query $\Rightarrow$ The original query $\text{Smart}(\text{Ram})$ is **logically entailed** by the KB. Therefore, *Ram is smart*. QED.

This proof is **sound** (every step uses a valid inference rule) and **complete** (resolution can derive the empty clause for *any* entailed sentence in CNF).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Representation Schemes

| Scheme | Expressiveness | Inference Speed | Typical NLP Use | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Propositional Logic** | Low (no variables) | Very Fast (SAT solvers) | Sentiment polarity, simple bots | Cannot handle objects/relations |
| **First-Order Logic (FOL)** | Very High | Slow (semi-decidable) | QA systems, formal semantics | Undecidable in general |
| **Horn Clauses** | Medium (limited disjunction) | Very Fast (linear) | Prolog, Datalog, rule-based NLU | Cannot express full FOL |
| **Semantic Networks** | Medium (graph-based) | Fast (graph traversal) | WordNet, lexical KBs | No formal semantics |
| **Frames (Minsky)** | Medium-High (slot-filler) | Fast (slot lookup) | Dialogue systems, IE templates | Limited default reasoning |
| **Description Logic (OWL)** | High (decidable fragments) | Medium (tableau) | Web ontologies, biomedical NLP | Cannot express full FOL |
| **Production Rules** | Medium | Fast (Rete algorithm) | Expert systems, business rules | No unified semantics |
| **Probabilistic (Bayesian)** | High (uncertainty) | Medium (MCMC, belief prop.) | Information extraction, NER disambiguation | Computationally expensive |
| **Neural KG Embeddings (TransE)** | High (learned) | Very Fast (vector ops) | KG completion, link prediction | Black-box, not symbolic |
| **Conceptual Graphs (Sowa)** | High | Medium (graph matching) | Multilingual semantics | Adoption limited |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Forward Chaining Trace

> **Use this structure for any "trace the inference" KTU question.**

**Rules (Horn form):**
- $R_1$: If $\text{Fever}(x) \land \text{Cough}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Flu}(x)$.
- $R_2$: If $\text{Flu}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Sick}(x)$.
- $R_3$: If $\text{Sick}(x) \Rightarrow \text{StayHome}(x)$.

**Initial Facts:** $\text{Fever}(\text{Anu})$, $\text{Cough}(\text{Anu})$.

**Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad & R_1 \text{ fires (Fever(Anu) + Cough(Anu) match)} \Rightarrow \text{add Flu(Anu)} \\
\text{Iteration 2:} \quad & R_2 \text{ fires (Flu(Anu) present)} \Rightarrow \text{add Sick(Anu)} \\
\text{Iteration 3:} \quad & R_3 \text{ fires (Sick(Anu) present)} \Rightarrow \text{add StayHome(Anu)} \\
\text{Fixed Point:} \quad & \text{No new facts derivable; halt.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Unification Algorithm

Given $\text{Loves}(\text{Ram}, x)$ and $\text{Loves}(y, \text{Sita})$, find the MGU.

$$
\text{Unify} \bigl( \text{Loves}(\text{Ram}, x), \text{Loves}(y, \text{Sita}) \bigr)
$$

- Argument 1: constant $\text{Ram}$ vs. variable $y$ $\Rightarrow$ substitute $y \setminus \text{Ram}$.
- Argument 2: variable $x$ vs. constant $\text{Sita}$ $\Rightarrow$ substitute $x \setminus \text{Sita}$.

$$
\theta_{\text{MGU}} = \{ y \setminus \text{Ram},\, x \setminus \text{Sita} \}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not substitute a variable with a term that *contains* that same variable — this creates an *occurs-check* failure (infinite loop). Example: binding $x$ to $\text{Parent}(x)$ is illegal.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The KR&R Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Lexical and Syntactic Analysis]
    B --> C[Semantic Role Labeler]
    C --> D[FOL and Logic Form Translator]
    D --> E[Knowledge Base Store]
    E --> F[Inference Engine Module]
    F --> G[Reasoning Controller]
    G --> H[Forward Chaining Module]
    G --> I[Backward Chaining Module]
    G --> J[Resolution Refuter]
    H --> K[Derived Facts Layer]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Ontology Reasoner Module]
    L --> M[Consistency Checker]
    M --> N[SPARQL Query Handler]
    N --> O[Final Structured Answer]
    M --> O
    E --> P[Common Sense KB]
    P --> L
    E --> Q[Domain Ontology]
    Q --> L
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline moves from unstructured text (left) through linguistic analysis to a structured knowledge base, then the *Inference Engine Module* dispatches the appropriate reasoning strategy (forward, backward, or resolution) based on the query type. The *Ontology Reasoner Module* ensures global consistency and answers SPARQL queries for the final structured output.
