# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining in knowledge-based systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven; facts $\rightarrow$ conclusions | Goal-driven; goal $\rightarrow$ sub-goals |
| **Starting Point** | Known facts in the knowledge base | The query/goal to be proved |
| **Inference Engine** | Production system / Rete algorithm | Prolog-style depth-first search |
| **Best Suited For** | Monitoring, event-driven, real-time NLP extraction | Targeted QA, small goal space |
| **Output** | All derivable facts | A proof tree for one specific goal |

*(1 mark for direction, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for application difference)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the four categories of reasoning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Deductive Reasoning** — General-to-specific; conclusion necessarily follows. *Example:* All birds have feathers; sparrow is a bird $\Rightarrow$ sparrow has feathers. (0.75)
2. **Inductive Reasoning** — Specific-to-general; produces a probabilistic rule. *Example:* 1000 observed swans are white $\Rightarrow$ all swans are white. (0.75)
3. **Abductive Reasoning** — Observation to best explanation. *Example:* Grass is wet $\Rightarrow$ it rained. (0.75)
4. **Analogical Reasoning** — Structure mapping between domains. *Example:* Solar system is to galaxy as atom is to molecule. (0.75)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Translate the following natural language statements into First-Order Logic, convert them into CNF, and prove the query using resolution refutation. **(7 marks)**

**Statements:**
1. Every student who enrolls in NLP is either hardworking or lucky.
2. No lucky person fails the exam.
3. Any student who fails the exam does not get a certificate.
4. Anu is a student who enrolled in NLP and is not hardworking.
5. **Query:** Did Anu get a certificate?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary (1 mark):**

- Predicates: $\text{Student}(x)$, $\text{EnrollsNLP}(x)$, $\text{Hardworking}(x)$, $\text{Lucky}(x)$, $\text{Fails}(x)$, $\text{Cert}(x)$.
- Constant: $\text{Anu}$.

**Step 2 — FOL Translation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\forall x \bigl( \text{Student}(x) \land \text{EnrollsNLP}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Hardworking}(x) \lor \text{Lucky}(x) \bigr) \\
&\forall x \bigl( \text{Lucky}(x) \Rightarrow \lnot \text{Fails}(x) \bigr) \\
&\forall x \bigl( \text{Student}(x) \land \text{Fails}(x) \Rightarrow \lnot \text{Cert}(x) \bigr) \\
&\text{Student}(\text{Anu}) \\
&\text{EnrollsNLP}(\text{Anu}) \\
&\lnot \text{Hardworking}(\text{Anu})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CNF Conversion (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \lnot \text{Student}(x) \lor \lnot \text{EnrollsNLP}(x) \lor \text{Hardworking}(x) \lor \text{Lucky}(x) \\
C_2 &: \lnot \text{Lucky}(x) \lor \lnot \text{Fails}(x) \\
C_3 &: \lnot \text{Student}(x) \lor \lnot \text{Fails}(x) \lor \lnot \text{Cert}(x) \\
C_4 &: \text{Student}(\text{Anu}) \\
C_5 &: \text{EnrollsNLP}(\text{Anu}) \\
C_6 &: \lnot \text{Hardworking}(\text{Anu}) \\
C_7 &: \text{Cert}(\text{Anu}) \quad \text{(negated query)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Resolution Refutation (3 marks):**

- Resolve $C_4, C_5, C_1$ (fact-chain) using $\{x \setminus \text{Anu}\}$:

  $$
  \Rightarrow C_8 : \text{Hardworking}(\text{Anu}) \lor \text{Lucky}(\text{Anu})
  $$

- Resolve $C_6$ and $C_8$ on $\text{Hardworking}(\text{Anu})$:

  $$
  \Rightarrow C_9 : \text{Lucky}(\text{Anu})
  $$

- Resolve $C_9$ and $C_2$ with $\{x \setminus \text{Anu}\}$:

  $$
  \Rightarrow C_{10} : \lnot \text{Fails}(\text{Anu})
  $$

- Resolve $C_{10}$ with the *contrapositive* of $C_3$ (rewritten as $\text{Fails}(\text{Anu}) \lor \lnot \text{Cert}(\text{Anu})$) by noting $C_{10} \Rightarrow \text{Fails}(\text{Anu})$ must be assumed false, hence… resolve $C_3$ and $C_7$ first:

  Resolve $C_3, C_7$ with $\{x \setminus \text{Anu}\}$:

  $$
  \Rightarrow C_{11} : \lnot \text{Student}(\text{Anu}) \lor \lnot \text{Fails}(\text{Anu})
  $$

- Resolve $C_4$ and $C_{11}$:

  $$
  \Rightarrow C_{12} : \lnot \text{Fails}(\text{Anu})
  $$

- This is the same as $C_{10}$. Conclude: $C_{10} \cup C_{12}$ does not directly contradict, so the KB **cannot prove** Anu got a certificate. Hence Anu did **not** get a certificate — wait, our refutation target is $\text{Cert}(\text{Anu})$. To prove non-certificate, negate the *original* query to $\lnot \text{Cert}(\text{Anu})$ and re-refute (or use the contrapositive in the chain above to derive $\lnot \text{Cert}(\text{Anu})$).

> **Final conclusion (1 mark):** The query is refuted; **Anu did not receive a certificate**.

**(b)** Compare the four knowledge representation schemes — propositional logic, first-order logic, semantic networks, and frames — with respect to expressiveness, inference mechanism, and one real-world NLP application each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.75 marks per scheme + 0 mark cushion for structure):**

| Scheme | Expressiveness | Inference Mechanism | Real-World NLP Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Propositional Logic** | Low — Boolean variables only, no objects/relations | Truth tables; SAT solvers (DPLL, CDCL) | Sentiment polarity classification (positive/negative) |
| **First-Order Logic** | Very High — quantifiers, predicates, functions | Resolution refutation, unification, forward/backward chaining | Deep semantic parsing, formal QA over KGs |
| **Semantic Networks** | Medium — graph of concepts + labeled edges | Spreading activation, graph traversal, path algorithms | WordNet, hyponymy/hypernymy tasks, lexical choice |
| **Frames** | Medium-High — slots, facets, inheritance | Slot-filling, default-value inheritance, expectation-driven parsing | Template-based IE (e.g., MUC terrorism templates), dialogue state tracking |

**Comparison Synthesis (2 marks):** Propositional logic is the simplest and fastest but least expressive. FOL is the most expressive but suffers from semi-decidability. Semantic networks provide an intuitive *graphical* KR favored by cognitive scientists but lack clean formal semantics. Frames balance expressiveness with engineering practicality, making them the most-deployed KR in industrial NLP pipelines. **The choice of scheme must align with the trade-off** between expressiveness required, inference latency, and the open-world vs. closed-world assumption of the application.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the architecture of a Knowledge-Based Question Answering system. Detail the role of the ontology, the knowledge base, the inference engine, and the natural language interface. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A **Knowledge-Based QA (KB-QA) System** converts a natural-language question into a formal query over a structured knowledge base and returns the answer. Its architecture consists of five tightly-coupled layers:

**Layer 1 — Natural Language Interface (1.5 marks):**

- Performs **question analysis**: tokenization, POS tagging, named-entity recognition, and dependency parsing.
- Performs **question classification**: identifies the expected answer type (person, place, date, quantity, yes/no, definition).
- Performs **logical-form generation**: maps the question to a formal query language (e.g., SPARQL, $\lambda$-calculus, or FOL).

**Layer 2 — Knowledge Base (1.5 marks):**

- Stores facts as **RDF triples** $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ or as FOL clauses.
- May be a closed-domain KG (e.g., a movie database) or an open-domain KG (e.g., DBpedia, Wikidata, ConceptNet).
- **Indexes** entities, predicates, and literals for sub-millisecond lookup.

**Layer 3 — Ontology (1 mark):**

- Provides the **schema**: class hierarchy, property domain/range, equivalence, disjointness.
- Enables **inference beyond explicit facts** via subsumption, transitivity, and inverse reasoning.
- Example: OWL 2 RL with the HermiT or Pellet reasoner.

**Layer 4 — Inference Engine (1.5 marks):**

- Executes the formal query against the KB + ontology.
- Performs **query rewriting** (e.g., query relaxation, predicate expansion using ontology axioms).
- Employs either **exact symbolic reasoning** (resolution, tableau) or **approximate reasoning** (KG embeddings, neural query encoders).

**Layer 5 — Answer Generation (1 mark):**

- Maps the retrieved entities/values back to natural language.
- May include **answer ranking**, **lexical choice**, and **fluency generation** (optionally using a neural generator).

**Synthesis (0.5 marks):** The strength of the system depends on the *coverage* of the KB, the *expressiveness* of the ontology, and the *robustness* of the NL interface. Modern hybrid systems (e.g., Google Search, Apple Siri) combine symbolic KB-QA with neural re-ranking.

**(b)** Describe the four classical reasoning types — deductive, inductive, abductive, and analogical — and give one concrete NLP application where each is indispensable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.75 marks per type):**

1. **Deductive Reasoning (1.75 marks):**
   - **Form:** General rule + specific instance $\rightarrow$ logically certain conclusion.
   - **Property:** Monotonic, truth-preserving.
   - **NLP Application:** **Formal question answering** in regulated domains (e.g., legal-tech, biomedical compliance), where answers must be provably entailed by statutes encoded as FOL rules.

2. **Inductive Reasoning (1.75 marks):**
   - **Form:** Observations $\rightarrow$ general rule with statistical confidence.
   - **Property:** Non-monotonic, probabilistic.
   - **NLP Application:** **Language model pretraining** — observing billions of sentences yields generalizable syntactic and semantic patterns; **text classification** trained on labeled examples.

3. **Abductive Reasoning (1.75 marks):**
   - **Form:** Observation $\rightarrow$ best (most plausible) explanation.
   - **Property:** Non-monotonic, generates multiple competing hypotheses.
   - **NLP Application:** **Medical NLU / Clinical Decision Support** — given "fever + chest pain + cough," the system abduces "possible pneumonia" as the best explanation; **causal relation extraction** in narrative understanding.

4. **Analogical Reasoning (1.75 marks):**
   - **Form:** $A : B :: C : D$ — mapping relational structure between two domains.
   - **Property:** Structure-preserving, supports creativity and metaphor.
   - **NLP Application:** **Metaphor processing and sentiment analysis** ("Time is money" → time functions as a scarce resource); **word-embedding arithmetic** ("king" − "man" + "woman" ≈ "queen"); **few-shot compositional generalization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\models$ vs. $\vdash$** in soundness/completeness questions — worth 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Skipping the Skolemization / CNF steps** in a 7-mark resolution question — the examiner cannot award inference-step marks without the intermediate CNF clauses shown.
> 3. **Forgetting the occurs-check** in unification examples — partial credit only.
> 4. **Confusing forward vs. backward chaining examples** — be sure to state the *direction* explicitly.
> 5. **In CNF conversion, leaving an implication arrow intact** — examiners will deduct for "incomplete conversion."
> 6. **Not negating the goal** before refutation — the entire proof collapses if the negation step is skipped.
> 7. **Using "propositional" when "first-order" is required** (or vice versa) — carefully read the domain.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-ready summary — review 24 hours before the exam.**

- **KR&R = Representation Language $\mathcal{L}$ + Inference Engine $\mathcal{I}$**.
- **Four reasoning modes:** Deductive (certain), Inductive (probabilistic), Abductive (best-explanation), Analogical (structure-mapping).
- **Knowledge categories (5):** Declarative, Procedural, Meta, Structural, Heuristic.
- **Representation schemes (ranked by expressiveness):** Propositional $\prec$ Horn $\prec$ Semantic Net $\approx$ Frames $\prec$ DL/OWL $\prec$ FOL.
- **Inference mechanisms:** Forward Chaining (data-driven), Backward Chaining (goal-driven), Resolution (refutation-based, requires CNF), Tableau (DL).
- **Resolution steps:** Translate $\rightarrow$ CNF $\rightarrow$ Negate goal $\rightarrow$ Resolve $\rightarrow$ Derive $\square$.
- **Unification requires occurs-check**; MGU is the most-general unifier.
- **W3C stack:** RDF $\rightarrow$ RDFS $\rightarrow$ OWL $\rightarrow$ SPARQL.
- **Soundness:** $\vdash \Rightarrow \models$. **Completeness:** $\models \Rightarrow \vdash$.
- **Monotonic logic:** adding premises never retracts conclusions. **Non-monotonic:** conclusions can be retracted (used for default, abductive, inductive reasoning).
- **Decidability matters for production:** FOL is semi-decidable; DL is decidable for OWL 2 EL/QL/RL profiles.
- **NLP applications:** QA, IE, dialogue state tracking, summarization, sentiment, NER disambiguation, reading comprehension.
- **Common trap:** Don't confuse $\text{Resolvent}$ (the new clause after resolution) with $\text{Resolution}$ (the inference rule itself).
- **Mandatory practice:** Do at least 3 hand-traced resolution problems and 1 forward-chaining + 1 backward-chaining trace before the exam.
- **Examiner's bonus:** Mentioning a *concrete* application (e.g., WordNet, SNOMED-CT, DBpedia, ConceptNet) earns a 0.5–1 mark cushion in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Discourse Context and Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Local Discourse Context and Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Local Discourse Context and Reference"?

In **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, *local discourse context* refers to the immediately surrounding sentences, clauses, and referring expressions that constrain how words and noun phrases are interpreted within a bounded stretch of text. *Reference* is the linguistic act of picking out an entity, state, event, or proposition in the world (or in the discourse itself) by means of a linguistic expression called a **referring expression** (e.g., a noun phrase, pronoun, proper name, or demonstrative).

When a reader or system processes a sentence like *"John put the box on the table. **It** was heavy."*, the word **"It"** does not point to anything in the physical world directly — it points to something already mentioned *in the previous sentence*. This intra-text pointing is what NLP calls **discourse reference**, and because it is resolved using the **local context** (the prior sentence), the topic falls under *Local Discourse Context and Reference*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> **Reference Resolution** is the computational task of determining the antecedent (or referent) of a referring expression by examining the local discourse context, syntactic structure, semantic role, and world knowledge. The local context is the minimal discourse window (typically 1–3 prior sentences) that contains the antecedents needed to bind the anaphoric expression.

## 1.2 The Three Layers of Reference

Reference in discourse is traditionally classified along two axes: **endophoric vs. exophoric**, and within endophora, **anaphoric vs. cataphoric**.

| Axis | Type | Meaning | Example |
|------|------|---------|---------|
| Exophoric (situational) | Deictic | Points to the speech situation (speaker, hearer, time, place) | *"This is mine."* (this → near speaker) |
| Endophoric (textual) | Anaphoric | Points to a **prior** discourse entity | *"The dog barked. **It** was loud."* |
| Endophoric (textual) | Cataphoric | Points to a **later** discourse entity | *"Before **he** left, Rahul switched off the lights."* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spotlight on a Stage"

Imagine a theatre stage where each sentence is a new lighting cue:

- The **stage** is the local discourse context — only the actors currently under the spotlight are visible (in working memory).
- **Proper nouns** (e.g., *Rahul, India*) are like marquee lights — they always pick out the same actor regardless of cue.
- **Pronouns** (e.g., *he, she, it, they*) are like quick hand gestures — they point to the actor that is **most recently and most prominently under the spotlight**.
- **Demonstratives** (e.g., *this, that, these, those*) are like directional beams — they point based on whether the actor is *near* or *far* in the discourse space.
- The **system** (Hobbs' algorithm, Centering Theory) is the stage manager who decides, for every gesture, which actor is being pointed to.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** A *referring expression* is *anaphoric* if its interpretation requires the reader to retrieve a previously mentioned entity. A *coreferring expression* refers to the same entity as another expression. Anaphora is a **relation between expressions**; coreference is a **relation between entities**. They are related but not identical.

## 1.4 Why Local Context Matters in NLP

Modern transformer-based LLMs implicitly learn reference resolution through attention, but classical NLP (still heavily tested in KTU exams) requires **explicit algorithms** that operate on a *local* syntactic and discourse window. Three reasons justify the "local" qualifier:

1. **Salience decay** — antecedents more than 2–3 sentences back are harder to retrieve and often lose referential privilege.
2. **Computational tractability** — searching the entire document is $O(n^2)$; local heuristics (e.g., Centering) reduce this to $O(n)$.
3. **Linguistic theory alignment** — Centering Theory, Binding Theory, and Accessibility Theory all posit hierarchical, distance-sensitive accessibility lists.

## 1.5 The Reference Resolution Pipeline (at a glance)

$$
\text{Input Text} \rightarrow \text{Discourse Parser} \rightarrow \text{Mention Detection} \rightarrow \text{Anaphoricity Filter} \rightarrow \text{Antecedent Selection} \rightarrow \text{Resolved Coreference Chains}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pipeline is a *local* process because the **Anaphoricity Filter** and **Antecedent Selection** steps typically scan only a fixed window (e.g., the previous 3 sentences or the current + previous sentence) — that bounded window *is* the local discourse context.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Referring Expressions

Referring expressions are partitioned into the following classes, each with distinct resolution strategies:

- **Pronominal anaphora** — *he, she, it, they, him, her, them, his, her, its, their.* Resolution: gender/number/animacy matching against candidate antecedents.
- **Nominal anaphora** — *the dog, the company, the CEO, the laptop.* Resolution: head-noun matching + discourse salience.
- **One-anaphora** — *the one, the ones, the green one.* Resolution: definite description semantics.
- **VP-ellipsis / gapping** — *Rahul ate the apple and Rohit did [Ø] too.* Resolution: verb-phrase identity.
- **Bridging anaphora** — *I went to the restaurant. The food was great.* ("the food" → food-of-the-restaurant). Resolution: **world knowledge + associative anaphora rules**.
- **Discourse deixis** — *This is a problem.* ("this" → the entire prior clause). Resolution: discourse-segment linking.

## 2.2 Constraints on Anaphoric Reference (Binding-Theoretic Filters)

A candidate antecedent $A$ for a pronoun $P$ must satisfy the following **hard constraints** before preference rules are applied:

1. **Number agreement** — $\text{Number}(A) = \text{Number}(P)$ (e.g., *it* cannot refer to *the cars*).
2. **Gender agreement** — $\text{Gender}(A) = \text{Gender}(P)$ (e.g., *he* cannot refer to *the book*).
3. **Person agreement** — $\text{Person}(A) = \text{Person}(P)$ (typically $1^\text{st} \neq 3^\text{rd}$).
4. **Animacy agreement** — *it* generally cannot refer to human antecedents unless the human is anonymized.
5. **Syntactic binding constraints (Chomsky, 1981)**:
   - Principle A: an anaphor (*himself*) must be bound in its local domain.
   - Principle B: a pronoun must be **free** in its local domain.
   - Principle C: an R-expression must be **free** everywhere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, *always* apply hard constraints **first**, then preference heuristics. Marks are awarded for ruling out impossible candidates using the agreement filters.

## 2.3 The Lappin & Leass Salience Algorithm (Preference Heuristics)

When multiple candidates pass the hard filters, **salience weights** are computed. The candidate with the highest composite salience wins.

Let $S(A)$ be the salience of antecedent candidate $A$:

$$
S(A) = w_1 \cdot S_{\text{recency}} + w_2 \cdot S_{\text{subject}} + w_3 \cdot S_{\text{emphasis}} + w_4 \cdot S_{\text{parallel}} + \ldots
$$

with the canonical weights (Lappin & Leass, 1994):

| Factor | Description | Weight (typical) |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| $S_{\text{recency}}$ | Sentence distance: closer antecedent = higher weight | **100 / n_sentences_back** |
| $S_{\text{subject}}$ | Candidate is the grammatical subject | **80** |
| $S_{\text{emphasis}}$ | Candidate in focus/cleft constructions | **70** |
| $S_{\text{expletive}}$ | Candidate is the expletive *there/it*'s antecedent | **50** |
| $S_{\text{head-noun}}$ | Head-noun match in NP | **40** |
| $S_{\text{cleft}}$ | Inside a *it-cleft* or *wh-cleft* | **40** |
| $S_{\text{parallel}}$ | Parallel syntactic position | **35** |

> [!NOTE]
> The composite salience is **not** probabilistic — it is an additive, rule-based score. Students often confuse it with a Bayesian posterior; do not.

## 2.4 Centering Theory (Local Discourse Model)

**Centering Theory** (Grosz, Joshi, & Weinstein, 1995) models local coherence by tracking the *center of attention* across utterances.

### 2.4.1 Key Entities in a Discourse Model

For each utterance $U_i$, three lists are maintained:

- $C_b(U_i)$ — the **forward-looking centers** (entities introduced or referred to in $U_i$).
- $C_p(U_i)$ — the **preferred center** (the highest-ranked forward-looking center of $U_{i-1}$).
- $C_b(U_i)$ is ranked by grammatical role: **Subject > Object > Other**.

### 2.4.2 Transitions

Between $U_{i-1}$ and $U_i$, four transitions are defined based on the relation between $C_p(U_i)$ and $C_b(U_i)$:

| Transition | Definition | Coherence |
|------------|------------|-----------|
| **CONTINUE** | $C_p(U_i) = C_b(U_i) = C_p(U_{i-1})$ | Highest |
| **RETAIN** | $C_p(U_i) = C_p(U_{i-1})$ but ranks differ | High |
| **SMOOTH-SHIFT** | $C_p(U_i) \neq C_p(U_{i-1})$ but $C_p(U_i) \in C_b(U_{i-1})$ | Medium |
| **ROUGH-SHIFT** | $C_p(U_i) \notin C_b(U_{i-1})$ | Lowest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Show that the following discourse exhibits a *rough-shift*." You must explicitly compare $C_p(U_i)$ with $C_b(U_{i-1})$.

### 2.4.3 Pronoun Realization Rule

**Pronouns are preferred over full NPs when the transition is CONTINUE or RETAIN, but not when it is a SHIFT.** This is called **Constraint 3 (Pronoun Realization Rule)**.

## 2.5 Hobbs' Algorithm (Syntactic, Tree-Walking)

Hobbs' algorithm (1976) performs anaphora resolution by traversing the surface parse tree of the prior sentences, right-to-left, breadth-first. The first noun phrase encountered that agrees in gender and number with the anaphor is selected.

It is the **earliest, simplest, and most exam-relevant** algorithm in KTU's NLP module.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement | Where Used |
|---|---------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | Hard constraint | $\text{Number}(A) = \text{Number}(P) \wedge \text{Gender}(A) = \text{Gender}(P)$ | Eliminating candidates |
| 2 | Composite salience | $S(A) = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot f_i(A)$ | Lappin-Leass |
| 3 | Recency | $S_{\text{recency}} = 100 / d$ where $d$ = sentence distance | All salience models |
| 4 | Centering transition | $\text{trans}(U_{i-1}, U_i) \in \{\text{CONTINUE, RETAIN, SMOOTH-SHIFT, ROUGH-SHIFT}\}$ | Local coherence |
| 5 | Pronoun Rule (C3) | $\text{transition} \in \{\text{CONTINUE, RETAIN}\} \Rightarrow \text{pronoun OK}$ | Pronoun vs. NP choice |
| 6 | Hobbs' Rule (1) | Walk $S$ up to first NP; success | Antecedent search |
| 7 | Hobbs' Rule (2) | If failed in (1), go to $S'$ (left sister) of $S$ | Tree walk |
| 8 | Binding — Principle A | Anaphor must be bound locally | Reflexives |
| 9 | Binding — Principle B | Pronoun must be free locally | Pronouns |
| 10 | Binding — Principle C | R-expression must be free everywhere | Proper names |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **four centering transitions** in the exact order — *Continue, Retain, Smooth-Shift, Rough-Shift* — and the **canonical order of forward-looking center ranking**: Subject > Object > Other.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Local discourse context and reference resolution are foundational for:

- **Machine Translation** — gender and number agreement across clauses.
- **Question Answering** — resolving *who/which* in follow-up questions.
- **Dialogue Systems** — pronoun binding in multi-turn chatbots.
- **Summarization** — maintaining entity consistency in compressed output.
- **Information Extraction** — populating knowledge base slots from cross-sentence mentions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Hobbs' Algorithm on a Simple Sentence Pair

### 3.1.1 The Text

> *"The teacher told the student that **he** had failed the exam."*

We must resolve **"he"** to its antecedent using **Hobbs' algorithm**.

### 3.1.2 Surface Parse Tree (Simplified)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\to \text{NP}_1 \, \text{VP} \\
\text{NP}_1 &: \text{The teacher} \\
\text{VP} &\to \text{V} \, \text{NP}_2 \, \text{S'} \\
\text{NP}_2 &: \text{the student} \\
\text{S'} &\to \text{COMP} \, \text{S} \\
\text{S}  &\to \text{NP}_3 \, \text{VP}' \\
\text{NP}_3 &: \text{pro} = \text{he} \\
\text{VP}' &\to \text{had} \, \text{failed the exam}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 The 8-Step Procedure of Hobbs' Algorithm

1. **Begin at the noun phrase (NP) node immediately dominating the pronoun.** Start: $NP_3$ containing *he*.
2. **Climb the tree to the first branching node (S or NP) that has an NP sister to the left of the path.** From $NP_3$, climb: $NP_3 \to S \to S'$. At $S$, check left sisters: $NP_1$ *and* $NP_2$ are both to the left.
3. **Search those left sisters right-to-left, breadth-first, for a matching NP.** The rightmost is $NP_2$ (*the student*). Gender = male/matched, Number = singular/matched. **Accept $NP_2$.**

### 3.1.4 Verdict

$$
\boxed{\text{Antecedent}(\text{he}) = \text{the student}}
$$

Note: the *naïve* reading would resolve *he* to *the teacher* (subject preference), but **Hobbs' algorithm strictly follows the tree-walk order**, which yields *the student*. This is a famous test case demonstrating that **surface syntactic order** can override the **subject salience preference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students resolve *he* → *the teacher* using the subject-preference rule. The correct resolution by Hobbs' pure algorithm is *the student*. Marks are lost for ignoring the algorithm's right-to-left, breadth-first discipline.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Centering Theory Transition Classification

### 3.2.1 The Discourse

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &: \text{John gave Mary the book.}\\
U_2 &: \text{She thanked him.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Forward-Looking Centers

For each utterance, list forward-looking centers ranked by syntactic prominence (Subject > Object > Other):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_b(U_1) &= \langle \text{John}, \text{Mary}, \text{the book} \rangle \\
C_b(U_2) &= \langle \text{Mary}, \text{John} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Preferred Center Computation

The preferred center $C_p(U_i)$ is the **highest-ranked element of $C_b(U_{i-1})$ that is also realized (as a pronoun or NP) in $U_i$**.

$$
C_p(U_2) = \text{Mary} \quad (\text{Mary is realized as "She" in } U_2)
$$

### 3.2.4 Transition Comparison

Compare $C_p(U_2)$ with $C_b(U_1)$:

$$
C_p(U_2) = \text{Mary} \;\in\; C_b(U_1) = \langle \text{John}, \text{Mary}, \text{the book} \rangle \;\checkmark
$$

Also, $C_p(U_2) \neq C_p(U_1)$. Recall $C_p(U_1) = \text{John}$ (since $U_0$ is null, we conventionally take the first entity of $U_1$).

Because $C_p(U_2) \neq C_p(U_1)$ **but** $C_p(U_2) \in C_b(U_1)$, the transition is:

$$
\boxed{\text{trans}(U_1, U_2) = \text{SMOOTH-SHIFT}}
$$

This is a *coherent* discourse but with a shift in attention.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Lappin-Leass Salience Scoring

### 3.3.1 The Discourse

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &: \text{The CEO of the company called the manager.} \\
S_2 &: \text{He invited him to the board meeting.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Resolve the two pronouns in $S_2$: **He** and **him**.

### 3.3.2 Candidates

Let us denote the candidates:

- $A_1 = \text{the CEO of the company}$ — subject of $S_1$
- $A_2 = \text{the manager}$ — object of $S_1$

### 3.3.3 Salience Scores

Using canonical Lappin-Leass weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(A_1) &= S_{\text{recency}} + S_{\text{subject}} \\
       &= \tfrac{100}{1} + 80 = 180 \\
S(A_2) &= S_{\text{recency}} \\
       &= \tfrac{100}{1} + 0 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Resolution

Apply hard constraints first:

- **He** (Subject pronoun, masculine, singular, animate): both $A_1$ and $A_2$ pass. Highest salience = $A_1$ (CEO).
- **Him** (Object pronoun, masculine, singular, animate): both pass. Same scores; **object pronoun rule** says *him* is the object form, which disprefers the antecedent if the antecedent is currently a subject — but in this sentence *he* is the subject and *him* is the object. The local salience ranking still gives $A_1$ first.

**However**, the *parallel function rule* boosts $A_2$ for *him* (since the manager was the object in $S_1$ and *him* is the object in $S_2$):

$$
S(A_2 \text{ for him}) = S_{\text{recency}} + S_{\text{parallel}} = 100 + 35 = 135
$$

This still loses to $A_1 = 180$. So:

$$
\boxed{\text{He} \to \text{the CEO}, \quad \text{Him} \to \text{the CEO}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CEO is *also* the most plausible referent for *him* under the composite salience. Real-world preference would split (he = CEO, him = manager), illustrating that **pure salience models can violate the parallel-function rule**. KTU accepts the salience-winner as the model answer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Dimension | Hobbs' Algorithm | Lappin-Leass | Centering Theory |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Search space | Surface parse tree (prior sentences) | Linear prior text | Discourse model with $C_b$, $C_p$ |
| Constraints | Gender + number only | Gender, number, person, recency, subject, parallel | Coherence transitions |
| Output | Single best antecedent | Single best per salience rank | Transition class + center stack |
| Best for | Tree-walk exam questions | Weighted salience exam questions | Discourse coherence exam questions |
| Weakness | Ignores pragmatics & parallelism | Ignores structural embedding | Ignores fine-grained lexical semantics |
| Complexity | $O(d \cdot n)$ tree walks | $O(n^2)$ candidate pair scoring | $O(n)$ per utterance |

## 3.5 Edge Case — Reflexive vs. Pronoun (Binding Theory Application)

Consider:

> *"John_i told Bill_j that he_i/j liked pizza."*

Apply **Principle A** and **Principle B**:

- *himself* (reflexive) would be local → must bind *John*.
- *he* (pronoun) must be **free** in local domain → cannot be *John* (the local subject), so it must be *Bill* (a non-local antecedent or the matrix subject) — but in matrix context, *he* may be Bill or even an extralinguistic referent.

$$
\boxed{\text{himself} \to \text{John}, \quad \text{he} \to \text{Bill (likely)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often resolve *he* → *John* using the "subject-first" rule. Binding Principle B forbids it. **Always apply Binding Principles before salience.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Resolution — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Sentence Splitter]
    B --> C[Tokeniser and POS Tagger]
    C --> D[Parse Tree Builder]
    D --> E[Mention Detector]
    E --> F{Anaphoricity Filter}
    F -- "Anaphoric" --> G[Hard Constraint Check]
    F -- "Not Anaphoric" --> H[Exophoric Skip]
    G --> I[Candidate Antecedent Set]
    I --> J[Salience Scorer]
    J --> K[Best Antecedent Selected]
    K --> L[Coreference Chain Output]
    H --> L
    L --> M[Discourse Representation Update]
    M --> N[Centering Transition Classifier]
    N --> O[Local Coherence Verdict]
```

> The diagram above traces the **standard anaphora resolution pipeline**. The bounded scope over which the **Mention Detector** searches (typically 1–3 sentences back) is the operational definition of the *local* discourse context.

## 4.2 Centering Theory State Machine Across Three Utterances

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> U1Init: Utterance 1 begins
    U1Init --> SmoothShift: U2 introduces new preferred center
    SmoothShift --> Continue: U3 preserves same center
    Continue --> Retain: U3 keeps center but reorders
    Retain --> RoughShift: U4 introduces unrelated entity
    RoughShift --> SmoothShift: U5 re-introduces prior entity
    SmoothShift --> [*]
```

> The state machine above encodes the **four Centering transitions**: CONTINUE, RETAIN, SMOOTH-SHIFT, and ROUGH-SHIFT. The state names match the actual transition labels used in the analytical matrix.

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Hobbs' Tree-Walking Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start at Pronoun NP node] --> S1[Climb to first branching S or NP]
    S1 --> S2{Left sister NP exists?}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3[Search left sisters right to left]
    S3 --> S4{Match on gender and number?}
    S4 -- "Yes" --> S5[Return antecedent and stop]
    S4 -- "No" --> S6[Continue breadth first]
    S6 --> S1
    S2 -- "No" --> S7[Climb to next branching node]
    S7 --> S1
```

> The block diagram above formalises the **iterative climbing-and-searching behaviour** of Hobbs' algorithm. Each block maps to a numbered step in Section 3.1.3.

## 4.4 Accessibility Hierarchy (Ariel, 1990)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Zero Pronoun in pro drop languages] --> A2[Clitics se le la]
    A2 --> A3[Unstressed Pronouns him her it]
    A3 --> A4[Stressed Pronouns HIM HER IT]
    A4 --> A5[Demonstratives this that]
    A5 --> A6[Definite NPs the CEO]
    A6 --> A7[Proper Names John]
    A7 --> A8[Full NP Descriptions the tall man in the corner]
```

> The hierarchy above reflects **Ariel's Accessibility Theory** — the more reduced the referring expression, the higher the assumed cognitive salience of the antecedent. This is the foundational empirical motivation for the **Pronoun Realization Rule** of Centering Theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Differentiate between anaphoric and cataphoric reference with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Anaphoric reference** is a type of endophoric reference in which a referring expression points to a **previously mentioned** entity in the discourse. The referent lies in the **prior** linguistic context.
> *Example:* "The dog barked. **It** was loud." — The pronoun *it* refers back to *the dog*.
>
> **Cataphoric reference** is a type of endophoric reference in which a referring expression points to a **subsequently mentioned** entity. The referent lies in the **following** linguistic context.
> *Example:* "After **he** finished, Rahul went home." — The pronoun *he* refers forward to *Rahul*.
>
> Both are endophoric (textual), in contrast to **exophoric** (situational) reference. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 example + 1 contrast.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the four transitions defined by Centering Theory and arrange them from most coherent to least coherent.**

**Model Answer:**

> The four Centering transitions, ordered from **most coherent to least coherent**, are:
> 1. **CONTINUE** — $C_p(U_i) = C_b(U_i) = C_p(U_{i-1})$; the preferred center remains unchanged.
> 2. **RETAIN** — $C_p(U_i) = C_p(U_{i-1})$ but the forward-looking center list changes; center preserved with reordering.
> 3. **SMOOTH-SHIFT** — $C_p(U_i) \neq C_p(U_{i-1})$ but $C_p(U_i)$ was at least mentioned in $U_{i-1}$.
> 4. **ROUGH-SHIFT** — $C_p(U_i)$ was not mentioned in $U_{i-1}$ at all; sharpest coherence break.
> *(3 marks: 1 mark per transition + 0.5 for correct ordering + 0.5 for definitions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest Paper]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain Hobbs' algorithm for pronominal anaphora resolution. Apply it to the following text and identify the antecedent of the underlined pronoun:
> *"The astronomer told the journalist that **she** had discovered a new star."*

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Discuss the limitations of Hobbs' algorithm and how Lappin-Leass salience weighting overcomes them.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Hobbs' Algorithm — Algorithm Outline** *(3 marks)*
   - Hobbs' algorithm traverses the surface syntactic tree of prior sentences, right-to-left, breadth-first, starting from the NP node dominating the pronoun.
   - The first NP that agrees in gender and number is selected as the antecedent.
   - Eight procedural steps define the tree-walk; the algorithm makes a single deterministic pass without probabilities.

2. **Application to the Given Text** *(4 marks)*
   - Parse the prior sentence "The astronomer told the journalist that she had discovered a new star."
   - Pronoun *she* is in the embedded S node.
   - Walking the tree left-to-right search, the first NP that matches gender (female) and number (singular) is **"the journalist"**.
   - The subject "The astronomer" is a male antecedent (commonly gendered) and is ruled out by gender agreement — though even without gender, the tree-walk order favours the embedded S clause's left sister.
   - **Verdict:** *she* → *the journalist* (or, with world-knowledge override, *the astronomer* if we know the astronomer is female, in which case Hobbs' algorithm requires re-running with gender-relaxed search).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Limitations of Hobbs' Algorithm** *(3.5 marks)*
   - Strictly syntactic — ignores salience, parallel-function, and discourse prominence.
   - Fails on long-distance antecedents across paragraph boundaries.
   - Cannot distinguish multiple antecedents of the same gender/number.
   - Highly sensitive to parser errors; performance is bounded by the parser's accuracy.
   - Treats all prior sentences as equally accessible — no recency weighting.

2. **How Lappin-Leass Overcomes These** *(3.5 marks)*
   - Introduces **composite salience weighting** combining recency, grammatical role (subject, object), emphasis, expletive, head-noun match, and parallel-function factors.
   - Recency factor $S_{\text{recency}} = 100/d$ explicitly penalises distant antecedents.
   - Subject preference (weight 80) and parallel-function weight (35) handle cases where Hobbs' pure tree-walk fails.
   - The salience model is **empirically trained** against an annotated corpus, making it more robust to parser noise.
   - However, it loses the formal correctness of the syntactic walk and depends on annotated data for weight calibration.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main Paper]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Define the Centering Theory constructs $C_b(U_i)$ and $C_p(U_i)$. Given the discourse:
> $U_1$: *The professor praised the student.*
> $U_2$: *She gave him a scholarship.*
> $U_3$: *It was awarded for his research.*
> Identify $C_b$ for each utterance, $C_p(U_2)$ and $C_p(U_3)$, and the transitions between $U_1 \to U_2$ and $U_2 \to U_3$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain Binding Principles A, B, and C (Chomsky 1981) and show how they interact to license the choice between *John saw himself* and *John saw him* in the sentence *"The CEO_i told the manager_j that he_i/*j approved the merger."*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definitions** *(2 marks)*
   - $C_b(U_i)$ — **forward-looking centers** of $U_i$: the set of discourse entities realised in $U_i$, ranked by grammatical prominence (Subject > Object > Other).
   - $C_p(U_i)$ — **preferred center** of $U_i$: the highest-ranked entity in $C_b(U_{i-1})$ that is also realised in $U_i$.

2. **Identification** *(5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_b(U_1) &= \langle \text{The professor}, \text{the student} \rangle \\
C_b(U_2) &= \langle \text{The professor (She)}, \text{the student (him)} \rangle \\
C_b(U_3) &= \langle \text{The scholarship (It)}, \text{the student (his)} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
C_p(U_2) = \text{The professor} \quad \text{(realised as "She" in } U_2)
$$

$$
C_p(U_3) = \text{The student} \quad \text{(realised as "his" in } U_3)
$$

**Transition $U_1 \to U_2$:** $C_p(U_2) = \text{professor} = C_p(U_1) = \text{professor}$, and $C_b(U_2) = \langle \text{professor, student} \rangle$ preserves the same ranking as $C_b(U_1)$. **Transition = CONTINUE.**

**Transition $U_2 \to U_3$:** $C_p(U_3) = \text{student} \neq C_p(U_2) = \text{professor}$. Also, $C_p(U_3) = \text{student} \in C_b(U_2)$. **Transition = SMOOTH-SHIFT.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Binding Principles** *(3 marks)*
   - **Principle A** — An anaphor (e.g., *himself, herself*) must be **bound** in its local domain (the smallest clause containing it and a subject).
   - **Principle B** — A pronoun (e.g., *he, she, it*) must be **free** (i.e., not bound) in its local domain.
   - **Principle C** — An R-expression (a referring expression like *John*, *the merger*) must be **free everywhere** in the sentence.

2. **Application** *(4 marks)*
   - "himself" inside the embedded clause "approved the merger" must be bound by the local subject *the CEO* → **himself = the CEO**. This obeys Principle A.
   - "he" in the embedded clause cannot be bound by the local subject *the manager* (because of Principle B) → *he* is **free** in its domain.
   - Therefore *he* must refer to an entity outside the local domain — the matrix subject *the CEO* is a strong candidate by recency and subject preference.
   - "he" = *the CEO* satisfies Principle B (free locally) and Principle C (no conflict with any R-expression).
   - "him" (object form) follows the same logic and likely refers to *the manager* (the local object) because the manager is **outside the embedded clause's binding domain** for the pronoun... Actually, *him* in the embedded clause is the local object, so the manager as the local object is permissible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students apply only **Principle A** and forget **Principles B and C**. Marks are split as **2 marks for A**, **2 marks for B**, **2 marks for C**, and **1 mark for synthesis**. Skipping B and C forfeits 4 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 ways students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing anaphora with coreference.** Anaphora is a *textual* relation; coreference is an *entity* relation. They overlap but are not identical. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Resolving pronouns using only the subject-preference rule** without applying hard constraints (gender/number) first. The gender filter often rules out the subject. (–1.5 marks)
> 3. **Omitting Binding Principles B and C** in long-answer questions. Most students only mention Principle A. (–2 to –4 marks depending on the question)
> 4. **Forgetting the local-context window.** When asked to resolve a pronoun, always state the **sentence-distance** and the **search window** (e.g., "searched the previous 2 sentences"). (–1 mark)
> 5. **Treating Hobbs' algorithm as probabilistic.** It is deterministic and tree-driven, not statistical. (–1.5 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Local Discourse Context and Reference**
>
> - **Three layers of reference:** Exophora (situational/deictic), Endophora (textual) split into **anaphora** (backward) and **cataphora** (forward).
> - **Anaphora vs. coreference:** anaphora is a relation between *expressions*; coreference is a relation between *entities*.
> - **Hard constraints first, salience second.** Gender, number, person, animacy agreement must be verified before applying any salience heuristic.
> - **Hobbs' algorithm** = deterministic, syntactic, right-to-left, breadth-first tree walk over the prior sentences. First matching NP wins.
> - **Lappin-Leass salience:** $S(A) = \sum w_i f_i(A)$ with canonical weights **100 (recency), 80 (subject), 70 (emphasis), 50 (expletive), 40 (head-noun/cleft), 35 (parallel)**.
> - **Centering Theory four transitions** in coherence order: **CONTINUE > RETAIN > SMOOTH-SHIFT > ROUGH-SHIFT**.
> - **Forward-looking centers $C_b$** are ranked Subject > Object > Other.
> - **Preferred center $C_p(U_i)$** is the highest-ranked element of $C_b(U_{i-1})$ also realised in $U_i$.
> - **Pronoun Realization Rule (C3):** Pronouns are preferred over full NPs in CONTINUE and RETAIN transitions; full NPs are preferred in SHIFTs.
> - **Binding Principles:** A (anaphor bound locally) | B (pronoun free locally) | C (R-expression free everywhere).
> - **Bridging anaphora** ("the restaurant" → "the food") requires world knowledge and **associative anaphora** rules, not just coreference.
> - **Hobbs' algorithm fails on long-distance and non-subject antecedents**; Lappin-Leass salience addresses this with weighted scoring.
> - **Local context window** is typically the previous 1–3 sentences for traditional algorithms; transformer-based models extend this implicitly via self-attention but the *classical* exam window is local.
> - **Always state the search window, the constraints applied, and the algorithm used** in KTU answers — the valuation scheme awards marks for the *methodology* as much as the *answer*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using World Knowledge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using World Knowledge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**World Knowledge** in Natural Language Processing (NLP) refers to the structured and unstructured information about the real world that an NLP system draws upon to interpret, disambiguate, and reason about natural language text. It encompasses facts about entities, their attributes, relationships, events, conceptual hierarchies, and commonsense rules that go beyond what is explicitly written in the input sentence.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, World Knowledge is treated as an external knowledge resource (lexical, ontological, or commonsense) that augments statistical and neural language models to perform tasks such as:

- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)**
- **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)**
- **Question Answering (QA)**
- **Textual Entailment / Natural Language Inference (NLI)**
- **Coreference Resolution**
- **Discourse Coherence Analysis**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim)**: World Knowledge is the background information about the world, including facts, concepts, relations, and commonsense, that a language understanding system must access to bridge the gap between surface linguistic form and intended meaning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you read the sentence:
*"He saw the bark and jumped."*

A child who has never seen a dog might be confused. But a person with **world knowledge** instantly reasons:
- "bark" → sound a dog makes
- "jumped" → startled reaction
- Therefore "he" likely saw a dog.

That accumulated human experience is **world knowledge**. For a machine, we must explicitly encode such knowledge in machine-readable structures (ontologies, knowledge graphs, lexical databases).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy**: Think of World Knowledge as the *mental encyclopedia* a human uses while reading. NLP systems without world knowledge are like tourists reading a menu in a foreign language — they can see the words but cannot understand the cultural dish names, ingredients, or customs behind them.

## 1.3 Sources of World Knowledge

| **Source Type** | **Examples** | **Granularity** |
|---|---|---|
| Lexical Databases | WordNet, VerbNet | Word-level |
| Ontologies | SUMO, DOLCE, SNOMED-CT | Concept-level |
| Knowledge Graphs | Google Knowledge Graph, Wikidata, DBpedia, Freebase | Entity-level |
| Commonsense KBs | ConceptNet, ATOMIC, TransOMCS | Inference-level |
| Distributional Knowledge | Pre-trained embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT) | Latent / Statistical |
| Frame Semantic KBs | FrameNet, PropBank | Predicate-argument level |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in KTU Context

- **Coverage** — fraction of words/concepts in a corpus that appear in the KB.
- **Precision / Recall / F1** — for knowledge-based disambiguation tasks.
- **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** — used when ranking candidate senses.
- **Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Rate** — proportion of unseen concepts.
- **Knowledge Base Density** — average number of relations per entity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a World-Knowledge-Augmented NLP System

A typical pipeline for using world knowledge in NLP follows these structured steps:

1. **Input Text Acquisition** → Raw text or speech transcript is received.
2. **Linguistic Pre-processing** → Tokenization, POS tagging, lemmatization, parsing.
3. **Entity & Concept Recognition** → Identify named entities, multi-word expressions, and lexical concepts.
4. **Knowledge Retrieval** → Query the world knowledge base (KB) to fetch candidate meanings, relations, frames, or facts.
5. **Knowledge Integration** → Combine retrieved knowledge with the linguistic context (e.g., vector concatenation, graph propagation, attention conditioning).
6. **Inference / Decision** → Apply the augmented representation to the downstream task (WSD, QA, NLI, etc.).
7. **Response Generation / Output** → Produce the final NLP output.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: The "Integration" stage is where modern systems differ. Classical systems used **symbolic integration** (rule-based), while modern systems use **neural integration** (e.g., knowledge-graph embeddings fused with transformer hidden states).

## 2.2 Knowledge Representation Schemes

### 2.2.1 Taxonomic / Hierarchical (WordNet Style)

Concepts are organized using **hypernymy** (is-a) and **hyponymy** (specific-of) relations.

$$\text{Apple} \xrightarrow{\text{hypernym}} \text{Fruit} \xrightarrow{\text{hypernym}} \text{Food} \xrightarrow{\text{hypernym}} \text{Entity}$$

### 2.2.2 Graph-Based (Knowledge Graph Style)

Entities are **nodes**; relations are **labeled, directed edges**.

$$(\text{Paris}, \text{capitalOf}, \text{France}) \in E$$

### 2.2.3 Frame-Based (FrameNet Style)

Frames describe events or situations; words evoke frames and fill semantic roles.

$$\text{[Buyer] bought [Goods] from [Seller] for [Price]}$$

### 2.2.4 Vector-Based (Distributional)

Concepts are encoded as dense vectors in $\mathbb{R}^d$ such that semantic similarity is approximated by geometric proximity.

$$\text{sim}(w_i, w_j) = \cos(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j) = \frac{\mathbf{v}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_j}{\vert\vert\mathbf{v}_i\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\mathbf{v}_j\vert\vert}$$

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Knowledge-Enhanced NLP

| **Challenge Without World Knowledge** | **How World Knowledge Solves It** |
|---|---|
| Lexical ambiguity ("bank" = river/money) | WordNet senses with contextual sense ranking |
| Vocabulary gap in QA | Knowledge Graph lookup (e.g., "Who wrote Hamlet?") |
| Inconsistent semantics in NLI | ConceptNet commonsense relations |
| Limited generalization in neural LMs | KG embeddings as inductive bias |
| Implicit information ("He drank the coffee. It was hot.") | Event-based frame reasoning |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Notation** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{u}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\mathbf{v}\vert\vert}$ | Word/sense similarity in vector KBs |
| Lesk Score | $\text{Lesk}(s_i, c) = \vert \text{gloss}(s_i) \cap \text{context}(c) \vert$ | Word Sense Disambiguation |
| PMI (Pointwise Mutual Information) | $\text{PMI}(w_1,w_2) = \log\dfrac{P(w_1,w_2)}{P(w_1)P(w_2)}$ | Collocation / knowledge extraction |
| TF-IDF Knowledge Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}_{t,d} \cdot \log\dfrac{N}{\text{df}_t}$ | Concept importance in KB lookup |
| Knowledge Graph Triple | $(h, r, t) \in E$ | Fact representation |
| TransE Score | $f_r(h,t) = \vert\vert \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \vert\vert_2$ | KG embedding scoring |
| PageRank on KB Graph | $PR(v) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \dfrac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | Concept importance ranking |
| ConceptNet Confidence | $c \in [0, 10]$ | Edge reliability weighting |
| F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Evaluation of disambiguation |
| WSD Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\#\text{correct senses}}{\#\text{total instances}}$ | Task-level evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: World knowledge is the *scaffolding* that powers modern AI assistants — Siri, Alexa, Google Search, and ChatGPT all rely on knowledge graphs and commonsense KBs (e.g., Google's Knowledge Graph with **>500 billion facts**, ConceptNet with **>34 million edges**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lesk Algorithm for WSD (Canonical KTU Problem)

The **Simplified Lesk Algorithm** selects the sense whose dictionary definition (gloss) shares the maximum number of content words with the target word's context.

### Formal Definition

Given a target word $w$ in context $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ with candidate senses $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$ each having a gloss $G(s_i)$, the chosen sense is:

$$
s^{*} = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,\ldots,k\}} \; \vert G(s_i) \cap C \vert
$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Sentence**: *"The **bank** of the river was muddy."*

**Candidate Senses of "bank" (WordNet)**:
- Sense 1 (noun): *"Sloping land (especially the slope beside a body of water)"* → gloss words: {sloping, land, slope, beside, body, water}
- Sense 2 (noun): *"A financial establishment that accepts deposits and channels funds"* → gloss words: {financial, establishment, accepts, deposits, channels, funds}
- Sense 3 (noun): *"A supply or stock held in reserve"* → gloss words: {supply, stock, held, reserve}

**Context words (after stop-word removal)**: $\{river, muddy\}$

**Computation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overlap}(s_1, C) &= \vert \{sloping, land, slope, beside, body, water\} \cap \{river, muddy\} \vert \\
&= 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Overlap}(s_2, C) &= \vert \{financial, establishment, accepts, deposits, channels, funds\} \cap \{river, muddy\} \vert \\
&= 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Overlap}(s_3, C) &= \vert \{supply, stock, held, reserve\} \cap \{river, muddy\} \vert \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

All overlaps are zero (basic Lesk fails on short contexts). We therefore apply the **Extended Lesk** with weighted overlap using inverse document frequency.

**IDF-weighted overlap** (toy values shown):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{idf}(\text{river}) &= 2.4, \quad \text{idf}(\text{muddy}) = 3.1 \\
\text{score}(s_1) &= \text{idf}(\text{water}) \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{water}\in C] \\
&\text{ (water and river are semantically related through gloss overlap expansion)} \\
\text{score}(s_1) &= 2.4 \quad (\text{via lemma expansion: river} \to \text{water}) \\
\text{score}(s_2) &= 0 \\
\text{score}(s_3) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{s^{*} = s_1 = \text{``sloping land beside water'' sense}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Knowledge Graph Triple Scoring (TransE)

TransE is the foundational translational KG-embedding model.

### Objective

For a true triple $(h, r, t)$, the embedding relation must satisfy:

$$
\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} \approx \mathbf{t}
$$

### Loss Function

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(h,r,t) \in S} \sum_{(h',r,t') \in S'} \max\left(0, \; \gamma + d(h+r,t) - d(h'+r,t')\right)
$$

where:

- $S$ = set of valid triples
- $S'$ = set of **corrupted** (negative) triples
- $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert\vert_2$ (L2 distance)
- $\gamma$ = margin hyperparameter

### Worked Numerical Example (Mini-KG)

Knowledge base with 2 facts:
- $f_1$: (Paris, capitalOf, France)
- $f_2$: (Berlin, capitalOf, Germany)

Embeddings (assume $d=2$, learned as):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{h}_{\text{Paris}} &= (0.6, 0.2), \quad \mathbf{r}_{\text{capitalOf}} = (0.3, 0.5), \quad \mathbf{t}_{\text{France}} = (0.9, 0.7) \\
\mathbf{h}_{\text{Berlin}} &= (0.5, 0.3), \quad \mathbf{t}_{\text{Germany}} = (0.8, 0.8)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Score of $f_1$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(h_{\text{Paris}}+r, t_{\text{France}}) &= \vert\vert (0.6+0.3, 0.2+0.5) - (0.9, 0.7) \vert\vert_2 \\
&= \vert\vert (0.9, 0.7) - (0.9, 0.7) \vert\vert_2 \\
&= 0.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Excellent fit — the embeddings are consistent.

**Score of corrupted triple** (Paris, capitalOf, Germany):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(h_{\text{Paris}}+r, t_{\text{Germany}}) &= \vert\vert (0.9, 0.7) - (0.8, 0.8) \vert\vert_2 \\
&= \sqrt{(0.1)^2 + (-0.1)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.141
\end{aligned}
$$

The positive triple scores 0.00 and the negative triple scores 0.141 → the model **correctly discriminates**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Knowledge Sources

| **Criterion** | **WordNet** | **ConceptNet** | **DBpedia** | **FrameNet** | **Distributional KB** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Lexical | Commonsense | Encyclopedic | Predicate-argument | Latent |
| Structure | Synset graph | Bipartite graph | RDF graph | Frame-schemas | Vector space |
| Manual Curation | High | Medium | Low | High | None |
| Multilingual | Partial | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| Symbolic Reasoning | Strong | Strong | Strong | Strong | Weak |
| Neural Integration | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Strong |
| KTU Typical Use | WSD, similarity | NLI, QA | Entity linking | SRL, NLI | Similarity, retrieval |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse WordNet (lexical) with ConceptNet (commonsense). Remember — WordNet is curated by Princeton linguists and contains **synsets**; ConceptNet is crowdsourced and contains **assertions** of the form `(concept1, relation, concept2)` with a confidence weight.
