   - Segment embeddings: 0 for tokens of A, 1 for tokens of B.
   - The `[CLS]` final hidden state $h_{[\text{CLS}]}$ is passed through a binary classifier:
     $$P(\text{IsNext} \mid A, B) = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{NSP}}\, h_{[\text{CLS}]} + b_{\text{NSP}})$$
   - Loss:
     $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}} = -\log P(y \mid A, B)$$

2. **Application to Natural Language Inference (NLI):**  
   - **Task:** Given a *premise* and a *hypothesis*, classify the relationship as **entailment**, **contradiction**, or **neutral**.
   - **Input format:** `[CLS] Premise [SEP] Hypothesis [SEP]`
   - **Output:** The `[CLS]` vector is passed through a 3-class softmax classifier.
   - During fine-tuning, the entire model + classification head is trained on NLI-labeled data (e.g., SNLI or MultiNLI dataset).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing BERT with GPT:** Many students write that BERT is "auto-regressive" or "generative". BERT is **only an encoder** — it produces contextual embeddings; it **cannot** generate text. GPT models are decoders and are auto-regressive.
> 2. **Forgetting the 80/10/10 split:** Stating that BERT "hides 15% of words with `[MASK]`" is incomplete. Examiners want the 80/10/10 breakdown.
> 3. **Mixing up `[CLS]` and `[SEP]`:** `[CLS]` is for classification aggregation (placed at the *start*); `[SEP]` is for sentence separation (placed between and at the end of sentences).
> 4. **Confusing "deeply bidirectional" with "shallow bidirectional":** ELMo is shallowly bidirectional (concatenates L→R and R→L LSTMs at output). BERT is **deeply bidirectional** (every layer attends in both directions simultaneously).
> 5. **Wrong parameter count:** BERT-base = 110M, BERT-large = 340M. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 6. **Skipping the loss derivation:** For 7-mark questions, examiners expect a formula, not just a description. Always write the cross-entropy expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **BERT = Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers** — encoder-only, no decoder.
- ⭐ **Two pre-training tasks:** Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP).
- ⭐ **15% masking rate** for MLM, with the **80% `[MASK]` / 10% random / 10% unchanged** split.
- ⭐ **Input embedding = Token + Segment + Position embeddings** (element-wise sum).
- ⭐ **Special tokens:** `[CLS]` (classification aggregation, sequence start), `[SEP]` (sentence separator).
- ⭐ **BERT-base:** 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, 110M parameters. **BERT-large:** 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, 340M parameters.
- ⭐ **WordPiece tokenizer** with vocabulary of ~30,000 subword units (uses `##` prefix for continuations).
- ⭐ **Maximum sequence length** = **512** tokens.
- ⭐ **Pre-training corpus:** BooksCorpus (800M words) + English Wikipedia (2.5B words) ≈ 3.3B words.
- ⭐ **Deeply bidirectional** — every encoder layer attends to both left and right context (unlike ELMo, which is only shallowly bidirectional).
- ⭐ **Pre-training uses unsupervised text** — no human labels required.
- ⭐ **Fine-tuning** is task-specific, uses small learning rates (2e-5 to 5e-5), 2–4 epochs, and updates **all** parameters.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Sentiment analysis, NER, Question Answering (SQuAD), NLI, Search, Chatbots.
- ⭐ **Cannot generate text** — encoder-only. For generation, use GPT-family (decoder) or T5 (encoder-decoder).
- ⭐ **BERT uses learned position embeddings**, not the fixed sinusoidal embeddings of the original Transformer.
- ⭐ The `[CLS]` token's final hidden state is the **pooled representation** for sentence-level tasks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPT-2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GPT-2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GPT-2 (Generative Pre-trained Transformer 2)** is a large-scale, unsupervised, **decoder-only transformer language model** released by OpenAI in February 2019 (Radford et al., *Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners*). It is trained using a **causal language modeling objective** (predicting the next token given all previous tokens) over a massive corpus of approximately **40 GB** of internet text called **WebText**. The flagship configuration contains roughly **1.5 billion parameters**, **48 transformer layers**, a hidden dimension of **1600**, and **25 attention heads**, with a context window of **1024 tokens**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GPT-2 is the canonical example used in the NLP syllabus to illustrate *decoder-only autoregressive transformers*, *multi-task zero-shot learning*, and *sub-word tokenization (Byte-Pair Encoding)*. It is the direct conceptual ancestor of GPT-3, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4o.

The probability distribution over a token sequence $u = (u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n)$ is factorized using the **chain rule of probability** as:

$$P(u) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(u_i \mid u_{1}, u_{2}, \dots, u_{i-1})$$

This conditional probability is what GPT-2 learns to model.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Greedy Storyteller"

Imagine a student who has read every book, newspaper, blog post, and Reddit thread in a giant library — but has **never been explicitly taught grammar, never taken an exam, and never been told what a "task" is**. When you whisper the first sentence of a story to this student, they can keep telling the story word-by-word, sounding uncannily fluent. They have internalized patterns: how sentences open, how arguments close, how summaries follow long passages, even how to translate French to English if the prompt hints at it.

That student is **GPT-2**.

- **The library** $\rightarrow$ the WebText dataset (~40 GB).
- **The student** $\rightarrow$ the 1.5 B-parameter transformer.
- **Whispering a sentence** $\rightarrow$ the **prompt** (context window of up to 1024 tokens).
- **Telling the story word-by-word** $\rightarrow$ **autoregressive generation** (next-token sampling).
- **"Never been told the task"** $\rightarrow$ **zero-shot / unsupervised multitask learning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from BERT:** GPT-2 uses a **uni-directional (left-to-right) decoder** with **masked self-attention**, so token $i$ can only attend to tokens $1 \dots i$. BERT, in contrast, is **bi-directional**. This asymmetry is what makes GPT-2 a generative model and BERT a discriminative one.

## 1.3 Position of GPT-2 in the KTU Syllabus

Within **Module 4 (Large Language Models / Transformer-based Generative Architectures)**, GPT-2 is the bridge between:

| Predecessor (typically covered earlier) | GPT-2 (this topic) | Successor (covered later) |
|---|---|---|
| Vanilla Transformer (Encoder-Decoder, Vaswani et al., 2017) | Decoder-only causal LM | GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA, Mistral |
| Word2Vec / GloVe (static embeddings) | Contextual sub-word embeddings (BPE) | Instruction-tuned chat models |
| Task-specific NLP pipelines | Zero-shot task transfer | Few-shot / in-context learning |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition

GPT-2 is built by **stacking $N$ identical decoder blocks**. Each block contains two sub-layers, both wrapped in a **residual connection** and followed by **layer normalization**.

### 2.1.1 The Two Sub-Layers per Block

1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — lets each token look at previous tokens and itself.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — a non-linear two-layer MLP applied independently to each position.

> [!NOTE]
> GPT-2 uses **Post-LN** (LayerNorm after the residual addition), which is the original Vaswani-2017 placement. (Note: many modern reproductions use Pre-LN for stability.)

### 2.1.2 End-to-End Pipeline (Forward Pass)

The input text is processed in this sequence:

1. **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenization** — converts raw text into sub-word integer IDs.
2. **Token embedding lookup** — $W_E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, where $V = 50257$ (GPT-2's vocabulary size) and $d$ is the hidden dimension.
3. **Positional embedding addition** — $W_P \in \mathbb{R}^{1024 \times d}$ adds a learnable position vector.
4. **Pass through $N$ transformer decoder blocks** (with **masked** self-attention).
5. **Final layer normalization.**
6. **Linear projection + softmax** — $W_E^{\top}$ produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary.

## 2.2 Core Components — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Masked (Causal) Self-Attention

- **Why:** A language model predicting the next token must not "cheat" by looking at future tokens. We **mask** attention logits for positions $j > i$ to $-\infty$ before the softmax, forcing attention weights for the future to be zero.
- **How:** A token at position $i$ projects into three vectors $\mathbf{q}_i, \mathbf{k}_i, \mathbf{v}_i$ via learned matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$.
- The attention score between positions $i$ and $j$ is scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ (where $d_k = d / h$ and $h$ = number of heads).

### 2.2.2 Multi-Head Concatenation

- **Why:** A single attention head can only focus on one relationship type. Multiple heads let the model simultaneously track syntax, coreference, semantic similarity, etc.
- **How:** Project queries, keys, and values into $h$ subspaces, run attention in parallel, concatenate, then mix with $W^O$.

### 2.2.3 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network

- **Why:** Attention is a weighted average — inherently linear. The FFN injects **non-linearity** and increases model capacity.
- **How:** Two affine transformations with a **GELU** activation in between, expanding the hidden size by a factor of 4.

### 2.2.4 Residual Connections

- **Why:** Deep stacks of layers suffer from **vanishing gradients**. Residual shortcuts let gradients flow directly to early layers during back-propagation.
- **How:** Output of each sub-layer is $\text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$.

### 2.2.5 Initialization Scaling (GPT-2 Specific)

- **Why:** When stacking many residual layers, the variance of activations grows proportionally to depth $n$, causing training instability.
- **How:** GPT-2 rescales residual connections by $1/\sqrt{n}$ (where $n$ is the number of residual layers), keeping the forward-pass variance bounded. This is one of GPT-2's key engineering contributions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Master them cold. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Autoregressive factorization | $P(u_1, \dots, u_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(u_i \mid u_{<i})$ | $u_{<i} = (u_1, \dots, u_{i-1})$ |
| 2 | Scaled dot-product attention (one head) | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V$ | $M_{ij} = 0$ if $j \leq i$, else $-\infty$ (causal mask) |
| 3 | Multi-head attention | $\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O$, $\text{head}_i = \text{Attn}(XW^Q_i, XW^K_i, XW^V_i)$ | $h$ = number of heads |
| 4 | Layer normalization | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Computed per-token, across feature dim $d$ |
| 5 | Residual + LayerNorm block | $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Post-LN variant used in GPT-2 |
| 6 | Position-wise FFN | $\text{FFN}(x) = W_2 \cdot \text{GELU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2$ | Inner dim $= 4d$ |
| 7 | GELU activation (exact) | $\text{GELU}(x) = x \cdot \Phi(x)$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF |
| 8 | GELU activation (tanh approx.) | $\text{GELU}(x) \approx 0.5x\!\left(1 + \tanh\!\left[\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715 x^3)\right]\right)$ | Used in TF/PyTorch defaults |
| 9 | Output probability | $P(u_{i+1} \mid u_{\leq i}) = \text{softmax}(h_i W_E^{\top})[u_{i+1}]$ | $h_i$ = final hidden state at position $i$ |
| 10 | GPT-2 residual scale | $y = x + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \text{Sublayer}(x)$ | $n$ = number of residual layers |
| 11 | GPT-2 model dims (XL / 1.5 B) | $N{=}48,\ d{=}1600,\ h{=}25,\ d_k{=}64,\ V{=}50257,\ L{=}1024$ | Flagship configuration |
| 12 | Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(u_i \mid u_{<i}; \theta)$ | Training objective |
| 13 | BPE merge rule | $(a,b) \rightarrow c$ where $\text{count}(ab)$ is highest | Iterative greedy merging |
| 14 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\log P(u_i \mid u_{<i})\right)$ | Lower is better |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Creative content generation:** blog drafts, marketing copy, code completion (Copilot lineage).
- **Zero-shot NLP:** sentiment classification, translation, summarization — performed via clever prompt engineering rather than fine-tuning.
- **Foundation for downstream models:** every modern instruct-tuned LLM traces its architecture lineage back to GPT-2.
- **Research benchmark:** introduced the canonical methodology for evaluating large LMs (zero-shot perplexity, LAMBADA, Winograd, etc.).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Causal Masked Self-Attention Output

We derive the attention output at a single position $i$, showing explicitly why the mask is necessary.

**Step 1 — Project inputs.**
For input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, project into queries, keys, values:

$$
Q = XW^Q, \quad K = XW^K, \quad V = XW^V
$$

where $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$.

**Step 2 — Compute raw attention scores.**
The raw score for the link from token $j$ (key) to token $i$ (query) is:

$$
s_{ij} = \dfrac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \dfrac{(X_i W^Q)(X_j W^K)^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply causal mask.**
We want token $i$ to attend **only** to positions $j \leq i$. Define the mask:

$$
M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases}
$$

So the masked score becomes:

$$
\tilde{s}_{ij} = s_{ij} + M_{ij}
$$

**Step 4 — Row-wise softmax.**
The attention weight from $i$ to $j$ is:

$$
\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(\tilde{s}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{i} \exp(\tilde{s}_{ik})}
$$

For $j > i$: $\exp(-\infty) = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_{ij} = 0$, enforcing the causal constraint.

**Step 5 — Weighted sum of values.**
The output vector at position $i$ is:

$$
z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \alpha_{ij} v_j
$$

**Step 6 — Stack into matrix form.**
Combining all positions:

$$
Z = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** Because $q \cdot k = \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} q_l k_l$ is a sum of $d_k$ products with unit variance, so its variance is $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1, preventing the softmax from saturating (i.e., producing near-one-hot outputs that kill gradients).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why GPT-2 Initializes Residual Layers with $1/\sqrt{n}$

**Setup.** Consider a stack of $n$ residual blocks. At block $t$, the input $x_t$ is updated as:

$$
x_{t+1} = x_t + F_t(x_t)
$$

**Assume** $F_t(x_t)$ has zero mean and covariance $\sigma^2 I_d$, and is independent of $x_t$.

**Step 1 — Compute mean.** $\mathbb{E}[x_{t+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x_t] + 0$, so the mean is preserved.

**Step 2 — Compute variance.**
Let $\text{Var}(x_t) = v_t I_d$. Then:

$$
\text{Var}(x_{t+1}) = \text{Var}(x_t) + \text{Var}(F_t(x_t)) = v_t + \sigma^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the recurrence.**
With $v_0 = 1$ (unit-variance input embedding):

$$
v_t = 1 + t\sigma^2 \approx t\sigma^2 \quad \text{for large } t
$$

So variance grows **linearly with depth $n$**.

**Step 4 — Apply scaling.**
GPT-2 rescales the residual contribution: $F_t \leftarrow F_t / \sqrt{n}$. Then $\text{Var}(F_t) = \sigma^2 / n$, and:

$$
v_t \approx 1 + t \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

Setting $t = n$ gives $v_n \approx 1 + \sigma^2$, a **constant** independent of depth.

**Conclusion.** Scaling residuals by $1/\sqrt{n}$ keeps the forward-pass activation variance bounded as $n \to \infty$, dramatically improving training stability for very deep transformer stacks like GPT-2's 48 layers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Probability via Tied Embeddings

**Setup.** After $N$ transformer blocks, position $i$ produces a hidden vector $h_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. GPT-2 ties the output projection back to the input embedding matrix $W_E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$.

**Step 1 — Linear projection.**
Compute raw logits for every vocabulary token $v$:

$$
\ell_v^{(i)} = h_i \cdot W_E[v]^{\top}
$$

This is a dot product between the hidden state and the embedding row of vocabulary token $v$. Tying reduces parameter count and improves sample efficiency.

**Step 2 — Softmax normalization.**
Convert logits to a probability distribution:

$$
P(u_{i+1} = v \mid u_{\leq i}) = \dfrac{\exp(\ell_v^{(i)})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\ell_w^{(i)})}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-likelihood loss.**
The negative log-likelihood of the training corpus is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(u_{i+1}^* \mid u_{\leq i}; \theta)
$$

where $u_{i+1}^*$ is the ground-truth next token. Backpropagation through the entire 48-layer stack updates $\theta = \{W_E, W_P, W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O, W_1, W_2, \gamma, \beta\}$ across all layers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GPT-2 vs. Other Transformers

| Property | Vanilla Transformer (Vaswani 2017) | BERT | **GPT-2** | GPT-3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Encoder-Decoder | Encoder-only | **Decoder-only** | Decoder-only |
| Attention direction | Bidirectional (enc) + Causal (dec) | Bidirectional | **Causal (masked)** | Causal |
| Pre-training objective | (Supervised MT) | Masked LM + NSP | **Next-token LM** | Next-token LM |
| Parameters (base) | ~65 M (enc+dec) | 110 M (base), 340 M (large) | **117 M / 345 M / 762 M / 1.5 B** | 175 B |
| Vocabulary | ~37 K (BPE) | 30 K (WordPiece) | **50 257 (BPE)** | 50 257 (BPE) |
| Context length | Variable | 512 | **1024** | 2048 |
| Position embedding | Sinusoidal (fixed) | Learned absolute | **Learned absolute** | Learned |
| In-context learning | None | None | **Zero-shot** | Few-shot |
| LayerNorm placement | Post-LN | Post-LN | **Post-LN** (with $1/\sqrt{n}$ rescale) | Pre-LN |
| Activation | ReLU | GELU | **GELU** | GELU |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End GPT-2 Forward-Pass Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram renders the high-level functional architecture of GPT-2's forward pass, from raw text input to predicted next-token probabilities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText["RAW TEXT CORPUS"] --> tok["BYTE PAIR ENCODING TOKENIZER"]
    tok --> ids["INTEGER TOKEN IDS LENGTH n"]
    ids --> emb["TOKEN EMBEDDING LOOKUP WE E dim V x d"]
    pos["POSITIONAL EMBEDDING WE P dim 1024 x d"] --> emb
    emb --> stack["STACK OF N IDENTICAL DECODER BLOCKS"]
    stack --> subA["SUB-LAYER 1 MASKED MULTI HEAD SELF ATTENTION"]
    subA --> resA["RESIDUAL ADD plus LAYER NORM"]
    resA --> subB["SUB-LAYER 2 POSITION WISE FEED FORWARD GELU 4d"]
    subB --> resB["RESIDUAL ADD plus LAYER NORM"]
    resB --> nextBlock{"MORE BLOCKS REMAINING"}
    nextBlock -- YES --> stack
    nextBlock -- NO --> finalLN["FINAL LAYER NORM"]
    finalLN --> proj["LINEAR PROJECTION TIED TO WE TRANSPOSE"]
    proj --> softmax["SOFTMAX OVER VOCABULARY V EQUALS 50257"]
    softmax --> probs["NEXT TOKEN PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION P u i plus 1 GIVEN u LEQ i"]
    probs --> sampled["SAMPLING TOP K NUCLEUS OR GREEDY"]
    sampled --> outToken["OUTPUT TOKEN APPENDED TO SEQUENCE"]
    outToken --> tok
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram reading guide:** The loop arrow `outToken → tok` represents autoregressive generation — each newly sampled token is fed back into the input for the next forward pass until a stop token or max length is reached.

---

## 4.2 Inside One Decoder Block — Detailed Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["INPUT X dim n x d"] --> projQ["LINEAR Q WQ"]
    inX --> projK["LINEAR K WK"]
    inX --> projV["LINEAR V WV"]
    projQ --> maskM["CAUSAL MASK TRIANGULAR"]
    projK --> maskM
    projQ --> score["SCALE 1 OVER SQRT d_k"]
    projK --> score
    maskM --> score
    score --> softA["SOFTMAX ROW WISE"]
    softA --> weightedV["WEIGHTED SUM WITH V"]
    projV --> weightedV
    weightedV --> headOut["HEAD OUTPUT dim n x d_k"]
    headOut --> allH["CONCATENATE h HEADS"]
    allH --> wO["LINEAR PROJECTION WO"]
    wO --> resA["ADD RESIDUAL X plus HEAD OUTPUT"]
    resA --> lnA["LAYER NORM"]
    lnA --> ffn1["LINEAR W1 dim d to 4d"]
    ffn1 --> gelu["GELU ACTIVATION"]
    gelu --> ffn2["LINEAR W2 dim 4d to d"]
    ffn2 --> resB["ADD RESIDUAL plus FFN OUTPUT"]
    resB --> lnB["LAYER NORM"]
    lnB --> outY["BLOCK OUTPUT Y dim n x d"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the causal mask in GPT-2's self-attention mechanism. Why is it essential for autoregressive language modeling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In GPT-2, self-attention uses a **causal (triangular) mask** $M$ that is added to the scaled dot-product logits before the softmax. Formally:

$$
\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M_{ij}\right)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_i \cdot k_k}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M_{ik}\right)}, \quad M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0, & j \leq i \\ -\infty, & j > i \end{cases}
$$

The mask sets the attention weight to **0** for all future positions $j > i$, ensuring token $i$ can only aggregate information from tokens $1, \dots, i$. This is **essential** because GPT-2's training objective is to predict $u_{i+1}$ given $u_1, \dots, u_i$ — without masking, the model could trivially "cheat" by peeking at the answer, making the loss meaningless. The mask preserves the autoregressive factorization $P(u) = \prod_i P(u_i \mid u_{<i})$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four released GPT-2 model sizes along with their parameter counts and number of layers. Mention any one distinguishing architectural feature of the largest variant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Variant | Parameters | Layers (N) | Hidden dim (d) | Heads (h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-2 Small | 117 M | 12 | 768 | 12 |
| GPT-2 Medium | 345 M | 24 | 1024 | 16 |
| GPT-2 Large | 762 M | 36 | 1280 | 20 |
| GPT-2 XL | **1.5 B** | **48** | **1600** | **25** |

All variants share vocabulary $V = 50257$, context length $L = 1024$, GELU activation, and tied input/output embeddings.

**Distinguishing feature of the 1.5 B XL variant:** GPT-2 XL uses a **residual scaling factor of $1/\sqrt{N}$** (where $N$ = number of residual layers) at the initialization of each residual connection, in order to keep activation variance bounded across the 48-layer stack — a stability fix not strictly required for shallower variants like GPT-2 Small. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the overall architecture of **GPT-2**. Explain the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **scaled masked multi-head self-attention** formulation used by GPT-2. Clearly show how the causal mask is incorporated and why the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor is necessary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

GPT-2 is a **stack of $N$ identical transformer decoder blocks** operating over a context window of **1024 tokens**. The major components are:

1. **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenizer:** Converts raw text into sub-word integer IDs from a vocabulary of size $V = 50257$.
2. **Token embedding matrix $W_E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$:** Maps each token ID to a $d$-dimensional learned vector.
3. **Positional embedding matrix $W_P \in \mathbb{R}^{1024 \times d}$:** Adds a learned position-dependent vector to each token embedding, giving the model awareness of token order.
4. **Decoder block (repeated $N$ times):**
    - *Masked multi-head self-attention:* captures dependencies among previous tokens.
    - *Position-wise feed-forward network (FFN):* a two-layer MLP with GELU and inner dimension $4d$, adding non-linearity.
    - *Residual connection + LayerNorm* around each sub-layer.
5. **Final LayerNorm:** applied to the topmost block's output.
6. **Tied output projection + softmax:** $W_E^{\top}$ projects the final hidden state to vocabulary logits, followed by softmax to produce $P(u_{i+1} \mid u_{\leq i})$.

> **Block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** and **Section 4.2** of these notes for the Mermaid-rendered GPT-2 architecture diagram (to be reproduced on the answer sheet as a hand-drawn or neatly labeled sketch).

**(7 marks — 1 mark per correct identification + 1 mark for the diagram)**

---

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Linear projections.** For an input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ at one head:

$$
Q = XW^Q, \quad K = XW^K, \quad V = XW^V
$$

with $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$, $d_k = d/h$.

**Step 2 — Raw scores.** The dot product between query at position $i$ and key at position $j$ is $q_i \cdot k_j = X_i W^Q (X_j W^K)^{\top}$. Stacked:

$$
S = \dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the causal mask.** Define $M_{ij} = 0$ if $j \leq i$ else $-\infty$. Then $\tilde{S} = S + M$.

**Step 4 — Softmax (row-wise).**

$$
A_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(\tilde{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\tilde{S}_{ik})}
$$

By construction, $A_{ij} = 0$ for $j > i$, enforcing causality.

**Step 5 — Multiply by $V$.** The single-head output is:

$$
Z = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}
$$

**Step 6 — Multi-head concatenation.**

$$
\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_h) \, W^O
$$

**Justification for the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling:**
If $q$ and $k$ components are i.i.d. with zero mean and unit variance, then $\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = d_k$. Without scaling, large $d_k$ pushes dot products into regions where $\exp$ saturates (gradient $\approx 0$). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the score variance back to 1, keeping softmax in a well-conditioned region.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per major derivation step + 1 mark for the scaling justification)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** as used in GPT-2. Describe the algorithm with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how GPT-2 performs **zero-shot task transfer** without any fine-tuning. Provide two illustrative examples (e.g., translation, summarization, or question-answering). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) BPE Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Byte-Pair Encoding** is a **sub-word tokenization** algorithm that GPT-2 uses to build its 50 257-token vocabulary. It iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols.

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize the vocabulary with all 256 byte values.
2. Represent the training corpus as a sequence of bytes.
3. **Repeat** until vocabulary size $V = 50257$ is reached:
    - Find the most frequently occurring adjacent byte pair $(a, b)$.
    - Add a new token $c$ to the vocabulary.
    - Replace every occurrence of $(a,b)$ in the corpus with $c$.
4. Stop when target vocabulary size is reached.

**Worked example on the toy corpus** `["low", "low", "low", "new", "new", "newer", "newer", "newer", "newest"]`:

| Iteration | Most-frequent pair | Merge |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (init) | `(l, o)` | `lo` |
| 1 | `(lo, w)` | `low` |
| 2 | `(n, e)` | `ne` |
| 3 | `(ne, w)` | `new` |
| 4 | `(new, er)` | `newer` |
| 5 | `(new, est)` | `newest` |

**Resulting vocabulary:** `{l, o, w, n, e, r, s, t, lo, low, ne, new, newer, newest}`.

**Why BPE for GPT-2?** It eliminates the **out-of-vocabulary (OOV) problem** (any text can be encoded as a sequence of bytes), balances vocabulary size against sequence length, and lets the model generalize across morphological variants. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Zero-Shot Task Transfer (7 marks):**

GPT-2's defining empirical claim (Radford et al., 2019) is that **a sufficiently large generative language model, trained purely on next-token prediction over diverse internet text, implicitly learns to perform many downstream NLP tasks without any explicit supervision or fine-tuning.** This phenomenon is called **zero-shot task transfer**.

**Why does it work?** Because the WebText corpus naturally contains examples of almost every task — translations, summaries, dialogues, Q&A, sentiment — embedded in their natural context. GPT-2's training objective forces it to model the conditional distribution $P(\text{next token} \mid \text{prompt})$, so when prompted with text that *looks like* the start of a translation or summary, the model continues with the correct task format.

**Example 1 — Translation (English $\to$ French):**
> Prompt: `Translate the following English sentence to French.`
> `English: The cat sat on the mat.`
> `French:`

GPT-2 generates: `Le chat était assis sur le tapis.`

The model has never been told that translation is its job. It picks up the pattern from the prompt structure and from the many bilingual code-switching examples in WebText.

**Example 2 — Summarization:**
> Prompt: `Article: The Kerala state government announced a new digital literacy mission... (300 words)`
> `TL;DR:`

GPT-2 generates a one-sentence summary, because the training corpus frequently contains "TL;DR:" headers followed by condensed content.

**Example 3 — Question Answering:**
> Prompt: `Q: What is the capital of France?`
> `A:`

GPT-2 generates: `Paris.`

**Key insight:** The prompt is the programming interface. By formatting the prompt appropriately, the user elicits the desired task — no parameter updates required. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in GPT-2 questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling** and its justification — examiners specifically look for "variance normalization" reasoning.
> 2. **Confusing GPT-2 with BERT:** GPT-2 is **decoder-only** with **causal masking**; BERT is **encoder-only** and **bidirectional**. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the residual scaling $1/\sqrt{n}$:** This is a **GPT-2-specific engineering trick**, not generic transformer knowledge. State it explicitly when discussing GPT-2's design.
> 4. **Drawing BERT's MLM head instead of GPT-2's tied softmax head:** The output layer for GPT-2 uses $W_E^{\top}$, not a separate output matrix.
> 5. **Using the wrong vocabulary size:** GPT-2 uses **50 257**, not 30 000 (BERT) or 50 000 (GPT-1).
> 6. **Ignoring the BPE step:** Many answers jump straight from "text" to "embedding" — examiners expect the tokenization step to appear explicitly.
> 7. **Hand-drawn diagrams must include all sub-layers** (attention, FFN, residual, LN). A single "transformer block" box earns partial credit; full marks require the internal components.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GPT-2 = Decoder-only transformer**, autoregressive, 1.5 B parameters, 48 layers, 1600 hidden, 25 heads, 1024 context, 50 257 vocab.
- **Architecture:** tokenize (BPE) → embed (token + position) → $N \times$ [masked MHA → residual+LN → FFN → residual+LN] → final LN → tied softmax projection.
- **Objective:** $P(u) = \prod_i P(u_i \mid u_{<i})$, optimized via cross-entropy.
- **Causal mask:** $M_{ij} = -\infty$ for $j > i$ — enforces left-to-right information flow.
- **Scaling:** $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation; $1/\sqrt{n}$ residual scaling keeps activation variance bounded across deep stacks.
- **Activation:** GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit) — smoother than ReLU, allows small negative gradients.
- **Tied embeddings:** Output projection matrix = $W_E^{\top}$, reducing parameters and improving sample efficiency.
- **BPE tokenization:** iterative merging of the most frequent adjacent byte pairs until $V = 50257$ — eliminates OOV.
- **Zero-shot transfer:** GPT-2 performs tasks by recognizing the *format* of the prompt, not via explicit task heads. Prompt design is the "programming language."
- **Dataset:** WebText ≈ 40 GB, scraped from outbound Reddit links with at least 3 upvotes — emphasizes quality-filtered natural text.
- **Distinguishing GPT-2 from BERT:** GPT-2 is generative + unidirectional; BERT is discriminative + bidirectional.
- **Distinguishing GPT-2 from the original Transformer (Vaswani 2017):** GPT-2 has **no encoder**, no cross-attention, no task-specific heads; it's pure decoder stack with tied embeddings.
- **Lineage:** GPT-2 → GPT-3 (175 B, few-shot) → InstructGPT (RLHF) → ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) → GPT-4 / GPT-4o (multimodal).
- **Exam mantra:** *"GPT-2 is a deep decoder-only stack trained to predict the next token, using causal masking, GELU, tied embeddings, and BPE."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ELMO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ELMO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ELMO

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models)** is a deep contextualized word representation technique introduced by Peters et al. (2018) that generates word vectors by learning from the internal states of a deep **bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (biLSTM)** language model. Unlike traditional static word embeddings (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe), ELMo representations are a **function of the entire input sentence**, meaning the same word receives different embeddings depending on its surrounding context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> ELMo is fundamentally a **contextual embedding** model. It does NOT produce a single fixed vector per word. Instead, it produces vectors that are dynamically computed from all layers of a pre-trained biLSTM. This is the single most important conceptual distinction between ELMo and Word2Vec/GloVe, and is a guaranteed high-weight question in KTU examinations.

Formally, given a sequence of $N$ tokens $(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N)$, ELMo computes a $2L+1$ set of representations for each token $t_k$, where $L$ is the number of biLSTM layers:

$$R_k = \{x_k^{LM}, \overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}^{LM}, \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}^{LM} \mid j = 1, \ldots, L\}$$

The final ELMo vector for token $t_k$ is a learned linear combination of these representations:

$$ELMo_k = E(R_k) = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ELMo like a "smart translator who listens to the full conversation"**

Imagine you are reading the English sentence: **"He went to the **bank** to deposit money."** versus **"She sat on the **bank** of the river."**

- In **Word2Vec/GloVe**, the word "bank" is ALWAYS represented by the **same fixed vector** — like a translator who has memorized one definition and forces it upon every sentence.
- In **ELMo**, the word "bank" receives **two completely different vectors** because ELMo "listens" to the surrounding context words ("deposit money" vs. "river"). It is like a translator who actually pays attention to the conversation and infers the right meaning.

A more vivid analogy: think of ELMo as a **chameleon** 🎨. A chameleon changes its color based on the environment (context). Similarly, ELMo's word embeddings change based on the sentence in which the word appears.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "bi-directional" matters:** ELMo uses TWO LSTMs — one reading the sentence left-to-right (forward pass) and one reading right-to-left (backward pass). This is like reading a mystery novel: you need to know what happened BEFORE (backward LSTM) and what happens AFTER (forward LSTM) to fully understand any given chapter (word).

## 1.3 Key Components at a Glance

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Character CNN (CharCNN)** | Converts raw text characters into initial word vectors $x_k^{LM}$ |
| **Forward LSTM ($\overrightarrow{LSTM}$)** | Models probability of next token given left context |
| **Backward LSTM ($\overleftarrow{LSTM}$)** | Models probability of previous token given right context |
| **Residual connections** | Pass intermediate layer outputs to the final combiner |
| **Layer weighting $s_j$** | Learned softmax weights per layer per task |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold)

- **Hidden state dimension: $d = 1024$** (in the original ELMo large model)
- **Number of biLSTM layers: $L = 2$** (original implementation)
- **Context-independent embedding size: $d_{char} = 512$**
- **Vocabulary size used in training: ~800K tokens (1B Word Benchmark)**
- **Training corpus size: 1 Billion Word Language Model Benchmark**
- **Final output vector size: $2L+1 = 5$ representations per token** stacked per layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Contextual vs. Static — A Direct Comparison:**
> - **Static (Word2Vec/GloVe):** bank → always the **same vector** regardless of context.
> - **Contextual (ELMo):** bank → **different vectors** in financial vs. river contexts.
> - **ELMo is a function $f: (w, \text{context}) \to \mathbb{R}^d$**, not a lookup table.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Breakdown — The "How"

The ELMo architecture can be decomposed into **three hierarchical stages**. Let us walk through each one:

### Stage 1: Character-Aware Input Embedding (Context-Independent)

ELMo does NOT use a traditional embedding lookup table. Instead, it uses a **Character Convolutional Neural Network (CharCNN)** to build initial word embeddings.

**Why?** Because characters can handle **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV)** words. Even if a word was never seen in training, its characters were.

$$x_k = CharCNN(t_k) \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

The CharCNN operates as:
1. Each character is mapped to a character embedding $c \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{char}}$.
2. A 1D convolution slides over the character sequence of the word.
3. **Max-pooling** over time extracts the most salient features.
4. A highway network and a final linear projection produce $x_k$.

### Stage 2: Bidirectional LSTM Layers (Context-Dependent)

For each LSTM layer $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$:

**Forward pass** (predicts next token given left history):

$$\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j} = \overrightarrow{LSTM}(x_k, \overrightarrow{h}_{k-1,j})$$

**Backward pass** (predicts previous token given right history):

$$\overleftarrow{h}_{k,j} = \overleftarrow{LSTM}(x_k, \overleftarrow{h}_{k+1,j})$$

**Why two directions?** A unidirectional LSTM only sees past context. The backward LSTM allows the model to know what comes AFTER the current word, which is critical for tasks like Question Answering where a question's meaning depends on the answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint training objective:** ELMo maximizes the log-likelihood of the forward AND backward directions simultaneously:
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \log P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}; \Theta_x, \overrightarrow{\Theta}_{LSTM}, \Theta_s) + \log P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N; \Theta_x, \overleftarrow{\Theta}_{LSTM}, \Theta_s) \right)$$

### Stage 3: Linear Combination Layer (Task-Specific)

The final ELMo vector is a **weighted sum of all biLSTM layers + the input embedding**:

$$ELMo_k^{task} = E(R_k; \Theta^{task}) = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}$$

Where:
- $\gamma^{task}$ is a scalar multiplier learned per task (allows scaling the entire ELMo vector).
- $s_j^{task}$ are softmax-normalized weights learned per layer per task.
- $h_{k,j}^{LM}$ is the concatenation of forward and backward hidden states: $h_{k,j} = [\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}; \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}]$.
- $h_{k,0}^{LM} = x_k$ (the context-independent input embedding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Insight — Why "Deep" matters:**
> Different layers of ELMo capture **different linguistic information**:
> - **Lower layer (L=1):** Captures **syntactic** information (POS tags, dependencies).
> - **Higher layer (L=2):** Captures **semantic** information (word sense, context meaning).
> - The learnable $s_j$ weights automatically decide how much of each layer to use.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $t_k$ | The $k^{th}$ token in the sequence | String or integer index |
| $N$ | Total number of tokens in a sentence | Variable, depends on input |
| $L$ | Number of biLSTM layers | **2** in original ELMo |
| $d$ | Hidden state dimension | **1024** (large) or **512** (small) |
| $x_k^{LM}$ | Context-independent token embedding (output of CharCNN) | $\in \mathbb{R}^{d_{char}}$ |
| $\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}$ | Forward LSTM hidden state at layer $j$, position $k$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $\overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}$ | Backward LSTM hidden state at layer $j$, position $k$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $h_{k,j}^{LM}$ | Concatenation: $[\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}; \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}]$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$ |
| $R_k$ | Set of $2L+1$ representations for token $k$ | A multi-layer set |
| $s_j^{task}$ | Softmax weight for layer $j$, task-specific | $\sum s_j = 1$ |
| $\gamma^{task}$ | Task-specific scaling factor for entire ELMo vector | Scalar, learned |
| $ELMo_k$ | Final ELMo embedding for token $t_k$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{2d}$ typically |
| $P(t_k \mid \cdot)$ | Probability of token given context (forward or backward) | Log-likelihood objective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe-symbol rule for KTU tables:** When writing the ELMo formula in your exam, AVOID the bare pipe character in math — always write it as $\vert$ or $\mid$. For instance, use $\mid s_j^{task} \mid$ instead of $\vert s_j^{task} \vert$ when nesting inside a table row.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

ELMO has been deployed in:
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Reduced F1 error by ~1.7% on CoNLL-2003.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Improved SST-5 accuracy by ~1.0%.
- **Question Answering (SQuAD):** Boosted F1 by ~1.9%.
- **Coreference Resolution:** State-of-the-art on CoNLL-2011 shared task.
- **Zero-shot transfer:** As a pre-trained feature extractor, ELMo can be plugged into virtually any downstream NLP architecture (BiLSTM-CRF, BERT-style encoders, etc.) without re-training.

The engineering insight: ELMo democratized **transfer learning in NLP**. Before ELMo, NLP was dominated by task-specific architectures. ELMo proved that large-scale unsupervised pre-training of language models yields universally useful representations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ELMo Combination Formula

We will now derive the final ELMo vector step by step. This is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation question in KTU 2024.

### Step 1: Define the Input Representation

The first step is to map each token $t_k$ in the input sequence to a dense vector $x_k^{LM}$. This is done via a Character-CNN:

$$x_k^{LM} = CharCNN(t_k), \quad x_k^{LM} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{char}}$$

**Reasoning:** Characters handle OOV words better than word-level lookups. Even for unseen words, the character composition is known.

### Step 2: Forward Language Model Probability

The forward LSTM models the probability of the sequence in the **left-to-right** direction. By the chain rule of probability:

$$P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(t_k \mid t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_{k-1})$$

Each conditional probability is computed using a softmax over the vocabulary $V$:

$$P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}) = \text{softmax}(\overrightarrow{h}_{k,L} \cdot W_V + b_V)_k$$

where $\overrightarrow{h}_{k,L}$ is the top-layer forward hidden state and $W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$ is the output projection matrix.

### Step 3: Backward Language Model Probability

Similarly, the backward LSTM models the sequence in the **right-to-left** direction:

$$P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, t_{k+2}, \ldots, t_N)$$

$$P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N) = \text{softmax}(\overleftarrow{h}_{k,L} \cdot W_V + b_V)_k$$

### Step 4: The Joint Log-Likelihood Objective

The ELMo model is trained by **maximizing the sum of the two log-likelihoods**:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ \log P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}; \overrightarrow{\Theta}) + \log P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N; \overleftarrow{\Theta}) \right]$$

We then update the parameters $\overrightarrow{\Theta}$ and $\overleftarrow{\Theta}$ via backpropagation through time (BPTT).

### Step 5: Stack All Layer Representations

For each token $t_k$, we collect the representations from **every layer** (input embedding + all biLSTM layers):

$$R_k = \{x_k^{LM}, h_{k,1}^{LM}, h_{k,2}^{LM}, \ldots, h_{k,L}^{LM}\}$$

where $h_{k,j}^{LM} = [\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}; \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}]$ is the concatenated forward and backward hidden state at layer $j$.

**Why include $x_k^{LM}$ (the input)?** Because the input embedding captures **morphological** features (root word, suffixes) that the LSTMs may smooth over.

### Step 6: Learn the Task-Specific Combination

For a downstream task (e.g., NER), we learn task-specific scalar weights $s_j^{task}$ for each layer and a global scaling $\gamma^{task}$:

$$ELMo_k^{task} = E(R_k; \Theta^{task}) = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}$$

The softmax constraint $\sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} = 1$ ensures the combination behaves like a probability distribution over layers.

### Step 7: Plug ELMo into a Downstream Model

Typically, the ELMo vector is **concatenated** with the token's existing embedding in the downstream model. For instance, in a BiLSTM-CRF for NER:

$$v_k = [x_k^{token}; ELMo_k^{task}]$$

This combined vector $v_k$ is then fed into the task-specific BiLSTM-CRF, allowing the downstream model to leverage both task-specific word embeddings and pre-trained contextual information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway of the Derivation:** ELMo's power comes from THREE properties:
> 1. **Contextual** — depends on the entire sentence.
> 2. **Deep** — combines representations from multiple layers.
> 3. **Task-Adaptive** — different layers weighted differently per task.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Layer Weight Computation)

Suppose $L=2$ and we initialize weights for a sentiment task. The raw layer scores are $u_0=0.5$, $u_1=2.0$, $u_2=1.0$.

**Compute softmax weights $s_j$:**

$$s_j = \frac{e^{u_j}}{\sum_{i=0}^{2} e^{u_i}}$$

$$e^{u_0} = e^{0.5} \approx 1.649, \quad e^{u_1} = e^{2.0} \approx 7.389, \quad e^{u_2} = e^{1.0} \approx 2.718$$

$$\sum_{i=0}^{2} e^{u_i} = 1.649 + 7.389 + 2.718 = 11.756$$

$$s_0 = \frac{1.649}{11.756} \approx 0.140, \quad s_1 = \frac{7.389}{11.756} \approx 0.628, \quad s_2 = \frac{2.718}{11.756} \approx 0.231$$

**Interpretation:** The downstream task places **62.8%** weight on the top biLSTM layer (semantic), 23.1% on the lower biLSTM (syntactic), and only 14.0% on the raw input embedding. This matches the linguistic insight that **higher layers carry more semantic information**.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: ELMo vs. Static Embeddings vs. BERT

| Property | Word2Vec/GloVe | **ELMo** | BERT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Embedding type | Static | **Contextual** | Contextual |
| Architecture | Shallow (CBOW/Skip-gram) | **Deep biLSTM (2 layers)** | Deep Transformer (12+) |
| Directionality | N/A | **Bidirectional** | Bidirectional (truly) |
| Handles polysemy | ❌ No | **✅ Yes** | ✅ Yes |
| Handles OOV | ❌ No (Word2Vec), Partial (FastText) | **✅ Yes (CharCNN)** | ✅ Yes (subword) |
| Pre-training objective | Word co-occurrence | **Bidirectional LM** | Masked LM + NSP |
| Trainable per task | No (frozen) | **Yes (combination weights)** | Yes (fine-tunes everything) |
| Release year | 2013/2014 | **2018** | 2018 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ELMo End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Sentence: t1 t2 ... tN] --> B[Character CNN CharCNN]
    B --> C[Context-Independent Token Embeddings xkLM]
    C --> D[Forward LSTM Layer 1]
    C --> E[Backward LSTM Layer 1]
    D --> F[Forward LSTM Layer 2]
    E --> G[Backward LSTM Layer 2]
    F --> H[Concat Forward and Backward States Layer 2]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Concat Forward and Backward States Layer 1]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J[Stack All Layer Representations Rk]
    C --> J
    J --> K[Learned Softmax Weights sjtask]
    K --> L[Task-Specific Scalar Gamma task]
    L --> M[Final ELMo Vector ELMok]
    M --> N[Concatenate with Downstream Task Embedding]
    N --> O[Task-Specific BiLSTM CRF or Classifier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The sentence flows upward through CharCNN into the input embeddings, which then split into two parallel paths (forward and backward LSTMs). At the end, all layers are combined with learned weights to produce the final contextual vector.

## 4.2 Layer-by-Layer Information Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    L0[Layer 0: Character CNN Input Embedding] -->|Morphology| L1[Layer 1: biLSTM 1]
    L1 -->|Part of Speech Syntax| L2[Layer 2: biLSTM 2]
    L2 -->|Word Sense Semantics| LC[Layer Combiner Weighted Sum]
    L0 --> LC
    L1 --> LC
    LC --> OUT[ELMo Output Vector]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Hierarchy in ELMo:**
> - **Layer 0** → Morphological features (prefix, suffix, root).
> - **Layer 1** → Syntactic features (POS tags, dependency labels).
> - **Layer 2** → Semantic features (word sense, sentiment polarity).
> - **Combiner** → Linear blend of all three, learned per task.

## 4.3 BiLSTM Internal Cell Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XK[Input xk] --> FG[Forget Gate ft]
    XK --> IG[Input Gate it]
    XK --> CAND[Candidate Cell State Ctil]
    FK[Previous Cell State Ck-1] --> FG
    FG --> M1[Multiply]
    FK --> M1
    IG --> M2[Multiply]
    CAND --> M2
    M1 --> NEW[New Cell State Ck]
    M2 --> NEW
    NEW --> TANH[tanh Activation]
    TANH --> OUTG[Output Gate ot]
    OUTG --> HK[Hidden State hk]
    NEW --> HK
```

> [!TIP]
> **This LSTM cell is REPEATED for the forward direction AND in REVERSE for the backward direction.** The two directions have **separate, non-shared parameters**. This is what makes ELMo "bi-directional" in the truest sense.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between static word embeddings (Word2Vec) and contextual word embeddings (ELMo). Why is ELMo considered superior for NLP tasks involving polysemy?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Word2Vec | ELMo |
|---|---|---|
| Embedding type | Static (one vector per word) | Contextual (different vectors per context) |
| Handles polysemy | No — "bank" always has the same vector | Yes — "bank" differs in financial vs. river context |
| Architecture | Shallow (2-layer) | Deep biLSTM with residual connections |
| OOV handling | Limited | Excellent (Character CNN input) |

**Polysemy Explanation (1 mark):** A polysemous word has multiple meanings. In "river bank" vs. "money bank," the meaning is determined by surrounding words. ELMo's biLSTM encodes the entire sentence's context into the embedding, so the word "bank" receives **two different vectors**, one for each meaning. Word2Vec cannot do this because its embedding is learned from co-occurrence statistics that are merged across all contexts. Hence, ELMo resolves polysemy while Word2Vec does not.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the three components of an ELMo embedding. Why does ELMo use a Character CNN instead of a standard word-level embedding lookup?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three components of ELMo (2 marks):**
1. **Context-independent embedding** $x_k^{LM}$ (from CharCNN) — captures morphology.
2. **Intermediate biLSTM layer representations** $h_{k,1}^{LM}$ — capture syntax.
3. **Top biLSTM layer representations** $h_{k,2}^{LM}$ — capture semantics.

These are linearly combined with learned weights $s_j$ and a scalar $\gamma$ to form the final ELMo vector.

**Why CharCNN (1 mark):** The Character CNN allows ELMo to handle **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) words**. Even if a word was never seen during pre-training, its characters (or sub-words) were, so the model can still produce a meaningful embedding. A standard word-lookup table returns nothing for OOV words.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of ELMo in detail. Discuss the role of the bidirectional LSTM, the Character CNN input layer, and the final combination layer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the ELMo representation formula. Show how the joint log-likelihood of the forward and backward language models is used as the training objective. Include all mathematical steps.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models)** architecture consists of three principal stages:

**Stage 1 — Character-Aware Input Embedding:**

The raw input sentence is first tokenized into words. For each token $t_k$, a **Character-level Convolutional Neural Network (CharCNN)** produces a context-independent embedding $x_k^{LM} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{char}}$. The CharCNN slides convolutional filters over the characters of each word, applies max-pooling over time, and projects through a highway network. This is critical because:
- It handles **OOV words** via character composition.
- It captures **morphological** features (prefix, root, suffix).

**Stage 2 — Bidirectional LSTM Stack:**

The context-independent embeddings are fed into $L$ stacked biLSTM layers (typically $L=2$ in the original ELMo). Each layer contains:
- A **forward LSTM** $\overrightarrow{LSTM}$: processes the sentence left-to-right, producing $\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}$ at each position.
- A **backward LSTM** $\overleftarrow{LSTM}$: processes right-to-left, producing $\overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}$.

The two directions have **independent parameters** and are concatenated: $h_{k,j}^{LM} = [\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}; \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}]$.

**Why biLSTM?** A unidirectional LSTM only sees past context. The backward LSTM gives access to **future context**, which is essential for tasks like Question Answering where a question's intent is only clear after seeing the answer.

**Stage 3 — Task-Specific Combination Layer:**

For a downstream task, ELMo learns a softmax distribution $s_j^{task}$ over all $2L+1$ representations (input + L biLSTM layers) and a scalar $\gamma^{task}$:

$$ELMo_k^{task} = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}$$

The downstream model concatenates $ELMo_k^{task}$ with its own token embedding before feeding into the task-specific architecture.

**Key Properties (1 mark):** ELMo is contextual, deep, and task-adaptive.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation of the ELMo Representation:**

**Step 1:** Define the input as a sequence of $N$ tokens: $(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_N)$. Convert each token to a vector via CharCNN:

$$x_k^{LM} = CharCNN(t_k), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

**Step 2:** Forward language model probability. The forward LSTM models:

$$P(t_1, \ldots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}; \Theta_x, \overrightarrow{\Theta}_{LSTM}, \Theta_s)$$

**Step 3:** Backward language model probability. The backward LSTM models:

$$P(t_1, \ldots, t_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N; \Theta_x, \overleftarrow{\Theta}_{LSTM}, \Theta_s)$$

**Step 4:** Joint training objective. ELMo maximizes the **sum of both log-likelihoods**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\Theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ \log P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}; \overrightarrow{\Theta}) + \log P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N; \overleftarrow{\Theta}) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Collect layer representations. For each $t_k$, we have:

$$R_k = \{x_k^{LM}, h_{k,1}^{LM}, h_{k,2}^{LM}\}$$

**Step 6:** Linear combination. The final ELMo vector:

$$ELMo_k^{task} = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}, \quad \text{where} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} = 1$$

This is a softmax-weighted sum of all layers, scaled by a task-specific factor $\gamma^{task}$. The combination weights $s_j^{task}$ are learned via backpropagation on the downstream task.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The derivation shows that ELMo is a **linear combination of all internal biLSTM states**, allowing different NLP tasks to emphasize different linguistic levels (morphology, syntax, semantics) automatically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare ELMo with Word2Vec and GloVe. Explain with examples how ELMo resolves the polysemy problem. Mention the key differences in pre-training objectives.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of "Deep Bidirectional" language modeling in ELMo. Show the training objective mathematically and explain why bi-directionality is essential for contextual embeddings.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Word2Vec | GloVe | **ELMo** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 2013 | 2014 | **2018** |
| Architecture | Shallow NN | Co-occurrence matrix factorization | **Deep biLSTM (2L layers)** |
| Embedding type | Static | Static | **Contextual** |
| Polysemy | No | No | **Yes** |
| Pre-training objective | Predict center/context word | Reconstruct co-occurrence matrix | **Bidirectional LM log-likelihood** |
| OOV handling | No | No | **Yes (CharCNN)** |
| Contextual depth | None | None | **Multiple layers** |

**Polysemy Resolution Example (2 marks):**
- Sentence 1: "I deposited money in the **bank**." (Financial meaning)
- Sentence 2: "We had a picnic on the river **bank**." (Geographic meaning)
- In Word2Vec/GloVe: "bank" → same vector $\vec{v}_{bank}$.
- In ELMo: "bank" → $\vec{v}_{bank,fin}$ in sentence 1, $\vec{v}_{bank,geo}$ in sentence 2. The biLSTM layers encode the surrounding context ("deposited money" vs. "river"), producing different hidden states and thus different output embeddings.

**Pre-training Objective (1 mark):** Word2Vec uses a local context window with negative sampling. GloVe uses global co-occurrence matrix reconstruction. ELMo uses the bidirectional language modeling objective, which is a much richer signal.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Concept of "Deep Bidirectional" (3 marks):**

The term **"Deep"** in ELMo refers to the stacking of multiple biLSTM layers (typically $L=2$). Each layer captures progressively higher-level linguistic information:
- Layer 1 (lower): Syntactic — POS tags, dependency parse features.
- Layer 2 (higher): Semantic — word sense, sentiment, discourse-level features.

The term **"Bidirectional"** refers to the parallel processing of the sentence in both directions. The forward LSTM reads left-to-right; the backward LSTM reads right-to-left. The two directions have **independent, non-shared parameters** and are later concatenated.

**Training Objective (3 marks):**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ \log P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}; \Theta) + \log P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N; \Theta) \right]$$

This objective trains the model to predict each token using **both its left AND right context simultaneously**, which is the mathematical embodiment of bidirectionality.

**Why bi-directionality is essential (1 mark):**

Consider the word "tall" in:
- Sentence A: "He is **tall** and loves basketball." (height-related)
- Sentence B: "The building is **tall** and has 30 floors." (size-related)

A unidirectional (forward-only) LSTM, when encoding "tall" in sentence A, has only seen "He is" so far. It cannot use "basketball" to disambiguate. The backward LSTM, by contrast, processes "loves basketball" first, providing the future context. Concatenating forward and backward states gives the word "tall" a **context-rich representation** that captures both directions of context.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\gamma^{task}$ scalar:** Many students write $ELMo_k = \sum s_j h_{k,j}$ but forget the task-specific scaling factor $\gamma^{task}$. This is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Saying "ELMo is a Transformer":** ELMo uses **biLSTM, not Transformers**. Mixing up with BERT is a frequent error that costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting CharCNN:** Many answers skip the character-level input stage, leading to loss of 1 mark and missing the OOV explanation.
> 4. **Writing "ELMo fine-tunes the entire model":** This is **WRONG**. ELMo is a **feature extractor** — it does NOT fine-tune the biLSTM during downstream training. Only the combination weights $s_j$ and $\gamma$ are learned. The biLSTM remains frozen.
> 5. **Wrong softmax constraint:** Some students write the weights as simple coefficients without the softmax normalization $\sum s_j = 1$. This loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing "contextual" with "context-window":** Word2Vec uses a context window but is NOT contextual. ELMo is contextual because the embedding is a function of the ENTIRE sentence, not just a fixed window.
> 7. **Not mentioning residual connections:** In the original ELMo paper, residual (skip) connections are added from the input to each LSTM layer. Omitting this loses 0.5-1 mark if a detailed architecture question is asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ELMo = Embeddings from Language Models** (Peters et al., 2018).
- [x] **Architecture:** CharCNN → $L$ stacked biLSTM layers → task-specific weighted combination.
- [x] **BiLSTM has TWO LSTMs with independent parameters:** forward (left-to-right) + backward (right-to-left).
- [x] **Hidden state dimension $d = 1024$** in the original large model; **$L=2$** layers.
- [x] **Final formula:** $ELMo_k^{task} = \gamma^{task} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{task} h_{k,j}^{LM}$.
- [x] **$s_j$ are softmax-normalized weights** learned per task; $\gamma$ is a scalar multiplier learned per task.
- [x] **$h_{k,j}^{LM} = [\overrightarrow{h}_{k,j}; \overleftarrow{h}_{k,j}]$** — concatenation of forward and backward states.
- [x] **ELMo is CONTEXTUAL, not static.** Same word → different vectors in different contexts.
- [x] **Lower biLSTM layer → syntactic info; upper layer → semantic info.**
- [x] **CharCNN input handles OOV words.**
- [x] **Training objective:** joint maximization of forward + backward log-likelihoods.
- [x] **ELMo is a feature extractor, NOT a fine-tuned model.** The biLSTM is frozen during downstream training.
- [x] **Polysemy resolution is ELMo's headline advantage** over Word2Vec/GloVe.
- [x] **Released by AI2 (Allen Institute for AI) in 2018** — same year as BERT, but based on biLSTM, not Transformer.
- [x] **Pre-trained on the 1 Billion Word Language Model Benchmark.**
- [x] **For KTU exams, always mention the CharCNN, the two-direction biLSTM, the residual connections, and the linear combination with task-specific weights.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RoBERTa Applications and Challenges - Machine Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** RoBERTa Applications and Challenges - Machine Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is RoBERTa?

**RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach)** is a transformer-based language model introduced by Yinhan Liu et al. (Facebook AI Research, 2019) as a replication study of Google's BERT. It is fundamentally an **encoder-only** transformer architecture that was retrained with substantially more data, larger batch sizes, longer training sequences, and by dynamically masking tokens during pretraining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: RoBERTa is **NOT** a new architecture. It is BERT with **better training recipes** — a key distinction students must remember in ESE answers.

### Key Characteristics of RoBERTa

- **Architecture**: 12-layer (Base) / 24-layer (Large) Transformer Encoder with multi-head self-attention.
- **Parameters**: ~125M (RoBERTa-Base), ~355M (RoBERTa-Large).
- **Pretraining Objective**: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) **without** Next Sentence Prediction (NSP).
- **Training Data**: ~160 GB of uncompressed text (CC-NEWS, OpenWebText, Stories, Books).
- **Dynamic Masking**: Tokens are masked differently in every epoch (unlike BERT's static masking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (RoBERTa)**:
> Imagine BERT as a student who studied the same set of flashcards **once** before an exam. RoBERTa is the same student who studied the **same flashcards, but in different random orders, with more study material, for longer hours, and without a useless "next sentence prediction" pop quiz that distracted them**. Result: better retention and deeper language understanding.

---

## 1.2 What is Machine Translation (MT)?

**Machine Translation (MT)** is the automated process of converting a sequence of text in a **source language** into an equivalent sequence of text in a **target language** while preserving meaning, fluency, and contextual nuance.

### Evolution Timeline of MT Paradigms

| Era | Paradigm | Key Idea | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1950s–1980s** | Rule-Based MT (RBMT) | Linguistic rules + dictionaries | Brittle, expensive |
| **1990s–2000s** | Statistical MT (SMT) | Phrase tables + language models | Data-hungry, limited context |
| **2014–2016** | Neural MT (NMT) | Encoder–Decoder RNNs | Long-range dependency loss |
| **2017–Present** | Transformer-Based MT | Self-attention, parallelization | Compute heavy |
| **2020–Present** | LLM-Based MT (incl. RoBERTa-augmented) | Pretrained + fine-tuned | Hallucination, bias |

---

## 1.3 How Do RoBERTa and Machine Translation Connect?

At first glance, RoBERTa is an **encoder-only** model, while Machine Translation traditionally requires an **encoder–decoder** structure (seq2seq). Their connection lies in:

1. **Encoder Pretraining for MT**: RoBERTa's powerful contextual encoder provides **rich source-language representations** that can be fed into a decoder for translation.
2. **Cross-lingual Transfer Learning**: Multilingual variants like **XLM-RoBERTa** enable zero-shot translation.
3. **Quality Estimation & Re-ranking**: RoBERTa scores translation hypotheses.
4. **Back-translation & Data Augmentation**: RoBERTa generates synthetic parallel data.
5. **Translation Error Correction**: Post-editing translation outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: When asked about RoBERTa in MT, students often confuse it with sequence-to-sequence models. The correct answer is: **RoBERTa serves as an ENCODER FRONT-END or AUXILIARY MODULE**, not as a standalone seq2seq translator (unless adapted via prefix-LM or decoder head fine-tuning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Machine Translation)**:
> Machine Translation is like having two human translators sitting back-to-back. The **encoder** (RoBERTa's role) reads the source sentence and builds a "mental picture" — a dense vector representation capturing the *meaning*. The **decoder** then writes this picture in the target language, word by word, like painting a landscape someone described to them.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024

- Tests understanding of **transformer architecture variants**.
- Links **pretraining** theory with downstream NLP tasks.
- Real-world applications: Google Translate, Microsoft Translator, IndicTrans (Indian languages).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RoBERTa Architecture in Detail

RoBERTa removes BERT's **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** loss and replaces it with **FULL-SENTENCES** (without NSP) input sampling. The MLM objective remains, but the masking pattern is regenerated for each training pass.

### 2.1.1 Masked Language Modeling (MLM) Loss

In MLM, ~15% of the input tokens are selected. Of those selected tokens:
- **80%** are replaced with `[MASK]`
- **10%** are replaced with a random token
- **10%** are kept unchanged

The model predicts the original token using cross-entropy loss only at masked positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 80/10/10 split?** It prevents the model from being certain a token is masked (avoiding `[MASK]` token overfitting during fine-tuning).

### 2.1.2 Self-Attention Mechanism (Core to RoBERTa)

For each token, **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, and **Value (V)** vectors are computed. Attention weights capture how much each token "attends to" every other token.

### 2.1.3 Dynamic vs. Static Masking

| Aspect | BERT | RoBERTa |
|---|---|---|
| Masking | Pre-computed once, fixed | Regenerated every epoch |
| NSP Loss | Yes | **Removed** |
| Batch Size | 128 | up to **8192** |
| Training Data | 16 GB | ~160 GB |

---

## 2.2 Machine Translation: Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

Given a source sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ in language $\mathcal{L}_s$, MT finds the most probable target sentence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m)$ in language $\mathcal{L}_t$:

$$\hat{Y} = \arg\max_{Y} P(Y \mid X)$$

### 2.2.2 Encoder–Decoder Paradigm (The MT Backbone)

- **Encoder**: Reads source $X$ → produces context vectors $C = (c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$.
- **Decoder**: Generates $Y$ token-by-token, conditioned on $C$ and previously generated tokens.

### 2.2.3 Why RoBERTa as the Encoder?

| Requirement | RoBERTa Fits? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Deep contextual representation | ✅ | 12–24 layers of self-attention |
| Bidirectional context | ✅ | Sees left + right context |
| Pretrained on huge data | ✅ | 160 GB training corpus |
| Sequence-to-sequence generation | ❌ | Encoder-only — needs decoder |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Translation Objective** | $\hat{Y} = \arg\max_{Y} P(Y \mid X)$ | Inference |
| 2 | **Bayes Decomposition** | $P(Y \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{m} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$ | Autoregressive generation |
| 3 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{t=1}^{m} \log P(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X)$ | Training MT models |
| 4 | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | RoBERTa attention |
| 5 | **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | RoBERTa attention |
| 6 | **BLEU Score** | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | MT evaluation |
| 7 | **Brevity Penalty** | $BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1-r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$ | BLEU penalty |
| 8 | **Perplexity (PPL)** | $PPL = 2^{H(X)} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(x_i)\right)$ | Language modeling quality |
| 9 | **MLM Loss (RoBERTa)** | $\mathcal{L}_{MLM} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i})$ | RoBERTa pretraining |
| 10 | **ROUGE-L (F1)** | $F_{LCS} = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2)R_{LCS}P_{LCS}}{R_{LCS} + \beta^2 P_{LCS}}$ | Complementary MT metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Units**:
> - BLEU: range $[0, 1]$ (or $[0, 100]$ as percentage). $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = reference length.
> - Perplexity: lower is better; units = bits or nats depending on log base.
> - $d_k$: dimension of key vectors (typically **64**).
> - $w_n$: typically uniform $w_n = 1/N$ with $N = 4$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multilingual Customer Support**: RoBERTa-based encoders power IndicTrans2 (IIT-M) for Indian languages.
- **Localization Industry**: Speeds up translation 10x; human post-editors refine.
- **Legal/Medical Translation**: RoBERTa-Large + domain fine-tuning on corpora.
- **Low-Resource Languages**: Cross-lingual transfer from English-rich RoBERTa pretraining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BLEU Score Step-by-Step

The **Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU)** score is the de facto MT evaluation metric. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Compute Modified n-gram Precision

For n-gram order $n$ (typically $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$):

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \min\!\big(\text{count}_{\text{clip}}(C,\text{ngram}),\,\max_{R \in \text{Refs}}\text{count}(R,\text{ngram})\big)}{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{count}(C,\text{ngram})}$$

The **clipping** ensures no n-gram is credited more times than it appears in any reference.

### Step 2: Combine n-gram Precisions via Geometric Mean

Take the log, multiply by uniform weights $w_n = 1/N$, and exponentiate:

$$\text{BLEU}_{\text{raw}} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

### Step 3: Apply Brevity Penalty

To penalize short (incomplete) translations:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

### Step 4: Final BLEU

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (BLEU Score)

**Reference (R):** `"the cat is on the mat"`
**Candidate (C):** `"the the the cat mat"`

**Step A — Tokenize**: 6-word reference, 5-word candidate.

**Step B — Unigram Precision (n=1)**:

Candidate unigrams: `{the, the, the, cat, mat}`. Counts: `the=3, cat=1, mat=1`.

Reference max counts: `the=2, cat=1, mat=1`.

Clipped counts: `the = min(3, 2) = 2`, `cat = 1`, `mat = 1`.

$$p_1 = \frac{2 + 1 + 1}{3 + 1 + 1} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

**Step C — Bigram Precision (n=2)**:

Candidate bigrams: `(the,the), (the,the), (the,cat), (cat,mat)`.

None of these bigrams appear in the reference (the reference has `(the,cat), (cat,is), (is,on), (on,the), (the,mat)`).

$$p_2 = \frac{0}{4} = 0$$

**Step D — Apply Uniform Weights ($N = 4$, $w_n = 0.25$)**:

$$\sum w_n \log p_n = 0.25(\log 0.8 + \log 0 + \log 0 + \log 0) = -\infty$$

So raw BLEU $\to 0$. With brevity penalty: $c=5$, $r=6$.

$$BP = e^{1 - 6/5} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.819$$

$$\text{BLEU} = 0.819 \times 0 = \mathbf{0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson**: When ANY n-gram precision is 0, BLEU collapses to **0**. This is why **smoothing** is used in practice (e.g., add-1, NIST, Chen-Cherry smoothing).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for MT

During training, an MT model takes source $X$ and reference $Y^* = (y_1^*, y_2^*, \dots, y_m^*)$ and predicts $\hat{P}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$.

The cross-entropy loss (negative log-likelihood) per sentence:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{m} \log \hat{P}(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X)$$

For a batch of size $B$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} = -\dfrac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\sum_{t=1}^{m_b} \log \hat{P}_b(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X_b)$$

Minimizing this $\Leftrightarrow$ Maximizing the log-probability of correct translations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

Given queries $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, keys $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, values $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$:

**Step 1**: Compute raw scores $S = QK^{\top}$.

**Step 2**: Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent softmax saturation (large dot-products $\Rightarrow$ tiny gradients):

$$S' = \dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 3**: Apply row-wise softmax:

$$A = \text{softmax}(S') = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)$$

**Step 4**: Weighted sum with values:

$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A \cdot V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

**Why $\sqrt{d_k}$?** Assuming $q_i, k_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ independent, variance of $q_i \cdot k_j$ is $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes variance to **1**, keeping softmax inputs well-scaled.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: RoBERTa Applications vs. Challenges in MT

| Aspect | Application / Advantage | Associated Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder Pretraining** | Provides deep multilingual embeddings | Pretraining compute = weeks on 1024 GPUs |
| **Cross-lingual Transfer (XLM-R)** | Zero-shot translation across 100 langs | English-bias in tokenization |
| **Quality Estimation** | Scores translations without references | Requires labeled QE data |
| **Back-translation Augmentation** | Generates synthetic parallel corpora | Noisy synthetic data risks error propagation |
| **Domain Adaptation** | Fine-tune on medical/legal MT | Catastrophic forgetting of general MT |
| **Re-ranking N-best Lists** | Improves MT output by 2–4 BLEU points | Slow at inference (multi-pass) |
| **Document-Level MT** | Captures discourse via long context | Limited context window (512 tokens) |
| **Low-Resource MT** | Transfer from high-resource language | Script/grammar mismatch degrades |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RoBERTa Pretraining Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Corpus 160GB] --> B[Byte Pair Encoding Tokenization 50K vocab]
    B --> C[Dynamic Masking 15 percent tokens per epoch]
    C --> D[Transformer Encoder 12 to 24 layers]
    D --> E[Masked Language Modeling Prediction Head]
    E --> F[Cross Entropy Loss]
    F --> G{Converged}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Fine tune on Downstream Tasks]
    H --> I1[Classification]
    H --> I2[NER Tagging]
    H --> I3[QA Systems]
    H --> I4[MT Encoder Front end]
```

## 4.2 Machine Translation with RoBERTa as Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Source Sentence EN] --> TokS[Tokenizer]
    TokS --> EmbS[Source Embeddings]
    EmbS --> RoBERTa[RoBERTa Encoder 12 layers]
    RoBERTa --> Ctx[Context Vectors H1 to Hn]
    Ctx --> Dec[Transformer Decoder 6 layers]
    DecOut[Decoded Tokens] --> Out[TGT Language Output]
    Out[Decoded Tokens] --> OutT[Target Translation FR]
```

## 4.3 Attention Flow in a Translation Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Source Word bonjour] --> Q[Query Q]
    X2[Source Word le] --> K1[Key K1]
    X3[Source Word chat] --> K2[Key K2]
    Q --> Score1[Score QK1]
    Q --> Score2[Score QK2]
    Score1 --> Softmax[Softmax with sqrt dk]
    Score2 --> Softmax
    Softmax --> W1[Weight w1]
    Softmax --> W2[Weight w2]
    X1 --> V1[Value V1]
    X2 --> V2[Value V2]
    X3 --> V3[Value V3]
    W1 --> Comb[Weighted Sum]
    W2 --> Comb
    V1 --> Comb
    V2 --> Comb
    V3 --> Comb
    Comb --> OutAtt[Context Vector for Target Token hello]
```

## 4.4 Full RoBERTa-Augmented MT Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parallel Corpus XY] --> B[Tokenize and Subword]
    B --> C[RoBERTa Encoder Forward Pass]
    C --> D[Decoder Generates Yhat]
    D --> E[Compare with Y reference]
    E --> F[Compute Cross Entropy Loss]
    F --> G[Backprop through Decoder]
    G --> H[Optional Backprop into RoBERTa Layers]
    H --> I{Validation BLEU Improved}
    I -- Yes --> J[Save Checkpoint]
    I -- No --> K[Early Stop or Adjust LR]
    J --> L[Final MT System]
    K --> L
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Why RoBERTa Fits MT Encoder Role

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Source Tokens x1 to xn]
    end
    subgraph TokenEmbed[Token and Position Embeddings]
        E1[Token Embedding E tok]
        E2[Position Embedding E pos]
        I1 --> E1
        I1 --> E2
    end
    subgraph EncoderStack[RoBERTa Encoder Stack L times]
        E1 --> MHA1[Multi Head Self Attention]
```
