```mermaid
        E2 --> MHA1
        MHA1 --> AddNorm1[Add and LayerNorm]
        AddNorm1 --> FFN1[Feed Forward Network]
        FFN1 --> AddNorm2[Add and LayerNorm]
        AddNorm2 --> MHA2[Next Layer]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output to Decoder]
        AddNorm2 --> CtxOut[Contextual Vectors h1 to hn]
        CtxOut --> CrossAtt[Cross Attention in Decoder]
        CrossAtt --> DecOut[Target Translation]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU**: Notice that **RoBERTa contributes only the Encoder Stack** (the gray block). The Decoder is a separate module — usually a 6-layer Transformer decoder with cross-attention over RoBERTa's output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define RoBERTa. How does it differ from BERT? [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]
**Model Answer (8–10 lines)**:

RoBERTa (**Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach**) is a transformer-encoder language model developed by Facebook AI (Liu et al., 2019). It shares BERT's architecture but improves pretraining by:

1. **Removing NSP loss** — RoBERTa uses FULL-SENTENCES sampling without Next Sentence Prediction.
2. **Dynamic masking** — The masked positions are regenerated every epoch, unlike BERT's static masking.
3. **Larger training data** — ~160 GB vs BERT's 16 GB.
4. **Larger batch sizes** — Up to 8192 sequences vs 128 in BERT.
5. **More training steps** — Longer training schedule.

**Key takeaway**: RoBERTa is BERT with **better optimization**, not a new architecture.

---

### Q2. List any three applications of RoBERTa in NLP systems. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Model Answer**:

1. **Encoder in Machine Translation** — Provides rich multilingual contextual embeddings fed to a Transformer decoder.
2. **Text Classification** — Sentiment analysis, spam detection (GLUE/Sentiment benchmarks).
3. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Token classification via the last hidden layer.
4. **Question Answering** — Extractive QA (SQuAD-style).
5. **Cross-lingual Transfer** — XLM-RoBERTa for zero-shot cross-lingual tasks.

*(Write any three with one-line justification.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of RoBERTa in detail. Discuss how Masked Language Modeling (MLM) works with the **80/10/10 strategy**. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Model Solution**:

**Architecture**:

RoBERTa consists of **L stacked Transformer Encoder layers** (L = 12 for Base, 24 for Large). Each layer contains:

- **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer
- **Feed-Forward Network** (FFN) sub-layer
- **Residual connections** and **Layer Normalization** around each sub-layer

The input is formed by summing:
- Token embeddings (vocabulary size **50,265** byte-pair tokens)
- Position embeddings (max sequence length **512**)
- (No segment embeddings, since NSP is removed)

**MLM Working**:

In MLM, ~15% of input tokens are randomly selected. For each selected token $x_i$:

- **80% probability** → replace with `[MASK]` token. This trains the model to predict masked words from bidirectional context.

- **10% probability** → replace with a random token from the vocabulary. This forces the model to be robust and not rely solely on `[MASK]` cues.

- **10% probability** → keep the original token unchanged. This preserves the model's ability to represent the original input distribution.

The model produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each masked position and is trained using **cross-entropy loss**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{MLM} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log \hat{P}(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i})$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of masked positions and $x_{\setminus i}$ denotes all context tokens.

> **Why MLM?** It enables bidirectional pre-training — unlike causal LMs, RoBERTa sees both left and right context simultaneously, producing richer representations for downstream MT encoder use.

---

**(b)** Discuss the role of **RoBERTa in Machine Translation** systems. Mention at least **four applications** and **two challenges**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Role of RoBERTa in MT**:

Traditional SMT/NMT systems relied on shallow or RNN-based encoders. RoBERTa (and XLM-RoBERTa) provide **deep, bidirectional, context-aware multilingual embeddings** that significantly improve translation quality, especially for low-resource languages.

**Four Applications**:

1. **Encoder Front-end for NMT**:
   RoBERTa's pretrained encoder replaces randomly-initialized encoders in seq2seq models. The decoder attends over RoBERTa's output. Empirically improves BLEU by **2–6 points** on WMT tasks.

2. **Cross-lingual Transfer (XLM-RoBERTa)**:
   Pretrained on **100 languages** using CommonCrawl. Enables **zero-shot translation** — train on English–French, test on English–Hindi.

3. **Translation Quality Estimation (QE)**:
   RoBERTa regresses a quality score (0–1) on translation hypotheses without needing reference translations. Used in CAT (Computer-Assisted Translation) tools.

4. **Back-Translation & Data Augmentation**:
   RoBERTa generates monolingual target-language text used to synthesize parallel data, augmenting low-resource corpora.

5. **N-best List Re-ranking**:
   An NMT generates K candidates; RoBERTa scores each, re-ranking for best output.

**Two Challenges**:

1. **Pretraining Cost** — Training RoBERTa-Large requires **1024 V100 GPUs for ~1 day**, with carbon footprint concerns. Reproducing results is impractical for academic institutions.

2. **Encoder-Only Limitation** — RoBERTa cannot natively generate text. For MT it must be paired with a decoder, increasing architecture complexity and inference latency.

3. **English-Centric Bias** — Tokenizer and pretraining data skew toward English, hurting truly low-resource languages (e.g., Manipuri, Bodo).

4. **Context Window Limit** — RoBERTa supports only **512 tokens**, restricting document-level translation.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**(a)** Explain the **encoder–decoder architecture** for Machine Translation. Show how the **cross-entropy loss** is computed during training. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Model Solution**:

**Encoder–Decoder Architecture**:

The encoder–decoder (seq2seq) framework maps a variable-length source sequence $X = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ to a target sequence $Y = (y_1, \dots, y_m)$.

- **Encoder** (e.g., RoBERTa): Reads $X$ token by token, applying self-attention at each layer. Outputs a sequence of hidden vectors $H = (h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$.

- **Decoder** (Transformer decoder with cross-attention): Generates $Y$ one token at a time. At step $t$:

$$\hat{P}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{softmax}(W_o \cdot \text{DecoderState}_t)$$

The decoder state is computed via:
1. **Masked self-attention** over previously generated tokens $y_{<t}$.
2. **Cross-attention** over encoder output $H$ (this is where RoBERTa encoder information enters).
3. **Feed-forward** transformation.

**Cross-Entropy Loss Computation**:

Given a parallel training pair $(X, Y^*)$ where $Y^* = (y_1^*, y_2^*, \dots, y_m^*)$ is the reference translation:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE}(X, Y^*) = -\sum_{t=1}^{m} \log \hat{P}(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X)$$

For a minibatch of $B$ sentence pairs:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} = \dfrac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\mathcal{L}_{CE}(X_b, Y_b^*)$$

Gradients of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}}$ with respect to encoder and decoder parameters are computed via **backpropagation through time (BPTT)**, and parameters are updated using **Adam** or similar optimizers.

**Teacher Forcing**: During training, the decoder uses the *ground-truth* previous tokens $y_{<t}^*$ (not its own predictions) to avoid compounding errors.

---

**(b)** Explain the **BLEU evaluation metric** for Machine Translation. Derive its formula and discuss its **limitations**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Definition**:

BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) measures n-gram overlap between a candidate translation $C$ and one or more reference translations $R$.

**Derivation**:

**Step 1 — Modified n-gram precision** for order $n$:

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \min\!\big(\text{count}_C(\text{ngram}),\;\max_R \text{count}_R(\text{ngram})\big)}{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{count}_C(\text{ngram})}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric mean over multiple n-gram orders** ($N$ typically = 4):

$$\text{BLEU}_{\text{raw}} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

**Step 3 — Brevity Penalty** to discourage overly short translations:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

**Final**:

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)}$$

where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = closest reference length.

**Limitations**:

1. **No semantic understanding** — "good" and "fine" treated as different from "good" and "well".
2. **Zero-score problem** — If any $p_n = 0$, the entire BLEU score becomes 0 (requires smoothing).
3. **Reference dependency** — Score varies dramatically with reference quality and number.
4. **Language bias** — Works poorly for morphologically rich languages (e.g., Malayalam, Tamil) and agglutinative languages.
5. **No synonymy or paraphrase** — "automobile" vs "car" are not credited.
6. **Doesn't measure fluency** — A grammatical but meaningless translation can score high on local n-gram matches.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Confusing RoBERTa with a seq2seq model**: Many students wrongly describe RoBERTa as having a "decoder." RoBERTa is **encoder-only**. Marks lost in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the brevity penalty**: Writing BLEU formula without $BP$ loses 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing BLEU with ROUGE**: BLEU = precision-oriented (MT); ROUGE = recall-oriented (summarization). Don't interchange.
> 4. **Not mentioning NSP removal**: When asked "How is RoBERTa different from BERT?", skipping "removal of NSP loss" loses a guaranteed 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Zero-score in BLEU derivation**: If a student writes the BLEU formula but doesn't explain the **zero-score handling** (smoothing), partial credit is lost.
> 6. **Omitting $d_k$ scaling rationale**: In attention derivation, the $\sqrt{d_k}$ term has a purpose (variance normalization). Examiners love this. Mention it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **RoBERTa = BERT retrained better** — same architecture, better recipe.
- [x] **Encoder-only** transformer; uses **MLM only** (no NSP).
- [x] **Dynamic masking** + **160 GB training data** + **batch size up to 8192**.
- [x] **80/10/10 masking split** for MLM robustness.
- [x] RoBERTa's role in MT is as an **encoder**, not as a standalone translator.
- [x] **XLM-RoBERTa** enables cross-lingual / zero-shot MT across 100 languages.
- [x] Applications in MT: encoder pretraining, QE, back-translation, re-ranking, low-resource transfer.
- [x] Challenges: compute cost, encoder-only limitation, English bias, 512-token context.
- [x] MT objective: $\hat{Y} = \arg\max_Y P(Y \mid X) = \arg\max_Y \prod_t P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$.
- [x] Cross-entropy loss: $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_t \log P(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X)$.
- [x] Attention: $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- [x] **BLEU** = brevity-penalty × geometric mean of clipped n-gram precisions.
- [x] BLEU = 0 if any $p_n = 0$ → use smoothing in practice.
- [x] **Perplexity** = $\exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P(x_i))$ — lower is better.
- [x] **Teacher Forcing** during MT training prevents error compounding.
- [x] For KTU 2024, remember to mention **NEP 2020 outcome-based articulation** when answering: knowledge (CO1), application (CO2), analysis (CO3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Retrieval and Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Retrieval and Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Information Retrieval (IR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Information Retrieval (IR)* is the science and engineering of searching for information in documents, searching for documents themselves, searching for metadata that describes documents, or searching within databases, whether relational databases or hypertext databases such as the World Wide Web. Formally, given a **user query** $q$ and a **document collection** $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$, the goal of IR is to find a ranked subset of documents $R \subseteq D$ that are *relevant* to the information need expressed in $q$.

**Key Distinction from Data Retrieval:**
| Aspect | Data Retrieval (DBMS) | Information Retrieval (IR) |
|---|---|---|
| Match | Exact match | Partial / Best match |
| Query | Well-defined structured | Ambiguous, natural language |
| Result | Deterministic set | Ranked list with relevance scores |
| Data | Structured tables | Unstructured text |
| Example | `SELECT * FROM students` | "What is photosynthesis?" |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Library Librarian:**
> Imagine walking into a massive library with **10 million books** and asking the librarian, *"I need a book about black holes."* The librarian cannot read every book; instead, they consult a **giant index card catalog** (this is the *inverted index*). The cards tell them exactly which shelves contain books matching your topic. They then rank the books — most relevant on top, least relevant below. This entire workflow is **Information Retrieval**: indexing → matching → ranking.

## 1.2 Information Extraction (IE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Information Extraction* is the automatic extraction of structured information from unstructured and/or semi-structured machine-readable documents. Given a document $D$ written in natural language, IE produces **structured records** (entities, relations, events) that can be stored in a database for downstream querying and analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Newsroom Intern:**
> Think of IE as a smart intern who reads a stack of 1,000 newspaper articles and fills out a structured form for each: *Who* is mentioned? *Where* did the event happen? *When*? *What happened*? The intern doesn't summarize — they **extract** specific structured fields. IE is precisely that: transforming unstructured prose into structured tuples such as `<Person, Organization, Role>` or `<Event, Date, Location>`.

## 1.3 Standard IR/IE Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following quantitative metrics are the **de facto standard** for evaluating IR/IE systems and appear consistently in KTU University Examination questions:
>
> - **Precision ($P$)** — fraction of retrieved items that are *relevant*
> - **Recall ($R$)** — fraction of *relevant* items that are retrieved
> - **F-measure ($F_1$)** — harmonic mean of precision and recall
> - **TF-IDF Weight** — term importance score combining local and global statistics
> - **Cosine Similarity** — angular distance between document and query vectors

---

## 1.4 High-Level IR System Architecture

The standard IR pipeline is composed of four major subsystems:

1. **Document Processor** — performs tokenization, stopword removal, and stemming on raw documents.
2. **Indexer** — builds an *inverted index* mapping each term to its posting list (documents containing it).
3. **Query Processor** — transforms the user query into a representation compatible with the index.
4. **Retriever & Ranker** — scores documents against the query and outputs a *ranked list*.

> [!NOTE]
> The dominant paradigm taught in the KTU 2024 scheme is the **Vector Space Model (VSM)** combined with the **TF-IDF weighting scheme** and the **inverted index** data structure.

---

## 1.5 High-Level IE System Architecture

The classical IE pipeline (as in the MUC — Message Understanding Conferences) is composed of four subtasks:

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — identify and classify named entities (PERSON, ORG, LOC, DATE, MONEY…).
2. **Coreference Resolution** — link different mentions of the same entity within a document.
3. **Relation Extraction** — identify semantic relations between entities (e.g., *founded-by*, *located-in*).
4. **Event Extraction / Template Filling** — populate a structured template describing a real-world event.

---

## 1.6 Why These Topics Matter in Modern Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Search engines** (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo) are industrial-scale IR systems.
> - **Voice assistants** (Alexa, Siri) use IR to find documents and IE to extract structured answers.
> - **Healthcare NLP pipelines** use IE to extract drug–disease–symptom triples from clinical notes.
> - **Legal Tech** uses IR to find precedents and IE to extract parties, dates, and rulings from judgments.
> - **Financial Analytics** uses IE to extract merger events, earnings, and executive moves from news.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inverted Index — The Backbone of IR

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> An *inverted index* is a hashmap-like data structure that maps each term $t$ in the vocabulary $V$ to a **posting list** — the sorted list of document IDs in which $t$ occurs, often augmented with term frequency and positional information.

### Logical Structure

$$ \text{Index} = \{ t \mapsto \text{Postings}(t) \mid t \in V \} $$

$$ \text{Postings}(t) = \langle d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{k_t} \rangle $$

where $k_t$ is the number of documents containing term $t$ (i.e., the **document frequency** $\text{df}_t$).

### Components of a Posting

- **Document ID** — unique integer identifier.
- **Term Frequency (TF)** — number of times the term appears in that document.
- **Positional Information** — character/token offsets (used for phrase queries).

### Why It Is "Inverted"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *forward index* maps `document → terms` (like a book's table of contents). An *inverted index* maps `term → documents` (like a book's index page at the back). For query processing, we almost always need the *term → documents* direction, hence the inversion.

---

## 2.2 Boolean Retrieval Model

The Boolean model treats queries as **Boolean expressions** over terms using operators $\land$ (AND), $\lor$ (OR), $\lnot$ (NOT). A document either matches the query fully or not at all — there is **no partial matching, no ranking**.

### Standard Boolean Query Example

$$ q = \text{“deep} \land \text{learning} \land \lnot\text{neural”} $$

This returns documents that contain *both* "deep" and "learning" but **not** the word "neural."

### Algorithmic Intuition

1. Parse the Boolean expression into a **query tree** (leaves = terms, internal nodes = operators).
2. For each term, fetch its posting list from the inverted index.
3. Apply set operations ($\cap$ for AND, $\cup$ for OR, $\setminus$ for NOT) iteratively.
4. Return the resulting document set (unsorted).

### Pros and Cons

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Simple, predictable, fast | No ranking — all results are "equal" |
| Precise control via operators | User must think in Boolean logic |
| Easy to implement with posting lists | Returns too few or too many results |
| Used in legal/medical search | No notion of partial relevance |

---

## 2.3 Vector Space Model (VSM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> The Vector Space Model represents each document $d_j$ and each query $q$ as a vector in a high-dimensional space, where each dimension corresponds to a unique term in the vocabulary. The relevance of $d_j$ to $q$ is then computed as a **similarity function** between the two vectors.

### Representational Form

$$ \vec{d_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \ldots, w_{|V|j}) $$

$$ \vec{q} = (w_{1q}, w_{2q}, \ldots, w_{|V|q}) $$

where $w_{ij}$ is the weight of term $i$ in document $j$, computed using **TF-IDF** (see §2.4).

### Underlying Assumptions

1. **Bag-of-Words (BoW):** term order and syntactic structure are *ignored* — only term *presence* and *frequency* matter.
2. **Term Independence:** terms are assumed to be statistically independent.
3. **Relevance is geometric:** similar documents lie close to one another in the vector space.

---

## 2.4 TF-IDF Weighting Scheme

The single most important formula in classical IR.

### Term Frequency (TF)

The raw count of a term in a document. To dampen the effect of high-frequency terms, the **log-normalized TF** is used:

$$ \text{tf}(t, d) = \begin{cases} 1 + \log_{10} f_{t,d} & \text{if } f_{t,d} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the raw frequency of term $t$ in document $d$.

### Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

A measure of how *rare* (and therefore *informative*) a term is across the entire collection.

$$ \text{idf}(t) = \log_{10} \frac{N}{\text{df}_t} $$

where:
- $N$ = total number of documents in the collection
- $\text{df}_t$ = document frequency of term $t$ (number of documents containing $t$)

### Combined TF-IDF Weight

$$ w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works intuitively:**
> A term that appears *frequently in one document* (high TF) but *rarely in the whole collection* (high IDF) is highly discriminative — it pinpoints that document. Conversely, a term like "the" is frequent everywhere → low IDF → low weight → correctly downweighted.

---

## 2.5 Cosine Similarity — The Ranking Function

The standard similarity measure for VSM. Instead of Euclidean distance, we measure the **angle** between two vectors — this normalizes for document length.

$$ \text{sim}(d_j, q) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{d_j} \cdot \vec{q}}{\Vert \vec{d_j} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \vec{q} \Vert_2} $$

Expanding the dot product explicitly:

$$ \text{sim}(d_j, q) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{|V|} w_{i,j} \cdot w_{i,q}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{|V|} w_{i,j}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{|V|} w_{i,q}^2}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cosine and not dot product?**
> Document 1 may be 10,000 words long; document 2 may be 50 words long, but both discuss the same topic. Pure dot product would unfairly favor the longer document. Cosine similarity is **length-invariant** because the L2 normalization divides by magnitudes.

---

## 2.6 Evaluation Metrics for IR Systems

### The Confusion Matrix (at the document level)

| | Relevant | Not Relevant |
|---|---|---|
| **Retrieved** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Not Retrieved** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

### Precision, Recall, and F-measure

$$ P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \qquad R = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} $$

$$ F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2 + 1) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R} $$

The most common form is the **balanced F1-score** with $\beta = 1$:

$$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — Precision vs Recall trade-off:**
> - $\uparrow$ **Precision** means *"what I returned is correct"* — important in legal/medical search.
> - $\uparrow$ **Recall** means *"I didn't miss anything"* — important in patent/e-discovery search.
> - $F_1$ balances both.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Log-normalized TF | $\text{tf}(t,d) = 1 + \log_{10} f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = raw count | Document indexing |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10} \frac{N}{\text{df}_t}$ | $N$ = corpus size, $\text{df}_t$ = doc freq | Term importance |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \times \text{idf}(t)$ | — | Building document vectors |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(d,q) = \frac{\vec{d}\cdot\vec{q}}{\Vert d\Vert \cdot \Vert q\Vert}$ | Vectors $\vec{d}, \vec{q}$ | Ranking documents |
| Precision | $P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ | TP, FP | IR evaluation |
| Recall | $R = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}$ | TP, FN | IR evaluation |
| F1 Score | $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | P, R | Combined metric |
| Mean Average Precision (MAP) | $\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{|Q|}\sum_{q}\text{AP}(q)$ | Per-query AP | TREC-style evaluation |
| Normalized DCG | $\text{NDCG@k} = \frac{\text{DCG@k}}{\text{IDCG@k}}$ | Discounted cumulative gain | Ranked-list evaluation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pipe-related error:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ in a table — it breaks Markdown. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead.

---

## 2.8 Named Entity Recognition (NER) — Foundations

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> NER is the IE subtask of locating and classifying named entity mentions in unstructured text into predefined categories such as **PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION, DATE, MONEY, PERCENT, TIME**.

### Typical Approaches

1. **Rule-based / Regular Expression** — handcrafted patterns (e.g., Mr./Mrs. → PERSON).
2. **Dictionary-based** — lookup in gazetteers (e.g., GeoNames for locations).
3. **Sequence Labeling (ML)** — Hidden Markov Models (HMM), Conditional Random Fields (CRF).
4. **Neural Sequence Labeling** — BiLSTM-CRF, BERT-based token classification.

### BIO Tagging Scheme

The most common label encoding:

- **B-PER** — Beginning of a PERSON entity
- **I-PER** — Inside (continuation) of a PERSON entity
- **O** — Outside any entity
- **B-LOC**, **I-LOC**, **B-ORG**, **I-ORG**, etc.

Example: "Steve [B-PER] Jobs [I-PER] founded Apple [B-ORG] in Cupertino [B-LOC]."

---

## 2.9 Relation Extraction (RE)

Given a sentence and two entities $e_1, e_2$, classify the relation $r \in R$ (e.g., *born-in*, *employed-by*, *located-in*).

### Standard Pipeline

1. **Entity Pair Selection** — produce candidate $(e_1, e_2)$ pairs.
2. **Sentence Classification** — classify the relation between the pair.
3. **Filtering** — keep high-confidence tuples.

### Feature-based and Neural Approaches

- **Supervised:** SVM, CNN, BiLSTM with attention.
- **Distant Supervision:** automatically label training data by aligning a knowledge base (e.g., Freebase, Wikidata) with text.
- **Open IE:** unsupervised extraction of relation triples from web-scale text.

---

## 2.10 Event Extraction

An *event* is a structured occurrence: *trigger word* (often a verb) + *arguments* (participants).

Standard **ACE (Automatic Content Extraction)** annotation defines:

- **Event Trigger** — the word that most clearly expresses the event occurrence.
- **Event Type** — predefined taxonomy (e.g., *Attack*, *Die*, *Transfer-Money*).
- **Event Arguments** — entities playing roles (e.g., *Attacker*, *Target*, *Instrument*).
- **Event Argument Role** — semantic role of each argument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IE Pipeline Order:** Tokenization → POS Tagging → NER → Coreference → Relation Extraction → Event Extraction. Each step's output feeds the next.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark derivation question**. We work it out exhaustively below.

### Given Data

A toy corpus of $N = 3$ documents:

| Doc | Content |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | "the cat sat on the mat" |
| $d_2$ | "the dog sat on the rug" |
| $d_3$ | "the cat and the dog played" |

**Query:** $q$ = "cat dog"

We are asked to:
1. Build the term-document incidence matrix.
2. Compute TF-IDF weights.
3. Rank documents by cosine similarity to the query.

---

### Step 1 — Vocabulary Construction

After lowercasing and tokenization, the unique terms are:

$$ V = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug, and, played}\} $$

So $|V| = 9$.

---

### Step 2 — Raw Term Frequencies

| Term | $f_{t,d_1}$ | $f_{t,d_2}$ | $f_{t,d_3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| cat | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| sat | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| on | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| rug | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| played | 0 | 0 | 1 |

---

### Step 3 — Document Frequencies ($\text{df}_t$)

Number of documents containing each term:

| Term | $\text{df}_t$ |
|---|---|
| the | 3 |
| cat | 2 |
| sat | 2 |
| on | 2 |
| mat | 1 |
| dog | 2 |
| rug | 1 |
| and | 1 |
| played | 1 |

---

### Step 4 — Inverse Document Frequency

Using the formula $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\frac{N}{\text{df}_t}$ with $N = 3$:

| Term | $\text{idf}(t)$ (decimal) | $\text{idf}(t)$ (4-dp) |
|---|---|---|
| the | $\log_{10}(3/3) = \log_{10}(1)$ | **0.0000** |
| cat | $\log_{10}(3/2)$ | **0.1761** |
| sat | $\log_{10}(3/2)$ | **0.1761** |
| on | $\log_{10}(3/2)$ | **0.1761** |
| mat | $\log_{10}(3/1)$ | **0.4771** |
| dog | $\log_{10}(3/2)$ | **0.1761** |
| rug | $\log_{10}(3/1)$ | **0.4771** |
| and | $\log_{10}(3/1)$ | **0.4771** |
| played | $\log_{10}(3/1)$ | **0.4771** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The term *the* has IDF = 0 because it appears in *all* documents — it carries no discriminative power. This is why stopword removal is implicitly handled by TF-IDF.

---

### Step 5 — Log-Normalized TF for Each Document

$$ \text{tf}(t, d) = 1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d}) \quad \text{for } f_{t,d} > 0 $$

Document $d_1$ (raw → log-normalized):

| Term in $d_1$ | $f$ | $\text{tf}$ |
|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | $1 + \log_{10}(2) = 1.3010$ |
| cat | 1 | $1 + \log_{10}(1) = 1.0000$ |
| sat | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| on | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| mat | 1 | $1.0000$ |

Document $d_2$:

| Term in $d_2$ | $f$ | $\text{tf}$ |
|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | $1.3010$ |
| dog | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| sat | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| on | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| rug | 1 | $1.0000$ |

Document $d_3$:

| Term in $d_3$ | $f$ | $\text{tf}$ |
|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | $1.3010$ |
| cat | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| dog | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| and | 1 | $1.0000$ |
| played | 1 | $1.0000$ |

---

### Step 6 — TF-IDF Vectors

Compute $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \times \text{idf}(t)$:

| Term | $w_{\cdot,d_1}$ | $w_{\cdot,d_2}$ | $w_{\cdot,d_3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | $1.3010 \times 0.0000$ = 0 | $1.3010 \times 0.0000$ = 0 | $1.3010 \times 0.0000$ = 0 |
| cat | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $0$ | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 |
| sat | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $0$ |
| on | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $0$ |
| mat | $1.0000 \times 0.4771$ = 0.4771 | $0$ | $0$ |
| dog | $0$ | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 |
| rug | $0$ | $1.0000 \times 0.4771$ = 0.4771 | $0$ |
| and | $0$ | $0$ | $1.0000 \times 0.4771$ = 0.4771 |
| played | $0$ | $0$ | $1.0000 \times 0.4771$ = 0.4771 |

Query $q$ = "cat dog" — TF = 1 for both terms (treated as a fresh document):

| Term | $w_{\cdot,q}$ |
|---|---|
| cat | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 |
| dog | $1.0000 \times 0.1761$ = 0.1761 |
| (all others) | 0 |

---

### Step 7 — L2 Norms

$$ \Vert \vec{d_j} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_i w_{i,j}^2} $$

For $d_1$:

$$ \Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{0^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.3206} \approx 0.5662 $$

For $d_2$:

$$ \Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{0 + 0 + 0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0 + 0} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.3206} \approx 0.5662 $$

For $d_3$:

$$ \Vert \vec{d_3} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{0 + 0.1761^2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0.1761^2 + 0 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.0310 + 0.2276 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.5172} \approx 0.7192 $$

For the query $q$:

$$ \Vert \vec{q} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.0620} \approx 0.2490 $$

---

### Step 8 — Dot Products and Cosine Similarity

$$ \text{sim}(d_j, q) = \frac{\vec{d_j} \cdot \vec{q}}{\Vert \vec{d_j} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \vec{q} \Vert_2} $$

**For $d_1$:**

$$ \vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{q} = 0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0 \times 0.1761 = 0.0310 $$

$$ \text{sim}(d_1, q) = \frac{0.0310}{0.5662 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0310}{0.1410} \approx 0.2199 $$

**For $d_2$:**

$$ \vec{d_2} \cdot \vec{q} = 0 \times 0.1761 + 0.1761 \times 0.1761 = 0.0310 $$

$$ \text{sim}(d_2, q) = \frac{0.0310}{0.5662 \times 0.2490} \approx 0.2199 $$

**For $d_3$:**

$$ \vec{d_3} \cdot \vec{q} = 0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0.1761 \times 0.1761 = 0.0620 $$

$$ \text{sim}(d_3, q) = \frac{0.0620}{0.7192 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0620}{0.1791} \approx 0.3462 $$

---

### Step 9 — Final Ranking

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_3$ | **0.3462** |
| 2 | $d_1$ | 0.2199 (tied) |
| 2 | $d_2$ | 0.2199 (tied) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Document $d_3$ is ranked highest because it contains **both query terms** ("cat" *and* "dog"). Documents $d_1$ and $d_2$ each contain only one query term, yielding equal (lower) similarity.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Precision, Recall, F1 Computation

Suppose an IR system returns 8 documents for a query. The relevance judgments (gold standard) say there are 5 truly relevant documents in the entire collection.

Among the 8 returned, 4 are relevant and 4 are not.

### Step-by-Step

$$ \text{TP} = 4, \quad \text{FP} = 4, \quad \text{FN} = 5 - 4 = 1 $$

$$ P = \frac{4}{4+4} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5 $$

$$ R = \frac{4}{4+1} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 $$

$$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.8}{0.5 + 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{1.3} \approx 0.615 $$

> [!NOTE]
> High recall (0.8) with moderate precision (0.5) is typical of **recall-oriented** systems such as e-discovery or patent search.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IR Models at a Glance

| Property | Boolean | Vector Space | Probabilistic (BM25) | Language Model (LM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Match Type | Exact | Partial | Partial | Partial |
| Ranking | None | Cosine | BM25 score | KL divergence |
| Term Weighting | Binary | TF-IDF | BM25 TF + IDF | P(term $\mid$ doc) |
| Partial Credit | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| KTU Relevance | High | **Highest** | Medium | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IE Subtasks at a Glance

| Subtask | Input | Output | Classical Method | Modern Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NER | Sentence | Tagged tokens (BIO) | CRF, MaxEnt | BiLSTM-CRF, BERT |
| Coreference | Document | Clusters of mentions | Rule-based, sieves | End-to-end neural |
| Relation Extraction | Sentence + 2 entities | Relation label | SVM, logistic regression | BERT fine-tuning |
| Event Extraction | Sentence | Trigger + Arguments | Pipeline CRF | Joint neural models |
| Template Filling | Document | Structured record | MUC-style rules | Sequence-to-sequence |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classical IR System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Document Collection] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal]
    C --> D[Stemmer / Lemmatizer]
    D --> E[Inverted Index Builder]
    E --> F[(Inverted Index Storage)]

    U[User Query] --> Q1[Query Preprocessor]
    Q1 --> Q2[Query Representation]
    F --> R1[Retrieval Engine]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[TF-IDF / Cosine Scorer]
    R2 --> RK[Ranked Document List]

    RK --> EV[Evaluator]
    EV -->|Precision / Recall / F1| M[Performance Metrics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow has two parallel streams — an *offline indexing* path (A→F) and an *online query* path (U→Q2). Both converge at the **Retrieval Engine**, which produces a ranked list, which is then evaluated against ground truth.

---

## 4.2 Information Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Raw Text Document] --> S2[Sentence Splitter]
    S2 --> S3[Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    S3 --> S4[Named Entity Recognition NER]
    S4 --> S5[Coreference Resolution]
    S5 --> S6[Relation Extraction]
    S6 --> S7[Event Extraction and Template Filling]
    S7 --> S8[(Structured Knowledge Base)]

    S4 -->|PERSON, ORG, LOC, DATE| S5
    S5 -->|Entity Clusters| S6
    S6 -->|Relation Triples| S7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each stage's output **feeds the next**. Errors propagate — a bad NER tag will corrupt coreference, which will corrupt relation extraction. This is called **error propagation** in IE pipelines and is a classic KTU question.

---

## 4.3 Inverted Index Data Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V[Vocabulary]
        T1[term the]
        T2[term cat]
        T3[term dog]
        T4[term sat]
    end
    subgraph P[Posting Lists]
        PL1[d1, d2, d3]
        PL2[d1, d3]
        PL3[d2, d3]
        PL4[d1, d2]
    end
    T1 --> PL1
    T2 --> PL2
    T3 --> PL3
    T4 --> PL4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The *Vocabulary* on the left maps each unique term to a *Posting List* on the right. The posting list contains the document IDs in which that term occurs, often stored sorted for efficient intersection ($\cap$) operations during query processing.

---

## 4.4 Boolean Query Evaluation Tree

For query $q = \text{“deep} \land \text{(learning} \lor \text{NLP)} \land \lnot\text{chatbot”}$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[AND] --> A1[deep]
    ROOT --> A2[OR]
    ROOT --> A3[NOT]
    A2 --> B1[learning]
    A2 --> B2[NLP]
    A3 --> C1[chatbot]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaluation strategy:** The Boolean query is parsed into a tree. The engine processes the leaves (single-term lookups via the inverted index), then applies set operations bottom-up: first OR on {learning, NLP}, then NOT on {chatbot}, then the top-level AND.

---

## 4.5 Cosine Similarity as Geometric Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin 0,0] --> D1[Vector d1]
    O --> D2[Vector d2]
    O --> Q[Vector q]
    D1 -.->|theta 1| Q
    D2 -.->|theta 2| Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Two document vectors $\vec{d_1}$ and $\vec{d_2}$ emanate from the origin. The query vector $\vec{q}$ forms angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ with them. The *smaller* the angle, the *higher* the cosine similarity. The diagram is a 2-D simplification — real VSM uses $|V|$-dimensional space.
