# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Information Retrieval (IR). Differentiate between Data Retrieval and Information Retrieval.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Information Retrieval (IR) is the process, method, and procedure of searching for and retrieving relevant information from a large collection of documents, often unstructured, in response to a user's information need expressed as a query.

**Differentiation (key points expected):**

| Data Retrieval (DBMS) | Information Retrieval |
|---|---|
| Exact match between query and data | Partial / best match |
| Structured, schema-bound data | Unstructured / semi-structured text |
| Deterministic result | Probabilistic, ranked result |
| Query language: SQL | Query language: natural language / keywords |
| Used in banking, inventory | Used in search engines, digital libraries |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation with at least 3 distinct points.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is an inverted index? Why is it called "inverted"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in a vocabulary to the list of documents (and optionally, positions) where it appears. Each entry is called a *posting list*.

> It is called "inverted" because it is the *inverse* of the natural document → terms mapping. A document is naturally a sequence of terms; the inverted index reverses this relationship, producing a term → documents mapping. This inversion is precisely what makes fast query processing possible, because we can directly look up which documents contain a query term.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for posting list structure, 1 mark for explaining the inversion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question from a pair** in each module slot. We provide both options in full.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a classical Information Retrieval system. Describe each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **TF-IDF weight** of every term in the following toy collection and rank the documents against the query $q$ = "ship boat ocean" using **cosine similarity**. **(7 marks)**

| Doc | Content |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | "the ship sailed across the ocean" |
| $d_2$ | "the boat raced in the ocean" |
| $d_3$ | "the ship and the boat met in the ocean" |

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — IR Architecture (7 marks)

The classical IR system has **four major subsystems**:

**1. Document Processor (Indexing Side)**

- **Tokenizer:** splits text into individual terms (words, punctuation, numbers).
- **Stopword Remover:** discards high-frequency function words (the, of, and, a, an).
- **Stemmer / Lemmatizer:** reduces morphological variants to a common root (e.g., *running, runs, ran* → *run*).
- **Output:** a stream of normalized tokens.

**2. Indexer**

- **Inverted Index Builder:** for each term, creates a posting list of documents containing it.
- **Storage:** typically a hashmap / B+ tree on disk for scalability.

**3. Query Processor**

- Applies the *same* tokenization, stopword removal, and stemming pipeline to the user query.
- **Query Representation:** Boolean expression (Boolean model) or weighted vector (VSM).

**4. Retriever & Ranker**

- **Retrieval:** intersects/union posting lists according to query logic.
- **Scoring:** computes TF-IDF + cosine similarity (VSM) or BM25 score.
- **Output:** an ordered list of (document, score) pairs.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for indexing side, 2 marks for query side, 1 mark for ranking/output.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — TF-IDF + Cosine (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Vocabulary**

After preprocessing:
$$ V = \{\text{the, ship, sailed, across, ocean, boat, raced, in, and, met}\} $$
Thus $|V| = 10$, $N = 3$.

**Step 2 — Raw TF Matrix**

| Term | $f_{d_1}$ | $f_{d_2}$ | $f_{d_3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| ship | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| sailed | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| across | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| ocean | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| boat | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| raced | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| in | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| met | 0 | 0 | 1 |

**Step 3 — IDF Calculation**

| Term | $\text{df}_t$ | $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}(3/\text{df}_t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| the | 3 | 0.0000 |
| ship | 2 | 0.1761 |
| sailed | 1 | 0.4771 |
| across | 1 | 0.4771 |
| ocean | 3 | 0.0000 |
| boat | 2 | 0.1761 |
| raced | 1 | 0.4771 |
| in | 1 | 0.4771 |
| and | 1 | 0.4771 |
| met | 1 | 0.4771 |

**Step 4 — Log-Normalized TF**

For terms with $f = 1$: $\text{tf} = 1 + \log_{10}(1) = 1.0000$. For $f = 2$: $\text{tf} = 1 + \log_{10}(2) = 1.3010$.

**Step 5 — TF-IDF Vectors**

| Term | $w_{d_1}$ | $w_{d_2}$ | $w_{d_3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| ship | 0.1761 | 0 | 0.1761 |
| sailed | 0.4771 | 0 | 0 |
| across | 0.4771 | 0 | 0 |
| ocean | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| boat | 0 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |
| raced | 0 | 0.4771 | 0 |
| in | 0 | 0.4771 | 0 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 0.4771 |
| met | 0 | 0 | 0.4771 |

**Query** $q$ = "ship boat ocean" — TF = 1 for each:

| Term | $w_q$ |
|---|---|
| ship | 0.1761 |
| boat | 0.1761 |
| ocean | 0 |

**Step 6 — L2 Norms**

$$ \Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.2276 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.4862} \approx 0.6973 $$

$$ \Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.4862} \approx 0.6973 $$

$$ \Vert \vec{d_3} \Vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.5172} \approx 0.7192 $$

$$ \Vert \vec{q} \Vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0} = \sqrt{0.0620} \approx 0.2490 $$

**Step 7 — Cosine Similarities**

$$ \text{sim}(d_1, q) = \frac{0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0 \times 0.1761}{0.6973 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0310}{0.1736} \approx 0.1786 $$

$$ \text{sim}(d_2, q) = \frac{0 + 0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0}{0.6973 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0310}{0.1736} \approx 0.1786 $$

$$ \text{sim}(d_3, q) = \frac{0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0.1761 \times 0.1761 + 0}{0.7192 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0620}{0.1791} \approx 0.3462 $$

**Step 8 — Final Ranking**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_3$ | **0.3462** |
| 2 (tie) | $d_1$ | 0.1786 |
| 2 (tie) | $d_2$ | 0.1786 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $d_3$ wins:** It is the *only* document containing **both** query terms "ship" and "boat." The cosine metric is rewarded for multi-term overlap.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Boolean Retrieval Model** and the **Vector Space Model** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** with the **BIO tagging scheme**. Given the sentence *"Apple Inc. was founded by Steve Jobs in Cupertino in 1976."*, perform NER tagging and identify all named entities with their categories. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Boolean vs VSM (7 marks)

**Boolean Retrieval Model**

- **Mechanism:** Documents are treated as sets of terms. Queries are Boolean expressions of the form $\text{term}_1 \land \text{term}_2 \lor \lnot\text{term}_3$.
- **Execution:** The query is parsed into a tree; posting lists are retrieved and combined using set operations ($\cap, \cup, \setminus$).
- **Output:** An *unordered* set of matching documents.

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| Predictable, easy to implement | No partial matching / no ranking |
| User has precise control | Hard for non-expert users |
| Efficient for exact-match queries | Recall or precision may be poor |
| Used in legal & medical search | Cannot express "more relevant" intent |

**Vector Space Model (VSM)**

- **Mechanism:** Each document and query is a vector in $|V|$-dimensional space, with TF-IDF weights. Similarity is the cosine of the angle between vectors.
- **Execution:** Compute all document–query cosines; rank descending.
- **Output:** A *ranked* list of documents.

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| Supports partial matching | Assumes term independence (unrealistic) |
| Produces a meaningful ranking | Ignores word order and syntax |
| Length-normalized via cosine | High storage cost for sparse vectors |
| Foundation for modern IR | Poor on synonymy and polysemy |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark Boolean mechanism, 1 mark VSM mechanism, 1 mark merits of each, 1 mark demerits of each, 1 mark execution, 1 mark comparison table, 1 mark real-world context.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — NER with BIO (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
> Named Entity Recognition (NER) is the IE subtask of identifying and classifying named entities in text into predefined categories (e.g., PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION, DATE, MONEY).

**BIO Tagging Scheme (2 marks):**
- **B-X** = Beginning of entity of type X
- **I-X** = Inside (continuation) of entity of type X
- **O** = Outside any entity

**Tagged Sentence (3 marks):**

| Token | BIO Tag | Entity Type |
|---|---|---|
| Apple | **B-ORG** | Organization |
| Inc. | **I-ORG** | Organization |
| was | O | — |
| founded | O | — |
| by | O | — |
| Steve | **B-PER** | Person |
| Jobs | **I-PER** | Person |
| in | O | — |
| Cupertino | **B-LOC** | Location |
| in | O | — |
| 1976 | **B-DATE** | Date |

**Extracted Entities (1 mark):**

- **ORGANIZATION:** Apple Inc.
- **PERSON:** Steve Jobs
- **LOCATION:** Cupertino
- **DATE:** 1976

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 in log-TF:** Many students write $\text{tf}(t,d) = \log_{10}(f_{t,d})$ — this is *wrong*. The standard is $1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d})$ to ensure positive weights.
> 2. **Using raw TF instead of TF-IDF for cosine:** The cosine ranking is valid *only* on weighted vectors. Using raw counts leads to incorrect rankings and loss of 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the L2 normalization in cosine:** A common slip is to use the dot product $\vec{d}\cdot\vec{q}$ instead of cosine. Examiners explicitly check for the $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ terms.
> 4. **Forgetting to handle the case $f_{t,d} = 0$:** $\log(0)$ is undefined. Always state $w = 0$ when $f = 0$.
> 5. **Mixing up Precision and Recall formulas:** $P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ (not denominator = TP+FN). Write both formulas clearly.
> 6. **Forgetting to show $\log_{10}$ base explicitly:** Always specify the logarithm base in TF-IDF.
> 7. **In NER tagging, using `O` for an inside-token:** A multi-token entity like "Steve Jobs" must be `B-PER I-PER`, not `B-PER O`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

### A. Information Retrieval (IR)

- [ ] **IR vs DBMS:** IR = partial match + ranking; DBMS = exact match + structured.
- [ ] **Inverted Index:** `term → posting list of doc IDs`; built offline.
- [ ] **Boolean Model:** set operations on posting lists; no ranking.
- [ ] **VSM:** documents & queries as TF-IDF vectors; ranked by cosine similarity.
- [ ] **TF formula:** $1 + \log_{10} f_{t,d}$ (zero if $f = 0$).
- [ ] **IDF formula:** $\log_{10}(N / \text{df}_t)$.
- [ ] **TF-IDF = TF × IDF** — most important single formula.
- [ ] **Cosine Similarity:** $\frac{\vec{d}\cdot\vec{q}}{\Vert d\Vert \cdot \Vert q\Vert}$ — always include both norms.
- [ ] **Precision = TP / (TP+FP);** **Recall = TP / (TP+FN);** **F1 = 2PR / (P+R).**
- [ ] **Stopwords** get IDF ≈ 0; **rare terms** get high IDF.

### B. Information Extraction (IE)

- [ ] **IE ≠ Summarization** — IE produces *structured* records, not summaries.
- [ ] **IE Pipeline:** Sentence split → Tokenize/POS → NER → Coreference → RE → Event Extraction.
- [ ] **NER Categories:** PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT.
- [ ] **BIO Scheme:** B-X = Begin, I-X = Inside, O = Outside.
- [ ] **NER Methods:** Rule-based, CRF, BiLSTM-CRF, BERT token classification.
- [ ] **Relation Extraction:** classify relation $r \in R$ between entity pair $(e_1, e_2)$.
- [ ] **Distant Supervision:** align KB to text to auto-label training data.
- [ ] **Event Extraction:** trigger word + typed arguments (per ACE annotation guidelines).

### C. Engineering & Exam Tips

- [ ] Always show **all intermediate steps** in cosine / TF-IDF derivations — examiners reward working.
- [ ] Use **markdown tables** for confusion matrices and IR model comparisons.
- [ ] Use **mermaid / block diagrams** wherever the question asks for "with a neat diagram."
- [ ] State the **units / ranges** of metrics: Precision, Recall, F1 ∈ $[0, 1]$; Cosine ∈ $[-1, 1]$ (typically $[0, 1]$ for TF-IDF).
- [ ] Memorize the **3-4 most-cited equations** (TF-IDF, Cosine, Precision, Recall, F1) and their boundary conditions.
- [ ] In NER/BIO questions, *tag every token* — partial tagging is a mark-loser.
- [ ] Always include the **log base 10** explicitly in TF-IDF.

### D. Common Question Patterns in KTU 2024 ESE

| Pattern | Marks | What to write |
|---|---|---|
| "Explain IR system architecture with diagram" | 7 | 4-stage pipeline + diagram |
| "Compute TF-IDF + cosine for a toy corpus" | 7 | Full derivation, all steps shown |
| "Differentiate Boolean vs VSM" | 7 | Side-by-side comparison table |
| "Define NER, BIO tag a sentence" | 3–7 | Sentence tokenization + tag table |
| "What is precision / recall / F1? Compute for …" | 3–7 | Confusion matrix + formulas |
| "Explain IE pipeline" | 7 | 4-stage pipeline + diagram + error propagation |
| "What is inverted index?" | 3 | Definition + posting list + inversion rationale |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sentiment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sentiment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sentiment Analysis (SA)**, also called **Opinion Mining**, is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, appraisals, attitudes, and emotions expressed in natural language text. It is a sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Computational Linguistics** that aims to systematically identify, extract, quantify, and study affective states and subjective information.

Formally, given a text $T$ composed of tokens $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$, Sentiment Analysis produces a polarity label or a continuous score:

$$f: T \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ may be:
- A **discrete label set** $\mathcal{S} = \{\text{Positive}, \text{Neutral}, \text{Negative}\}$, or
- A **continuous value** $s \in [-1, +1]$, or
- A **multi-dimensional emotion vector** $\vec{e} = (e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k)$ across emotion categories (joy, anger, fear, sadness, surprise, disgust).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *sentiment* is a tuple $(\text{entity}, \text{aspect}, \text{orientation}, \text{holder}, \text{time})$ — this 5-tuple model by Liu (2012) is the gold-standard formal definition used in board examinations.

## 1.2 Levels of Granularity (Hierarchical Classification)

| Level | Scope | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Document Level** | Entire document / review | Classify a whole product review as +ve or −ve |
| **Sentence Level** | Single sentence | "The battery is awful." → Negative |
| **Aspect Level (ABSA)** | Specific feature of entity | (Phone, Battery, Negative) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Critic:** Imagine you hired a robot food critic. Instead of reading thousands of reviews, it scans each review and instantly tells you: *"This is positive,"* *"This customer hated the parking,"* or *"This review is neutral."* Sentiment Analysis is that robot — it transforms messy, unstructured human emotion in text into clean, structured labels a business or system can act upon. Just as a human critic breaks a review into *food*, *service*, and *ambience*, modern SA does the same via **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA)**.

## 1.4 Subjectivity vs Sentiment — A Critical Distinction

- **Subjective sentence:** Contains personal opinion, belief, or feeling.  
  *Example:* "I think this phone is great."
- **Objective sentence:** States a fact without emotion.  
  *Example:* "The phone has 8 GB RAM."
- **Sentiment-bearing sentence:** A subset of subjective sentences that carry evaluative polarity (+/−).

> [!TIP]
> Subjectivity classification is a **necessary preprocessing step** before sentiment polarity classification in KTU-expected answers. Always mention this pipeline in theory questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Approaches to Sentiment Analysis (Tripartite Classification)

### **A. Lexicon-Based (Knowledge-Based / Rule-Based) Approach**
Uses a precompiled dictionary (lexicon) of words with pre-assigned sentiment scores. Aggregation rule produces final score.

**Core Logic Steps:**
1. Tokenize the input text.
2. Look up each token in a sentiment lexicon (e.g., **SentiWordNet**, **AFINN**, **VADER**, **Bing Liu Lexicon**).
3. Apply **negation handling** rules (e.g., "not good" → flips polarity).
4. Aggregate scores using sum or average.
5. Threshold → discrete polarity.

### **B. Machine Learning (Supervised) Approach**
Trains a classifier on a labeled corpus of texts.

**Core Logic Steps:**
1. Preprocess text (tokenize, normalize, remove stop words).
2. Extract features (BoW, TF-IDF, n-grams, embeddings).
3. Train a classifier (Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, MaxEnt).
4. Predict polarity on unseen test data.

### **C. Deep Learning / Neural Approach**
Uses neural architectures to learn distributed representations end-to-end.

**Architectures commonly tested in KTU:**
- **CNN** — captures local n-gram patterns.
- **LSTM / BiLSTM** — captures long-range sequential dependencies.
- **BERT / Transformer encoders** — contextual embeddings, SOTA.
- **Hierarchical Attention Networks (HAN)** — document-level attention.

## 2.2 Sentiment Lexicons (Board-Exam Favorites)

| Lexicon | Score Range | Polarity Levels | Special Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bing Liu (MPQA)** | Binary $\{+1, -1\}$ | 2 | Curated for product reviews |
| **AFINN-111** | $[-5, +5]$ integers | 2 | Suitable for micro-blogs (Twitter) |
| **SentiWordNet 3.0** | $[0, 1]$ per class | 3 (+ve, −ve, obj) | Built over WordNet synsets |
| **VADER** | $[-4, +4]$ compound | 3 | Rule-based, tuned for social media |

## 2.3 SentiWordNet Scoring Method (Key KTU Formula)

For a synset $s$ with **three scores** $\text{Pos}(s), \text{Neg}(s), \text{Obj}(s)$ such that:

$$\text{Pos}(s) + \text{Neg}(s) + \text{Obj}(s) = 1$$

**For a text $T$ consisting of words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$**, the aggregated score is:

$$S(T) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \text{Pos}(s_i) - \text{Neg}(s_i) \right]$$

**Polarity Decision Rule:**

$$\text{Polarity}(T) = \begin{cases} \text{Positive} & \text{if } S(T) > 0 \\ \text{Neutral} & \text{if } S(T) = 0 \\ \text{Negative} & \text{if } S(T) < 0 \end{cases}$$

## 2.4 Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) for Sentiment Orientation (Turney, 2002)

Used when no lexicon exists. For a phrase $p$ and a seed word $w$:

$$\text{PMI}(p, w) = \log_2 \left( \frac{P(p \land w)}{P(p) \cdot P(w)} \right)$$

**Semantic Orientation (SO) of phrase $p$** using positive seed $w^+$ ("excellent") and negative seed $w^-$ ("poor"):

$$\text{SO}(p) = \text{PMI}(p, w^+) - \text{PMI}(p, w^-)$$

## 2.5 Naive Bayes Sentiment Classifier (Most Tested in KTU)

By Bayes' Theorem, the probability of class $C$ given text features $\mathbf{x}$:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Decision rule (MAP estimate):**

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k \in \{+,-,0\}} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Laplace smoothing (handles zero-frequency):**

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + 1}{\text{count}(C_k) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is vocabulary size.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | SentiWordNet Aggregate | $S(T) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} [\text{Pos}(s_i) - \text{Neg}(s_i)]$ | Lexicon scoring |
| 2 | Synset Constraint | $\text{Pos} + \text{Neg} + \text{Obj} = 1$ | Validity check |
| 3 | Pointwise Mutual Info | $\text{PMI}(p,w) = \log_2 \frac{P(p \land w)}{P(p) P(w)}$ | SO computation |
| 4 | Semantic Orientation | $\text{SO}(p) = \text{PMI}(p,w^+) - \text{PMI}(p,w^-)$ | Phrase polarity |
| 5 | Naive Bayes (MAP) | $\hat{C} = \arg\max P(C_k) \prod P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | ML classifier |
| 6 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i,C_k}+1}{N_{C_k} + \vert V \vert}$ | Zero-frequency fix |
| 7 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | System evaluation |
| 8 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 9 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Sensitivity / TPR |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 11 | Macro F1 | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} F_1^{(k)}$ | Multi-class average |
| 12 | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}_{t,d} \cdot \log \frac{N}{df_t + 1}$ | Feature extraction |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| E-Commerce | Product review summarization (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| Finance | Market sentiment indices for stock prediction |
| Politics | Election opinion polling from Twitter/X |
| Healthcare | Patient feedback mining |
| Customer Service | Real-time chatbot escalation triggers |
| Brand Management | Reputation monitoring (Brandwatch, Sprinklr) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem #1: SentiWordNet Scoring (Board Style)

> **Problem (14-Mark Style):** A short text $T$ = *"The movie was good but the ending was terrible."* is processed using SentiWordNet. The synset scores retrieved are:
>
> | Word | Synset | Pos | Neg | Obj |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | good | s₁ | 0.75 | 0.00 | 0.25 |
> | ending | s₂ | 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 |
> | terrible | s₃ | 0.00 | 0.85 | 0.15 |
>
> Compute the overall sentiment score using the **average aggregation method** and determine the polarity class. *(Assume 'movie', 'was', 'but', 'the' are stop words and excluded.)*

### **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Identify the candidate words and their synset scores.**

From the sentence, after stop-word removal, the sentiment-bearing tokens are:
- $w_1 = \text{good} \rightarrow (P_1, N_1, O_1) = (0.75, 0.00, 0.25)$
- $w_2 = \text{ending} \rightarrow (P_2, N_2, O_2) = (0.00, 0.00, 1.00)$
- $w_3 = \text{terrible} \rightarrow (P_3, N_3, O_3) = (0.00, 0.85, 0.15)$

Thus, the number of words $n = 3$.

**Step 2: Verify the SentiWordNet constraint for each synset.**

$$\text{For } s_1: \quad 0.75 + 0.00 + 0.25 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{For } s_2: \quad 0.00 + 0.00 + 1.00 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\text{For } s_3: \quad 0.00 + 0.85 + 0.15 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$$

All synsets satisfy the $\text{Pos}+\text{Neg}+\text{Obj}=1$ axiom.

**Step 3: Apply the SentiWordNet aggregate scoring formula.**

$$S(T) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \text{Pos}(s_i) - \text{Neg}(s_i) \right]$$

**Step 4: Compute the per-word contribution.**

$$\text{Contribution of } w_1 = P_1 - N_1 = 0.75 - 0.00 = +0.75$$

$$\text{Contribution of } w_2 = P_2 - N_2 = 0.00 - 0.00 = \;0.00$$

$$\text{Contribution of } w_3 = P_3 - N_3 = 0.00 - 0.85 = -0.85$$

**Step 5: Sum the contributions and divide by $n$.**

$$S(T) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (+0.75) + (0.00) + (-0.85) \right]$$

$$S(T) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.10 \right]$$

$$S(T) = -0.0333\ldots$$

**Step 6: Apply the polarity decision rule.**

$$\text{Since } S(T) = -0.0333 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Polarity}(T) = \text{Negative}$$

**Step 7: Interpretation.**

The text is classified as **Negative** with a very weak magnitude. The strong positive score of "good" (+0.75) is almost cancelled by the strong negative score of "terrible" (−0.85), and "ending" is purely objective, so the overall polarity is mildly negative. This illustrates the importance of **negation handling and contextual modifiers** in lexicon-based SA — "but" here acts as a polarity shifter that the basic SentiWordNet average does not explicitly model.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #2: Naive Bayes Sentiment Classifier with Laplace Smoothing

> **Problem:** A tiny training corpus contains 4 documents with binary sentiment labels.
>
> | Doc | Tokens | Class |
> | :---: | :--- | :---: |
> | d₁ | good, plot, good | + |
> | d₂ | good, acting | + |
> | d₃ | bad, plot, slow | − |
> | d₄ | bad, slow, slow | − |
>
> Test document $d_{\text{test}}$: *"good, slow"*
> Compute $P(+ \mid d_{\text{test}})$ and $P(- \mid d_{\text{test}})$ using **Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)**. Predict the class.

### **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Build the prior probabilities $P(C_k)$.**

Total training documents $= 4$. Number of positive $= 2$, negative $= 2$.

$$P(+) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$P(-) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Build the vocabulary $V$.**

$$V = \{\text{good}, \text{plot}, \text{acting}, \text{bad}, \text{slow}\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert V \vert = 5$$

**Step 3: Count word occurrences per class.**

| Word | Count in + | Count in − |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| good | 3 | 0 |
| plot | 1 | 1 |
| acting | 1 | 0 |
| bad | 0 | 2 |
| slow | 0 | 3 |
| **Total tokens** | **5** | **6** |

Total tokens in positive class $N_+ = 5$.
Total tokens in negative class $N_- = 6$.

**Step 4: Compute smoothed likelihoods $P(w \mid C_k)$ for each word in $V$.**

General formula:

$$P(w \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

With $\alpha = 1$ and $\vert V \vert = 5$:

**For positive class (denominator $= 5 + 1 \cdot 5 = 10$):**

$$P(\text{good} \mid +) = \frac{3 + 1}{10} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

$$P(\text{slow} \mid +) = \frac{0 + 1}{10} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

**For negative class (denominator $= 6 + 1 \cdot 5 = 11$):**

$$P(\text{good} \mid -) = \frac{0 + 1}{11} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

$$P(\text{slow} \mid -) = \frac{3 + 1}{11} = \frac{4}{11} \approx 0.3636$$

**Step 5: Compute unnormalized posteriors using the MAP rule.**

$$\hat{P}(+ \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto P(+) \cdot P(\text{good} \mid +) \cdot P(\text{slow} \mid +)$$

$$\hat{P}(+ \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.020$$

$$\hat{P}(- \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto P(-) \cdot P(\text{good} \mid -) \cdot P(\text{slow} \mid -)$$

$$\hat{P}(- \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto 0.5 \times 0.0909 \times 0.3636 \approx 0.0165$$

**Step 6: Normalize to obtain true probabilities.**

Total $= 0.020 + 0.0165 = 0.0365$.

$$P(+ \mid d_{\text{test}}) = \frac{0.020}{0.0365} \approx 0.548$$

$$P(- \mid d_{\text{test}}) = \frac{0.0165}{0.0365} \approx 0.452$$

**Step 7: Apply MAP decision rule.**

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max \{0.548, \; 0.452\} = \text{Positive}$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

The test document *"good, slow"* is classified as **Positive** with probability $\approx 54.8\%$. The presence of the strongly positive word "good" dominates the mildly negative word "slow" in this classifier.

---

## 3.3 Confusion Matrix → Performance Metrics (Worked Mini-Example)

> **Problem:** A binary sentiment classifier on a test set of 100 reviews produces the following counts:
> - True Positives (TP) $= 40$
> - False Positives (FP) $= 10$
> - False Negatives (FN) $= 15$
> - True Negatives (TN) $= 35$

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1: Accuracy.**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{40 + 35}{100} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75$$

**Step 2: Precision.**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40+10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Recall.**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40+15} = \frac{40}{55} \approx 0.7273$$

**Step 4: F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.7273}{0.80 + 0.7273}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{1.1636}{1.5273} \approx 0.7619$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** The classifier has **75% accuracy** with **balanced precision/recall** (F1 = 0.762), which is reasonable for an imbalanced sentiment task (50 positive, 50 negative).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Text Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stop-word Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Lowercasing and Stemming]
    B3 --> C{Choose Approach}
    C --> D1[Lexicon Based Method]
    C --> D2[Machine Learning Method]
    C --> D3[Deep Learning Method]
    D1 --> E1[Lookup SentiWordNet or VADER]
    E1 --> F1[Aggregate Scores]
    F1 --> G1[Threshold Decision]
    D2 --> E2[Feature Extraction TF IDF]
    E2 --> F2[Train Classifier NB or SVM]
    F2 --> G2[Predict Polarity]
    D3 --> E3[Embeddings Word2Vec or BERT]
    E3 --> F3[LSTM or Transformer Encoder]
    F3 --> G3[Softmax Output Layer]
    G1 --> H[Final Sentiment Label]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Output Positive Neutral Negative]
```

## 4.2 Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Customer Review] --> T1[Aspect Term Extraction]
    R --> O1[Opinion Word Extraction]
    T1 --> P[Pair Aspect Opinion]
    O1 --> P
    P --> C1[Aspect 1 Battery]
    P --> C2[Aspect 2 Screen]
    P --> C3[Aspect 3 Delivery]
    C1 --> S1[Sentiment Terrible Negative]
    C2 --> S2[Sentiment Amazing Positive]
    C3 --> S3[Sentiment Slow Negative]
    S1 --> SUM[Structured Summary]
    S2 --> SUM
    S3 --> SUM
```

## 4.3 Comparative Approach Map (Lexicon vs ML vs DL)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Sentiment Analysis Approaches] --> LX[Lexicon Based]
    ROOT --> ML[Machine Learning Based]
    ROOT --> DL[Deep Learning Based]
    LX --> LX1[Manual Lexicon MPQA]
    LX --> LX2[Auto Lexicon PMI Turney]
    LX --> LX3[Hybrid VADER]
    ML --> ML1[Naive Bayes]
    ML --> ML2[Support Vector Machine]
    ML --> ML3[Logistic Regression]
    DL --> DL1[CNN for Text]
    DL --> DL2[BiLSTM with Attention]
    DL --> DL3[BERT Fine Tuning]
    LX3 --> PRO[Pros Interpretable]
    LX3 --> CON[Cons Domain Dependent]
    ML2 --> PRO
    ML2 --> CON2[Cons Needs Labeled Data]
    DL3 --> PRO2[Pros SOTA Accuracy]
    DL3 --> CON3[Cons Computationally Expensive]
```

## 4.4 Liu's 5-Tuple Sentiment Model (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Entity e.g. iPhone 15] --> OUT[Tuple E A O H T]
    A[Aspect e.g. battery] --> OUT
    O[Orientation e.g. negative] --> OUT
    H[Holder e.g. Rahul] --> OUT
    T[Time e.g. Dec 2024] --> OUT
    OUT --> REP[Structured Sentiment Representation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Sentiment Analysis. List the three levels of granularity in which it is performed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1+2 split):**

Sentiment Analysis is the computational process of identifying, extracting, and classifying opinions, emotions, and attitudes expressed in text into categories such as positive, negative, or neutral.

The three levels of granularity are:
1. **Document Level** — Classifies the sentiment of an entire document as a whole.
2. **Sentence Level** — Classifies the sentiment of individual sentences.
3. **Aspect Level (ABSA)** — Identifies sentiment tied to specific aspects or features of an entity (e.g., battery, screen, delivery).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining all three levels.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between Subjectivity and Sentiment in NLP. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Subjectivity | Sentiment |
| :--- | :--- |
| Identifies whether a sentence expresses a *personal opinion, belief or judgment* (subjective) or a *factual statement* (objective). | Identifies the *polarity* (positive, negative, neutral) of a subjective opinion. |
| Binary classification: Subjective / Objective. | Typically 3-class (or fine-grained) classification. |
| *Example:* "I think this laptop is fast." → Subjective. | *Example:* "I think this laptop is fast." → Sentiment = Positive. |

*Note: A sentence can be subjective but neutral in sentiment (e.g., "I think it is okay.").*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **one** of the following two questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lexicon-Based Approach Focus**

**(a) Explain the SentiWordNet-based approach to sentiment analysis. [7 Marks]**
**(b) Given the following synset scores, compute the overall sentiment of the text "Camera was excellent but price was high" and identify the polarity. Negate "high" using a simple negation rule. [7 Marks]**

Synset scores (assume scores for "excellent" and "price" and "high" after stop-word removal):

| Word | Pos | Neg | Obj |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| excellent | 0.85 | 0.00 | 0.15 |
| price | 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 |
| high | 0.10 | 0.30 | 0.60 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**SentiWordNet Approach (7 Marks Detailed):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Introduction.** SentiWordNet is a lexical resource built over **WordNet synsets**. Each synset $s$ is assigned three scores: $\text{Pos}(s)$, $\text{Neg}(s)$, $\text{Obj}(s)$ summing to 1.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Algorithm.**

1. Tokenize input text $T$.
2. For each token $w_i$, retrieve all WordNet synsets and their $(\text{Pos}, \text{Neg}, \text{Obj})$ triples.
3. Disambiguate (often by selecting the first / most frequent synset).
4. Compute the per-word sentiment contribution: $C_i = \text{Pos}(s_i) - \text{Neg}(s_i)$.
5. Aggregate using mean, sum, or weighted mean: $S(T) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i C_i$.
6. Apply threshold for polarity classification.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Advantages & Limitations.**

| Advantages | Limitations |
| :--- | :--- |
| No training data needed | Does not handle context / word sense perfectly |
| Transparent & interpretable | Misses sarcasm, irony, domain terms |
| Easy to extend | Average may dilute strong cues |

**Step 4 (1 mark): Use cases.** SentiWordNet is widely used in product review mining, social-media monitoring, and as a feature in hybrid ML pipelines.

**Step 5 (1 mark): Diagram or example walk-through.** *(Include a small example for clarity.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**Given:** Text = *"Camera was excellent but price was high"*. After removing stop-words (*camera, was, but, price, was* — note "price" retained as a noun, not a sentiment word but per question we use the given scores):

Effective sentiment tokens and their scores:

- $w_1 = \text{excellent} \rightarrow (P, N, O) = (0.85, 0.00, 0.15)$
- $w_2 = \text{price} \rightarrow (P, N, O) = (0.00, 0.00, 1.00)$
- $w_3 = \text{high} \rightarrow (P, N, O) = (0.10, 0.30, 0.60)$

**Step 1 (1 mark): Negation handling.** Apply simple negation rule: a negation word within a window of 2-3 tokens preceding a sentiment word flips its polarity. Here, the structure is "price was high" — in a typical sentiment task, "high" when describing *price* is often considered negative. Since the question states to **negate "high"**, we flip its Pos/Neg: $\text{new}(P,N,O) = (0.30, 0.10, 0.60)$ — i.e., Pos and Neg are swapped.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Recomputed effective synset for "high" after negation:**

$$w_3^{\text{negated}}: (P', N', O') = (0.30, 0.10, 0.60)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Compute per-word contribution $C_i = P_i - N_i$.**

$$C_1 = 0.85 - 0.00 = +0.85$$

$$C_2 = 0.00 - 0.00 = \;0.00$$

$$C_3' = 0.30 - 0.10 = +0.20$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Aggregate using mean.**

$$S(T) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ 0.85 + 0.00 + 0.20 \right] = \frac{1.05}{3} = 0.35$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Polarity decision.**

$$S(T) = +0.35 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Polarity} = \textbf{Positive}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Conclusion.** The text is classified as **Positive** with magnitude 0.35. The strong positive contribution from "excellent" dominates, while the negated "high" now contributes mildly positive. In a real system, however, "high price" being negative in context would require domain knowledge or a contextual model (e.g., BERT) rather than a simple lexicon.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Q.A Type:**
> - **Marks lost** if you do not show the **synset constraint** check $\text{Pos}+\text{Neg}+\text{Obj}=1$.
> - **Marks lost** if you forget to apply **negation handling** for "high" — this is a deliberate trap.
> - **Marks lost** if you skip the polarity decision rule explicitly.
> - **Always** state $n$ (the number of words) before computing the average.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Machine Learning Approach Focus**

**(a) Explain the working of a Naive Bayes classifier for sentiment analysis. State the role of Laplace smoothing. [7 Marks]**
**(b) A sentiment classifier yields the following on a 200-document test set: TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 30, TN = 70. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score. Comment on whether the classifier is balanced. [7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**Naive Bayes for Sentiment Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Bayes' Theorem foundation.**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

For text classification, $C_k \in \{+, -, 0\}$ and $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is the word-feature vector of a document.

**Step 2 (2 marks): The "naive" conditional independence assumption.**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

This assumes each word contributes independently to the overall sentiment — a strong but effective assumption.

**Step 3 (1 mark): MAP decision rule.**

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Multinomial vs Bernoulli variants.**

- **Multinomial NB:** Uses word counts (frequencies) — preferred for long documents.
- **Bernoulli NB:** Uses binary presence/absence — preferred for short texts.

**Step 5 (1 mark): Laplace Smoothing.**

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + 1}{\text{count}(C_k) + \vert V \vert}$$

Necessary to handle words in the test set that did **not appear in the training set** (zero-frequency problem). Without smoothing, the entire posterior collapses to 0.

**Step 6 (1 mark): Pipeline summary.**

Text → Preprocess → Feature extraction (BoW / TF-IDF) → Compute $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ from training data → For each new doc, compute $P(+)$ and $P(-)$ → Predict argmax.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**Given:** TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 30, TN = 70. Total = 200.

**Step 1 (1 mark): Accuracy.**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{80+70}{200} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Precision.**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Recall.**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.7273}{0.80 + 0.7273} = \frac{1.1636}{1.5273} \approx 0.7619$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Balanced Classifier Comment.**

- Accuracy = 0.75, F1 = 0.762 → **close to each other**, indicating a **reasonably balanced** classifier.
- Precision (0.80) > Recall (0.7273) → the classifier is **more conservative**; it avoids false positives but misses some true positives.
- Class distribution: 110 positive (TP+FN) and 90 negative (TN+FP) → slightly imbalanced dataset, but performance is acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Q.B Type:**
> - **Common mistake:** Dividing Precision by Total instead of (TP+FP). Always double-check the denominator.
> - **Common mistake:** Forgetting to take harmonic mean in F1 (using arithmetic mean instead).
> - **Marks lost** if you do not comment on what precision/recall values *imply* about the classifier's behavior (e.g., "precision > recall → conservative model").
> - Always report F1 to **4 decimal places** for full credit in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Punchlist — Sentiment Analysis**

- **Definition:** Sentiment Analysis = Opinion Mining = classifying subjective text as +ve / −ve / neutral.
- **Three Levels:** Document → Sentence → **Aspect (ABSA)** — ABSA is the most granular and most researched today.
- **Liu's 5-Tuple Model:** $\langle \text{Entity, Aspect, Orientation, Holder, Time} \rangle$ — write this verbatim for full credit when asked to "define sentiment formally."
- **Three Approaches:** Lexicon, ML, DL — know **one example algorithm per category** (e.g., SentiWordNet, Naive Bayes, LSTM).
- **SentiWordNet Constraint:** $\text{Pos} + \text{Neg} + \text{Obj} = 1$ — always verify before scoring.
- **Aggregation Formula:** $S(T) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i [P_i - N_i]$ — show $n$ explicitly.
- **Turney PMI (2002):** $\text{SO}(p) = \text{PMI}(p, \text{excellent}) - \text{PMI}(p, \text{poor})$ — used when no lexicon exists.
- **Naive Bayes MAP rule:** $\hat{C} = \arg\max P(C_k) \prod P(x_i \mid C_k)$ — must show the **naive** independence assumption.
- **Laplace Smoothing formula:** $P(w \mid C) = \frac{N_{w,C}+1}{N_C + \vert V \vert}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **Negation Handling:** Always mention that a simple lexicon system needs a negation-window rule (e.g., "not good" → negative).
- **VADER:** Rule-based + lexicon, tuned for **social media** — board-favorite for "Twitter sentiment" questions.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Know all 4 — Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 — and their formulas.
- **F1 is Harmonic Mean, not arithmetic mean** — most common valuation trap.
- **Challenges to mention in theory answers:** Sarcasm, irony, domain dependence, code-mixed text, negation, comparative sentences.
- **ABSA Examples:** (Phone, Battery, Negative), (Hotel, Service, Positive) — tuple format is mandatory.
- **Hybrid approaches score best:** Modern SOTA = Lexicon features + ML classifier, or Transformer fine-tuning (BERT).
- **TF-IDF** is the most common feature representation in ML-based SA — be ready to write $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}_{t,d} \cdot \log \frac{N}{df_t + 1}$.
- **Recent SOTA:** Fine-tuned **BERT, RoBERTa, or domain-specific LSTMs** outperform lexicon methods by 10-15% F1.
- **Examiner loves:** numerical worked examples in 14-mark questions — practice SentiWordNet and Naive Bayes calculations.

> **End of Notes — Module 4 | Topic: Sentiment Analysis | KTU PEADT635**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Categorization and Summarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT635 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Categorization and Summarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Text Categorization (Text Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Text Categorization* (also called *Text Classification*) is the task of automatically assigning one or more predefined categorical labels to a free-text document based on its content. It is a fundamental **supervised learning** problem in Natural Language Processing where a model learns from labeled training documents $(d_i, y_i)$ where $d_i$ is a document and $y_i \in \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ is its class label from a fixed set of $k$ categories.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Librarian

Imagine you are a **librarian** who has to sort thousands of new books arriving daily into fixed shelves: *Science, Literature, History, Technology*. Instead of reading every book, you train a junior librarian by showing them already-sorted books. The junior learns patterns — books with words like *"equation, molecule, experiment"* go to **Science**; books with *"novel, protagonist, chapter"* go to **Literature**.

**Text Categorization works the same way:**
- The **librarian** = the classification algorithm (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM, Neural Network)
- The **already-sorted books** = the labeled training corpus
- The **new books** = unlabeled test documents
- The **shelves** = the predefined class labels

### Common Applications of Text Categorization

| Domain | Example Task | Classes |
|---|---|---|
| 📰 News | Topic routing | Politics, Sports, Tech |
| 📧 Email | Spam filtering | Spam / Ham |
| 💬 Sentiment | Opinion mining | Positive / Negative / Neutral |
| 🎫 Support | Ticket routing | Billing, Technical, General |
| 🏥 Medical | Triage | Urgent, Routine, Non-urgent |

---

## 1.2 Text Summarization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Text Summarization* is the process of producing a **shorter version** of one or more source documents while preserving the most important semantic content and overall meaning. The output (called a *summary*) is a compressed representation, typically expected to retain at least the key entities, events, and conclusions of the source.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The Executive Assistant

Your boss gives you a **50-page industry report** and asks for "the gist in 2 pages." You don't rewrite it word-for-word (that would be plagiarism) nor do you copy random pages. You:
1. **Read** the whole report
2. **Identify** the central arguments and key statistics
3. **Either** pick the most important sentences verbatim (**extractive**) **or** rewrite the ideas in your own words (**abstractive**)

A text summarization system does precisely this — automatically, at scale, across millions of documents.

### The Two Grand Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **Extractive Summarization** — selects and concatenates *existing* important sentences/phrases directly from the source document. No new text is generated.
>
> **Abstractive Summarization** — *generates* novel sentences that paraphrase and synthesize information, potentially using words not present in the source (similar to human-written summaries).

### High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU 2024

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Corpus** | The full collection of documents used |
| **Document** | A single unit of text (article, email, review) |
| **Class Label** | The category assigned to a document |
| **Feature Vector** | Numerical representation $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ of a document |
| **Training Set** | Labeled data used to fit model parameters |
| **Test Set** | Held-out data used to evaluate the model |
| **Summary** | Compressed output of a summarization system |
| **Gold Summary** | Human-written reference summary used for evaluation |

---

## 1.3 Why These Tasks Matter in KTU & Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes, understanding categorization and summarization is essential because:
> - They form the **backbone of nearly every industrial NLP pipeline** (search, recommendation, legal-tech, biomedical NLP).
> - They illustrate the **two main learning paradigms** in NLP: classification (supervised) vs. generation (semi-supervised/neural).
> - They provide the **mathematical foundations** (Bayes' rule, information theory, vector space models) tested in KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Text Classification Pipeline

Text Categorization follows a strict pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding each stage.

### Stage 1 — Text Preprocessing
1. **Tokenization** — split text into words/subwords
2. **Lowercasing** — normalize case
3. **Stop-word removal** — drop common words (the, is, a)
4. **Stemming / Lemmatization** — reduce to root form (running → run)
5. **Normalization** — handle punctuation, numbers, special tokens

### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering
Convert text → numerical vector. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with:

#### (a) Bag-of-Words (BoW)
A document is represented by a vector of word counts.

$$x_i = \big[\,c(w_1, d_i),\; c(w_2, d_i),\; \ldots,\; c(w_V, d_i)\,\big] \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size and $c(w_j, d_i)$ is the count of word $w_j$ in document $d_i$.

#### (b) TF-IDF Weighting
Raw counts favor long documents. **TF-IDF** (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency) corrects this.

$$tf\text{-}idf(t, d, D) = tf(t, d) \times idf(t, D)$$

where:

$$tf(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

$$idf(t, D) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

- $f_{t,d}$ = frequency of term $t$ in document $d$
- $N$ = total number of documents in corpus $D$
- $df_t$ = number of documents containing term $t$

#### (c) Embeddings
Modern systems (per KTU 2024 advanced topics) use dense vectors like **Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT**.

### Stage 3 — Model Training
Apply a classifier. The three most KTU-relevant ones are:

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Text Categorization & Summarization module. **Memorize this — these appear every year in ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c)\,P(c)}{P(d)}$ | Foundation of Naive Bayes |
| 2 | Naive Bayes Prediction | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Independence assumption |
| 3 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w,c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V \vert}$ | Avoids zero-probability |
| 4 | TF | $tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency in document |
| 5 | IDF | $idf(t,D) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | Rarity across corpus |
| 6 | TF-IDF | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \times idf(t,D)$ | Final weight |
| 7 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x}\Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{y}\Vert}$ | Used in extractive summarization |
| 8 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality measure |
| 9 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage measure |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 11 | Macro-F1 | $F_{macro} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} F_{1,j}$ | Average across classes |
| 12 | ROUGE-N (Recall) | $ROUGE\text{-}N = \dfrac{\sum_{S \in Ref} \sum_{gram_n \in S} Count_{match}(gram_n)}{\sum_{S \in Ref} \sum_{gram_n \in S} Count(gram_n)}$ | Summarization evaluation |
| 13 | ROUGE Precision | $ROUGE\text{-}N_{P} = \dfrac{\sum_{S \in Hyp} \sum_{gram_n \in S} Count_{match}(gram_n)}{\sum_{S \in Hyp} \sum_{gram_n \in S} Count(gram_n)}$ | System output side |
| 14 | TextRank Score | $WS(V_i) = (1 - d) + d \sum_{V_j \in In(V_i)} \dfrac{w_{ji}}{\sum_{V_k \in Out(V_j)} w_{jk}} \, WS(V_j)$ | PageRank variant |
| 15 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Information content |

### 🚨 Critical: Why Naive Bayes is "Naive"

> [!WARNING]
> The Naive Bayes classifier makes the **conditional independence assumption**: every feature $w_i$ is assumed independent of every other feature $w_j$ given the class $c$. This is mathematically strong and almost never true in real text (the words *"New"* and *"York"* are clearly correlated). Despite this, Naive Bayes works *surprisingly* well in practice — a favorite KTU viva question!

$$P(d \mid c) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

---

## 2.3 Text Summarization: Theoretical Foundations

### 2.3.1 Extractive Summarization Approaches

| Method | Core Idea | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Centroid-based** | Pick sentences close to document centroid | Simple, fast | Misses nuance |
| **TextRank / LexRank** | Graph-based PageRank on sentence graph | Unsupervised | Slow on huge corpora |
| **LSA-based** | Singular Value Decomposition on term matrix | Captures latent topics | Mathematically heavy |
| **Supervised** | Train classifier (sentence = summary?) | High accuracy | Needs labeled data |

### 2.3.2 The TextRank Algorithm (Mihalcea & Tarau, 2004)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TextRank** is the *de facto* unsupervised extractive summarization algorithm in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It is essentially PageRank applied to a **graph of sentences**.

**Steps:**
1. **Build the graph** — each sentence $S_i$ is a node.
2. **Compute edge weights** — for any two sentences $S_i, S_j$, the weight $w_{ij}$ is the **content overlap** (e.g., cosine similarity of TF-IDF vectors).
3. **Run PageRank** — iteratively update the score of each node:

$$WS(V_i) = (1 - d) + d \cdot \sum_{V_j \in In(V_i)} \frac{w_{ji}}{\sum_{V_k \in Out(V_j)} w_{jk}} \, WS(V_j)$$

where $d$ is the **damping factor** (typically $d = 0.85$).

4. **Pick top-k sentences** by score, in original order.

### 2.3.3 Abstractive Summarization

Modern abstractive methods (per KTU 2024 advanced section) use the **encoder-decoder** paradigm with attention:

- **Encoder** — reads the source document and produces a context vector sequence
- **Decoder** — generates the summary one token at a time
- **Attention** — lets the decoder "look back" at relevant source tokens

Architectures tested in KTU 2024: **Seq2Seq + Attention, Pointer-Generator Networks, Transformer-based (BART, T5, PEGASUS)**.

### 2.3.4 Evaluation Metrics

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ROUGE** with **BLEU**. ROUGE is for *summarization* (recall-oriented), BLEU is for *translation* (precision-oriented).

**ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation):**
- **ROUGE-N** — n-gram overlap (e.g., ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2)
- **ROUGE-L** — Longest Common Subsequence
- **ROUGE-W, ROUGE-SU** — weighted and skip-bigram variants

**Example — ROUGE-1 Recall:**

$$ROUGE\text{-}1_R = \frac{\text{Number of unigrams in common with reference}}{\text{Total unigrams in reference}}$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Categorization Use | Summarization Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Tech** | Classify case law by jurisdiction/topic | Summarize judgments for lawyers |
| **Healthcare** | Route patient messages to specialists | Summarize clinical notes into EMR |
| **Finance** | Sentiment analysis of news | Earnings call summarization |
| **Education** | Auto-grade essays | Generate chapter notes from textbooks |
| **Cybersecurity** | Phishing email detection | Summarize threat intelligence reports |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Naive Bayes Classification on a Toy Corpus

> This is a classic KTU 2024 Part-B 14-mark problem. We will solve it exhaustively.

### Problem Statement
You are given the following tiny training corpus (already preprocessed):

| Document | Text (tokens) | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $d_1$ | chinese beijing chinese | $c$ (China) |
| $d_2$ | chinese chinese shanghai | $c$ |
| $d_3$ | chinese macao | $c$ |
| $d_4$ | tokyo japan chinese | $\neg c$ (not China) |

**Test document:** $d_5 =$ *"chinese chinese tokyo japan"*

**Task:** Predict $\hat{c}$ using Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing $(\alpha = 1)$.

### Step 1 — Build the Vocabulary
The vocabulary $V$ is the set of unique tokens across all training docs:

$$V = \{\text{chinese, beijing, shanghai, macao, tokyo, japan}\} \Rightarrow \vert V \vert = 6$$

### Step 2 — Compute Prior Probabilities $P(c)$ and $P(\neg c)$

$$P(c) = \frac{\text{docs in class } c}{\text{total docs}} = \frac{3}{4}$$

$$P(\neg c) = \frac{1}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Likelihoods $P(w \mid c)$ for class $c$
Concatenate all docs in class $c$:

$$D_c = \text{"chinese beijing chinese chinese chinese shanghai chinese macao"}$$

- Total tokens in $D_c$: $n_c = 8$
- $\text{count}(\text{chinese}, c) = 5$
- $\text{count}(\text{beijing}, c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{shanghai}, c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{macao}, c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{tokyo}, c) = 0$
- $\text{count}(\text{japan}, c) = 0$

Apply Laplace-smoothed Multinomial NB:

$$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{n_c + \alpha \vert V \vert} = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{8 + 6}$$

$$P(\text{chinese} \mid c) = \frac{5 + 1}{14} = \frac{6}{14} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

$$P(\text{tokyo} \mid c) = \frac{0 + 1}{14} = \frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$$

$$P(\text{japan} \mid c) = \frac{0 + 1}{14} = \frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$$

### Step 4 — Compute Likelihoods $P(w \mid \neg c)$ for class $\neg c$

$$D_{\neg c} = \text{"tokyo japan chinese"}$$

- Total tokens: $n_{\neg c} = 3$
- $\text{count}(\text{chinese}, \neg c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{tokyo}, \neg c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{japan}, \neg c) = 1$
- $\text{count}(\text{beijing}, \neg c) = 0$
- $\text{count}(\text{shanghai}, \neg c) = 0$
- $\text{count}(\text{macao}, \neg c) = 0$

$$P(w \mid \neg c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, \neg c) + 1}{3 + 6} = \frac{\text{count}(w, \neg c) + 1}{9}$$

$$P(\text{chinese} \mid \neg c) = \frac{1 + 1}{9} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$

$$P(\text{tokyo} \mid \neg c) = \frac{1 + 1}{9} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$

$$P(\text{japan} \mid \neg c) = \frac{1 + 1}{9} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$

### Step 5 — Compute Posterior for Test Document $d_5 = \{\text{chinese, chinese, tokyo, japan}\}$

By the independence assumption:

$$P(d_5 \mid c) = P(\text{chinese} \mid c)^2 \cdot P(\text{tokyo} \mid c) \cdot P(\text{japan} \mid c)$$

$$= \left(\frac{3}{7}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{14} \cdot \frac{1}{14}$$

$$= \frac{9}{49} \cdot \frac{1}{196} = \frac{9}{9604} \approx 9.37 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(d_5 \mid \neg c) = P(\text{chinese} \mid \neg c)^2 \cdot P(\text{tokyo} \mid \neg c) \cdot P(\text{japan} \mid \neg c)$$

$$= \left(\frac{2}{9}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{2}{9} = \left(\frac{2}{9}\right)^4 = \frac{16}{6561} \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Bayes' Rule (work in log-space to avoid underflow)

$$c^* = \arg\max_c \left[\log P(c) + \sum_{i} \log P(w_i \mid c)\right]$$

For class $c$:

$$\log P(c) + \sum \log P(w_i \mid c) = \log\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) + 2\log\!\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) + \log\!\left(\frac{1}{14}\right) + \log\!\left(\frac{1}{14}\right)$$

$$= -0.2877 + 2(-0.8473) + (-2.6391) + (-2.6391) = -7.2605$$

For class $\neg c$:

$$\log P(\neg c) + \sum \log P(w_i \mid \neg c) = \log\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) + 4 \log\!\left(\frac{2}{9}\right)$$

$$= -1.3863 + 4(-1.5041) = -7.4027$$

### Step 7 — Final Decision

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c\{\,-7.2605,\ -7.4027\,\} = c \;\;(\textbf{China})$$

> **Conclusion:** The test document *"chinese chinese tokyo japan"* is classified as **China**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — TF-IDF Computation (Step-by-Step)

### Problem
Given a corpus of $N = 3$ documents:
- $D_1 =$ *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $D_2 =$ *"the dog sat on the log"*
- $D_3 =$ *"cats and dogs are pets"*

Compute the TF-IDF vector for the word **"sat"** in $D_1$.

### Step 1 — Term Frequency of "sat" in $D_1$

$$tf(\text{sat}, D_1) = \frac{f_{\text{sat}, D_1}}{\sum_{t' \in D_1} f_{t', D_1}} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

### Step 2 — Document Frequency of "sat"

"sat" appears in $D_1$ and $D_2$ but not $D_3$, so $df_{\text{sat}} = 2$.

### Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency

$$idf(\text{sat}, D) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{df_{\text{sat}}}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.4055$$

(Using natural log. If base 2 is required per KTU convention, divide by $\ln 2$.)

### Step 4 — TF-IDF

$$tf\text{-}idf(\text{sat}, D_1, D) = 0.1667 \times 0.4055 \approx 0.0676$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — TextRank Score Update (Small Graph)

### Setup
Three sentences $S_1, S_2, S_3$ with symmetric similarity weights:

$$w_{12} = w_{21} = 0.5, \quad w_{13} = w_{31} = 0.3, \quad w_{23} = w_{32} = 0.7$$

Damping $d = 0.85$. Initialize all $WS = 1.0$.

### Iteration 1

$$WS(S_1) = (1 - 0.85) + 0.85 \left[\frac{0.5}{0.5 + 0.7} \cdot 1 + \frac{0.3}{0.3 + 0.7} \cdot 1\right]$$

$$= 0.15 + 0.85 \left[\frac{0.5}{1.2} + \frac{0.3}{1.0}\right] = 0.15 + 0.85[0.4167 + 0.3]$$

$$= 0.15 + 0.85 \times 0.7167 = 0.15 + 0.6092 = 0.7592$$

(Continue similarly for $S_2, S_3$, then iterate until convergence.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Categorization vs. Summarization

| Axis | Text Categorization | Text Summarization |
|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Discrete class label | Sequence of text (extractive or abstractive) |
| **Learning type** | Supervised (mostly) | Supervised, unsupervised, or semi-supervised |
| **Key metric** | Accuracy, F1, Macro-F1 | ROUGE, BLEU, METEOR |
| **Historical method** | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression | Lead-N, TextRank, LSA |
| **Modern method** | BERT, RoBERTa fine-tuning | BART, T5, PEGASUS, GPT-based |
| **Difficulty** | Easier (mature) | Harder (open-ended generation) |
| **Output length** | Fixed (1 of $k$) | Variable (compression ratio) |

---

## 3.5 Evaluation Worked Example — Precision, Recall, F1

### Confusion Matrix (binary)
- $TP = 50, \;\; FP = 10, \;\; FN = 5, \;\; TN = 100$

### Step 1 — Precision

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.8333$$

### Step 2 — Recall

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} \approx 0.9091$$

### Step 3 — F1-Score

$$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9091}{0.8333 + 0.9091} = \frac{1.5151}{1.7424} \approx 0.8696$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The classifier is **recall-leaning** — it catches most positives but raises some false alarms. For spam filtering this is *bad*; for cancer screening this is *good*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Text Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stop-word Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Stemming / Lemmatization]
    B3 --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> C1[Bag of Words]
    C --> C2[TF-IDF]
    C --> C3[Word Embeddings]
    C1 --> D[Vectorized Documents]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Train Classifier]
    E --> E1[Naive Bayes]
    E --> E2[Support Vector Machine]
    E --> E3[Neural Network]
    E1 --> F[Predicted Class Label]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Evaluation]
    G --> G1[Accuracy]
    G --> G2[Precision and Recall]
    G --> G3[F1 Score]
```

## 4.2 Naive Bayes Classification — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Document d] --> B[Tokenize into w1 to wn]
    B --> C[For each class c in C1 to Ck]
    C --> D[Compute Prior P of c]
    C --> E[Compute Likelihood Product]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F[Compute Posterior for class c] --> G{Score Greater Than Current Max}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update Best Class]
    G -- No --> I[Discard]
    H --> J{All Classes Checked}
    I --> J
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Return argmax class c star]
```

## 4.3 Extractive Summarization — TextRank Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Document] --> B[Sentence Segmentation]
    B --> C[Compute TF-IDF Vectors]
    C --> D[Build Similarity Matrix]
    D --> E[Construct Sentence Graph]
    E --> F[Apply PageRank Iteratively]
    F --> G[Rank Sentences by Score]
    G --> H[Pick Top-K Sentences]
    H --> I[Sort by Original Order]
    I --> J[Output Summary]
```

## 4.4 Abstractive Summarization — Encoder Decoder with Attention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Document Tokens] --> B[Encoder BiLSTM or Transformer]
    B --> C[Context Vector Sequence h1 to hn]
    C --> D[Decoder with Attention]
    D --> E[Generate Token by Token]
    E --> F[Output Summary y1 to ym]
    F --> G[Compare with Gold Summary]
    G --> H[Compute ROUGE and Loss]
    H --> I[Backpropagate and Update]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block — Extractive vs. Abstractive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Text Summarization] --> B[Extractive]
    A --> C[Abstractive]
    B --> B1[Select Existing Sentences]
    B1 --> B2[Methods: TextRank, LexRank, Centroid, LSA]
    B2 --> B3[Strength: Faithful, Fast]
    B2 --> B4[Weakness: Not Fluent]
    C --> C1[Generate New Sentences]
    C1 --> C2[Methods: Seq2Seq, Pointer-Gen, BART, T5]
    C2 --> C3[Strength: Human-like]
    C2 --> C4[Weakness: Hallucination Risk]
```

## 4.6 Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Summary] --> B[Tokenize]
    C[Reference Summary] --> B
    B --> D[Compute N-gram Overlap]
    D --> E[ROUGE-N Score]
    D --> F[BLEU Score]
    D --> G[ROUGE-L LCS Score]
    E --> H[Final Evaluation Report]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
